###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-29T11:51:39-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EAE9F8B.80800@newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <01c30ac6$981ac740$e893f243@chottel> <3eaabd5d.167825654@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
 >
 > To further confuse the issue, there are two algorithms with that
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
 > is the latter?

Erm, Radix Exchange is most definitely a radix sort.  It sorts based on
the absolute values of the keys, not their values relative to one
another.  That's what distinguishes radix sorts from comparison sorts.

I don't have a copy of Knuth v2 handy here, but I'd be surprised if the
Radix Exchange sort he describes is different from the standard Radix
Exchange sort (which is not "radix sort followed by an exchange sort").
Standard radix exchange swaps elements based on the value of the
appropriate bit position in their keys.  (In the standard example, after
the first pass, all the records with keys that begin with a 0 bit will
come before all records with keys that begin with a 1 bit.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-04-30T03:43:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c30eca$891f0500$ac95f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <01c30ac6$981ac740$e893f243@chottel> <3eaabd5d.167825654@news.optonline.net> <3EAE9F8B.80800@newsguy.com>`

```
From Knuth's volume 3 Sorting and Searching, 2nd printing, March 1976, page
123:

"Radix Exchange, ... makes use of the binary representation of the keys, so
it is intended only for binary computers. Instead of comparing two keys
with each other, this method inspects individual bits of the keys, to see
if they are 0 or 1. In other respects it has the characteristics of
exchange sorting, and , in fact it is rather similiar to quicksort. Since
it depends on radix 2 representations, we call it "radix exchange sorting".
"

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eaf3bc2.462370524@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <01c30ac6$981ac740$e893f243@chottel> <3eaabd5d.167825654@news.optonline.net> <3EAE9F8B.80800@newsguy.com>`

```
Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>another.  That's what distinguishes radix sorts from comparison sorts.

Read this:

http://www.geocities.com/josephgama/GamaSort.htm
>
>I don't have a copy of Knuth v2 handy here, but I'd be surprised if the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>come before all records with keys that begin with a 1 bit.)

You're wrong on several counts: 

1.  Radix proceeds right to left, not left to right. After the first pass,
records that END with 0 would preceed records that end with 1. 

2.  Knuth's description was poor.  It shows in his timings, posted here.

3.  Radix Exchange normally IS a Radix sort followed by an Exchange (or other)
sort. That is clear in Sedgewick, not so clear in Knuth. 

Forget appeals to authority. Look at the code and results posted here. My
radix-insertion, written in COBOL, runs seven times faster than the Micro Focus
library function. 

Granted, some of the speed can be explained by lack of generalization. If I were
writing a generalized sort and they key was not a string, I'd hand it off to the
MF library function. The corrolary is that when the key IS a string (which it is
99% of the time), the library function should have used the radix-insertion
algorithm.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Radix sort

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-25T02:09:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea86ae5.15618351@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>There was a paper that I can't get to at
>http://www.etk.com/papers/sorting/sorting.htm
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>complicated and long the Quicksort algorithm was compared to the others.

Quicksort is not difficult or lengthy, but it requires recursion. Historically
and traditionally, COBOL didn't support recursion. Newer compilers do, using
local-storage (MF) or similar. 

>If your sort is 23 times faster than QuickSort (with any but specialized data),
>then you probably should patent it.  

It's public domain. You can find a good treatment in Sedgwick and a poor one in
Knuth. Heck, you can find it an IBM 084 card sorter manual. <g>

I wrote a COBOL radix sort this morning in about two hours. Improvement was 7:1,
not quite the 10:1 I predicted. Later I realized I should have turned off bounds
checking and turned on optimization. I'll try that tomorrow. 

I used Micro Focus because it has a readily available 2002-style table sort. I'm
guessing it's probably a Quicksort. Performance seems to be N log(N). With
another compiler I'd have to write my own baseline algorithm or call a C library
function. I didn't want to spend the time. This one sorts 50,000 30-byte random
numbers in .24 seconds compared to Micro Focus' 1.7 seconds. With the right
compiler options, it'll probably go to .15 seconds. 

I'd appreciate timings for MF on a PC platform. I don't have a native code MF PC
compiler. 

My 23:1 was for hand crafted asm vs. whatever the C library was written in. If
it was written in C, that would explain half the difference. 

      $SET SOURCEFORMAT"FREE"
 identification division.
 program-id. radix.
 author. Robert Wagner.
* Testbed for radix-sort.
* results: Sparc Ultra 10, 450 MHz, 10 x sort
*
*    N log(N)      16.75
*    radix cN+      2.40
*
 data division.
 working-storage section.
 01  test-data GLOBAL.
     05  n                           comp  pic s9(09). *> current card
     05  first-n                     comp  pic s9(09). *> first card
     05  radix-columns               comp  pic s9(02).
     05  card      occurs 50000.   *> the data to sort
         10  sort-key.
             15  key-column occurs 30      pic x(01).
     05  next-ns.     *> link to next card
         10  next-n occurs 50000     comp  pic s9(09).
     05  next-n-1s.   *> new order for next iteration
         10  next-n-1 occurs 50000   comp pic s9(09).

 01  init-data.
     05  init-n                      comp  pic s9(09).
     05  init-first value 1          comp  pic s9(09).
     05  init-columns value 7        comp  pic s9(02).
     05  init-card occurs 50000.
         10  init-key-1                    pic v9(18).
         10  init-key-2                    pic v9(12).
     05  .
         10  init-next occurs 50000  comp  pic s9(09).
     05  .
         10  init-next-1 occurs 50000 comp pic s9(09).

 01  timer-variables.
     05  repeat-factor value 10      comp  pic s9(09).
     05  previous-key                      pic  x(30).
     05  card-count                  comp  pic s9(09).
     05  start-time.
         10  start-hours                   pic  9(02).
         10  start-minutes                 pic  9(02).
         10  start-seconds                 pic  9(02).
         10  start-hundredths              pic  9(02).
     05  end-time.
         10  end-hours                     pic  9(02).
         10  end-minutes                   pic  9(02).
         10  end-seconds                   pic  9(02).
         10  end-hundredths                pic  9(02).
     05  elapsed-time                      pic  z(04).99.

 procedure division.
   perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > 50000
       compute init-key-1 (n) = function random
       compute init-key-2 (n) = function random
       compute init-next (n) = n + 1
       compute init-next-1 (n) = 0
   end-perform
   move zero to init-next (50000)

   display 'N log(N) sort'
   perform timer-on
   perform repeat-factor times
       move init-data to test-data
       call 'mf-sort'
   end-perform
   perform verify-sort
   perform timer-off

   display 'radix sort'
   perform timer-on
   perform repeat-factor times
       move init-data to test-data
       call 'radix-sort'
   end-perform
   perform verify-sort
   perform timer-off

   goback

 . verify-sort.
   move zero to previous-key, card-count
   move first-n to n
   if  n equal to zero
       display 'test failed, no first'
   end-if
   perform until n equal to zero
       if  sort-key (n) less than previous-key
           display 'test failed '
               previous-key space sort-key (n)
           move zero to n
       else
           move sort-key (n) to previous-key
           move next-n (n) to n
           add 1 to card-count
       end-if
   end-perform
   if  card-count not equal to 50000
       display 'test failed, count is ' card-count
   end-if

 . timer-on.
     accept start-time from time
 . timer-off.
     accept end-time from time
     compute elapsed-time rounded =
        (((((((end-hours * 60) +
           end-minutes) * 60) +
           end-seconds) * 100) +
           end-hundredths) -
        ((((((start-hours * 60) +
           start-minutes) * 60) +
           start-seconds) * 100) +
           start-hundredths)) / 100
     display  elapsed-time ' secs'
 .

 identification division.
 program-id. mf-sort.
* Performance of a typical N log(N) sort.
 procedure division.

* if you do not have MF, call qsort() or other
   sort card on ascending sort-key

   perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > 50000
       compute next-n (n) = n + 1
   end-perform
   move zero to next-n (50000)
   goback.
 end program mf-sort.

 identification division.
 program-id. radix-sort.
* Demo radix sort
 data division.
 working-storage section.
 01  radix-variables.
     05  c                         comp   pic s9(02).
* reverse this if your machine is small-endian
     05  pocket                    comp   pic s9(04).
     05       redefines pocket.
         10                               pic  x(01).
         10  pocket-column                pic  x(01).
     05  swap-count                comp   pic s9(09).
     05  last-n                    comp   pic s9(09).
     05  previous-n-0              comp   pic s9(09).
     05  previous-n-1              comp   pic s9(09).
     05  previous-n-2              comp   pic s9(09).
     05  previous-card.
         10  previous-key                 pic  x(30).
     05  radix-pockets.
         10  radix-pocket occurs 256.
             15  first-in-pocket   comp   pic s9(09).
             15  last-in-pocket    comp   pic s9(09).

 procedure division.
*  one iteration per column. Speed is cN
   perform varying c from radix-columns by -1 until c < 1
*  RUN through the input string, tossing entries into pockets
       move low-values to radix-pockets
       move zero to pocket
       move first-n to n
       perform until n equal to zero
*   determine pocket
           move key-column (n, c) to pocket-column
           add 1 to pocket
           if  first-in-pocket (pocket) equal to zero
               move n to first-in-pocket (pocket)
           end-if
           move last-in-pocket (pocket) to last-n
*   link to previous last in pocket
           if  last-n not equal to zero
               move n to next-n-1 (last-n)
           end-if
*   make this last in pocket
           move n to last-in-pocket (pocket)
           move next-n (n) to n
       end-perform
*  GATHER - link tail of each pocket to head of next
       move zero to first-n, last-n
       perform varying pocket from 1 by 1 until pocket > 256
           move first-in-pocket (pocket) to n
           if  n not equal to zero
               if  first-n equal to zero
                   move n to first-n     *> first card
               end-if
               if  last-n not equal to zero
                   move n to next-n-1 (last-n)  *> link first to last
               end-if
               move last-in-pocket (pocket) to last-n
           end-if
       end-perform
       move zero to next-n-1 (last-n) *> last card
*  MOUNT - input is now in the new order
       move next-n-1s to next-ns
   end-perform

*  If radix-columns = 30, the above is all you need.
*  By itself, it runs faster than the MF sort when radix-columns < 18.
*  The following is optimization. It permits radix-columns
*  to be set less than 30 for speed. It then merges out of order
*  cards into place. It almost always works in two iterations,
*  will run once if the above radix put entries into perfect sequence.
*  On the test data, this happens with radix-columns is 7 or more.

*  MERGE out of order cards
*  This is a modified insertion sort which does not
*  require backward links. When finding a step down, it searches
*  forward looking for a step up, then exchanges the big record before
*  the step down with the little record before the step up, then takes
*  a step backward. It cannot work in one pass because the step back
*  cannot find (n - 2) when there are two consecutive steps back.
*  I don't think this is a documented algorithm; I just made it up.

   move 1 to swap-count
   perform until swap-count = zero
       move zero to previous-key, previous-n-0, previous-n-1
       move zero to swap-count
       move first-n to n
       perform until n equal to zero
           if  sort-key (n) less than previous-key
               perform until n equal to zero or
                   sort-key (n) not less than previous-key
                   move n to previous-n-2
                   move next-n (n) to n
               end-perform
*  exchange two cards
   call 'cbl_xor' using card (previous-n-1) card (previous-n-2) by value 30
   call 'cbl_xor' using card (previous-n-2) card (previous-n-1) by value 30
   call 'cbl_xor' using card (previous-n-1) card (previous-n-2) by value 30
*  alternative to the above 3 lines, if you don't like xor
*              move card (previous-n-1) to previous-card
*              move card (previous-n-2) to card (previous-n-1)
*              move previous-card to card (previous-n-2)
               move previous-n-0 to n
               if  n equal to zero
                   move previous-n-1 to first-n, n
               end-if
               add 1 to swap-count
           end-if
           move previous-n-1 to previous-n-0
           move n to previous-n-1
           move sort-key (n) to previous-key
           move next-n (n) to n
       end-perform
   end-perform

   goback.
 end program radix-sort.
 end program radix.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Radix sort

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-25T15:09:00+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8a8uv$j7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea86ae5.15618351@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> Quicksort is not difficult or lengthy, but it requires recursion.

No. it doesn't _require_ recursion, though typically it is implemented 
using recursion.  It is just as easy to implement using iteration.

>>If your sort is 23 times faster than QuickSort (with any but specialized
>>data), then you probably should patent it.

I note that you didn't claim '23 times faster than Quicksort' but '23 times 
faster than qsort()'.  The latter is a very generalised routine that has 
unavoidable overheads to implement generality.  

> I used Micro Focus because it has a readily available 2002-style table
> sort. I'm guessing it's probably a Quicksort. Performance seems to be N
> log(N). 

I thought you were going to sort one of your 'lists'.

> My 23:1 was for hand crafted asm vs. whatever the C library was written
> in. If it was written in C, that would explain half the difference.

And generalising your code into a function with a similar spec to qsort() 
may explain the rest.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Radix sort

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-25T11:06:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea910da.58109665@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea86ae5.15618351@news.optonline.net> <b8a8uv$j7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>I note that you didn't claim '23 times faster than Quicksort' but '23 times 
>faster than qsort()'.  The latter is a very generalised routine that has 
>unavoidable overheads to implement generality.

True. Especially the call to a user supplied swap. They should have provided a
default function that swaps fixed length strings. 

>> I used Micro Focus because it has a readily available 2002-style table
>> sort. I'm guessing it's probably a Quicksort. Performance seems to be N
>> log(N). 
>
>I thought you were going to sort one of your 'lists'.

I did. The entries are sequenced by forward-pointing subscripts. I don't
physically rearrange entries except in the merge phase, which is not executed in
the demo because the radix sort does the whole job. The merge could have been
done by swapping pointers, as well, but the code would have been longer and
harder to understand at a glance. 

>> My 23:1 was for hand crafted asm vs. whatever the C library was written
>> in. If it was written in C, that would explain half the difference.
>
>And generalising your code into a function with a similar spec to qsort() 
>may explain the rest.

It was generalized, with the exception of the swap function. It sorted strings
and big-endian integers only, not floating point. It also sorted lists with true
pointers, which the 'generalized' qsort() cannot do.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Radix sort

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-04-29T01:33:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0304290033.3fdf9cf3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea86ae5.15618351@news.optonline.net> <b8a8uv$j7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ea910da.58109665@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >faster than qsort()'. 

> True. Especially the call to a user supplied swap. They should have provided a
> default function that swaps fixed length strings. 

No wonder your use of qsort() was slow, that user defined function is
not to swap the two, it is only to compare them.

    qsort(array, number, width, func)

    "" func is passed pointers to the two elements being compared, it
is to return an integer less than, equal to, or greater than zero
depending on ..."

For a default compare use strcmp() if appropriate.  That is _why_
strcmp returns the results it does.

If you've been swapping the two array elements, no wonder you made it
slow.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Radix sort

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-26T18:42:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eaacf33.172392018@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea86ae5.15618351@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

I tried optimizing the Radix code I posted here. 

1.  Turned bounds checking off and optimization on.

Speed improved only slightly. 

Also, I discovered my initial timing of Micro Focus' sort was off. There must
have been another job running when I timed it. At this point, Radix is 4.5 times
faster than Micro Focus.

2.  Changed compiler options.

When we discussed options to 'make all my COMPs something specific', we
overlooked one. Micro Focus has COMP, which says 'make all my COMPs COMP-X'.
That's machine native arithmetic, without COBOL's requirement for overflow
checking. In other words, bypass checking whether s9(4) exceeds 9,999. Sounds
like just the ticket. Results showed it to be slower. 

Overflow checking can be bypassed with the NOTRUNC option. That improved it
some. So did IBMCOMP, which is similar to putting SYNCHRONIZED on everything. It
inserts slack bytes in order to put variables on word boundaries. I wouldn't
have thought word boundaries mattered to a modern machine. They do to this RISCy
SPARC, but only slightly.

For laughs, I tried COMP-3. Results were only 1.5 times slower. Hmmm, maybe
numeric format isn't that important. Maybe it's spending most of its time doing
something else. That insight produced the answer below.

3.  Changed key size. 

Keep in mind, both of these sorts have speed proportional to N, the number of
entries. The number of characters (c) in an entry shouldn't make any difference.
Someone might ask, 'Doesn't it take twice as long to compare 60 bytes as 30?'
The answer is no. The machine or library function stops on the first unequal. It
takes twice as long to MOVE 60 bytes, but I don't move keys. I do it all with
pointer-like subscripts. 

           key len   30       18        60        30
    N log(N)      12.08     3.71      7.20   IBMCOMP
    radix            2.68     1.88      2.27      2.26
    ratio             4.5        2.0       3.2        5.3

The findings defy logical explanation. Radix speed was relatively flat, but
Micro Focus' performance was all over the place. Increasing the key from 18 to
30 made it run 3.2 times slower. Further increasing the key to 60 made run 1.6
times FASTER. How can that be? Could there be a boundary condition? With key
size 18 and 50,000 entries, the table size is 900K, just under the range of a
16-bit pointer. Increasing the size to 30 would kick it into 32-bit 'mode', if a
SPARC has such modes, which I doubt. I repeated each test at least five times to
make sure I wasn't getting a false reading. Also, I was watching 'top' in
another window to insure other tasks weren't active. 

Does this mean Micro Focus' sort is NOT an (n ln(n)) performer? My instinct says
no, it's something in the environment. I cannot imagine an algorithm which goes
faster when keys are bigger. 

4.  Eliminate library calls

Examining generated code shows MANY instructions per line of COBOL -- generally
10-20. Worse, there are library calls for every use of a subscript and every
string comparison. When there are two subscripts, sending and receiving, there
are two calls. After changing one of the subscripts to an index and examining
the code, I see the library call is gone. AHA. Speed is not a function of
numeric format -- comp vs. comp-5 -- nor bounds checking nor optimization (loop
control produces only 4-5 instructions), it is a function of library calls. 

I rewrote the program using exclusively indexes, no subscripts. Not because
indexes are inherently faster, rather because they produce inline code. Well
almost always. My first attempt was setting an index on one table to an index on
another. That produced a library call, which made performance about the same as
subscripted. The second version uses a single table. It generates no library
calls at all. It runs 1.6 times faster than typical subscripted versions. 

The latest finding: 1.72 seconds for 10 sorts, which is 7.0 times faster than
Micro Focus.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** joe_zitzelberger@vsam.org (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-04-29T10:53:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c99d007f.0304290953.4eba78a2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3EA8F0CB.BB80CBE2@worldnet.att.net>`

```
As promised a few weeks ago, Quicksort for pointers.

Easy to code, easy to understand.  If I were a smart-ass I might say
that some people need a few pointers on the ease (of ability) of
quicksorting linked lists, but they wouldn't be very punny.

Enjoy...

       Identification Division.
       Program-ID. PTRQSORT is recursive.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      * This is a cobol implementation of quicksort for a linked list.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       Environment Division.
       Data Division.
       Local-Storage Section.

       01 PTRQSORT-Local-Storage.

         05 Lo                   Pointer.
         05 Hi                   Pointer.

         05 Swap-Key             Pic X(8).
         05 Swap-Data            Pic X(72).

         05 Pivot                Pic 9(08) Value 0.

       Linkage Section.

       01 Lo-Item.
         05 Lo-Prev              Pointer.
         05 Lo-Next              Pointer.
         05 Lo-Key               Pic 9(08).
         05 Lo-Data              Pic X(72).

       01 Hi-Item.
         05 Hi-Prev              Pointer.
         05 Hi-Next              Pointer.
         05 Hi-Key               Pic 9(08).
         05 Hi-Data              Pic X(72).

       01 Low-Element            Pointer.
       01 High-Element           Pointer.

       Procedure Division using Low-Element High-Element.

           Set address of Lo-Item to Low-Element
           Set address of Hi-Item to High-Element

      * ----- A simple performance enhancement for Quicksort is to
      * ----- use a less resource intensive sort when the partitions
      * ----- become very small.  This will network sort partitions
      * ----- of size 2 or 3.  Otherwise it will continue
partitioning.
           Evaluate true

             when (Low-Element = High-Element)
                Continue

             when (Low-Element = Hi-Prev) or (Lo-Next = High-Element)
                Perform Network-Sort-2

             when (Lo-Next = Hi-Prev)
                Perform Network-Sort-3

             when other
              Set Lo to Low-Element
              Set Hi to High-Element
              Perform Select-Pivot

      * ----- Loop through table until indices cross
              Perform until (Hi = Lo)

      * -------- Locate an item that should not be in less partition
                 Set address of Lo-Item to Lo
                 Perform until (Lo = High-Element)
                            or (Lo = Hi)
                            or (Lo-Key >= Pivot)
                    Set Lo to Lo-Next
                    Set address of Lo-Item to Lo
                 End-Perform

      * -------- Locate an item that should not be in greater
partition
                 Set address of Hi-Item to Hi
                 Perform until (Hi = Low-Element)
                            or (Hi = Lo)
                            or (Hi-Key <= Pivot)
                    Set Hi to Hi-Prev
                    Set address of Hi-Item to Hi
                 End-Perform

      * -------- Exchange the two and keep looking
                 If ( Lo Not = Hi )
                    Perform Swap-Elements
                 End-If

              End-Perform

              Perform Qsort-Partitions

           End-Evaluate

      *--------------------------------------------------------------*
      * Return in a manner that does not wreck our environment
      *--------------------------------------------------------------*
           Goback.

      *--------------------------------------------------------------*
      * Select a pivot guarenteed not to be a worst case
      *--------------------------------------------------------------*
       Select-Pivot.
           Set address of Lo-Item to Lo
           Set address of Hi-Item to Hi
           Compute Pivot = Function Mean ( Lo-Key Hi-Key )
           Exit.

      *--------------------------------------------------------------*
      * Exchange elements in the wrong partition
      *--------------------------------------------------------------*
       Swap-Elements.
           Move Lo-Key    to Swap-Key
           Move Lo-Data   to Swap-Data
           Move Hi-Key    to Lo-Key
           Move Hi-Data   to Lo-Data
           Move Swap-Key  to Hi-Key
           Move Swap-Data to Hi-Data
           Exit.
           
      *--------------------------------------------------------------*
      * Sort the two sub-partitions
      *--------------------------------------------------------------*
       Qsort-Partitions.

      * -- Sort the less sub-partition
           Set address of Hi-Item to Hi
           If Hi Not = High-Element
              Call 'PTRQSORT' Using
                 Low-Element
                 Hi-Prev
              End-Call
           End-If

      * -- Sort the greater sub-partition
           Set address of Lo-Item to Lo
           If Lo Not = Low-Element
              Call 'PTRQSORT' Using
                 Lo
                 High-Element
              End-Call
           End-If

           Exit.
      
      *-----------------------------------------------------------
      * Sort small partitions using networks
      *------------------------------------------------------------
       Network-Sort-2.
           Set address of Lo-Item to Low-Element
           Set address of Hi-Item to High-Element
           If Lo-Key > Hi-Key
              Perform Swap-Elements
           End-If
           Exit.
     
       Network-Sort-3.
           Set address of Lo-Item to Low-Element
           Set address of Hi-Item to Lo-Next
           If Lo-Key > Hi-Key
              Perform Swap-Elements
           End-If
           Set address of Hi-Item to High-Element
           Set address of Lo-Item to Hi-Prev
           If Lo-Key > Hi-Key
              Perform Swap-Elements
           End-If
           Set address of Lo-Item to Low-Element
           Set address of Hi-Item to Lo-Next
           If Lo-Key > Hi-Key
              Perform Swap-Elements
           End-If
           Exit.
     
       End Program PTRQSORT.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-04-29T18:37:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0304291737.118171b2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3EA8F0CB.BB80CBE2@worldnet.att.net> <c99d007f.0304290953.4eba78a2@posting.google.com>`

```
joe_zitzelberger@vsam.org (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote 

> Easy to code, easy to understand.  If I were a smart-ass I might say
> that some people need a few pointers on the ease (of ability) of
> quicksorting linked lists, but they wouldn't be very punny.

This has two differences from what RW described as his 'lists' and
claimed to be advantageous.

First your list also has prior pointers which are used. While this is
certainly not wrong in any way, it is not how RW had created his list.
The requirement for prior pointers makes the list creation slower and
the code more complex.

The second is that RW had claimed an advantage for lists in the
ability to have variable sized data items. This means that data cannot
be simply swapped between two elements.  It may be that new elements
are malloc()ed and the old ones free()ed, or the pointers are adjusted
in the next and prior elements.

This again makes the code much more complex, and the run time much
slower.  As RW's lists were already slower (or slow as) sequentially
accessed tables, then the claimed 'advantages' were disappearing.

The issue that I raised was whether RW could sort one of _his_ lists,
not whether someone else could create a better list.

In fact the issue of sorting a list is a moot one anyway.  Why not
just create a tree and not only have it already sorted when complete,
but also have fast access via the tree structure.

Of course the code for that will be even more complex (don't forget
you may need rotations) and likely much slower than a table load, SORT
and SEARCH.

Solutions should be appropriate to the problem.  The issue that I took
with RW was his insistence 'that we should try them and won't look
back'.  He was arguing with his ego, his lists give no advantage over
a table for what I do.

(BTW: EXIT should be in a paragraph by itself and is just a noise
word, but I guess it is just a style thing unless you want
portability)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-29T11:43:05-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EAE9D89.8000708@newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
 > I was being conservative when I said 10:1. My testing results against
 >  the C library qsort() were 23:1.

Since the "C library qsort" can be implemented using any sorting
algorithm whatsoever, including a radix sort, and may be done well or
poorly, it's not a very useful benchmark (though as a first check it's a
data point, anyway).

Exchange-radix sorts tend to perform similarly to NlgN sorts like
(properly written) Quicksort.  Straight radix sorts can offer O(N) (ie
linear) performance, but only with significant additional memory
consumption, and the inner loop is relatively expensive.  (There's a
good short discussion of the tradeoffs in Sedgewick's _Algorithms_ books.)

The simplest kinds of radix sorts are bitwise - they consult one bit of
the key at a time - and so are tough to implement in COBOL dialects that
don't have fast bitwise operators.  The linear radix sort consumes the
key in chunks, which can be of native object size (eg bytes), so it
doesn't have this problem.

Radix sorts aren't ideal for all sorting applications, but they are an
important class of algorithms.  They're worth a look for those
interested in sorting problems.  For practical purposes, though, I still
think the best approach to a large sorting problem is to use an external
sorting package.  *I* don't want to make a career out of building a
better sort.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-30T08:02:33-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EAFBB59.4050904@newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <3EAE9D89.8000708@newsguy.com> <217e491a.0304291706.9829@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
 >
 > The function required by qsort() is a compare and the standard one
 > supplied by the library may be strcmp().

strcmp is not suitable as a comparison function for qsort.  qsort passes
to its comparison function pointers to the elements being compared.  If
you are sorting an array of strings in C, you're really sorting an array
of pointers to char; qsort will pass to the comparison function two
pointers to pointers to char, which strcmp will not operate on properly.

If you're sorting an array of char, strcmp will get pointers of the
right type, but obviously it won't do what you want, since it'll be
trying to treat your chars as strings.  (Not a problem, of course, if
there are no duplicate values, since strcmp's comparison will stop on
the first byte.  But not a good idea in the general case.)

The qsort discussed in the second edition of Kernighan and Richie,
section 5.11, isn't the standard C library qsort (and hence is illegal
for any conforming program in a hosted environment - it's not a good
choice for the name of an example function; probably held over from K&R1).

See the comp.lang.c FAQ, question 13.8.

Also, strcmp is the wrong type for a qsort comparison function.  qsort's
comparison function parameter is "int (*)(const void *, const void *)" -
a pointer to a function taking two void pointers to data it will not
modify, and returning an integer.  strcmp is "int strcmp(const char *,
const char *)".  Because the standard requires that void* and char* have
the same representation (a guarantee not made for pointers to
non-character types, though those pointers can be converted to and from
void*), a conforming implementation has to invoke the comparison
function in a way that's compatible with a call to strcmp (though it
won't work correctly when it gets there).

The implementation can, however, issue a diagnostic and refuse to
translate the translation unit if strcmp is supplied as the comparison
function without a cast.  Function-pointer types are only compatible if
their parameters are compatible types; pointer types are only compatible
if they point to compatible types; it's a constraint violation to supply
a parameter which is not assignment-compatible to an object of the type
declared in the function's prototype.  In fact, since it's a constraint
violation, a conforming implementation *must* issue a diagnostic if you
pass a bare "strcmp" to qsort as its comparison parameter.

In short, Robert's right on this one: the standard library doesn't
supply qsort comparison functions.  That's because it's trivial to make
your own, of course.

 > If indeed he had coded it as a swap then that may explain how he made
 >  it run so slowly.

Yup, that would be bad.
```

```
Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
> > I was being conservative when I said 10:1. My testing results against
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>good short discussion of the tradeoffs in Sedgewick's _Algorithms_ books.)

I cited Sedgewick. Straight radix is O(cN), where c = number of bytes in the
key. The code I posted did a radix sort on the first seven bytes to get entries
in approximate order (as it turned out, exact order) followed by a modified
insertion sort to shuffle nearby entries. I anologized this to using a card
sorter and collater. 

It turned out to be seven times faster than whatever Micro Focus provides, which
I'm guessing is an NlnN performer, probably Quicksort. My results are invariant
to key size whereas Micro Focus' performance is erratic when key size is
changed.  Granted, mine cannot sort floating-point nor negative integers as the
Micro Focus sort can. OTOH, mine could easily be modified to sort a linked list.


>The simplest kinds of radix sorts are bitwise - they consult one bit of
>the key at a time - and so are tough to implement in COBOL dialects that
>don't have fast bitwise operators.  The linear radix sort consumes the
>key in chunks, which can be of native object size (eg bytes), so it
>doesn't have this problem.

Bitwise would be too slow because it requires an iteration per bit. I considered
going the other way  -- a card sorter with 64K pockets. 

>Radix sorts aren't ideal for all sorting applications, but they are an
>important class of algorithms.  They're worth a look for those
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>better sort.

It's not a career, it's a demonstration that NlnN algorithms such as Quicksort
are NOT the fastest. It was a prompt to CS types to get over their fascination
with Quicksort and recursion.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T10:01:58+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6kvg4$vj3$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>Are lists always more cost effective than fixed format data?
> 
> Almost always. Speed is about the same, but lists have the advantage being
> (nearly) infinitely expandable and use only the memory required. In the
> jargon, they're 'scaleable'.

Tables and Lists have _different_ characteristics and are rarely 
interchangable.

Lists _might_ save space, that depends entirely on the nature of the data 
stored and the overheads.  If the text data is very variable in length then 
the ends of the text will be saved, but against that there is the 'next' 
pointer (and possibly 'previous') and the 'malloc' header overhead (which 
is invisible).  With text that is constrained and nearly always of full 
length than a list item will be _larger_ than a table item by many bytes.

Of course the table size is fixed and the data may use only the initial 
part of a table.  Whether the list is larger or smaller in total bytes 
depends on how well there is a match between the actual data size and the 
table size.

And, of course, if a table only uses the front part of a table then your 
argument was that the rest will never be paged in so who cares.

With speed, however, you are wrong.  Lists that are accessed via a 'next' 
pointer can _only_ be traversed serially.  Every random access must start 
at the first item and load every item until the one found.  This may be 
similar to serial table access.

But tables may be accessed at any item and binary chop searches are _much_ 
faster than serially searching a list.  Tables can also be used as hashed 
tables which are infinitely faster than serial lists.

Also try sorting a serial list using bubble or quick sort.

Evaluate the data, choose your algorithm, _then_ choose a suitable storage 
mechanism.  

If I want 'nearly infinite expansion' and scalability then I will probably 
also want sharability and reuse.  Lists don't do that.  With very large 
data sets the load and unload time may be unsuitable if there is a small 
hit rate.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T18:46:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8f02da.40002483@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b6kvg4$vj3$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>table size.

Your points apply only to a one-dimensional table. In a table with two or more
dimensions, the entire row will be paged in when the first element is
referenced. 

I know this is poor design but for the sake of example, picture a compiler
parsing into a table with a row per statement and a column per word. What's the
maximum number of words in a statement .. 500? When you touch the first word,
the other 499 (up to the 2K page boundary) will be paged in. Memory will be
sparsely populated. 

>And, of course, if a table only uses the front part of a table then your 
>argument was that the rest will never be paged in so who cares.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>similar to serial table access.

No, we can avoid the problem by building indexes or, better yet, trees. Files
and database 'tables' (a misnomer) are lists. We don't search them serially. 

>But tables may be accessed at any item and binary chop searches are _much_ 
>faster than serially searching a list.  Tables can also be used as hashed 
>tables which are infinitely faster than serial lists.

Advocates of such primitive techniques are the same kind who are afraid to use
DEPENDING ON. They are fond of saying they believe in the 'KISS principle'. They
fill the table's tail with high-values and let the binary chop waste most of its
time searching through them. 

>Also try sorting a serial list using bubble or quick sort.

Now you're into an area where I have lots of experience. I have demonstrated
that sorting lists is _much_ faster than sorting tables .. on the order of five
to ten times faster. Why? Because exchanging entries involves 4-6 pointer
operations rather than moving 500 byte 'records'. 

I wrote a list-based radix/insertion sort that was 23 times faster than the C
library's qsort(). It beat heap, shell and others by a similar margin. 

>If I want 'nearly infinite expansion' and scalability then I will probably 
>also want sharability and reuse.  Lists don't do that.  With very large 
>data sets the load and unload time may be unsuitable if there is a small 
>hit rate.

In that case, you do it on disk, as databases and indexed file systems do. They
let buffering/cacheing take care of memory management. 

In an ideal world, 'object space' would be the ONLY address space visible to a
program. First, hardware guys gave us the memory address space. When that proved
inadequate, software guys gave us file systems. When that too proved inadequate,
another set of guys gave us databases. Now we have three 'address spaces' for
programmers, administrators and software to manage .. none of them giving us
(directly) what we really want: access to an object. 

In the New Dawn operating system I wrote, 'address space' was the combined size
of hard disks. You referenced an object by its handle. Software translated that
to a linear address. When you touched a non-resident object, hardware signalled
page fault, which brought it into memory and constructed the appropriate
_hardware level_ mapping between virtual and memory address. There were no
memory addresses visible to the program; memory was a big cache. And there was
no file system. You could create the equivalent of directories, files and
records by using the collection class. 

That's start-from-scratch clean design. I had it working ten years ago.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Power Cobol Question

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Mudd Bug" <muddbug@cox.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-01T03:46:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fY7ia.61064$4v3.56827@news1.central.cox.net>`

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to compute Easter

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<3e85d13c.331687202@news.optonline.net> <3E87D98D.72A606F@fujitsu-siemens.com> <3E88E5E6.CCAA5E2@attglobal.net>`

```
Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net> wrote:

>I believe that the variable "easter" is still a relative day value, and has to
>be converted back to a Gregorian date.  Robert's code got as far as making it a
>Sunday date, but didn't quite finish the job. 

Correct. I responded to Rosa with code to convert Easter's 'absolute' date to
Gregorian.

> I simplified and recoded (see
>below), taking advantage of Instinsic Functions, and eliminating the need to
>consider Leap Year. 

The code below doesn't show that. 

>By the way, are you in Deutschland?  One of my wife's relatives, Franz
Hauschka,
>works for Fujitsu-Siemens in Augsburg (and Ingeborg Rosa is my wife's name).

You are asking Rosa. I changed my surname to Wagner to honor Richard Wagner,
emblematic of Germanic culture. If I had it to do over, I would have chosen
Bouche, DuVal or some other common French name.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to compute Easter

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-01T01:45:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e88e2be.100284850@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3e85d13c.331687202@news.optonline.net> <3E87D98D.72A606F@fujitsu-siemens.com>`

```
Rosa Fischer <Rosa.Fischer@fujitsu-siemens.com> wrote:

>
>
…[65 more quoted lines elided]…
>easter holidays.

037730 is the correct 'absolute' date for Easter in 2003. To convert that to a
Gregorian date, you need this additional code.  I added comments showing how it
arrives at the correct 20030420.


     05  gregorian-date.
         10  century                         pic  9(02).
         10  yymmdd.
             15  yy                         pic  9(02).
             15  month-da                   pic  x(04).
     05  gregorian-date-rd redefines gregorian-date.
         10  year                           pic  9(04).
         10  month                          pic  9(02).
         10  da                             pic  9(02).
     05  absolute-date                      pic  9(06).
     05  work-year                          pic  9(03).
     05  day-of-week-work                   pic  9(01).
     05  quotient                           pic  9(06).
     05  temp-date                          pic  9(04)v9(02).
     05  temp-date-rd redefines temp-date.
         10   temp-days                     pic  9(04).
         10   leap-year-flag                pic  9(02).
     05  pascal-year                        pic  9(04).
     05  pascal-sub                         pic  9(02).

     05  days-to-month-values value
              '000031059090120151181212243273304334365'.
         10   days-to-month occurs 13 indexed x-month pic  9(03).

     05  pascal-values value
              '04140403032304110331041804080328041604050325'.
        10   pascal-date   occurs 11       pic  x(04).

 compute-easter.
     compute work-year = year - 1900
     divide 19 into work-year giving quotient
         remainder pascal-year
     compute pascal-sub = pascal-year + 1
     if  pascal-year greater than 10
         subtract 11 from pascal-sub
     end-if
     move pascal-date (pascal-sub) to month-da
     if  pascal-year greater than 10
         subtract 1 from da
     end-if
     perform compute-absolute-date
     compute absolute-date = absolute-date - day-of-week-work + 7 *>modified
     perform convert-to-gregorian               *> new
     display 'Easter is on ' gregorian-date    *> new
     goback.

 compute-absolute-date.
     compute work-year = year - 1900
     compute absolute-date =
         (work-year * 365.25) + days-to-month (month) + da
     divide 4 into work-year giving temp-date
     if  leap-year-flag equal to zero and month less than 3
         subtract 1 from absolute-date
     end-if
     divide 7 into absolute-date
         giving quotient remainder day-of-week-work.

 convert-to-gregorian. *> entire paragraph is new
     divide 365.25 into century-date giving work-year *> 103
     compute temp-date = century-date - (work-year * 365.25) *> 109.25
     if  leap-year-flag not equal to zero                    *> 110.25
         add 1 to temp-days
     end-if
     set x-month to 2
     search days-to-month
       when temp-days not greater than days-to-month (x-month)
           continue
     end-search                                             *> 05
     compute year = work-year + 1900
     set month to x-month
     subtract 1 from month                                  *> 04
     compute da  = temp-days - days-to-month (x-month - 1). *> 20
     if  leap-year-flag equal to zero and month less than 3
         add 1 to da
         if  da greater than 31
             move '0201' to month-da
         end-if
     end-if.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to compute Easter

- **From:** Louis Krupp <lkrupp@pssw.NOSPAMPLEASE.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2003-04-01T02:53:11-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E896187.3040906@pssw.NOSPAMPLEASE.com.invalid>`
- **References:** `<3e85d13c.331687202@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303311521.2e46ced@posting.google.com> <3e88f477.104823251@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
<snip>
> My sources referred to the 'paschal full moon', which I assumed was a
> misspelling of Pascal. 
> 
> Did I screw up AGAIN?

Sort of, but don't be too hard on yourself;  there may be more of a
connection than you think.

As far as I can tell, "Paschal" means "of Easter," keeping in mind
that the Greek and Latin (and therefore French) words for Easter
(Greek: "Pascha," French: "Paques") come from the Hebrew word for
Passover, with which Easter coincides.

I looked up "Pascal" in a web site about French names, and it seems
to have been a popular first name in the Middle Ages, being given
to children born near Easter.  The first name may have become a
family name, Etienne Pascal was born Etienne Pascal, he named his
son Blaise, and the rest is history.

The English word "Easter" is said to come from the Anglo-Saxon name
for the goddess of Spring.

Louis
```

Joe,
I often make categorical statements, only to have to take them back.  I have
no CICS experience, so I tend to talk as if the on line world were made up
of IMS transactions, with which I am somewhat familiar.
Colin

> In article <3E888D63.9C7857E2@attglobal.net>,

> <snip>
> > *Mindless means doing a data base access to validate some data element
in
> > millions
> > of records, when there are only a few valid values for the data element.
> > After the
> > data base information is returned, and the validity is established, it
can be
> > tabled so that subsequent validations are a table lookup.  In one case
10
> > millions
> > DB2 calls were reduced to 95.  You can imagine how much CPU time was
reduced,
> > as
> > well as elapsed time.  Once, we found that an IMS data base was dumped
before
> > the
> > program that was doing "lookups" against it -- we changed the program to
a
> > two file
> > match and saved over 95% of CPU time.
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> performance problems...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Case Statement in DB2

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-01T01:03:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-3EE050.01032601042003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<20030327223829.08824.00000043@mb-fc.aol.com> <3E888D63.9C7857E2@attglobal.net> <joe_zitzelberger-0A8197.21240931032003@corp.supernews.com> <jo9ia.9019$1B.3756781@news3.news.adelphia.net>`

```
In article <jo9ia.9019$1B.3756781@news3.news.adelphia.net>,
 "Colin Campbell" <cmcampb@attglobal.net> wrote:

> Joe,
> I often make categorical statements, only to have to take them back.  I have
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I'm equally unfamilier with the IMS/DC world.  I've only ever used BMPs 
and CICS connections.

Under CICS transactions are allocated time slices under some unequal 
formula known to IBM and not me.  What I do know is that there are a 
finite amount, and they are not equal in size.  A short running 
transaction might be prempted by a long running transaction.  If 10,000 
people are running the short tran and 10 people running the long tran, 
the 10,000 might start seeing a much slower response as a result of the 
10.

In the above case the short tran becomes the symptom, but is not at all 
the problem.  The worst offenders tend to be dumps, but other things can 
bog down unrelated transactions.  Strobe is quite helpful in diagnosing 
these.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Case Statement in DB2

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2003-04-01T04:07:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030331230746.26171.00000311@mb-fd.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3E888D63.9C7857E2@attglobal.net>`

```
>From: Colin Campbell cmcampb@attglobal.net 
>Date: 3/31/03 1:48 PM Eastern Standard Time

>We have documented improvements in applications
>that have resulted in machine cost reductions of over $600,000.

If we could find improvements of this magitude it would justify the license. 
However so far the biggest CPU hogs aren't in things we as applications people
can control.  One of the biggest hogs we found was the DB2 optimizer of all
things.   

We have the product for 2 more weeks so I'm sure people are running strobes on
various other things besides what was used for an evaluation.  From what I've
heard around the coffee pot so far is no smoking gun has been found :).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Case Statement in DB2

- **From:** "Colin Campbell" <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-01T05:30:15+00:00

I wish you luck, but I think you wouldn't need luck if you had a report
showing where your biggest resource use is.  In the absence of a report, you
might try the following:
Do you have ANY batch jobs with an elapsed time greater than 1 hour?
Can you interview production control folks or operators to see which jobs
take a long time / cause processing windows to be missed / abend a lot?
Colin

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Case Statement in DB2

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2003-04-02T03:12:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030401221211.16972.00000113@mb-cp.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<Ht9ia.9020$1B.3757719@news3.news.adelphia.net>`

```
>From: "Colin Campbell" cmcampb@attglobal.net 
>Date: 4/1/03 12:30 AM Eastern Standard Time

>Do you have ANY batch jobs with an elapsed time greater than 1 hour?

None - I think the longest running job we have is maybe 15 minutes on a bad
day. The full daily schedule for my system runs about 35 minutes and the
monthly about 45.  I'm not real familiar with the warehousing system or
manufacturing systems etc.  They must run pretty fast as well as they are are
complete by the time mine kicks in at 11:30pm and our nightly schedule starts
at 8pm. 

>Can you interview production control folks or operators to see which jobs
>take a long time / cause processing windows to be missed / abend a lot?

I suppose I could - but 99% of the time processing windows are missed is
because the operators are 'out to lunch' so to speak and don't notify anyone a
job is down until maybe an hour or two after it crashed, or they have fooled
with it so many times trying to restart that they have their shorts in such a
knot a wedgie would feel good :)

As for abends - I get called maybe once a month on a job down - mostly because
of space (B37) abends.  Same for the others in my group.

Don't get me wrong - we're not an ideal shop by any means, it's just that our
major screw ups are handled mostly during normal working hours as no job crash
is critial to getting the on-lines operational the next morning except maybe 1
or 2 jobs that haven't abended in ages (I'll probably get called tonight
because I said this).
```

## Thread: COBOL index files — 15 msgs

### COBOL index files

- **From:** schmidtjo <member27397@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-01T04:20:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2713750.1049170852@dbforums.com>`

```

I have to write a short 1 page technical paper on how index files are
used in COBOL today (if they still are). Ive tried searching the net
but cant find any good resources. Any help would be greatly
appreciated, thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL index files

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-04-01T06:04:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E892CD6.54DB59D3@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<2713750.1049170852@dbforums.com>`

```


schmidtjo wrote:

> I have to write a short 1 page technical paper on how index files are
> used in COBOL today (if they still are). Ive tried searching the net
> but cant find any good resources. Any help would be greatly
> appreciated, thanks

Go to cobolreport.com and check out Bill Klein's COBOL FAQ. He doesn't
give you what you are after but lists all COBOL compiler vendors. Having
their site addresses, then search their on-line help books LRMs (Language
Reference Manuals) which itemize COBOL files in detail. They will list
only the technical background - and I'm afraid you will have to take it
from there.

What you may need to reference (again in Bill Klein's FAQ), are those
COBOL 'sites' that have examples using COBOL files.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL index files

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-01T12:59:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<03gia.44550$k8.1616548@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<2713750.1049170852@dbforums.com> <3E892CD6.54DB59D3@shaw.ca>`

```
Do a quick search on VSAM and ISAM using google - you will finds lots of nice information
about indexed file systems there.

You might also want to comment to your instructor that most database systems today, from the little
ones like MySQL to the very highest end DB/2, use indexed files at their core. They just layer lots of
other access protocols on top of them.

MySQL in particular, should give you some nice fodder for your paper.
-Paul

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL index files

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-01T18:12:00+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1rhj8vk8n95rb5l2m24khehe1kcmklklnk@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<2713750.1049170852@dbforums.com> <n8hj8vkmjhg33ndtm1aca2jgt86g0r8e9q@4ax.com>`

```
On Tue, 01 Apr 2003 19:59:11 +0300, Binyamin Dissen
<postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:

>On Tue, 01 Apr 2003 04:20:52 +0000 schmidtjo <member27397@dbforums.com> wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Typically index files are instantiated by the operating system.
Not correct.
Unix, Dos (and Windows) amonsgt others do NOT provide native indexed
files.
It is up to the COBOL vendor to implement this function with their own
(and normally proprietary) filesystem.
Others like IBM AS/400 do supply it in their base OS.

and I am sure others will point you to the right info on this one,
though it is widely available if you do a search in google.

FF

Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico _ fonseca at syssoft-int . com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL index files

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-01T17:44:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6cj5q$19o$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<2713750.1049170852@dbforums.com> <n8hj8vkmjhg33ndtm1aca2jgt86g0r8e9q@4ax.com> <1rhj8vk8n95rb5l2m24khehe1kcmklklnk@4ax.com>`

```

On  1-Apr-2003, Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com> wrote:

> >The question does not make a lot of sense.
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> files.

Which are more typical, CoBOL on these operating systems, or CoBOL on more
complete operating systems?

> It is up to the COBOL vendor to implement this function with their own
> (and normally proprietary) filesystem.

Only if there is none available.  Same thing with sorts.   When the OS includes
these things the CoBOL vendor does not need to implement this function and as
far as I know, they all interface with the native system to do the job.

If the vendor of the OS decides that it's job is to provide for mouses instead
of for file systems, it can call itself an OS.  But I don't have to.

> Others like IBM AS/400 do supply it in their base OS.

I've used two flavors of Univac, Burroughs, IBM mainframe, Vax, Honeywell & even
a beta operating system called Aspen.   They all had these basic functions
included in their operating systems.

So which is "typical"?
```

compilers are long extinct.

divide a into b on size error next sentence. It says next sentence should be
under IF, which confirms your point.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM and "Next Sentence" extension

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-01T14:54:20-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6d5as$1ud4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b6as5f$nv0$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3e890f0d.111630208@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e890f0d.111630208@news.optonline.net...

> There is no way to provide corrigible evidence because the '74 compliant
> compilers are long extinct.

Maybe on IBM systems, but not everywhere.  Unisys MCP/AS COBOL74 is alive
and well and has a very devoted following.  In that implementation there are
a few cases in which NEXT SENTENCE is allowed beyond those provided for in
the standard, but they are clearly documented as extensions to the standard.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM and "Next Sentence" extension

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-01T17:30:40-06:00

```
Chuck,
  This thread was simply limited to the (incorrect) claim that IBM allowed
NEXT SENTENCE in "non-Standard" situations.

FYI,
  Many sites do still have/use IBM's OS/VS COBOL (their '74 Standard
compiler).  If someone thinks that OS/VS COBOL *did* have such extensions,
they could post some code and I'll bet it could be tested with their
compiler.
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2003-04-01T10:44:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1049193780@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

## Thread: cobol identifiers — 7 msgs

### cobol identifiers

- **From:** Kris De Schutter <legolas.greenleaf@advalvas.be>
- **Date:** 2003-04-01T13:43:02+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6bto8$ak5$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be>`

```
Hi,

According to this (http://www.cs.vu.nl/grammars/browsable/vs-cobol-ii/) 
grammar of cobol an identifier can have something called a "leftmost 
character position".

Could any of you tell me what this is used for, and whether this is used 
frequently in real-life applications ?

Many thanks,

Kris De Schutter
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol identifiers

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-01T07:20:51-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AN6cnaSgktWuDxSjXTWcoA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<b6bto8$ak5$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be>`

```

"Kris De Schutter" <legolas.greenleaf@advalvas.be> wrote in message
news:b6bto8$ak5$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be...
> Hi,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Many thanks,

You have an alphanumeric data field. It contains characters (blank is a
character). The one on the left is the "leftmost character position."

Alternatively, you have an alphanumeric data field. It contains characters
(blank is a character). You want to access some of the field. The beginning
byte you wish to access is the leftmost character position. In the example:

01 MyData    Pic X(10) Value 'manuscript'.

Move MyData(3:2) to Whats-C-Over-Lamda

"n" is the leftmost character position of the intended move.

I'm guessing you are a beginner at COBOL. DO NOT read any more of the
document you referenced. The document is intended pick-noses, blowhards,
pedants, and four-flushers more interested in process than progress.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: cobol identifiers

- **From:** Kris De Schutter <legolas.greenleaf@advalvas.be>
- **Date:** 2003-04-01T15:49:22+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6c552$d5o$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<AN6cnaSgktWuDxSjXTWcoA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<b6bto8$ak5$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be> <AN6cnaSgktWuDxSjXTWcoA@giganews.com>`

```
Is this feature used often in realistic code ?

JerryMouse wrote:
> I'm guessing you are a beginner at COBOL.

True :-)

> DO NOT read any more of the
> document you referenced. The document is intended pick-noses, blowhards,
> pedants, and four-flushers more interested in process than progress.

Actually, I don't have much choice in the matter. I'm using their parser 
(in bison) for a project, and therefore that document is very relevant 
to my work.

Thanks for your help.

Kris De Schutter
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol identifiers

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-01T10:48:02-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f7ucnckLMLxcXxSjXTWcow@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<b6bto8$ak5$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be> <AN6cnaSgktWuDxSjXTWcoA@giganews.com> <b6c552$d5o$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be>`

```

"Kris De Schutter" <legolas.greenleaf@advalvas.be> wrote in message
news:b6c552$d5o$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be...
> Is this feature used often in realistic code ?
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The "left-most character" notation used in this document is an attempt to
define the obvious by academic fuddy-duddies. In the instance I gave, the
construct is called "Reference Modification." By that is meant: You
reference something (a data name), then you modify the reference (start at
the 15th byte) instead of using the implicit beginning of the variable as
originally contemplated by the language developers.

Almost every other language has the ability to reference data fields at the
byte level; COBOL did too, in the beginning, but the method was entirely too
cumbersome. Later implementations of the standard gave us other tools -
Reference Modification being one.

Is it used? You bet! Daily. Hourly. By almost every COBOL programmer.
Nobody, however, uses the "Leftmost Character" nomenclature.

If you can't avoid further reading of the document, drop the course and
demand your money back. Or at least threaten to put a hex (not base sixteen,
the other one) on the professor.
```

<a id="t-b5f6b23421"></a>
## Thread: Sort a working storage ? — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Sort a working storage ?

- **From:** "bunch" <bunch92(nospam)@yahoo.fr>
- **Date:** 2003-04-01T14:14:44+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6c393$lc3$1@lore.csc.com>`

```
Hello,
I need your help.

I have the following working storage :
01  W-SAVE-GI-RECORD.
    03  W-SAVE-GI OCCURS 10 TIMES INDEXED BY W-STORE-INDEX.
        05  W-SAVED-GI                      PIC X(3).

How can I sort the records in this working storage ?
Is there a special command or do I need to loop through the records and
store in a different table ?

Thank you for your help.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sort a working storage ?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-01T07:46:17-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6c57b$a33$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b6c393$lc3$1@lore.csc.com>`

```
Depends on your compiler.  For most '85 Standard compilers, you will need to
do the sorting yourself (via a loop).

However, Micro Focus (and possibly others - although I don't know of any) do
already support a "table sort" feature that is available in the 2002
Standard.

Finally, it is possible to use the "traditional file sort" (from the '85
Standard) statement with an input and output procedure to sort
working-storage items.  If you know how to use Input/Output procedures, you
just "release" all the elements of the table in your input procedure and
return them (in order) in your output procedure.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sort a working storage ?

- **From:** "bunch" <bunch92(nospam)@yahoo.fr>
- **Date:** 2003-04-01T14:54:25+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6c5jf$mlo$1@lore.csc.com>`
- **References:** `<b6c393$lc3$1@lore.csc.com> <b6c57b$a33$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b6c57b$a33$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net...
> Depends on your compiler.  For most '85 Standard compilers, you will need
to
> do the sorting yourself (via a loop).
>

Thanks, I'll have to go with the loop then.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sort a working storage ?

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-02T01:23:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8a2809.57796864@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b6c393$lc3$1@lore.csc.com>`

```
"bunch" <bunch92(nospam)@yahoo.fr> wrote:

>Hello,
>I need your help.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thank you for your help.

You don't need a different table; you can do it 'in situ'. When the number of
entries is small, as in 10, you can sort the table with a bubble sort. Pass
through comparing w-saved-gi (w-store-index) to w-saved-gi (w-store-index + 1).
If they are out of order, exchange them. When you make a pass with no exchanges,
you're done. 

When the number of entries is larger than approx. 100, bubble sort becomes
horribly inefficient compated to other algorithms. Those algorithms will be
introduced in the intermediate class. Here, it looks like the instructor is
prompting you to write a bubble sort.
```

- **References:** `<b6c393$lc3$1@lore.csc.com> <3e8a2809.57796864@news.optonline.net>`

```

> You don't need a different table; you can do it 'in situ'. When the number
of
> entries is small, as in 10, you can sort the table with a bubble sort.
Pass
> through comparing w-saved-gi (w-store-index) to w-saved-gi (w-store-index
+ 1).
> If they are out of order, exchange them. When you make a pass with no
exchanges,
> you're done.
>
> When the number of entries is larger than approx. 100, bubble sort becomes
> horribly inefficient compated to other algorithms. Those algorithms will
be
> introduced in the intermediate class. Here, it looks like the instructor
is
> prompting you to write a bubble sort.
>

Really clever ! I have done it the other way now but if I have the same
problem again, I will consider the bubble sort.
Cheers
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sort a working storage ?

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T23:27:17-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_R2cnSnsYbI_MRKjXTWcoA@wideopenwest.com>`
- **References:** `<b6c393$lc3$1@lore.csc.com> <3e8a2809.57796864@news.optonline.net>`

```
You may also try what I call an "Insert Sort" which means you basically use
a portion of a bubble sort to insert the values into the appropriate row as
they are populated. With this you could do anything from randomizing where
you start to look to using an "Index Sort"/"Shell Sort". Which means you
simply have another field which holds the index. Rows are simply appended
and then just the indexes are resorted as you insert them.
```
