#### ↳ Re: implicit scope termination (newbie)

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-03T15:04:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6hii0$o9h$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b6grbp$a8$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be>`

```

On  3-Apr-2003, Kris De Schutter <legolas.greenleaf@advalvas.be> wrote:

> Is this rule correct in general, or are there any exceptions I should be
> aware of (of course, with respect to COBOL'85) ?

Yes.   But I strongly recommend using explicit terminators and matching indents.
    I also strongly recommend that you never use "NEXT SENTENCE", which
transfers control to the next sentence (sentence is defined by a terminating
period).

Make it easy for the person doing maintenance to see all your terminators -
implicit or explicit.   The easiest way to do this is to make it explicit.

Others will go an extra step and tell you to never use periods except where
mandated by the compiler.   But if you follow the above recommendations, this
doesn't give you anything more useful than saving a moment in cutting and
pasting.
```

#### ↳ Re: implicit scope termination (newbie)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-03T13:29:44-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6i23b$8pq$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b6grbp$a8$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be>`

```
Kris,
  That 2nd bullet item was determined to be WRONG (or at least misleading)
in the original '85 Standard. The approved Correction Amendment (an official
part of the "revised third Standard COBOL" definition) was changed. The
following correction was made on page B-6 of X3.23b-1991,

"IV-27     Paragraph 4.4.4 entitled Explicit and Implicit Scope Terminators,
last paragraph, item 2,
    delete the last sentence that reads: "Examples of such phrases are ELSE,
WHEN, NOT AT END, etc."

The reason that this change was made was that you may ONLY have an implicit
scope terminator where a "conditional" statement (rather than an imperative
statement) is allowed - which basically limits it to IF statements.

This means that  the following is conforming

    If A = B
 *   Conditional statement is allowed in IF and follows
        If   C = D
            Perform Para1
       Else
          Display "implicit scope terminator is assumed to follow"
    Else
          Display "this statement is valid"
      .

While the following is NOT conforming (as you may not have a conditional
statement within a READ)

   Read  ABC
     At End
       If C = D
               Perform Para1
       Else
               Display  "implicit scope terminator is NOT allowed to end
this IF statement"
     Not At end
        Display "This statement will NOT compile with a conforming compiler
    End-Read

If you need a "full" discussion of why this correction was made, you need to
get a hold of the CIB (COBOL Information Bulletin).
```

#### ↳ Re: implicit scope termination (newbie)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-03T15:34:04-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6i9cg$1et$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b6grbp$a8$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be>`

```
Just wanted to add a couple of additional bits of information.

Your two examples ARE cases of "correct" use of implicit scope terminators
(because the conditional statements are nested within IF statements - where
such are allowed).

Also, in case it isn't clear, a "scope terminated" statement is  ALMOST
ALWAYS allowed where either a conditional or imperative statement is
allowed.  The "notable" exception is that you may NOT use an END-IF or
END-SEARCH phrase to delimit an IF or SEARCH statement where the NEXT
SENTENCE phrase is used - if you are limiting your code to ANSI/ISO
conforming source code.  Many implementations do allow even this - as an
extension.

Other than when a NEXT SENTENCE phrase is used in a nested program, I would
PERSONALLY recommend that you always use an explicit scope terminator - and
not rely on "implicit" ones.  When using a nested IF or SEARCH statement
with a NEXT SENTENCE phrase, I would see if you can/should "re-structure"
your code to get the desired effect
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: implicit scope termination (newbie)

- **From:** Kris De Schutter <legolas.greenleaf@advalvas.be>
- **Date:** 2003-04-04T09:36:31+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6jcdl$so3$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<b6i9cg$1et$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b6grbp$a8$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be> <b6i9cg$1et$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Hello everybody,

I'd like to thank everyone for their help sofar. However, there are 
still a few things I'm not clear on...

William M. Klein wrote:
> Other than when a NEXT SENTENCE phrase is used in a nested program, I would
> PERSONALLY recommend that you always use an explicit scope terminator - and
> not rely on "implicit" ones.

Of course; this is not an issue for me. However, I'm not writing COBOL 
code myself. I'm working on a legacy of COBOL 85 code, and this code 
does not take your advice to heart. But I still have to make sure that I 
understand these implicitly scoped statements correctly...

William M. Klein wrote:
> The 1st ADD statement is a CONDITIONAL statement with an implicit scope
> terminator - while the 2nd ADD statement is an "explicitly terminated"
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Does this make the differences clear?

Uhm, no. :-) I'm not following your terminology. (Apologies if this is 
supposed to be basic knowledge.)

What are considered implicit scope terminators ? Why not ELSE,
WHEN, NOT AT END, etc. ? (I tried to find that CIB via google, but no 
luck. Is there a place where I can find these ?)

Jerry Mouse wrote:
> There are always exceptions:
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Croaks.

How is this an exception ? If I had

IF A = B
   PERFORM 10 TIMES
     ADD 1 TO X
     MOVE X TO Y.

this would equal

IF A = B
   PERFORM 10 TIMES
     ADD 1 TO X
     MOVE X TO Y
   END-PERFORM
END-IF.

right ? And

PERFORM 10 TIMES
   IF A = B
     ADD 1 TO X
     MOVE X TO Y.

would be

PERFORM 10 TIMES
   IF A = B
     ADD 1 TO X
     MOVE X TO Y
   END-IF
END-PERFORM.

Basically my question is: when does a containing statement terminate the 
statements of a contained statement ?

Thanks,

Kris De Schutter
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: implicit scope termination (newbie)

- **From:** Kris De Schutter <legolas.greenleaf@advalvas.be>
- **Date:** 2003-04-04T11:28:51+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6jj08$urs$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<b6jcdl$so3$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be>`
- **References:** `<b6grbp$a8$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be> <b6i9cg$1et$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <b6jcdl$so3$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be>`

```
OK, my examples are incorrect as these PERFORMs have to be closed 
explicitly. Nevertheless, my question remains.

Regards,

Kris De Schutter
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: implicit scope termination (newbie)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-04T11:20:08-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6kes9$9k2$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b6grbp$a8$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be> <b6i9cg$1et$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <b6jcdl$so3$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be>`

```
Kris,
   What reference material (language reference or otherwise) are you using?

If you look at any of these - where they describe the formats of statements,
they should show you where you may nest an IMPERATIVE statement and where
you may nest a CONDITIONAL statement.  In fact, the ONLY place where you may
(in an ANSI/ISO conforming program) nest a conditional statement is within
an IF statement.

Because of this, an ELSE (which is legal within an IF statement) *may*
implicitly terminate a nested conditional statement.

On the other hand, a WHEN, an NOT AT END, or any other similar phrase
(besides ELSE) may only be used with statements that REQUIRE that what is
nested be an imperative statement.  This means that the nested statement
MUST end with an explicit scope terminator or it must already be an
imperative statement (with no conditional phrase of its own).

Does this help any?

P.S.  I will look and see if the appropriate CIB is available online.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: implicit scope termination (newbie)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Kris De Schutter <legolas.greenleaf@advalvas.be>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **In-Reply-To:** `<b6kes9$9k2$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

#### ↳ Re: Lower case to upper case conversion

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-03T11:04:12-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6hll3$3ck$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b6he6c$q4h$1@mailgate2.lexis-nexis.com>`

```
Hello,

        When you're about to convert them, are they still ASCII or have they
already been converted to EBCDIC?

        What version of COBOL are you executing?

        With the advent of COBOL2, the INSPECT command included the
CONVERTING keyword. With CONVERTING, if you setup your FROM and TO
character-strings as literals, COBOL will generate a single "TR" (Translate)
instruction, thus saving your a CALL to a COBOL Run-Time module. And yes,
I've been referred to as a CPU-Nazi ;o)

        With the advent of COBOL/370, the FUNCTION LOWER-CASE and UPPER-CASE
were added but the INSPECT CONVERTING with literals (COBOL2) is still more
efficient.

        Prior to COBOL2, you could convert from low-case to upper-case using
binary arithmetic, with the code being executed in-line.

        03  WS-LETTER PIC S9(04) COMP.
*
        MOVE +129 TO WS-LETTER.
        ADD +64 TO WS-LETTER.
*
        In the above example, decimal 129 (X'81') is a lower-case 'a' in
EBCDIC. By adding +64, it then becomes an upper-case 'A' (X'C1')

        Or you can use a table.

        03  WS-LETTER PIC S9(04) COMP VALUE +162.
        03  WS-LETTER-TBL-REC.
               05  FILLER PIC X(13) VALUE 'ABCDEFGHIJKLM'.
               05  FILLER PIC X(13) VALUE 'NOPQRSTUVWXYZ'.
        03  WS-LETTER-TBL REDEFINES WS-LETTER-TBL-REC
                                               OCCURS 26 TIMES PIC X(01).
        03  WS-SUB PIC S9(04) COMP.
*
        IF (WS-LETTER NOT <  129 AND
              WS-LETTER NOT > 137)
              COMPUTE WS-SUB = (WS-LETTER - 128)
        ELSE
               IF (WS-LETTER NOT <  145 AND
                     WS-LETTER NOT >  153)
                     COMPUTE WS-SUB = (WS-LETTER - 135)
               ELSE
                      COMPUTE WS-SUB = (WS-LETTER - 143).
*
        At this point, WS-SUB points to the table entry that contains the
corresponding upper-case letter. In this example, WS-SUB is equal to 19
(lower-case and upper-case 's').

HTH....

Bill

"Beverly" <Beverly.Owens@work.com> wrote in message
news:b6he6c$q4h$1@mailgate2.lexis-nexis.com...
> I'm processing a file from a PC system and the descriptions are in upper
> and lower case.  I would like to transform them to all upper case before

besides

>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Lower case to upper case conversion

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-03T13:57:01-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E8CAE2D.38F3B633@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<b6he6c$q4h$1@mailgate2.lexis-nexis.com> <b6hll3$3ck$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b6hom8$1ea$1@mailgate2.lexis-nexis.com>`

```
Beverly,
I wonder why you think that BEVERLY looks better than Beverly?  You could just
as well have fixed the data that is all upper case to change each word so that
characters 2 thru n were lower case, leaving character 1 as is.  Then all the
data in your report would be consistent, and would conform to the rules for
written language.

We used only upper case in the "old days" because computer printers ran faster
if the print trains didn't include those other 26 characters.  That doesn't
apply to today's laser printers on just about any platform.

More nostalgia:
Your users don't refer to their reports as "tab runs", do they?  (I can still
find bins at work which are labeled "tab runs only", meaning that computer
generated reports are to be put here for recycling.  The term "tab run"
referred to reports that were generated using an EAM (Electronic Accounting
Machine) called a Tabulator, which could be programmed using wired connections,
somewhat resembling a telephone switchboard.  I started in this business in
1966, and the company still had a few EAM's around, and a few guys who just
loved to "wire" them.  I never learned how.)
=====
Beverly wrote:

> (snip)
> Thanks
> Beverly (who is now happy her report will be in all UPPER case)
```

<a id="t-b2eeac7974"></a>
## Thread: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard? — 23 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-03T13:44:38-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E8CAB45.2477C152@attglobal.net>`

```
I couldn't help but put in my $0.02 after reading the entire thread
about Fujitsu COBOL, and the difficulty they have in telling users
whether a COBOL program conforms to the standard.

One reply from an employee of a vendor of hardware and COBOL that runs
on the hardware says that customers seem to be interested and expect
Unisys to participate in setting the standard and to conform to it.

One "user" pointed out that, having done many ports of COBOL
applications between different hardware platforms, the reason this
activity was usually so easy was -- the standard.

I submit that one reason several others said the standard is of no
concern to them was precisely because it _has_ existed for a long time
(at least, long in terms of the computer industry), and it _has_ been
followed by so many compiler creators.  If Unisys COBOL, IBM COBOL, MF
COBOL, Fujitsu COBOL, and many others didn't all treat a MOVE statement
exactly the same, as defined by the standard, it seems very likely to me
that there wouldn't BE so many COBOL compilers.  There wouldn't be more
COBOL programs in existence than any other language (or is it all others
combined?).  And there very likely WOULD be a Microsoft COBOL, and it
would be different from all the others!

We don't always have to think about, or even know, a standard for it to
be of value to us.

There is a standard named ISO 8601, titled "Data elements and
interchange formats - Information interchange - Representation of date
and times", which, if we'd all paid attention to it, might have saved
hundreds of billions of dollars in Y2K repair costs.  The "repairs" my
organization did for our customers still do not conform to that
standard.
```

#### ↳ Re: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-04T01:59:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3E8CAB45.2477C152@attglobal.net>`

```
Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net> wrote:


>There is a standard named ISO 8601, titled "Data elements and
>interchange formats - Information interchange - Representation of date
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>standard.

Most Y2K fixes I've seen in production code are incredibly short-sighted.  They
are good for 10 years. As we approach 2010, we'll have another date crisis. 

Some go out 30 or 50 years. They should use a 'sliding window' which would work
indefinitely.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-06T16:30:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e904f45.125114584@news.optonline.net>`

yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama) wrote:

>I haven't been in health care for about 9 years but at that time the subscriber
>databases and claims databases used twos complement to store dates.  Got some
>strange results if you forgot to use the formula to translate :)

They did that so dates would sort in descending order. Indexed files don't have
a descending option, so fields in the key must be complemented to accomplish
that.
```

have
>> a descending option, so fields in the key must be complemented to accomplish
>> that.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Some ISAM file handlers do allow descending sequence on keys.

Rather than using binary complement, I always do it 'the COBOL way'. Compute
key-date = 99999999 - regular-date followed by compute regular-date = 99999999 -
key-date. The formula is the same in both directions.
```

Fields are exactly the same size. Complementing doesn't save space.

>really had no reason to sort on them.

Wouldn't you want medical procedures sorted chronologically? Say, on a bill.

I'm familiar with dates in the context of price files, where the key is
part-number, date. When cutting an invoice, you look for the last price on or
before the invoice date.

I lived in Texas for many years and still maintain a residence there. So I'm
familar with conflict resolution Texas-style. In East Texas they use fists; in
West Texas they use wit.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T15:07:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EiCja.68$vQ2.50@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <20030404224216.25704.00000086@mb-cl.aol.com>`

```
The Y2K problem was anticipated by some in the 70s and 80s, but at that
time, we were assured that since the average system life span was 10-15
years, "Don't worry about it; use a two-digit year."  Look what happened.
:)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T10:41:06-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6mtei$p53$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <20030404224216.25704.00000086@mb-cl.aol.com> <EiCja.68$vQ2.50@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com>`

Terry Heinze <terryheinze@prodigy.net> wrote:
>The Y2K problem was anticipated by some in the 70s and 80s, but at that
>time, we were assured that since the average system life span was 10-15
>years, "Don't worry about it; use a two-digit year."

'Anticipated'?  Mr Heinze, I would argue that 'anticipation' occurred 
earlier; it might not be too far-fetched to conclude that someone in a 
bank, somewhere, said 'Hey... how are we going to calculate thirty-year 
mortgages?' in late 1969.

>Look what happened.
>:)

There is nothing unusual in programmers making a living due to managerial 
shortsightedness last I looked.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

```
I worked on a system, written in 1991, which used a ONE digit year. Why? Because
programmers were told it was temporary and would be replaced within 2-3 years.
When we fixed it in 1998, it was still writing accounts payable checks.


"Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net> wrote:

>The Y2K problem was anticipated by some in the 70s and 80s, but at that
>time, we were assured that since the average system life span was 10-15
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T19:12:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8f293c.49829208@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <20030404224216.25704.00000086@mb-cl.aol.com> <EiCja.68$vQ2.50@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Systems with a long time horizon, such as pension plans and bonds, always used a
four digit year. I was shocked to learn that most hospital systems used two
digits. We know they had patients older than 80. How did they compute the
patient's age? Well it came out negative. Sometimes software added 100, other
times it printed a negative number and 'everyone knew' how to make the
adjustment. Amazing.


"Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net> wrote:

>The Y2K problem was anticipated by some in the 70s and 80s, but at that
>time, we were assured that since the average system life span was 10-15
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T23:45:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yUJja.2189$kd1.2112121@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <20030404224216.25704.00000086@mb-cl.aol.com> <EiCja.68$vQ2.50@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com> <3e8f293c.49829208@news.optonline.net>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e8f293c.49829208@news.optonline.net...
> Systems with a long time horizon, such as pension plans and bonds, always
used a
> four digit year. I was shocked to learn that most hospital systems used
two
> digits. We know they had patients older than 80. How did they compute the
> patient's age? Well it came out negative. Sometimes software added 100,
other
> times it printed a negative number and 'everyone knew' how to make the
> adjustment. Amazing.

Electronic health care claims were (and still are) submitted in a format
known as "National Standard Format."

Until early 2001, two NSF versions (one for professional claims, one for
facility claims) were still in use - versions using a six-character "patient
date of birth." Hmm, does  '00' mean 1800, 1900 or 2000?

(New NSF standards were released in, oh, mid '98 or so using eight-character
dates. Just that not a whole lot of people found it very important to
update).

MCM
```

the same things?

Sanjay Minni <sminni_news@minisoftindia.com> wrote:
>> >COBOL team is very much there, but with
>> >expertise in MF-COBOL applications in PC environment
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>Can that be matched

Ummmmm... this is similar to an old joke.

'How much are your potatoes?'

'Ten cents a pound.'

'The fellow down the street sells them at seven cents a pound.'

'So... why don't you buy them there?'

'He doesn't have any.'

'Well, when *I* don't have any I sell them at five cents a pound.'

... and, likewise, your rate may be wonderful... but you have said your 
'CICS resource' is gone; if it were so easy to find another for so trivial 
a sum then it might be interesting to learn why you are asking questions 
here.

DD
```

This is quite an interesting question Sanjay.  Would it be better for
a company to hire an outfit with no real experience, but for a very
very very low rate, or would it be better to hire someone at a higher
rate who has very targeted experience.  Ultimately the latter may
actually end up being less expensive in the long run, but the former
is more attractive to the bean counters!

wish you every success.

Pete.

- **References:** `<b6imqi$5gicn$1@ID-62248.news.dfncis.de> <836a2f21.0304050010.2326f3cf@posting.google.com> <nKEja.15862$vI3.432595@twister.austin.rr.com> <b6obkp$7bh7j$1@ID-62248.news.dfncis.de> <b6qk56$2su$1@panix1.panix.com> <836a2f21.0304070211.19125937@posting.google.com> <3e91841d_1@127.0.0.1>`

```
Based on what you have mentioned
I will give you an example on how the whole thing can work
(this will also be my observations to docdwarf's joke & Thane's
apprehensions)

Supposing an organisation has a 50-100 personnel project of say
COBOL/CICS conversion or something

They trust you and are willing to hand you sitting there, their local
contact the job.

>>Q1. Can you get 50-100 persons ?

So you contact say your counterpart in this mass production factory of India
and even while negotiations are on we start training guys up
Obviously they will have some corresponding background
There will be a core set who are advanced and lead the rest
You will guide the core set thru the local head and liasion with the client
and overall manage the project.
Probably you will make much more than the going rate! serious!
I know of may. But then they are entrepreneurs

instead of 50-100 persons we get say double 100-200 to make up
for initial lower productivity etc (it will still be cost effective)

The rest you can work our for yourself

once this job is over the team goes on to the next skill where jobs are
available
In fact I recently had an enquiry for a person who wanted 50 developers in
Smalltalk

I do not know if that many are available free in the whole world

Anyway thats the way it goes
Hey New Zealand is a beautiful country
Read a lot of description of it in one of Gerald Durrells books
Saw a lot in an indian Movie
and is probably one of the few countries where I would like to settle
other than northern european countries

Is life really relaxed and gentle ?

Regards
Sanjay
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3e91841d_1@127.0.0.1...
> Sanjay,
>
> at least you have been honest and up-front about something that is a
problem
> for many of us.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> problems (unless you pay the going rate for my time and skill <G>), but I
do
> wish you every success.
>
```

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3e94b82c_2@127.0.0.1...
> Er...please correct me if I am wrong here Paul, but the last time I looked

access,
> how can this be the "right" product for him?

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

Yes, that's exactly what I'm saying. While a mainframe connection would be
the norm - Mainframe Express does not actually require a mainframe to
function and be useful and productive, it emulates all of the required
functions within the PC. In an off-shore development senario (such as this
appears to be) then MFE is the ideal product.

There are even some customers now that have ditched thier mainframes
altogether and are running thier apps on PC's using MFE's emulation
features. While this is not what MFE was designed for it has proved it's
worth here in some circumstances.

www.microfocus.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-04-04T02:46:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c2fa54$66c86080$46c2f943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net>`

```
Below are the results that I obtained for an IBM 2064-109 cpu. I had to use
a different timing method because your way gave extremely varaiable results
on the mainframe.  Not unexpected as the time of day clock keeps ticking
even when when a task is in a wait state. I will post the modified COBOL
program that I used and the assembler program it calls as separate replies
to this post.

LOOP OVERHEAD           .12
DISPLAY                 .67
PACKED                  .32
BINARY                  .14
INTEGER                 .13
COMP-5                  .15


Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in article
<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net>...
> A timing test of Fujitsu COBOL running on a Pentium shows decimal
arithmetic
> slow on both DISPLAY and PACKED-DECIMAL formats, medium speed on BINARY
with or
> without digits right of decimal point, high speed on native COMP-5
integer. The
> difference between DISPLAY and COMP-5 is 30:1. This supports my
recommendation
> to use comp-5 for 'bread and butter' variables. 
> 
…[109 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-04-04T02:49:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c2fa54$c3995d00$46c2f943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net> <01c2fa54$66c86080$46c2f943@chottel>`

```
//JOBNAME  JOB (ACCT,ROOM),'HOTTEL COBTIME',MSGCLASS=S,CLASS=K
//PROCLIB JCLLIB ORDER=USERID.DVL.PROC
//        SET TITLE1='PFX.COBTIME2'
//OUTPUT INCLUDE MEMBER=DESTCH
//*-------------------------------------------------------------------*
//*        COBOL II COMPILE                                           *
//*-------------------------------------------------------------------*
//STEP01   EXEC  COB3CLG,LIBRARY='DVL.SOURCE.LIBMAST',PROG=IEFBR14,
//    LOPT='AMODE=31,RMODE=ANY'
//COMP.SYSIN DD  *
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. COBTIME.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      **> AUTHOR. ROBERT WAGNER.                                      *
      **>  TESTING SPEED OF ARITHMETIC.                               *
      **>  FINDINGS:                                                  *
      **>   LOOP OVERHEAD    .12                                      *
      **>   DISPLAY          .67                                      *
      **>   PACKED           .32                                      *
      **>   BINARY           .14                                      *
      **>   INTEGER          .13                                      *
      **>   COMP-5           .15                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      /
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  UNQUALIFIED-VARIABLES.
           05  DISPLAY-NUMBER                    PIC S9(10)V9(03).
           05  PACKED-NUMBER      PACKED-DECIMAL PIC S9(10)V9(03).
           05  BINARY-NUMBER      BINARY         PIC S9(10)V9(03).
           05  INTEGER-NUMBER     BINARY         PIC S9(09).
           05  COMP-5-NUMBER      COMP-5         PIC S9(09).

           05  TEST-NAME                         PIC  X(20).
           05  START-TIME               BINARY   PIC  S9(18).
           05  END-TIME                 BINARY   PIC  S9(18).
           05  REPEAT-FACTOR VALUE 10000000 BINARY PIC S9(09).
           05  LOOP-OVERHEAD VALUE ZERO BINARY   PIC S9(18).
           05  ELAPSED-TIME             BINARY   PIC S9(18).
           05  ELAPSED-TIME-EDITED               PIC  Z(04).99.
      /
        PROCEDURE DIVISION.
        MAIN.
           MOVE 'LOOP OVERHEAD' TO TEST-NAME
           PERFORM START-TIMER
           PERFORM REPEAT-FACTOR TIMES
               CONTINUE
           END-PERFORM
           PERFORM STOP-TIMER
           MOVE ELAPSED-TIME TO LOOP-OVERHEAD

           MOVE 'DISPLAY' TO TEST-NAME
           MOVE ZERO TO DISPLAY-NUMBER
           PERFORM START-TIMER
           PERFORM REPEAT-FACTOR TIMES
               ADD 1 TO DISPLAY-NUMBER
           END-PERFORM
           PERFORM STOP-TIMER

           MOVE 'PACKED' TO TEST-NAME
           MOVE ZERO TO PACKED-NUMBER
           PERFORM START-TIMER
           PERFORM REPEAT-FACTOR TIMES
               ADD 1 TO PACKED-NUMBER
           END-PERFORM
           PERFORM STOP-TIMER

           MOVE 'BINARY' TO TEST-NAME
           MOVE ZERO TO BINARY-NUMBER
           PERFORM START-TIMER
           PERFORM REPEAT-FACTOR TIMES
               ADD 1 TO BINARY-NUMBER
           END-PERFORM
           PERFORM STOP-TIMER

           MOVE 'INTEGER' TO TEST-NAME
           MOVE ZERO TO INTEGER-NUMBER
           PERFORM START-TIMER
           PERFORM REPEAT-FACTOR TIMES
               ADD 1 TO INTEGER-NUMBER
           END-PERFORM
           PERFORM STOP-TIMER

           MOVE 'COMP-5' TO TEST-NAME
           MOVE ZERO TO COMP-5-NUMBER
           PERFORM START-TIMER
           PERFORM REPEAT-FACTOR TIMES
               ADD 1 TO COMP-5-NUMBER
           END-PERFORM
           PERFORM STOP-TIMER

           GOBACK
           .
      /
       START-TIMER.
           CALL 'TASKTIME' USING START-TIME
           .
      /
       STOP-TIMER.
           CALL 'TASKTIME' USING END-TIME
           COMPUTE ELAPSED-TIME = END-TIME - START-TIME
           COMPUTE ELAPSED-TIME-EDITED ROUNDED =
               (ELAPSED-TIME - LOOP-OVERHEAD) / 1000000
           DISPLAY TEST-NAME ELAPSED-TIME-EDITED
           .
//LKED.SYSLIB  DD
//             DD
//             DD DSN=XXXX.YYY.DVL.BATLOAD,DISP=SHR
//             DD DSN=USERID.TOOLS.LOAD,DISP=SHR
//LKED.SYSLMOD DD DSN=&&LOADLIB(COBTIME),DISP=(OLD,PASS)
//LKED.SYSIN DD *
  NAME COBTIME(R)
//GO.SYSUDUMP DD SYSOUT=*
//GO.SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*
//GO.SYSOUT   DD SYSOUT=*
//GO.SYSDBOUT DD SYSOUT=*

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-04-04T04:27:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c2fa62$6a53a1c0$1093f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net> <01c2fa54$66c86080$46c2f943@chottel> <01c2fa54$c3995d00$46c2f943@chottel>`

```
This probably does not make any practical difference for most "reasonable"
timings, but START-TIME and END-TIME should be unsigned.  The TIMEUSED
macro is returning microseconds in an unsigned doubleword. Sorry for the
error.

Charles Hottel <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in article
<01c2fa54$c3995d00$46c2f943@chottel>...
> //JOBNAME  JOB (ACCT,ROOM),'HOTTEL COBTIME',MSGCLASS=S,CLASS=K
> //PROCLIB JCLLIB ORDER=USERID.DVL.PROC
…[117 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-04-04T02:50:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c2fa54$efb1d0c0$46c2f943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net> <01c2fa54$66c86080$46c2f943@chottel>`

```
//JOBNAME  JOB (ACCT,ROOM),'HOTTEL TASKTIME',MSGCLASS=S,CLASS=K
//PROCLIB JCLLIB ORDER=USERID.DVL.PROC
//        SET TITLE1='PFX.TASKTIME'
//OUTPUT INCLUDE MEMBER=DESTCH
//*-------------------------------------------------------------------*
//*        ASSEMBLY                                                   *
//*-------------------------------------------------------------------*
//STEP02   EXEC HLASMCL
//ASM.SYSUT2 DD DSN=&&SYSUT2,UNIT=SYSDA,
//           SPACE=(1700,(600,100))
//ASM.SYSPUNCH DD DUMMY
//ASM.SYSIN DD *
TASKTIME TITLE 'GET CPU TIME IN MICROSECONDS SINCE TASK STARTED'
TASKTIME AMODE 31
TASKTIME RMODE ANY
TASKTIME START
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*      01  STARTED       PIC S9(18) COMP.                             *
*      01  ENDED         PIC S9(18) COMP.                             *
*      01  ELAPSED       PIC S9(18) COMP.                             *
*      01  RUNNING-TOTAL PIC S9(18) COMP VALUE ZERO.                  *
*                                                                     *
*          PERFORM X TIMES                                            *
*              CALL 'TASKTIME' USING STARTED                          *
*              . . .  CODE BEING TIMED                                *
*              CALL 'TASKTIME' USING ENDED                            *
*              COMPUTE ELAPSED = ENDED - STARTED                      *
*              DISPLAY ELAPSED ' CPU MICROSECONDS THIS ITERATION'     *
*              ADD ELAPSED TO RUNNING-TOTAL                           *
*          END-PERFORM                                                *
*          DISPLAY RUNNING-TOTAL ' IS TOTAL CPU FOR ALL ITERATIONS'   *
*                                                                     *
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
         YREGS
TASKTIME CSECT ,
         SAVE  (14,12),,TASKTIME.&SYSDATE..&SYSTIME
         LR    R12,R15             BASE REG
         USING TASKTIME,R12
         L     R5,0(R1)            POINT TO CALLER'S DBLWORD
         XR    R15,R15
         TIMEUSED STORADR=(5),LINKAGE=SYSTEM,CPU=MIC
         RETURN (14,12),RC=(15)
         END   TASKTIME
//LKED.SYSLMOD DD DSN=USERID.TOOLS.LOAD(TASKTIME),DISP=SHR
//LKED.SYSIN   DD *
 NAME TASKTIME(R)

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-04-04T04:27:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c2fa62$762e9ae0$1093f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net> <01c2fa54$66c86080$46c2f943@chottel> <01c2fa54$efb1d0c0$46c2f943@chottel>`

```
This probably does not make any practical difference for most "reasonable"
timings, but START-TIME and END-TIME should be unsigned.  The TIMEUSED
macro is returning microseconds in an unsigned doubleword. Sorry for the
error.



Charles Hottel <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in article
<01c2fa54$efb1d0c0$46c2f943@chottel>...
> //JOBNAME  JOB (ACCT,ROOM),'HOTTEL TASKTIME',MSGCLASS=S,CLASS=K
> //PROCLIB JCLLIB ORDER=USERID.DVL.PROC
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ IBM mainframe COBOL compile options for efficiency was Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T22:12:49-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E8F8D21.98216020@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net> <01c2fa54$66c86080$46c2f943@chottel> <01c2fa54$efb1d0c0$46c2f943@chottel> <01c2fa62$762e9ae0$1093f243@chottel> <b6j8l1$40u$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <01c2fb2a$88527a20$9b91f243@chottel>`

```
Charles Hottel wrote:
> 
> I tried it with OPTIMIZE(STD) and OPTIMIZE(FULL) and got no real
> difference. NUMPROC(NOPFD) and ARITH(COMPAT) are standard options at my
> organization. I don't see a BINARY parameter listed in the compile. This
> was IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM 2.2.1
You should try NUMPROC(MIG) instead of NUMPROC(NOPFD) as an arithmetic
option because it generates decent code while still treating both F and
C zones as numeric for signed fields (NUMPROC(PFD) would treat x'F9F9F9'
as not numeric if a field is defined as PIC S999).  For binary use
TRUNC(OPT) unless you have a proven case where TRUNC(BIN) will make a
difference or your shop actually wants decimal high order truncation to
fit the picture when doing binary arithmetic.  The main difference
between OPT(STD) and OPT(FULL) is the handling of unused level 01's with
value clauses in the 01 or subordinate levels (primarily affects
initialization and memory footprint).  


> 
> William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in article
…[94 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > >
```

Thanks for the mainframe timer. When I run timing tests on a mainframe, I simply
put my program in highest priority.

BINARY, INTEGER and COMP-5 are the same (within measurement error), meaning the
compiler used 32-bit hardware integers in all cases. Good job, compiler. 

I'm surprised to see PACKED so slow. Is 2064 in the z series? Has the z series
dropped hardware support?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-04-04T10:09:52-04:00

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> 
> "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> put my program in highest priority.

When running tests, I just used the step time in the JCL and had a parm
for the particular test I was running, including the null loop.

> 
> BINARY, INTEGER and COMP-5 are the same (within measurement error), meaning the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> dropped hardware support?
Packed always was slower than binary.  It's just hundreds to thousands
times faster than emulating decimal on a machine that doesn't have full
decimal arithmetic.  As to why you might want decimal rather than binary
go to www2.hursley.ibm.com/decimal and look at the FAQ.
```

> times faster than emulating decimal on a machine that doesn't have full

counter and comparing that value with a display field.   In this case, it can be
quicker than binary (the conversion is much quicker).

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

Robert Wagner wrote:

>>When equivalent sized variables are used then the results are much closer.
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> *>   comp-5 with decimals .55

I think that your program is suspect.  When I do a similar test with v7 I 
get a 20 fold difference between S9(9) COMP-5 and S9(10)V999 COMP-5 while 
the latter is about the same time of binary and only twice the time of 
decimal:

     COMP-5 S9(9)           0,02
     COMP-5 S9(10)V999      0.39
     BINARY S9(10)V999      0.42
     DISPLAY S9(10)V999     0.85

Of course mine does more than just some trivial ADD 1, as it includes a 
DIVIDE and MULTIPLY, but even so I cannot believe that you got the 
anomylous result of S9(9) COMP-5 and S9(10)V999 COMP-5 as the same unless 
the whole thing was optimised away.

Or you ballsed it up and ran the wrong code.


> *>  Environment: Intel P100, Fujitsu COBOL v3.
>  data division.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>      05  comp-5-with-decimals comp-5       pic s9(10)v9(05).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-06T13:30:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e901abb.111661913@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net> <3E8D9230.58CD45BB@istar.ca> <3e8e7d37.5785621@news.optonline.net> <b6ng5o$g6c$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8f504f.59833942@news.optonline.net> <b6octq$oq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>the whole thing was optimised away.

I share your suspicion. On a 32-bit Pentium, adding to two words should be twice
as slow as one word. I recompiled with the optimizer explicitly off. Times were
the same. Next I added to each loop "if [target] greater than repeat-factor
display 'error' end-if". That would prevent any code from being optimized away.
Execution times doubled, as expected for two references, except packed went up
eight fold and comp-5 with decimals went up four times. 

                        s9(10)v9(03) 32.14
packed-decimal  s9(10)v9(03) 86.07
binary                s9(10)v9(03)  5.44
binary                s9(09)           2.37
comp-5              s9(09)             .66
comp-5              s9(10)v9(05)  2.03

Version 7 contains improvements to BINARY, which _should_ be the same as comp-5.


It is understandable that multiply and divide on two words would be
disproportionately slower than one word. One word can be done with a single
inline instruction whereas two words produces a library call.  I don't think
your test reflects realistic  usage. We add and IF more frequently than we
multiply and divide. 

>Or you ballsed it up and ran the wrong code.

That can easily happen. The execution window doesn't always close right. It
stays active and keeps the .exe file locked. Subsequent links do not replace it,
but *do not report an error*. As a result, you're running old code rather than
the version you thought you just linked.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T18:11:56-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E8F54AC.7B76749D@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net> <01c2fa54$66c86080$46c2f943@chottel> <3e8d4857.3306807@news.optonline.net> <3E8D9230.58CD45BB@istar.ca> <3e8e7d37.5785621@news.optonline.net>`

```
I have gone to the web page

Robert Wagner wrote:
> 
> "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> thousands". The Web page does not support the author's assertions with evidence.

The FAQ at the www2.hursley.ibm.com/decimal contains the following
statement: "Complaints about the performance of decimal arithmetic are
extremely common. Software emulation is 100 to 1000 times slower than a
hardware implementation could be. For example, a JIT-compiled 9-digit
BigDecimal division in Java takes over 13,000 clock cycles on an Intel
Pentium. Even a simple 9-digit decimal addition takes at least 1,100
clock cycles. In contrast, a native hardware decimal type could reduce
this to a speed comparable to binary arithmetic (41 cycles for integer
division on the Pentium, or 3 for an addition)."  While Mike Cowlishaw
did not cite any specific source, he is at one of the major IBM labs and
probably obtained the information as part of his work in developing
JSR-000013 Decimal Arithmetic Enhancement for the JavaTM Programming
Language.  Indeed this site is well worth the hours that I have spent
reading the various information and proposals at that site.  Mike
Cowlishaw is the inventor of the REXX language which has been ported to
most systems (there is both an ANSI version and a Linux version) and is
an IBM Fellow.  I don't agree with all of the things there (I am still
not in favor of the proposed dense decimal encoding) but I regard the
topic and the discussion as worthy of far more interest than it has
received.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-06T03:49:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net> <01c2fa54$66c86080$46c2f943@chottel> <3e8d4857.3306807@news.optonline.net> <3E8D9230.58CD45BB@istar.ca> <3e8e7d37.5785621@news.optonline.net> <3E8F54AC.7B76749D@istar.ca>`

```
"Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:

>I have gone to the web page
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>> thousands". The Web page does not support the author's assertions with
evidence.
>
>The FAQ at the www2.hursley.ibm.com/decimal contains the following
>statement: "Complaints about the performance of decimal arithmetic are
>extremely common. Software emulation is 100 to 1000 times slower than a
>hardware implementation could be.

Let's see specifics and source code.

> For example, a JIT-compiled 9-digit
>BigDecimal division in Java takes over 13,000 clock cycles on an Intel
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>division on the Pentium, or 3 for an addition)."  

I thought integer addition took ONE clock. At any rate, this is a measure of the
JIT compiler efficiency rather than inherent machine speed. Java programmers are
not motivated to optimize 'business' calculations. 

COBOL compilers produce ratios of 30:1. If the number is converted to binary,
operations performed on it, and converted back to decimal, speed is about he
same, with a slight advantage to binary. Packed is twice as slow as the other
alternatives.
```

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

- **From:** Steve Thompson <n_o_spam.steve_t@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-06T23:21:57-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E90EED5.6030100@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net> <01c2fa54$66c86080$46c2f943@chottel>`

```
Charles Hottel wrote:
> Below are the results that I obtained for an IBM 2064-109 cpu. I had to use
> a different timing method because your way gave extremely varaiable results
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> COMP-5                  .15
<snip>

The S/360 thru z/ARCH systems from IBM are interrupt driven. 
Therefore, an "MVS" system tends to be quite noisy and the tests 
can't be run in a deterministic fashion. VM and VSE environments 
are also noisy, but since I don't get to work with them that 
much, it is difficult for me to tell you how noisy they may be.

The only way to run tests to find out the true times for 
instructions is to get the machine in stand-alone mode and run an 
architectural testing program (that's rather what you are 
attempting to do).

There are a few other things you need to know:

Cache lines are 256 bytes long for the G6 chip sets (as I 
recall). And these machines have I & D banks (shades of the old 
U/1100 machines!). So one needs to check the difference in 
fetches where data is mixed in with the instructions. And, where 
one has "generated instructions on the fly" there is the 
possibility that there will be serious performance degradations 
(ask SAS about that one).

Since different machines implement different stages for the CPUs, 
it would help to know how many bytes can be processed in one 
microcode cycle and how many stages there are and what their 
functions are.

Armed with this, you can then remove the problems of "locality of 
reference", timer 'rupts, I/O 'rupts, etc. to find out the "raw" 
time of an instruction.

But in the real world, the way programs are written (or object 
generated), the type of alignment a series of instructions has 
(page, double-word, cache-line, etc.), and how busy the system is 
will have large effect on the results.

I do respect your attempts to get these timings, and this is why 
IBM doesn't publish this kind of thing any more (they use to with 
the S/360 machines -- Functional Characteristics manuals). And so 
MIPs has truly come to be Meaningless Indicators of Performance.
```

## Thread: Animation issue with MF SERVER EXPRESS and ORACLE on HP-UX — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Animation issue with MF SERVER EXPRESS and ORACLE on HP-UX

- **From:** ctaliercio@yahoo.com (Chris)
- **Date:** 2003-04-04T09:16:08-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cc07162.0304040916.76f0d407@posting.google.com>`

```
I've created a new run time system (which I've named rtsora64) using
the make file provided by Oracle. Its running great, but I'm having
trouble getting one of the great features of SE working with the new
rtsora: unsolicited dynamic attachment.

As a test, I've compiled the tictac.cbl program supplied with SE to an
animatable .gnt file. If I use cobrun (which is a link to rts64) to
start tictac.gnt the from another session use anim (which is a link to
anim64) and the PID of the original rts64 process - the animator
window pops up and I am able to "take control" in the animator of the
remote process. However, when I use rtsora to start tictac.gnt, then
use anim from another session, I am unable to do anything in the
animator window.

Here is the command line I used (provided by Oracle) to build the new
rtsora64:

cob64 -C nolist -o /orasoft/app/oracle/product/9.2.0/precomp/lib/rtsora64
-t
-xe  /orasoft/app/oracle/product/9.2.0/precomp/lib/cobsqlintf.o
/orasoft/app/oracle/product/9.2.0/lib/scorept.o
/orasoft/app/oracle/product/9.2.0/lib/sscoreed.o
/orasoft/app/oracle/product/9.2.0/rdbms/lib/kpudfo.o -L
/orasoft/app/oracle/product/9.2.0/lib/ -lclntsh

My best guess is that anim64 is not entirely compatible with the new
rtsora64 that I've created. My questions then would be:

1) Has anyone else gotten this to work?

2) Is it possible for me to re-build anim64 into animora64 using a
build command similar to the one used above to create rtsora64?

Any help here would be appreciated.

Thanks,
Chris
```

#### ↳ Re: Animation issue with MF SERVER EXPRESS and ORACLE on HP-UX

- **From:** Wiggy <wignomore@btopenworld.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-04T22:26:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6l0q4$q6t$1@hercules.btinternet.com>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0304040916.76f0d407@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Chris.

Firstly, cobrun shouldn't be a link to rts64. The run-time executables
provided with Server Express -- rts32[_t] and rts64[_t] are separate
from the cobrun triggers. You shouldn't need to rebuild the anim* triggers,
nor the cobrun* triggers for working with Oracle. Rebuilding the run-time
(as you have) should be sufficient.

As the rtsora64 was linked with the -t cob flag -- i.e. it's built for
use with the threaded run-time  -- if anything, you should replace
rts64_t under $COBDIR/bin with this new executable.

I'd suggest you try this. I can't guarantee that it will resolve your
problem, but it's more likely to work. If you're still having problems,
then as Jimmy suggested, please contact Micro Focus Supportline.

Regards.

Simon.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Animation issue with MF SERVER EXPRESS and ORACLE on HP-UX

After playing with this problem all weekend I did manage to get the
Unsolicited Dynamic Attachment working with some limited success. I
used anim64_t PID to connect to and animate an rtsora64_t process
running in a separate session. It is odd though - when animator is
first invoked, I have no control over the code until the first "CALL"
statement is executed. Until that point the animator window is
completely blank.

After that I am somewhat confused about the functionality -
Perform/Exit does not work as it does with earlier versions of MF
COBOL. That may be a problem related to SE in and of itself as opposed
to my particular problem.

So - I am about 75% of the way home - if I can just figure out why the
initial attach doesn not appear to work successfully until after the
1st CALL statement is executed I'll be in great shape.

Thanks,
Chris

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Animation issue with MF SERVER EXPRESS and ORACLE on HP-UX

- **From:** "Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.microfocus.com>

```
Hi Chris. A few further questions for you :

1. Which version of Server Express & Oracle are you using?

2. Did you compile your COBOL app using Cobsql, or by
    invoking procob directly, and compiling the output?

    If the former, when animating, you should see the
    original source code. If the latter, then you will see
    the code generated by Pro*COBOL.

    Within your user code, at the time of attaching to the
    process, are you executing EXEC SQL statements, calls
    to third party APIs etc.?

3. Are you executing your app in int code, or in
    gnt/shared object/exe ?

Simon.
```

<a id="t-4d234ac614"></a>
## Thread: Performance of various data types (IBM's latest mainframe compiler) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Performance of various data types (IBM's latest mainframe compiler)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-04T11:44:19-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6kg9k$u2a$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
As a follow-on to a separate (degenerated) thread, I thought that I would
point out to those who are interested, IBM's own "performance analysis" of
various data types and compiler options for their Enterprise COBOL V3R1
compiler.  You can get the full performance paper from:


http://www-1.ibm.com/support/docview.wss?rs=203&q=7001475&uid=swg27001475&lo
c=en_US&cs=utf-8?=en

and within it look for the section:

   "Comparing Data Types"

Please note that my FAVORITE quote from this entire paper is on page 33
where it states

"The biggest performance improvement you can make is to use an  efficient
design. After the design is determined, then you can look at other things
such as efficient algorithms, data structures and data types, and coding
style."

Although this appears in an IBM mainframe targeted document, as many
participants within CLC have often stated, this is certainly a BASIC truth
that beginning programmers need to learn - LONG before they get into
"micro-tuning".
```

<a id="t-3bf01a512b"></a>
## Thread: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard? — 38 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Re: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T09:13:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3E8CAB45.2477C152@attglobal.net> <3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <b6k280$bd1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On  3-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>They should use 4 digit years.

The vast majority do NOT use 4 digit years. They simulate it by:

if  year-3-4 greater than '10'
    move '19' to year-1-2
else
    move '20' to year-1-2
end-if

I'm not making this up. I'm looking at hundreds of programs 'fixed' with this
kind of logic, at a major Wall Street company. 

COBOL shares some of the blame. As Y2K approached, the standard for ACCEPT foo
FROM DATE should have been changed to an eight digit date. It never was in the
standard, only by a few 'vendors'. We were abandoned to fight Y2K on our own
rather than getting any support from COBOL, which is widely thought to have
caused the problem in the first place. Admittedly it's a bum rap, but that was
the perception. The standards committee should have done more ... anything ...
to solve the problem. It didn't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T09:22:54-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v8tova7ns125ef@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3E8CAB45.2477C152@attglobal.net> <3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <b6k280$bd1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net>`

```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net...
[snip]
> COBOL shares some of the blame. As Y2K approached, the standard for ACCEPT
foo
> FROM DATE should have been changed to an eight digit date. It never was in
the
> standard, only by a few 'vendors'. We were abandoned to fight Y2K on our
own
> rather than getting any support from COBOL, which is widely thought to
have
> caused the problem in the first place. Admittedly it's a bum rap, but that
was
> the perception. The standards committee should have done more ... anything
...
> to solve the problem. It didn't.

Changing the standard for ACCEPT ... FROM DATE would
have broken some programs that would not have required
any changes. For example, a report program where the date
is shown on the report but not used otherwise.

Support for 8 digit dates was added in the "Intrinsic Functions"
amendment in 1989. For example,
    MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (1:8) TO MyCCYYMMDD

I was able to do all Y2K remediation by using features provided
in that amendment. In other words, the "standards committee"
did *everything* I needed to solve the problem, except write the
code for me!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T18:46:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8f21a7.47888311@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3E8CAB45.2477C152@attglobal.net> <3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <b6k280$bd1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net> <v8tova7ns125ef@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

>
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>is shown on the report but not used otherwise.

They did it by looking at the size of the receiving field. If 6, function as
before; if 8, return an eight digit date. That wouldn't break existing code. 
>
>Support for 8 digit dates was added in the "Intrinsic Functions"
>amendment in 1989. For example,
>    MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (1:8) TO MyCCYYMMDD

I never paid much attention to functions. To tell the truth, I was unaware they
were in the standard. I thought they were vendor extensions. 

>I was able to do all Y2K remediation by using features provided
>in that amendment. In other words, the "standards committee"
>did *everything* I needed to solve the problem, except write the
>code for me!

Big shops where I worked had languages in addition to COBOL. They went with a
language-independent solution -- a callable program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T16:39:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v8uiih8kha27e9@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3E8CAB45.2477C152@attglobal.net> <3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <b6k280$bd1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net> <v8tova7ns125ef@corp.supernews.com> <3e8f21a7.47888311@news.optonline.net>`

```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e8f21a7.47888311@news.optonline.net...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> COBOL shares some of the blame. As Y2K approached, the standard for
ACCEPT
> >foo
> >> FROM DATE should have been changed to an eight digit date. It never was
in
> >the
> >> standard, only by a few 'vendors'. We were abandoned to fight Y2K on
our
> >own
> >> rather than getting any support from COBOL, which is widely thought to
> >have
> >> caused the problem in the first place. Admittedly it's a bum rap, but
that
> >was
> >> the perception. The standards committee should have done more ...
anything
> >...
> >> to solve the problem. It didn't.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> They did it by looking at the size of the receiving field. If 6, function
as
> before; if 8, return an eight digit date. That wouldn't break existing
code.

Changing the standard for ACCEPT ... FROM DATE would
have broken some programs that would not have required
any changes. For example, a program that had already been
remediated. For example,

      *set ans85 flag"ans85" flagas"S"
       identification division.
       program-id. acc-date.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01 my-date pic 9(8).
       procedure division.
       begin.
           accept my-date from date
           display my-date
           if my-date < 500000
               add 20000000 to my-date
           else
               add 19000000 to my-date
           end-if
           display my-date
           stop run.

Changing the compiler to one with different behavior,
for ACCEPT ... FROM DATE, would have broken every
program that used the above method for windowing.
True, the correction would have been simple; but the
"existing code" would have been broken. <g>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ 4 vs 2 digit years and ANSI/ISO (was: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T18:25:46-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6ns7v$t3p$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3E8CAB45.2477C152@attglobal.net> <3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <b6k280$bd1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net> <v8tova7ns125ef@corp.supernews.com> <3e8f21a7.47888311@news.optonline.net> <v8uiih8kha27e9@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Not seeing all of this thread, I just wanted to make certain that everyone
knows

A) ANSI/ISO *has* enhanced ACCEPT FROM DAY/DATE by adding the (fully
compatible with previous COBOL Standards)
  -  YYYYMMDD
  -  YYYYDDD

keywords for requesting 4 rather than 2 digit year - while their omission
works as this statement always has.

B) This was a "quite stable" part of the 2002 Standard that many (I think
most - but not all) vendors adopted before the year 2000 - therefore, it has
been commonly available for close to 5 years now - depending upon the
compiler vendor.

C) The intrinsic function CURRENT-DATE became part of Standard COBOL in 1989
(which CERTAINLY should have been plenty of time for anyone wanting to use
"Standard COBOL" for doing Y2K remediation)

D) The rules for ACCEPT FROM DAY/DATE do (and as far as I know have always)
been defined as working "as if" a MOVE were done from a 6 or 5 (now possibly
8 or 7) digit numeric integer to a numeric receiving item.  This means that
they were WELL (and are still well) defined for cases where the receiving
item is *NOT* 6 or 5 (or 8 or 7) digits long.  This means that any "attempt"
at changing the meaning of this statement DEPENDENT upon the size of the
receiving item would CERTAINLY have broken programs that relied on well
defined ANSI/ISO conforming compiler behavior (where truncation and padding
worked "as expected" by knowledgeable programmers).

  ***

I hope that this thread doesn't turn out to be like  a previous thread where
one person was unable to understand how standard conforming COBOL works (and
has always worked) and got into a discussion of how he WANTED it to work -
thinking that this matters for existing COBOL programs - or language
definitions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 4 vs 2 digit years and ANSI/ISO (was: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T22:21:08-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6o9vm$s7p$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3E8CAB45.2477C152@attglobal.net> <3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <b6k280$bd1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net> <v8tova7ns125ef@corp.supernews.com> <3e8f21a7.47888311@news.optonline.net> <v8uiih8kha27e9@corp.supernews.com> <b6ns7v$t3p$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3e8f93a6.77075601@news.optonline.net>`

```
Wrong.

For IBM

  Accept 8-digit-field from Date

will return a date in the format YYMMDD (with padding depending on the
definition of 8-digit-field)

  Accept 8-digit-field from Date YYYYMMDD

is the way to get a 4-digit year and is defined at:

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGY3LR10/6.2.1.2

and following.

For Micro Focus, go to:

 http://supportline.microfocus.com/documentation/books/nx31sp1/lrpubb.htm

and search for YYYYMMDD in the Format 2 of the ACCEPT Statement

For Compaq, search for YYYYMMDD at

 http://www.helsinki.fi/atk/unix/dec_manuals/cobv27ua/cobrm_022.htm

    ***

There are probably others online - but this should get you started.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 4 vs 2 digit years and ANSI/ISO (was: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-07T09:21:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6s8i4$319e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3E8CAB45.2477C152@attglobal.net> <3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <b6k280$bd1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net> <v8tova7ns125ef@corp.supernews.com> <3e8f21a7.47888311@news.optonline.net> <v8uiih8kha27e9@corp.supernews.com> <b6ns7v$t3p$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b6ns7v$t3p$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net...

> D) The rules for ACCEPT FROM DAY/DATE do (and as far as I know have
always)
> been defined as working "as if" a MOVE were done from a 6 or 5 (now
possibly
> 8 or 7) digit numeric integer to a numeric receiving item.

It's 6 for DATE and 5 for DAY, same as the '85 and '74 standards.

It's 8 for the ... DATE YYYYMMDD variant and 7 for the ... DAY YYYYDDD
variant.   And just to be complete, all four are treated as unsigned.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-07T09:30:38-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6s93e$31j2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3E8CAB45.2477C152@attglobal.net> <3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <b6k280$bd1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net> <v8tova7ns125ef@corp.supernews.com> <3e8f21a7.47888311@news.optonline.net> <v8uiih8kha27e9@corp.supernews.com> <3e8f6d36.67233865@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e8f6d36.67233865@news.optonline.net...
> Fair enough. I've never seen a program accept date into a pic 9  field,
but
> concede  that it's possible.

Assuming you mean a PIC 9(6) (or larger) field, given the way the verbs are
documented, I'd think that'd be the first sort of destination one would pick
for a numeric source.

Yes, alphanumeric destinations for MOVE (and thus for ACCEPT) can be used
for numeric source values, but I'd expect the contents (today) to be "030407
" for an eight-character alphanumeric destination, according to the standard
alignment rules in both the '74 and '85 standards, which require
LEFT-justification in these cases.  And such are the results I obtain.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

eight-character ...

perhaps it might have been better expressed as

>  ... but I'd expect the contents (today) (using "b" to represent a single
space character)
> "030407bb" for an eight-character ...

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-07T09:17:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6s8a4$30tt$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3E8CAB45.2477C152@attglobal.net> <3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <b6k280$bd1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net> <v8tova7ns125ef@corp.supernews.com> <3e8f21a7.47888311@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e8f21a7.47888311@news.optonline.net...

> They did it by looking at the size of the receiving field. If 6, function
as
> before; if 8, return an eight digit date. That wouldn't break existing
code.

The standard says that the value is treated as an unsigned elementary
integer six digits long and is transferred into the destination according to
the rules for the MOVE statement.  Neither of these characteristics is
described in any way that could be considered ambiguous.   The '74 and '85
standards require that regardless of the length of the destination, the
source value is six digits long.

For destinations longer than six digits, your assertion would REQUIRE that
the results differ, and any code depending on the documented standard
results with such destinations would be BROKEN.   Given these two rules, how
would this suggestion NOT break existing ANSI-compliant programs that might
(for whatever reasons the user chose) have longer destinations?

       -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-08T06:37:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e9254fc.112437981@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3E8CAB45.2477C152@attglobal.net> <3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <b6k280$bd1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net> <v8tova7ns125ef@corp.supernews.com> <3e8f21a7.47888311@news.optonline.net> <b6s8a4$30tt$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>(for whatever reasons the user chose) have longer destinations?

A date is NOT an integer, it is a compound number. When I tried to explain this
earlier by saying 'you cannot add 1 to a date', my comment started a maelstrom
of protest. 

This shows how important semantics are. We start with seemingly innocuous rules
for move and end up with reasons why ACCEPT cannot return the date requested.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-08T07:51:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6unkn$1me1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3E8CAB45.2477C152@attglobal.net> <3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <b6k280$bd1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net> <v8tova7ns125ef@corp.supernews.com> <3e8f21a7.47888311@news.optonline.net> <b6s8a4$30tt$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e9254fc.112437981@news.optonline.net>`

```

I wrote:

> >For destinations longer than six digits, your assertion would REQUIRE
that
> >the results differ, and any code depending on the documented standard
> >results with such destinations would be BROKEN.   Given these two rules,
how
> >would this suggestion NOT break existing ANSI-compliant programs that
might
> >(for whatever reasons the user chose) have longer destinations?

Robert Wagner replied:

> A date is NOT an integer, it is a compound number. When I tried to explain
this
> earlier by saying 'you cannot add 1 to a date', my comment started a
maelstrom
> of protest.

"A date" may not BE an integer, but DATE in the context of ACCEPT is TREATED
AS an integer.  See below.

> This shows how important semantics are. We start with seemingly innocuous
rules
> for move and end up with reasons why ACCEPT cannot return the date
requested.

I'd say you really have to start with what the compiler is supposed to think
the data item LOOKS LIKE before you apply MOVE rules to it.  The rule
involving MOVE appears first because it applies to all the formats that
follow:

ISO/IEC 1989:2002 Page 410, 14.8.1.3, ACCEPT statement, general rule 6
(introduction to Format 2):  "The ACCEPT statement causes the information to
be transferred to the data item specified by identifier-2 according to the
rules for the MOVE statement.  ... DATE, DAY, ... are conceptual data items
and, therefore, are not described in the COBOL source unit."

Now, how do we determine what DATE in the context of ACCEPT is supposed to
look like?

ISO/IEC 1989:2002 Page 410, 14.8.1.3, ACCEPT statement, general rule 7:
"DATE without the phrase YYYYMMDD behaves as if it had been described as an
unsigned elementary integer data item of usage display six digits in length,
the character positions of which, numbered from left to right, are:
        Character Positions                Contents
        1-2                                        The two low-order digits
of the year in the Gregorian calendar.
        3-4                                        Two numeric characters of
the month in the year in the range 01 through 12.
        5-6                                        Two numeric characters of
the day of the month in the range 01 through 31.

ANSI X3.23-1974 / ISO 1989:1978 says the same thing, albeit in fewer words.

The semantic and syntactic rules of COBOL are important.  That's why it's
important to Read The Manual before deciding what you think, despite nearly
thirty years of documented precedent, what it Really Ought to Do.  What it
Really Ought to Do is what the compiler vendors' reference materials say it
Really Ought to Do, and if they're striving for ANSI compliance, what that
documentation says it Really Ought to Do should have some similarity to what
the standard says.

ANY COBOL compiler compliant with the '74 or later standards that, by
default, generates code for ACCEPT EIGHT-DIGIT-ITEM [or
EIGHT-CHARACTER-ITEM, for that matter] FROM DATE that ends up providing the
century in the high-order two digits of the destination is generating the
wrong answer.   That's a Priority-A fix-it-yesterday user-data-corruption
compiler bug, in my book.  And the '74, '85 and '02 standards bear me out.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-10T02:30:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e94b14c.267165721@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3E8CAB45.2477C152@attglobal.net> <3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <b6k280$bd1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net> <v8tova7ns125ef@corp.supernews.com> <3e8f21a7.47888311@news.optonline.net> <b6s9q3$b3a$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e925830.113258083@news.optonline.net> <b6upf2$hvo$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>On  8-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
>
>> I'm a grunt. The guys with yachts don't trust me to do analysis, just to
crank
>> code.
>
>Interesting.   Coding is the trivial part of programming.   Do you do test
>plans?

In large shops, application programmers do only Unit Test Plans, which are
pretty much meaningless. The Real Testing is done by a separate Test Team. Their
objectives are formally defined in the shop's methodology and occur at multiple
levels -- statement of work, business requirements, etc. Accenture (formerly
Anderson Consulting) makes this clear with its 'V model', where each testing
step on the right side corresponds to a planning step on the left side. 

You like to get 'insulted' when someone mentions periods. Well my hot button is
charactarizing programmers as "coders" and calling their work trivial. Those are
demeaning terms. You've been doing too many lunches with bean counters or
management honchos, and it shows in your attitude about programming.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-10T14:11:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b73u1s$8bv$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3E8CAB45.2477C152@attglobal.net> <3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <b6k280$bd1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net> <v8tova7ns125ef@corp.supernews.com> <3e8f21a7.47888311@news.optonline.net> <b6s9q3$b3a$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e925830.113258083@news.optonline.net> <b6upf2$hvo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e94b14c.267165721@news.optonline.net>`

```

On  9-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> >Interesting.   Coding is the trivial part of programming.   Do you do test
> >plans?
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> step on the right side corresponds to a planning step on the left side.

I work in a large shop and I certainly do test plans.

> You like to get 'insulted' when someone mentions periods.

I see here I had a failure in communication.   A big failure.   But I won't
re-hash what caused me to feel insulted, nor what my opinion is of periods.   I
tried for quite a few posts, and failed miserably.

> Well my hot button is
> charactarizing programmers as "coders" and calling their work trivial.

Again, I failed to communicate.    I tried to say that coding is just one aspect
of programming.   It is the easy part of programming.   Programming is much more
than coding.   It includes analyzing the users needs, knowing the data,
developing test plans, and giving the users a high level of confidence that my
solution met their needs.   These are all more difficult and time consuming than
coding.

Was I more clear this time?    Do you agree with me?   Or do you think these are
the easy parts?   Or do you think they aren't part of a programmer's job at all?

> Those are
> demeaning terms. You've been doing too many lunches with bean counters or
> management honchos, and it shows in your attitude about programming.

Was my mistake in using the word "trivial"?   Would "easy" have been a better
word?   Or is it insulting to imply that the harder parts of programming are
analysis and testing?

I don't want to insult people.  Programming isn't easy.   The easiest part of
programming, by far - is writing the code.
```
