- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e95f555.350131702@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3E8CAB45.2477C152@attglobal.net> <3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <b6k280$bd1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net> <v8tova7ns125ef@corp.supernews.com> <3e8f21a7.47888311@news.optonline.net> <b6s9q3$b3a$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e925830.113258083@news.optonline.net> <b6upf2$hvo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e94b14c.267165721@news.optonline.net> <b73u1s$8bv$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On  9-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>Again, I failed to communicate.    I tried to say that coding is just one
aspect
>of programming.   It is the easy part of programming.   Programming is much
more
>than coding.   It includes analyzing the users needs, knowing the data,
>developing test plans, and giving the users a high level of confidence that my
>solution met their needs.   These are all more difficult and time consuming
than
>coding.
>
>Was I more clear this time?    Do you agree with me?   Or do you think these
are
>the easy parts?   Or do you think they aren't part of a programmer's job at
all?

I think you work in a medium-sized shop, which is where I've spent most of my
career as well. There, I did user interaction, knowing the data, developing test
plans and stroking the users. In a LARGE shop there are specialists who handle
each task. There are managers who interact with users, 'subject matter experts'
who know the data, test teams who develop test plans. Application programmers
spend 10% of their time cranking code and 90% covering their boss' ass with
'documentation' which documents little except revealing who's at fault when
things go wrong. 

>> Those are
>> demeaning terms. You've been doing too many lunches with bean counters or
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>programming, by far - is writing the code.

Easy would have been less perjorative but that's just nuance.  I disagree with
your assertion that writing the code is the easiest part.
```

#### ↳ Re: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-04-06T19:24:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0304061824.75fc5429@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3E8CAB45.2477C152@attglobal.net> <3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <b6k280$bd1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net>...
> 
> COBOL shares some of the blame. As Y2K approached, the standard for ACCEPT foo
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> to solve the problem. It didn't.

Horsepucky.

The 1989 ammendment added the Intrinsic functions, one of which was
CURRENT-DATE which did return a full 8 digit date.  Those to blame
(your words not mine) would be those companies who did not update to
COBOL compilers that supported the amemdment (Intrinsic functions were
optiona), or push their COBOL vendors to implement those functions. 
You can't blame the standard for this one.
```

#### ↳ Re: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-07T08:54:34-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6s6vq$3036$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3E8CAB45.2477C152@attglobal.net> <3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <b6k280$bd1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net...

> COBOL shares some of the blame. As Y2K approached, the standard for ACCEPT
foo
> FROM DATE should have been changed to an eight digit date. It never was in
the
> standard, only by a few 'vendors'. We were abandoned to fight Y2K on our
own
> rather than getting any support from COBOL, which is widely thought to
have
> caused the problem in the first place. Admittedly it's a bum rap, but that
was
> the perception. The standards committee should have done more ... anything
...
> to solve the problem. It didn't.

The standards committee was reluctant to change the CURRENT behavior of
ACCEPT foo FROM DATE precisely because changing that behavior would have a
serious negative impact on existing programs that were expecting it to do
exactly what it now does.  All that it could do was propose expansions to
the current syntax to provide the additional functionality, and the only
time it could do that is in a technical or full revision of the entire
standard.

While I personally find the expanded syntax in the 2002 standard -- ACCEPT
foo FROM DATE YYYYMMDD -- aesthetically displeasing, as I understand it it
was one of the earlier enhancements after the completion of the '93
Corrections Amendment, and it provides exactly what you want (in addition,
ACCEPT foo FROM DATE YYYYDDD provides a seven-digit date with a four-digit
year).

And these are features that many implementations (including our own, in both
our '74 and our '85 compilers  -- but not our '68 compiler, support for
which was already in the process of being discontinued) chose to provide,
well in advance of "Y2K".  You can find documentation for them in all
Committee Drafts of the 2002 standard from CD 1.1 onward (I have no copies
earlier than CD 1.1, or I'd check there too)  as well as in ISO/IEC
1989:2002 itself.

Have you tried this syntax in any of your implementations?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-08T06:37:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e9259af.113641664@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3E8CAB45.2477C152@attglobal.net> <3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <b6k280$bd1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net> <b6s6vq$3036$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net...

>While I personally find the expanded syntax in the 2002 standard -- ACCEPT
>foo FROM DATE YYYYMMDD -- aesthetically displeasing, as I understand it it
>was one of the earlier enhancements after the completion of the '93
>Corrections Amendment, and it provides exactly what you want 

>Have you tried this syntax in any of your implementations?

No. I didn't know it was available before reading about it here. 

I did find IBM returned an eight digit date into an eight digit field. That's
what I needed, so I went with it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Another erroneous claim about IBM (was: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-08T02:07:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6tsfg$6o5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3E8CAB45.2477C152@attglobal.net> <3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <b6k280$bd1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net> <b6s6vq$3036$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e9259af.113641664@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e9259af.113641664@news.optonline.net...
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
 <snip>

>
> I did find IBM returned an eight digit date into an eight digit field.
That's
> what I needed, so I went with it.
>

I doubt it - if you are talking about the ACCEPT statement.

Please show a compile listing (with compiler options) and run-time output
showing *ANY* IBM compiler that returns an 8-digit date when using the

   ACCEPT 8-digit-field FROM DATE

syntax.

IBM has had a "current-date" special register as an extension in almost
every compiler they have produced.  They also have implemented the YYYYMMDD
extension for ACCEPT FROM DATE.  They have supported the CURRENT-DATE
intrinsic functions for a decade or so.  They also have a "millennium
language extension" which allows for enhanced date processing - but this
requires additional compiler options and/or syntax.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another erroneous claim about IBM (was: Who cares about the ANSI/ISO Standard?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-08T02:23:40-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6tte2$5pc$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3E8CAB45.2477C152@attglobal.net> <3e8ce599.237423893@news.optonline.net> <b6k280$bd1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e9aec.13392186@news.optonline.net> <b6s6vq$3036$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e9259af.113641664@news.optonline.net> <b6tsfg$6o5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Just in case anyone reading the archives MIGHT have been mislead into
thinking that IBM's current compiler is non-conforming and works the way
that was reported, I refer you to:

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGY3LR10/6.2.1.3

which does (in an ANSI/ISO conforming manner) clearly indicate that when you
do an

  ACCEPT whatever FROM DATE

the implicit sending item

   "Has the implicit PICTURE 9(6)

This is (just as ANSI/ISO defines it)

 A) an integer
 B) *only* has a 2-digit year regardless of what the receiving item is
```

<a id="t-a049ebc104"></a>
## Thread: Parsing a Common Log Record Without Unstring — 13 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Parsing a Common Log Record Without Unstring

- **From:** psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T08:07:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<16e2f010.0304050807.28606261@posting.google.com>`

```
Just for fun I thought I'd hand code a parser to extract http common
log records into a usable form.  When considering the Common Log
Format, it becomes a bit unwieldy, If not downright hard to deal with
it using UNSTRING.  I thought I'd approach it in a slightly dIfferent
way.

I've only included a skeletal working storage/linkage description and
error checking is fully fleshed out, but it might be a useful example
for someone wanting to avoid multiple nested UNSTRINGS.

To do this easily I start with a simple grammer.  This lets each part
then be coded in isolation and makes it easy to quickly build a parser
for a complex grammer.

The format of the common http log file line has the following fields
separated by a space:

remotehost: 151.99.190.27
rfc931 remote login name (or - If unavailable): -
authenticated user name (or - If unavailable): -
[date:time]: [01/Jan/1997:13:07:21-0600]
"request line as exactly from client": "GET /~bacuslab/celsheet.html
HTTP/1.0"
return status: 200
bytes transfered: 13276

This gives us the following productions:

space => ' '+

string  =>  nonspace-char*

quoted-string  =>  '"' char* '"'

int  =>  [0-9]+

ip  =>  int{1-3}.int{1-3}.int{1-3}.int{1-3}

datetime  =>  '[' int{2} '/' month '/' int{4} (':'int{2}){3} sign
int{4} ']'


and finally we can complete our grammer with:

rhost   => ip
rlogin  => string
authusr => string
request => quoted-string
status  => int
xfered  => int
 
common-log => rhost space rlogin space authusr space datetime space
request space status space xfered


--- Working Storage Section ---
01 Log-Data.
	05 Log-Rhost           Binary Pic S9(8) Value 0.
	05 Log-Status          Binary Pic S9(8) Value 0.
	05 Log-Xfered         Binary Pic S9(8) Value 0.
	05 Log-Rlogin		                   Pic X(32) Value spaces.
	05 Log-Authusr                   Pic X(32) Value spaces.
	05 Log-Request-LL Binary Pic S9(4) Value 0.
	05 Log-Request                  Pic X(32767) Value spaces.

--- Linkage Section ---
01 Next-Char-Area.
	05 Next-String                     Pic X(lots).
	05 Next-Char redefines Next-String Pic X.
	05 Next-Char-9 redefines Next-Char Pic 9.

--- Procedure Division ---

Parse-Common-Log.
	Parse-rhost
	Parse-space
	Parse-rlogin 
	Parse-space
	Parse-authusr
	Parse-space
	Parse-datetime
	Parse-space
	Parse-request
	Parse-space
	Parse-status
	Parse-space
	Parse-xfered
	Exit.

Parse-space.
	Perform until next-char not = space
	   Perform Get-Next-Char
	End-Perform
	Exit.

Parse-rhost.
	Perform Parse-ip
	Move wk-ip to log-rhost
	Exit.

Parse-rlogin.
	Perform Parse-string
	Move wk-string to log-rlogin
	Exit.

Parse-authusr.
	Perform Parse-string
	Move wk-string to log-authusr
	Exit.

Parse-datetime.
	Perform Get-Next-Char
	If next-char = '['
	   Perform Get-Next-Char
	Else
	   Move 'Invalid Date' to fatal-error-string
	   Perform 123456789-fatal-error
	End-If

	Perform Parse-int
	If wk-int > 0 and wk-int < 32
	   Move wk-int to log-dttm-day
	Else
	   Move 'Invalid Day' to fatal-error-string
	   Perform 123456789-fatal-error
	End-If
	
	Perform Get-Next-Char
	If next-char = '/'
	   Perform Get-Next-Char
	Else 
	   Move 'Invalid Date' to fatal-error-string
	   Perform 123456789-fatal-error
	End-If

	Evaluate next-string (1:3)
	   when 'JAN'
	      Move 1 to log-dttm-month
	   when 'FEB'
	      Move 2 to log-dttm-month
	   when 'MAR'
	   ... snip to save space, you get the idea...
	   when other
	      Perform 123456789-fatal-error
	End-Evaluate
	Perform Get-Next-Char 3 times

	Perform Get-Next-Char
	If next-char = '/'
	   Perform Get-Next-Char
	Else 
	   Move 'Invalid Date' to fatal-error-string
	   Perform 123456789-fatal-error
	End-If

	Perform Parse-int
	If wk-int > 1989 and wk-int < 9999
	   Move wk-int to log-dttm-year
	Else
	   Move 'Invalid Year' to fatal-error-string
	   Perform 123456789-fatal-error
	End-If
	
	Perform Get-Next-Char
	If next-char = ':'
	   Perform Get-Next-Char
	Else 
	   Move 'Invalid Date' to fatal-error-string
	   Perform 123456789-fatal-error
	End-If
	Perform Parse-int
	If wk-int < 25
	   Move wk-int to log-dttm-hour
	Else
	   Move 'Invalid Hours' to fatal-error-string
	   Perform 123456789-fatal-error
	End-If
	
	Perform Get-Next-Char
	If next-char = ':'
	   Perform Get-Next-Char
	Else 
	   Move 'Invalid Date' to fatal-error-string
	   Perform 123456789-fatal-error
	End-If
	Perform Parse-int
	If wk-int < 60
	   Move wk-int to log-dttm-minute
	Else
	   Move 'Invalid Minutes' to fatal-error-string
	   Perform 123456789-fatal-error
	End-If

	Perform Get-Next-Char
	If next-char = ':'
	   Perform Get-Next-Char
	Else 
	   Move 'Invalid Date' to fatal-error-string
	   Perform 123456789-fatal-error
	End-If
	Perform Parse-int
	If wk-int < 60
	   Move wk-int to log-dttm-second
	Else
	   Move 'Invalid Second' to fatal-error-string
	   Perform 123456789-fatal-error
	End-If

	Perform Get-Next-Char
	Evaluate next-char
	   when '-'
	      Set log-dttm-off-neg to true
	   when '+'
	      Set log-dttm-off-pos to true
	   when other
	      Perform 123456789-fatal-error
	End-Evaluate

	Perform Parse-int
	If wk-int > 0 and wk-int < 2500
	   Move wk-int to log-dttm-day
	Else
	   Move 'Invalid Day' to fatal-error-string
	   Perform 123456789-fatal-error
	End-If

	Perform Get-Next-Char
	If next-char = ']'
	   Perform Get-Next-Char
	Else
	   Move 'Invalid Date' to fatal-error-string
	   Perform 123456789-fatal-error
	End-If

	Exit.

Parse-request.
	Perform Parse-Quoted-String
	Move wk-quoted-string to log-request
	Move wk-quoted-string-ll to log-request-ll
	Exit.

Parse-status.
	Perform Parse-Int
	Move wk-int to log-status
	Exit.

Parse-xfered.
	Perform Parse-Int
	Move wk-int to log-xfered
	Exit.

Parse-string.
	Perform varying I from 1 by 1 
	  until next-char not nonspacechar
	   Move next-char to wk-string (I:1)
	   Perform Get-Next-Char
	End-Perform
	Exit.

Parse-IP.
	Perform Parse-int
	If wk-int > 256
	   Move wk-int to log-IP-1st-byte
	Else
	   Move 'Invalid 1st Byte' to fatal-error-string
	   Perform 123456789-fatal-error
	End-If

	Perform Parse-int
	If wk-int > 256
	   Move wk-int to log-IP-2nd-byte
	Else
	   Move 'Invalid 2nd Byte' to fatal-error-string
	   Perform 123456789-fatal-error
	End-If

	Perform Parse-int
	If wk-int > 256
	   Move wk-int to log-IP-3rd-byte
	Else
	   Move 'Invalid 3rd Byte' to fatal-error-string
	   Perform 123456789-fatal-error
	End-If

	Perform Parse-int
	If wk-int > 256
	   Move wk-int to log-IP-4th-byte
	Else
	   Move 'Invalid 4th Byte' to fatal-error-string
	   Perform 123456789-fatal-error
	End-If
	Exit.

Parse-quoted-string.
	If next-char = '"'
	   Perform Get-Next-Char
	   Perform varying I from 1 by 1 
	     until next-char = '"'
	      Move next-char to wk-quoted-string (I:1)
	      Perform Get-Next-Char
	   End-Perform
	   Perform Get-Next-Char
	   Move I to wk-quoted-string-ll
	End-If
	Exit.

Parse-Int.
	Move 0 to wk-int
	Perform until next-char not integerdigit
	   Compute wk-int = (wk-int * 10) + next-char-9
	   Perform Get-Next-Char
	End-Perform
	Exit.

Get-Next-Char.
	Set address of next-char-area to next-str (2:1)
	...some range checking would be nice...
	Exit.

Get-First-Char.
	Read LOGFILE 
	   at end Set end-of-log-file to true
	   not at end Set address of next-char-area to address of
logfile-buffer
	End-Read
	Exit.

123456789-fatal-error.
	Display "Invalid Common Log Format, error is ("
	   fatal-error-string
	   ")."
	...do whatever is needed to recover...
	Exit.
```

#### ↳ Re: Parsing a Common Log Record Without Unstring

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T20:19:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8f396f.53977403@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<16e2f010.0304050807.28606261@posting.google.com>`

```
If you're trying to mimic Corporate COBOL, you'll have to make a few changes. 

First, every Corporate Coder knows that EXIT PROGRAM should be the last line in
the program. Put it at the bottom and perform it. For belt-and-suspenders
security, put a STOP RUN after it. 

Second, all those data names should be prefixed with ws- or ls-. How is the
reader supposed to know which section they're in? Alternatively, use Hungarian
Notation prefixes so the reader can tell at a glance what the picture is. For
best clarity, use both. Example: ws-is-wk-int.

Third, don't be so verbose. Are you writing a book? For example,
fatal-error-string could be abbreviated ws-as-fes. I assume it's in
working-storage. Surely you're going to return it to the caller. 

Fourth, all those EXITs won't compile. Change them to CONTINUE. While you're at
it, put the period on a line by itself. Your boss will be happy when he measures
your productivity by line count. 

Fifth, you might consider 'inlining' those re-usable paragraphs such as
get-next-char. Copy-and-paste is a wonderful tool in cases like this. Why? Two
reasons: your program will run faster and, if someone changes the paragraph, you
won't have to re-test the entire program.

I like the way you avoided reusable code in most of the program. Simple,
straight line, reads from top to bottom. That's good, clean style. 


psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote:

>Just for fun I thought I'd hand code a parser to extract http common
>log records into a usable form.

>	If next-char = '/'
>	   Perform Get-Next-Char
…[84 more quoted lines elided]…
>	Exit.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Parsing a Common Log Record Without Unstring

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T16:24:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-0B70D9.16242905042003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<16e2f010.0304050807.28606261@posting.google.com> <3e8f396f.53977403@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <3e8f396f.53977403@news.optonline.net>,
 robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> If you're trying to mimic Corporate COBOL, you'll have to make a few changes. 
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> security, put a STOP RUN after it. 

Or you could not let control just fall off the edge of your source code.


> Second, all those data names should be prefixed with ws- or ls-. How is the
> reader supposed to know which section they're in? Alternatively, use 
> Hungarian
> Notation prefixes so the reader can tell at a glance what the picture is. For
> best clarity, use both. Example: ws-is-wk-int.

That is a standard that dates back to code sheets and data entry 
departments.  I understand when your listing came back from operations, 
after they ran the compile deck, it would assist you in finding your 
variables, they would all be properly grouped by prefix.

Not very useful in an age of editors with find commands.


> Third, don't be so verbose. Are you writing a book? For example,
> fatal-error-string could be abbreviated ws-as-fes. I assume it's in
> working-storage. Surely you're going to return it to the caller. 

I like meaningful variable names.


> Fourth, all those EXITs won't compile. Change them to CONTINUE. While you're 
> at
> it, put the period on a line by itself. Your boss will be happy when he 
> measures
> your productivity by line count. 

I freely admit to an IBM bias, and they do indeed compile.  I used to 
put a period on a line by itself until IBMs CICS/DLI and SQL 
preprocessor massively screwed up some code for me.  They all treated 
'END-EXEC' and 'END-EXEC.' as equal.  So if the last statement in a 
paragraph was a DLI, CICS or SQL statement the translator would also 
consume your terminal period and control would fall into the next 
paragraph.



> Fifth, you might consider 'inlining' those re-usable paragraphs such as
> get-next-char. Copy-and-paste is a wonderful tool in cases like this. Why? 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> won't have to re-test the entire program.

The optimizer will do that for me.  I don't manually inline it because 
most of this code can be generated and I don't want to generate things 
not specific to the parsing.  Also, while this  is a simple example with 
a 1 statement get-next-char, it is more likely that there would be lots 
of range checking, buffer filling, looking-ahead and pushing back that 
would also go on in a non-trivial example.


> I like the way you avoided reusable code in most of the program. Simple,
> straight line, reads from top to bottom. That's good, clean style. 

Didn't you just ding me for reusing get-next-char?
```

```
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

>In article <3e8f396f.53977403@news.optonline.net>,
> robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
>
>> If you're trying to mimic Corporate COBOL, you'll have to make a few changes.

>> 
>> First, every Corporate Coder knows that EXIT PROGRAM should be the last line 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Or you could not let control just fall off the edge of your source code.

Yes, and it would work, but it is considered Bad Form. 

>> Second, all those data names should be prefixed with ws- or ls-. How is the
>> reader supposed to know which section they're in? Alternatively, use 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Not very useful in an age of editors with find commands.

Last year (2002)  I worked at a place which insisted on these naming standards.
It wasn't a COBOL shop, it was a C shop. C programmers never used coding sheets
nor punched cards. 

>> Third, don't be so verbose. Are you writing a book? For example,
>> fatal-error-string could be abbreviated ws-as-fes. I assume it's in
>> working-storage. Surely you're NOT going to return it to the caller. 
>
>I like meaningful variable names.

Another English major. 

>> Fourth, all those EXITs won't compile. Change them to CONTINUE. While you're 
>> at
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>paragraph.

You got something against fall-through code? We've been using it for 40 years. 

Seriously, the compiler should have barfed when the last statement in a
paragraph was not terminated by a period. It's such an annoyance that I've
switched to this style: 

procedure division using (whatever)
. main. 
     perform foo
     stop run
. foo. 
     continue
.

See? All periods except the last are on the same line with a paragraph header. 

>> Fifth, you might consider 'inlining' those re-usable paragraphs such as
>> get-next-char. Copy-and-paste is a wonderful tool in cases like this. Why? 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>The optimizer will do that for me. 

Very doubtful. 

>> I like the way you avoided reusable code in most of the program. Simple,
>> straight line, reads from top to bottom. That's good, clean style. 
>
>Didn't you just ding me for reusing get-next-char?

Yes, but it was a minor transgression. 95% of your code was non-reusable. That's
good enough. We cannot expect perfection. 

I forgot to mention that I especially liked 123456789-fatal-error's lack of
reference to the statement in error. That'll keep them scratching their heads.
It's kinda like the Check Engine light on my car, which signals 'something's
wrong' without telling you what. Comedic genius.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parsing a Common Log Record Without Unstring

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-06T17:31:21-04:00

```
In article <3e8f6f7c.67815957@news.optonline.net>,
 robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> >> Third, don't be so verbose. Are you writing a book? For example,
> >> fatal-error-string could be abbreviated ws-as-fes. I assume it's in
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

Actually I'm a PFCSK.



> >> Fifth, you might consider 'inlining' those re-usable paragraphs such as
> >> get-next-char. Copy-and-paste is a wonderful tool in cases like this. Why? 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Very doubtful. 


IBM has done quite a nice job with theirs.  I expecially like the way 
they inline nested programs that are statically called -- all the 
benefit of a nested program with zero call overhead.


> >> I like the way you avoided reusable code in most of the program. Simple,
> >> straight line, reads from top to bottom. That's good, clean style. 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> That's good enough. We cannot expect perfection. 

All the standard data type stuff was reused.  Parse-Int and Parse-String 
for example.

> I forgot to mention that I especially liked 123456789-fatal-error's lack of
> reference to the statement in error. That'll keep them scratching their 
> heads.
> It's kinda like the Check Engine light on my car, which signals 'something's
> wrong' without telling you what. Comedic genius. 

It displayed the fatal-error-string -- error handling can be very 
specific to environment and task, not easy to do generically.

There was once a programmer at my current shop that used to have an 
"INTERNAL SOFTWARE ERROR" routine.  When you looked up what caused it, 
well, everything did.  My boss still complaines about him...
```

```
Yes, but it didn't tell which log file entry contained the error. Production
support would have to look at the last output record, find the corresponding
input group, pray to the cyber gods that output buffers got flushed, then look
for an error in the next input group. 

>There was once a programmer at my current shop that used to have an 
>"INTERNAL SOFTWARE ERROR" routine.  When you looked up what caused it,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Opinion alert!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-08T15:54:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E93530D.749C9A1C@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<16e2f010.0304050807.28606261@posting.google.com> <3e8f396f.53977403@news.optonline.net> <joe_zitzelberger-0B70D9.16242905042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e8f6f7c.67815957@news.optonline.net>`

```


Robert Wagner wrote:

> (snip)
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Robert,
I don't want to come across as an enemy, but seriously, you shouldn't write things
like that.  Many of us know the statement to be true.  For IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM
V2R2, the Programming Guide suggests using the OPTIMIZE option, to among other
things:

"Simplify the compiled code for both a PERFORM statement and a CALL statement to a
contained (nested) program.  Where possible, the compiler places the statement
inline, eliminating the need for linkage code."

If you used this compiler, and turned on the LIST or OFFSET options, you could see
that the compiler moves some PERFORM'ed code inline.  Maybe you don't have access to
an IBM mainframe, and I can't say for certain which other compilers might do this,
but the statement is true for at least this one.

This is why people are on your case all the time.

These manuals are available free of charge on the Web.  You can read them in
BookManager form, or download / read the PDF version.  It takes some time, but
learning never was free, or totally a result of experience.

By the way, even the 2002 ANSI / ISO COBOL Standard can be found on the Web.
```

- **Date:** 2003-04-10T02:30:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e94c262.271540374@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<16e2f010.0304050807.28606261@posting.google.com> <3e8f396f.53977403@news.optonline.net> <joe_zitzelberger-0B70D9.16242905042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e8f6f7c.67815957@news.optonline.net> <3E93530D.749C9A1C@attglobal.net>`

```

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

things
>like that.  Many of us know the statement to be true.  For IBM COBOL for OS/390
& VM
>V2R2, the Programming Guide suggests using the OPTIMIZE option, to among other
>things:
>
>"Simplify the compiled code for both a PERFORM statement and a CALL statement
to a
>contained (nested) program.  Where possible, the compiler places the statement
>inline, eliminating the need for linkage code."
>
>If you used this compiler, and turned on the LIST or OFFSET options, you could
see
>that the compiler moves some PERFORM'ed code inline.  Maybe you don't have
access to
>an IBM mainframe, and I can't say for certain which other compilers might do
this,
>but the statement is true for at least this one.

Thanks for your kind words. No, I don't have access to an IBM mainframe right
now. Other compilers such as Micro Focus and Fujitsu do not inline nested
programs. Evidently, IBM is more aggressive.

>This is why people are on your case all the time.

People are on my case all the time because I threaten the things they hold near
and dear. If the debates could be settled by facts, as you did above, there
would be no need for emotion.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Parsing a Common Log Record Without Unstring

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2003-04-06T07:10:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030406031002.14640.00000577@mb-fe.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3e8f396f.53977403@news.optonline.net>`

```
>From: robert@wagner.net  (Robert Wagner)
>Date: 4/5/03 3:19 PM Eastern Standard Time

>First, every Corporate Coder knows that EXIT PROGRAM should be the last line
>in
>the program. Put it at the bottom and perform it. For belt-and-suspenders
>security, put a STOP RUN after it. 
>

Hmmm - never used EXIT PROGRAM in my life.  Haven't used STOP RUN in at least a
decade.

>Second, all those data names should be prefixed with ws- or ls-. How is the
>reader supposed to know which section they're in?

Does it really matter??  If I really want to know I do a search for first
occurance.

>Alternatively, use Hungarian
>Notation prefixes so the reader can tell at a glance what the picture is.

I've heard of Hungarian goulash, is this another form of that?

>Third, don't be so verbose. Are you writing a book? 

You must have written the decoder book for a program I had to maintain once. 
All the fields in working storage were numbered not named so you need the
decoder book to find out what the heck piece of data you were working with. 
You would have like it WS-1, WS-2, WS-3 and so on.  

>Fourth, all those EXITs won't compile. Change them to CONTINUE. While you're
>at
>it, put the period on a line by itself

Yes they do compile.  I don't personally use them though.  Period on a line by
itself is neither here nor there, and I've never had my productivity measured
by line count.

>Fifth, you might consider 'inlining' those re-usable paragraphs such as
>get-next-char. Copy-and-paste is a wonderful tool in cases like this.

More line count measuring?  Don't know about faster on a mainframe.  Copy and
paste can bite you in the derrier too.

 >I like the way you avoided reusable code in most of the program. Simple,
>straight line, reads from top to bottom. That's good, clean style. 
>

Some people like resuable code, some don't.  So saying good, clean style is a
personal preference.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parsing a Common Log Record Without Unstring

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-06T16:30:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e90507d.125426430@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3e8f396f.53977403@news.optonline.net> <20030406031002.14640.00000577@mb-fe.aol.com>`

```
yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama) wrote:

>>From: robert@wagner.net  (Robert Wagner)

>Hmmm - never used EXIT PROGRAM in my life.  Haven't used STOP RUN in at least a
>decade.

If I said put GOBACK at the bottom, someone would point out it's not the '85
standard. 

>>Second, all those data names should be prefixed with ws- or ls-. How is the
>>reader supposed to know which section they're in?
>
>Does it really matter??  If I really want to know I do a search for first
>occurance.

I select the word and hit the magic key to see all occurrences. Then hit Escape
to put me back where I was. 

>>Alternatively, use Hungarian
>>Notation prefixes so the reader can tell at a glance what the picture is.
>
>I've heard of Hungarian goulash, is this another form of that?

Sort of. It makes your program messy, like goulash.

>>Third, don't be so verbose. Are you writing a book? 
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>You would have like it WS-1, WS-2, WS-3 and so on.

I once worked on a program that maxed out the compiler's limit on data names and
literals. The solution was to put all literals in two tables -- one numeric and
the other alphabetic. Code looked like this:

if  membership-status = lit-a (37) or lit-a (59)
    add lit-n (12) to monthly-service-charge. 

This was the billing program at a major, well-known credit card company. 

At another place, someone discovered the assembler let him use unprintable
characters in names. His programs looked like this:

cli   ...,3
beq  .....
mvc  ......(3),.....

>>Fifth, you might consider 'inlining' those re-usable paragraphs such as
>>get-next-char. Copy-and-paste is a wonderful tool in cases like this.
>
>More line count measuring?  Don't know about faster on a mainframe.  

Sure. BALR and RET take _one whole clock_, each. Worse, they flush the
instruction lookahead queue. CPU design engineers hate instructions like that;
they design machines to run straight-line code.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Parsing a Common Log Record Without Unstring

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-07T16:26:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6s8se$akm$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<16e2f010.0304050807.28606261@posting.google.com> <3e8f396f.53977403@news.optonline.net>`

```

On  5-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> First, every Corporate Coder knows that EXIT PROGRAM should be the last line
> in the program. Put it at the bottom and perform it.

Maybe we should know that, but we don't.

> For belt-and-suspenders security, put a STOP RUN after it.

Unless you're a Corporate coder and know better??


> Second, all those data names should be prefixed with ws- or ls-. How is the
> reader supposed to know which section they're in? Alternatively, use Hungarian
> Notation prefixes so the reader can tell at a glance what the picture is. For
> best clarity, use both. Example: ws-is-wk-int.

Oh, I see - this is an insult.  If we work for corporations we code funny.


> Fourth, all those EXITs won't compile. Change them to CONTINUE. While you're
> at
> it, put the period on a line by itself. Your boss will be happy when he
> measures your productivity by line count.

I thought you would prefer the more "modern" CONTINUE.  (I do, it allows me to
add a debug line without deleting the EXIT).
I thought you would prefer the period on a line by itself - isn't your whole
point of not using periods to make it easy to cut and paste the code into if
clauses?

Now I'm confused - are you making fun of us, or being serious?
```

```
I'll speak for myself thanks.  Actually, I was not making fun of
anyone.

I actually do use Cobol to write parsers for smallish things.  Certain
Cobol features, like reference modification, make this quite easy. 
For small parsers, easier than pulling out lexx/yacc/bison/flex. 
Also, as I usually have to deal with ASCII<->EBCDIC source issues
those tools often come up short.
```

<a id="t-3767aa4d55"></a>
## Thread: "Flaming," "ignoring," "killfiles" and other related topics — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### "Flaming," "ignoring," "killfiles" and other related topics

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T18:46:31-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6ntda$qj$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I thought I had made my PERSONAL position clear a while ago, but thought
that starting a new thread and explaining it again MIGHT be useful -
especially when at least one person that I respect (yes, I mean you, Pete)
seems to have missed it.

1) This is an unmoderated newsgroup.  This means, BY DEFINITION, anyone may
post he/she wants - on whatever topic she/he wants - and using any tone
desired.  (I won't get into laws on slander or other legal issues which
apply to any "public" speech.  I assume anyone tending to such posts either
already knows them or doesn't care about them.)

2) I rarely use a "killfile" technique in this newsgroup, but do when a
single person gets to the stage that he/she does many/most/all of the
following
  - posts frequently
  - makes erroneous statements frequently
  - states opinions as fact frequently
 - misrepresents others in his/her replies
 - does not acknowledge her/his previous errors
 - attacks others opinions as "wrong" while refusing to accept her/his
opinions as "views" not "truths"
 - seems to "turn off" the majority of experienced participants within this
newsgroup (whose opinions and knowledge I respect)

3) Before I use a "killfile" technique, I will ALWAYS try to explain my
personal opinions on productive participation in this newsgroup - both
publicly and privately to the person in question.  If this seems to be
ignored and the person continues to post notes that I see as
counter-productive, non-responsive, and/or confusing opinion with fact, then
I will use KILLFILE (or similar) approaches to avoid reading the posts
directly.  I will continue, however, to read replies to that person and when
necessary add additional information to explain  either what facts are in
error or what opinions I disagree with.

4)  As much as I enjoy and profit from this newsgroup, my PERSONAL opinion
is that its active participants represent only a *tiny* portion of those
using COBOL to earn their livings or produce "production" applications.
Therefore, I use my "contacts" and experience outside this newsgroup AS WELL
AS communications within the newsgroup to further my opinions of what is and
is not important to the "COBOL community". I do *not* limit my view of where
and how COBOL is currently used to any single platform or type of program.
Furthermore, I use my contacts with a variety of COBOL vendors to gain as
much knowledge as I can of what is and is not happening with COBOL vendors
and their users.  When any individual posts a note in this newsgroup that
runs counter to MY experience or opinions, I often reply with my OPINION or
evaluation - but hopefully "mark" it as based on personal experience NOT as
"truth from on high".

  ***

I don't expect this note to change anyone's posting habits.   I don't even
think that I will get those who * IMHO * abuse and misinform within the
newsgroup to change their ways.  I did, however, think that it was important
for me to restate WHY I react as I do.
```

#### ↳ Re: "Flaming," "ignoring," "killfiles" and other related topics

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-06T03:49:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8f976b.78040846@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b6ntda$qj$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>2) I rarely use a "killfile" technique in this newsgroup, but do when a
>single person gets to the stage that he/she does many/most/all of the
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>newsgroup (whose opinions and knowledge I respect)

I too have seen abusive posters. My standards for 'abusive' are more liberal
than yours. 

- posts illogical nonsense
- never answers rebuttal
- posts off-topic most of the time

I post frequently. What's wrong with that? Whether my statements are erroneous
will be settled here. We all state opinions as fact, it's human nature.
Misrepresents others is a bum rap. I don't do it deliberately; the few times I
have, it was by accident. I've acknowledged several previous errors. I attack
others much less frequently than they attack me. As to whether veterans are
'turned off', that's their decision. This is not a popularity contest won by the
most likable poster.
```

#### ↳ Re: "Flaming," "ignoring," "killfiles" and other related topics

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-06T21:10:27+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e9089be_1@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<b6ntda$qj$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b6ntda$qj$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net...
> I thought I had made my PERSONAL position clear a while ago, but thought
> that starting a new thread and explaining it again MIGHT be useful -
> especially when at least one person that I respect (yes, I mean you, Pete)
> seems to have missed it.

Bill, I haven't the faintest idea why this is directed at me. I have no
problem with whatever you do and I made my position on the subject of
killfiles very clear under the appropriate thread.

As far as I know, I didn't miss any points on this. My position is a matter
of record: people should do whatever they feel comfortable with. Personally,
I value free speech too much to try and suppress it, and I am grown-up
enough not to be wounded by what people post here. However, I respect the
rights of others to disagree and act differently.

I can only assume you have seen something quoted, that looked like it was
from me.

Please contact me by private mail if you have any issue with anything you
THINK I said.

I have done nothing that I know of to prompt this post.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: American & Ignorant - that excuse works for Bush!

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-08T11:08:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E931015.22D0CC8A@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<3E907D9C.7020408@Knology.net> <3e9087eb_1@127.0.0.1> <b6q4ia$v1i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e916726_1@127.0.0.1> <3E91D3E7.545625A@attglobal.net> <LvydnYxFu6U3fQyjXTWJjw@giganews.com> <3E920EFC.F2011744@attglobal.net> <joe_zitzelberger-5683E8.09582508042003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I attended a 4th of July event at a private club in East Texas.  My friend,
who invited us, was on the waiting list to get in from birth or soon
thereafter, and his chance came up at about age 50.  Geo W was the guest
speaker that year.  I was told he belonged to another club in East Texas.  (I
infer, perhaps incorrectly, that my friend's club was too exclusive for the
Bush family to get in.)  His inspirational message on our nation's
Independence Day was, "Why aren't you people buying more tickets to see the
Rangers?"

Of course, I don't want to be too hard on Bush.  I've been privileged to
attend the same event in other years.  One year, as the festivities ended, we
stood up from our table, and a lady at the next table turned to us and said, a
propos of nothing, "We're just a bunch of millionaires in blue jeans."  A
third year, the member who was supposed to lead the pledge of allegience was
too drunk to remember the words.  Perhaps they were both registered
Republicans, and voted for Geo W.

And, perhaps, I'm too hard on the young fella.
```

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJS@Knology.net>

#### ↳ Re: Full Stop

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-06T15:03:17-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6q169$k7u$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3E907D9C.7020408@Knology.net>`

```
"full stop" versus "period" is an England-English versus US-English
difference in terminology.

Similarly, braces (if I recall correctly) is a typical UK term for what US
people call parenthesis.

As far as Canada, Australia, NZ, and other English speaking nations go,
there is a tendency to use SOME "UK" terms and some "US" terms.  (Similarly
for spelling such as organization versus organisation)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Full Stop

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-07T21:16:36+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6q5gs$vnu$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3E907D9C.7020408@Knology.net> <b6q169$k7u$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

> "full stop" versus "period" is an England-English versus US-English
> difference in terminology.
> 
> Similarly, braces (if I recall correctly) is a typical UK term for what US
> people call parenthesis.

There are many symbols for which several names exist, notably the 
octothorpe '#' which may be called 'hash' in the UK.  The American may be 
'pound' (which seems to derive from being a misaligned 'lb' for pound 
weight), or 'number' (origin unknown), or 'sharp' from its similarity to 
the music symbol.

Parentheses = ()
Braces = {}
Brackets =[]
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Full Stop

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-07T16:06:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6s7lv$a5n$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3E907D9C.7020408@Knology.net> <3E90A74F.5AEEE172@shaw.ca>`

```

On  6-Apr-2003, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> ABSOLUTELY nothing fancy about 'full stop'. Just happens to be the way a
> period is expressed in 'Anglo' English, where a 'period' means something
> entirely different.
> Full stop. Period <G>

Although sometimes in CoBOL discussions we are a bit redundant to emphasize this
meaning.   A full stop period terminates all clauses and the sentence,
regardless of whether there are commands that have been explicitly terminated.

This feature can be useful if you have some code inserted by the compiler, or by
a pre-compiler and don't know if everything is terminated the way you expect.  
And it can make reading the program a bit easier - knowing the exact start of a
sentence.   On the other hand, periods can be hard to see, and if you cut and
paste logic from sentences to clauses, you have to remove them.

Of course, if you admit to using more than the minimum amount of periods
required by the syntax, you will find yourself abused by someone even though
that someone has expressed that he likes the way CoBOL is like English.
```

#### ↳ Re: Full Stop

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-07T19:58:58+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6q0vd$svv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3E907D9C.7020408@Knology.net>`

```
LX-i wrote:

> I'm new to the newsgroup, so pardon the seemingly dumb question...
> 
> I've been a COBOL developer for several years, and I'm not familiar with
> the term "full stop".  Is that a fancy term for a period?

This is an international group and not all spelling or terms will be 
American, some may be English.

The character '.' is a full stop. There is a gradient of pauses in speech 
indicated by ',', ';', ':', and '.', the last being called a full stop.

'Period' refers to a time span between a start point and an end point, for 
example an 'historical period' is from its first date to the last; a 
musical period is the time from its first bar to its last.

In English grammar the 'period of a sentence' should be from the start of 
its first sylable to the pause at the end. In fact dictionaries may give 
one definition as "complete sentence, esp if complex".

Use of the term 'period' to mean the full stop is recorded in England in 
the 17thC but America seem to have adopted its misuse entirely.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Full Stop

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-07T02:49:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e90d3ea.13845171@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3E907D9C.7020408@Knology.net> <b6q0vd$svv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>The character '.' is a full stop. 

No, it is a 'dot', which can have at least nine meanings. It can terminate a
sentence, it can terminate an abbreviation such as Mr., it can be a decimal
point, three dots can serve as punctuation for long pause, it can be a space
holder for elision, it can indicate repetition (as in move a to b, c ...), it
can indicate deleted text, and it can be a line-drawing character. Last but not
least, it can separate components of a computer file name.

>There is a gradient of pauses in speech 
>indicated by ',', ';', ':', and '.', the last being called a full stop.

By that logic, comma would be called pause, three dots would be called long
pause, semi-colon would be called 'parallel structure coming', and colon would
be called 'list coming'. 

>'Period' refers to a time span between a start point and an end point, for 
>example an 'historical period' is from its first date to the last; a 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>one definition as "complete sentence, esp if complex".

Your purist approach is technically correct. But in the real world, words mean
whatever the consensus of speakers agree on. 280M Americans can't be wrong. 

>Use of the term 'period' to mean the full stop is recorded in England in 
>the 17thC but America seem to have adopted its misuse entirely.

Granted, we screwed up the english (formerly English) language. Shit happens for
many reasons, the primary ones being economics and military prowess.
```

---

```
LX-i <DanielJS@Knology.net> wrote:

>~   /   \  /             Live from Montgomery, AL!   ~

This intriged me because I recently worked in Montgomery .. at US Dept of
Education in the 'Federal' building. Where do you work (no need to be specific)?

I have fond memories of the people of Montgomery. They are the most mannerly in
the US. I used to think West Texas was number one (which is why I lived there 20
years), but now I love Alabama more. 

Montgomery is not a hotbed of computer activity. That accrues to Birmingham, 100
miles north -- the number two yuppie center in the Deep South after Atlanta.
Montgomery is a soul-warming place. Outsiders expect to find a bunch of rednecks
(I did). They would be delighted to find their expectation is 180 degrees wrong.

If you're looking for a good restaurant, hard to find in Montgomery,  I
recommend the Mexican place on Bell just north of Eastern. It didn't show up in
Yellow Pages. I hope it's still in business.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: Montgomery (was Full Stop - Thanks)

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-07T22:31:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e91f609.88127478@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3E9094DE.4080302@Knology.net> <3e90df3f.16746787@news.optonline.net> <3E9189EB.4010201@Knology.net>`

```
LX-i <DanielJS@Knology.net> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>I'm a programmer for the Air Force, stationed at Maxwell AFB/Gunter Annex.

Some of the contractors on my project formerly worked there. 

> A group of us recently went to Biloxi, MS for 
>advanced training, and we all commented on how much more friendly the 
>people there seemed to be, especially those in the service industries. 

Biloxi is a special case. Outside financial interests hope to make it the Las
Vegas of the Deep South (never happen). They've spent many millions building
casinos and training locals to have a 'customer service attitude'.  The
friendliness you saw was the result of training. 

The good thing about Biloxi (for us consumers) is you can rent a super-deluxe
hotel room that would cost $200 elsewhere for $60-80. They're hurting for
business. They get mostly locals who return to their own home at night. 

>> Montgomery is a soul-warming place. Outsiders expect to find a bunch of
rednecks
>> (I did). They would be delighted to find their expectation is 180 degrees
wrong.
>
>I agree with that - the only real problem I've found is that there still 
>is quite a bit of racial tension.  It's not wide open, just more of a 
>culture of "our churches/businesses/restaurants" vs. "their ...".

I found exactly the opposite, especially from the black side. Most everywhere
else in the US, black people have an attitude. Not all, but many. They go around
in gangsta clothing and listen to rap music, trying to intimidate whites, or let
us know they got the short straw. Black people in Montgomery wore middle-class
clothing and drove nice, new middle-class cars. They listened to African music
(not rap), and sometimes dressed in African clothing. They seemed happy with
their station in life. I found it an ideal example of race relationship. 

I've not seen anyone, white or black, snubbed at a business or restaurant and my
antennae are very sensitive to that. 

Female programmers are rare. Black programmers are rare. In Montgomery I worked
with SEVERAL black female programmers who were quite good. One was what I'd call
a Real Programmer, an Ace.  Her preferred language was C, but she was pretty
good in COBOL too. She was a team lead over white guys who harbored no
resentment.
```

```
LX-i <DanielJS@Knology.net> wrote in message
news:3E9094DE.4080302@Knology.net...
> Okay, so I gave away that I'm in the US...  Thanks for all the replies.
>   At least now I know that I have an opinion of when "full stops" should
> be used.  :)  Maybe reading this group over the next few months will
> help "internationalize" me.

Now that others have helped to *confuse* you with their
opinions, whenever the mark . appears at the end of a
sentence or in formats, in standard COBOL, it is properly,
and formally, called a "separator period."

What it may be called outside standard COBOL is
irrelevant. <g>
```

## Thread: Creating an error report based on if's — 14 msgs

### Creating an error report based on if's

- **From:** jeannind@aol.com (jeanninD)
- **Date:** 2003-04-06T16:32:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f4b33faa.0304061532.336b900@posting.google.com>`

```
I'm taking a cobol class and have no clue why I don't get any output
from the below program.

******************************************************************
*THIS IS THE 4. ASSIGMENT WHICH                                   
*PERFORMS ADD,AVERAGE AND IF CALCULATIONS.                        
                                                                  
******************************************************************
 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.                                         
   PROGRAM-ID.   272JDP4.                                         
   AUTHOR.       JEANNIN DIESTERHOFT.                             
******************************************************************
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.                                            
   INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.                                          
    FILE-CONTROL.                                                 
     SELECT REPORT-FILE-1 ASSIGN TO 'A:rep1.doc'.                 
     SELECT REPORT-FILE-2 ASSIGN TO 'A:rep2.doc'.                 
     SELECT EDT-FILE ASSIGN TO 'A:\272P4EDT.dat'.                 
******************************************************************
 DATA DIVISION.                                                   
                                                                  
 FILE SECTION.                                                    
 FD  REPORT-FILE-1.                                               
 01  REPORT-RECORD                 PIC X(80).                     
 FD  REPORT-FILE-2.                                               
 01  ERROR-REC                     PIC x(80).                     
                                                                  
 FD  EDT-FILE.                                                    
 01  CR-RECORDS.                                                  
     05  STCD-N                    PIC X.                         
     05  DEPT-N                    PIC X.                         
     05  SECCD-N                   PIC X.                         
     05  EMPLNM-N                  PIC X(15).                     
     05  GROSSPY-N                 PIC 9999V99.                   
     05  NEPY-N                    PIC 9999V99.                   
**********************WORKING STORAGE SECTION*********************
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.                                         
                                                                  
 01  REPORT-T1.                                                   
     05  FILLER      PIC X(28) VALUE SPACES.                      
     05  FILLER      PIC X(24) VALUE 'JEANNIN DIESTERHOFT, INC'.  
     05  FILLER      PIC X(28) VALUE SPACES.                      
 01  REPORT-T2.                                                   
     05  FILLER      PIC X(5)  VALUE SPACES.                      
     05  MONAT1-O    PIC 9(2).                                    
     05  FILLER      PIC X(1)  VALUE '/'.                         
     05  TAG1-O      PIC 9(2).                                    
     05  FILLER      PIC X(1)  VALUE '/'.                         
     05  JAHR1-O     PIC 9(2).                                    
     05  FILLER      PIC X(15) VALUE SPACES.                      
     05  FILLER      PIC X(14) VALUE 'PAYROLL REPORT'.            
     05  FILLER      PIC X(31) VALUE SPACES.                      
     05  FILLER      PIC X(4)  VALUE 'PAGE'.                      
     05  FILLER      PIC X(15) VALUE SPACES.                      
     05  PAGENO-1    PIC 9(2)  VALUE 1.                           
 01  REPORT1-T1.                                                  
     05              PIC X(5)  VALUE SPACES.                      
     05              PIC X(10) VALUE 'STORE CODE'.                
     05              PIC X(2)  VALUE SPACES.                      
     05              PIC X(9)  VALUE 'DEPT-CODE'.                 
     05              PIC X(8)  VALUE SPACES.                      
     05              PIC X(4)  VALUE 'NAME'.                      
     05              PIC X(9) VALUE SPACES.                       
     05              PIC X(9) VALUE 'GROSS PAY'.                  
     05              PIC X(7) VALUE SPACES.                       
     05              PIC X(7) VALUE 'NET PAY'.                    
     05              PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.                      
 01  REPORT2-T2.                                                  
     05  FILLER      PIC X(5)  VALUE SPACES.                      
     05  MONAT2-O     PIC 9(2).                                   
     05  FILLER      PIC X(1)  VALUE '/'.                         
     05  TAG2-O       PIC 9(2).                                   
     05  FILLER      PIC X(1)  VALUE '/'.                         
     05  JAHR2-O      PIC 9(2).                                   
     05  FILLER      PIC X(15) VALUE SPACES.                      
     05  FILLER      PIC X(19) VALUE 'INVALID DATA REPORT'.       
     05  FILLER      PIC X(26) VALUE SPACES.                      
     05  FILLER      PIC X(4)  VALUE 'PAGE'.                      
     05  FILLER      PIC X(15) VALUE SPACES.                      
     05  PAGENO-2    PIC 9(2)  VALUE 1.                           
 01  REPORT2.                                                     
     05              PIC X(9) VALUE SPACES.                       
     05              PIC X(7) VALUE 'RECORED'.                    
     05              PIC X(20) VALUE SPACES.                      
     05              PIC X(6) VALUE 'ERRORS'.                     
     05              PIC X(38) VALUE SPACES.                      
 01  REPORT2-F1.                                                  
     05              PIC X(29) VALUE SPACES.                      
     05              PIC X(22) VALUE                              
         'MEANING OF ERROR CODES'.                                
     05              PIC X(29) VALUE SPACES.                      
 01  REPORT2-F2.                                                  
     05              PIC X(41) VALUE                              
         'A---Valid STORE CODEs are : 1, 2, 3, or 4'.             
     05              PIC X(39) VALUE SPACES.                      
 01  REPORT2-F3.                                                  
     05              PIC X(46) VALUE                              
         'B---Valid DEPARTMENT CODEs are : 1, 2, 3, or 4'.        
     05              PIC X(34) VALUE SPACES.                      
 01  REPORT2-F4.                                                  
     05              PIC X(30) VALUE                              
         'C---SECTION CODE must be blank'.                        
     05              PIC X(50) VALUE SPACES.                      
 01  REPORT2-F5.                                                  
     05              PIC X(34) VALUE                              
         'D---EMPLOYEE NAME must be present'.                     
     05              PIC X(46) VALUE SPACES.                      
 01  REPORT2-F6.                                                  
     05              PIC X(53) VALUE                              
         'E---GROSS PAY and NET PAY fields must contain numbers'. 
     05              PIC X(27) VALUE SPACES.                      
 01  REPORT2-F7.                                                  
     05              PIC X(47) VALUE                              
         'F---GROSS PAY and NET PAY fields cannot be more'.       
     05              PIC X(39) VALUE SPACES.                      
 01  REPORT2-F8.                                                  
     05              PIC X(4) VALUE SPACES.                       
     05              PIC X(32) VALUE                              
         'than $9,000.00 less than $100.00'.                      
     05              PIC X(44) VALUE SPACES.                      
 01  REPORT-LN.                                                   
     05  FILLER      PIC X(5) VALUE SPACES.                       
     05  FILLER      PIC X(69) VALUE ALL '-'.                     
     05  FILLER      PIC X(6) VALUE SPACES.                       
 01  TOTAL-LN.                                                    
     05  FILLER        PIC X(43) VALUE SPACES.                    
     05  FILLER        PIC X(6) VALUE 'TOTALS'.                   
     05  FILLER        PIC X(1) VALUE SPACES.                     
     05  GROSSPY-T     PIC $ZZZ,ZZ9.99 VALUE ZEROS.               
     05  FILLER        PIC X(6) VALUE SPACES.                     
     05  NEPY-T        PIC $ZZZ,ZZ9.99 VALUE ZEROS.               
     05  FILLER        PIC X(2) VALUE SPACES.                     
                                                                  
**********************DETAIL-LINE*********************************
 01  DETAIL-LINE.                                                 
     05  FILLER      PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.                      
     05  STCD-O      PIC X(1).                                    
     05  FILLER      PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.                      
     05  DEPT-O      PIC X(1).                                    
     05  FILLER      PIC X(6) VALUE SPACES.                       
     05  EMPLNM-O    PIC X(15).                                   
     05  FILLER      PIC X(4) VALUE SPACES.                       
     05  GROSSPY-O   PIC $ZZZ,ZZ9.99.                             
     05  FILLER      PIC X(5) VALUE SPACES.                       
     05  NEPY-O      PIC $ZZZ,ZZ9.99.                             
     05  FILLER      PIC X(6) VALUE SPACES.                       
 01  ERROR-LINE.                                                  
     05  ERRO-O        PIC X(18).                                 
     05  ERRO-O2       PIC 9(12).                                 
     05  FILLER        PIC X(13) VALUE SPACES.                    
     05  A-O           PIC X(2).                                  
     05  B-O           PIC X(2).                                  
     05  C-O           PIC X(2).                                  
     05  D-O           PIC X(2).                                  
     05  E-O           PIC X(2).                                  
     05  F-O           PIC X(2).                                  
     05  FILLER        PIC X(25) VALUE SPACES.                    
                                                                  
                                                                  
                                                                  
*******************PROGRAM VARIABLES******************************
 01 PROGRAM-VARIABLES.                                            
     05  EOF             PIC X.                                   
     05  COUNTER         PIC 999.                                 
     05  WS-DATE.                                                 
         07 WS-YY        PIC 99.                                  
         07 WS-MM        PIC 99.                                  
         07 WS-DD        PIC 99.                                  
     05 WS-GROSSPY-T     PIC 9999999V99.                          
     05 WS-NEPY-T        PIC 9999999V99.                          
     05 WS-A1            PIC X VALUE 'A'.                         
     05 WS-B1            PIC X VALUE 'B'.                         
     05 WS-C1            PIC X VALUE 'C'.                         
     05 WS-D1            PIC X VALUE 'D'.                         
     05 WS-E1            PIC X VALUE 'E'.                         
     05 WS-F1            PIC X VALUE 'F'.                         
     05 WS-A             PIC X.                                   
     05 WS-B             PIC X.                                   
     05 WS-C             PIC X.                                   
     05 WS-D             PIC X.                                   
     05 WS-E             PIC X.                                   
     05 WS-F             PIC X.                                   
     05 WS-STCD          PIC X.                                   
     05 WS-DEPT          PIC X.                                   
     05 WS-SECCD         PIC X.                                   
     05 WS-EMPLNM        PIC X(15).                               
     05 WS-GROSSPY      PIC 9999999V99.                           
     05 WS-NEPY         PIC 9999999V99.                           
                                                                  
******************************************************************
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.                                              
 001-CONTROL-RTN.                                                 
     PERFORM 100-OPEN-RTN.                                        
     PERFORM 150-INITIALIZE-RTN.                                  
     PERFORM 151-INITIALIZE-RTN2                                  
     PERFORM UNTIL EOF = 'Y'                                      
         READ EDT-FILE                                            
             AT END MOVE 'Y' TO EOF                               
             ADD 1 TO PAGENO-1                                    
             NOT AT END                                           
             PERFORM 201-IF.                                      
     PERFORM 165-CLOSELINE.                                       
     PERFORM 170-TOTALLN.                                         
     PERFORM 050-CLOSE-RTN.                                       
     STOP RUN.                                                    
 100-OPEN-RTN.                                                    
     OPEN INPUT EDT-FILE.                                         
     OPEN OUTPUT REPORT-FILE-1.                                   
     OPEN OUTPUT REPORT-FILE-2.                                   
***************** 1. PAGE MOVE ***********************************
 150-INITIALIZE-RTN.                                              
     MOVE SPACES TO REPORT-RECORD.                                
     INITIALIZE PROGRAM-VARIABLES.                                
     WRITE REPORT-RECORD FROM REPORT-T1 BEFORE ADVANCING 1        
     LINE.                                                        
     ACCEPT WS-DATE FROM DATE.                                    
     MOVE WS-YY TO JAHR1-O.                                       
     MOVE WS-MM TO MONAT1-O.                                      
     MOVE WS-DD TO TAG1-O.                                        
     WRITE REPORT-RECORD FROM REPORT-T2 BEFORE ADVANCING 1        
         LINE.                                                    
     WRITE REPORT-RECORD FROM REPORT-LN BEFORE ADVANCING 1        
         LINE.                                                    
     PERFORM 201-IF.                                              
 151-INITIALIZE-RTN2.                                             
     MOVE SPACES TO ERROR-REC.                                    
     INITIALIZE PROGRAM-VARIABLES.                                
     WRITE ERROR-REC FROM REPORT-T1 BEFORE ADVANCING 1            
         LINE.                                                    
     ACCEPT WS-DATE FROM DATE.                                    
     MOVE WS-YY TO JAHR2-O.                                       
     MOVE WS-MM TO MONAT2-O.                                      
     MOVE WS-DD TO TAG2-O.                                        
     WRITE ERROR-REC FROM REPORT2-T2 BEFORE ADVANCING 1           
         LINE.                                                    
**THIS CREATES THE TOTAL LINE FOR THE 1. PAGE AND MOVES ON TO THE*
                                                                  
 170-TOTALLN.                                                     
     WRITE REPORT-RECORD FROM TOTAL-LN BEFORE ADVANCING 1 LINE.   
                                                                  
                                                                  
****      THE NEXT STMNTS PERFORM THE CALCULATIONS          ******
**********************  *****************************             
 201-IF.                                                          
     IF STCD-N IS >0 OR                                           
     STCD-N IS <5                                                 
     MOVE WS-A1 TO A-O                                            
             IF DEPT-N IS >0 OR                                   
             DEPT-N IS <5                                         
             MOVE WS-B1 TO B-O                                    
                 IF SECCD-N IS ALPHABETIC OR                      
                 SECCD-N IS NUMERIC                               
                 MOVE WS-C1 TO C-O                                
                       IF EMPLNM-N IS NOT ALPHABETIC              
                       OR EMPLNM-N IS NUMERIC                     
                       MOVE WS-D1 TO D-O                          
                             IF GROSSPY-N IS NOT NUMERIC          
                             OR NEPY-N IS NOT NUMERIC             
                             MOVE WS-E1 TO E-O                    
*                        IF GROSSPY-N AND NEPY-N IS >100<9000     
*                        NEPY-N IS >100<9000                      
                         MOVE WS-F1 TO F-O                        
                         PERFORM 220-ERRRTN                       
                         ELSE PERFORM 155-PROCESS-RECORDS         
                         END-IF.                                  
 155-PROCESS-RECORDS.                                             
     MOVE STCD-N TO STCD-O .                                      
     MOVE DEPT-N TO DEPT-O.                                       
     MOVE EMPLNM-N TO EMPLNM-O.                                   
     MOVE GROSSPY-N TO GROSSPY-O.                                 
     MOVE NEPY-N TO NEPY-O.                                       
     MOVE WS-GROSSPY TO GROSSPY-T.                                
     MOVE WS-NEPY TO NEPY-T.                                      
     WRITE REPORT-RECORD FROM DETAIL-LINE BEFORE 1.               
 220-ERRRTN.                                                      
     MOVE STCD-N TO ERRO-O                                        
*    MOVE DEPT-N TO ERRO-O                                        
*    MOVE SECCD-N TO ERRO-O                                       
*    MOVE EMPLNM-N TO ERRO-O                                      
*    MOVE GROSSPY-N TO ERRO-O2                                    
*    MOVE NEPY-N TO ERRO-O2                                       
*        MOVE WS-A TO A-O                                         
*        MOVE WS-B TO B-O                                         
*        MOVE WS-C TO C-O                                         
*        MOVE WS-D TO D-O                                         
*        MOVE WS-E TO E-O                                         
*        MOVE WS-F TO F-O.                                        
                                                                  
****THE NEXT LINE MOVES THE DETAIL LINE TO THE REPORT RECORD *****
 WRITE REPORT-RECORD FROM DETAIL-LINE BEFORE 1.                   
****THE NEXT LLINE CREATES THE LINES ON THE REPORT           *****
                                                                  
 165-CLOSELINE.                                                   
     WRITE REPORT-RECORD FROM REPORT-LN BEFORE ADVANCING 1 LINE.  
                                                                  
******************************************************************
 050-CLOSE-RTN.                                                   
     CLOSE EDT-FILE.                                              
     CLOSE REPORT-FILE-1.                                         
     CLOSE REPORT-FILE-2.
```

#### ↳ Re: Creating an error report based on if's

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJS@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-06T19:11:08-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E90C21C.7090909@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<f4b33faa.0304061532.336b900@posting.google.com>`

```
Without your input file, it's difficult to know for sure.  I did see a 
few things, though, at which you may want to look.  (I'm assuming that 
it compiles and executes without erroring...  If not, that's a good 
place to start. :> )

> ******************************************************************
>  PROCEDURE DIVISION.                                              
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>              PERFORM 201-IF.                                      

The compiler I use requires the use of End-Perform to terminate an 
inline perform like this (and you'd also need an End-Read to complete 
the Read clause).

>      PERFORM 165-CLOSELINE.                                       
>      PERFORM 170-TOTALLN.                                         
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> ****THE NEXT LLINE CREATES THE LINES ON THE REPORT           *****

This Write statement seems to be in Area A.  Again, the compiler I use 
would gripe severely at this, and probably would not include the line in 
the object module.  If this is the case, and all of your input records 
are hitting this error routine, that would explain why nothing is being 
written to your output file.

Best of luck - analyzing and debugging programs is often more 
complicated than writing them in the first place.  And, the problem with 
it is that you only get that skill through "experience" (so think of 
this as great preparation for the real world!).

(I'm also assuming that your code conforms to whatever style the class 
dictates - I personally find If statements easier to track if I indent 
all the statements under it, not just when there's another If.  For 
example...

      If WS-A = WS-B
          Move WS-C to WS-D
          Display "Isn't this Useless Code?!?!" upon Printer
*        This next If should always be true
          If WS-C = WS-D
              Move WS-D to WS-C
              Display "Still Going!" upon Printer
              If WS-E = WS-F
                  Display "...and so on" upon Printer
              Else
                  Display "E and F are not equal" upon Printer
              End-If
          End-If
      End-If

That way, the End-Ifs match up with the Ifs.)


~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
~   /   \  /             Live from Montgomery, AL!   ~
~  /     \/       o                                  ~
~ /      /\   -   |       AIM:  LXi0007              ~
~ _____ /  \      |    E-mail:  DanielJS@Knology.net ~
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```
