## Thread: Object code efficiency - COBOL vs C. — 1 msgs

### Re: Object code efficiency - COBOL vs C.

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-04-10T15:11:24-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E95B3CC.C56259E7@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<200304101642.h3AGgWtu163860@westrelay04.boulder.ibm.com>`

```
Tom Ross wrote:
> 
> >I know that this is comparing apples to oranges (or maybe apples to horse
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> with the TRUNC(OPT) compiler option.

I have seen the code referred to by John when compiling using TRUNC(OPT)
with COBOL for MVS and VM.  I think that the double precision code is
generated to cater for the general case of ADD A B C D TO E where A B
and C have values of +900,000,000 while D and E have values of
-901,000,000.  I believe that I found a circumvention by defining
WS-FULLWORD as S9(8) BINARY.  

> 
> Cheers,
> TomR              >> COBOL is the Language of the Future! <<
>
```

<a id="t-dd7a3d19e6"></a>
## Thread: Looking for Cobol Job(s) in England - any ideas? — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### Looking for Cobol Job(s) in England - any ideas?

- **From:** lcjarvis@sbic.co.za (Lee)
- **Date:** 2003-04-10T12:18:54-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2c4867c8.0304101118.27a0ad77@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi All
I am hoping to find work in England and would like to know if anyone
knows any companies/organisations that use MVS Cobol with ADABAS
databases?
I have registered with some job sites that send daily emails.
Appreciate any info.
Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Looking for Cobol Job(s) in England - any ideas?

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-10T20:40:52+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e95c2cd_2@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<2c4867c8.0304101118.27a0ad77@posting.google.com>`

```
There are certainly some sites in Germany that still use ADABAS on the
mainframe.

The job site I have found most useful for Europe (obviously, including the
UK) is www.jobserve.com.

If you search there for "ADABAS" you might be lucky. In general the ones
that send you e-mail are not really much cop. By the time they send it the
job is gone and they use software to match on keywords which means they lack
subtlety.

There is really no substitute for searching manually on your own keywords.
Of course there is nothing to stop you using all of these methods; they are
not mutually exclusive.

Good Luck!

Pete.
```

## Thread: Micro Focus Cobol Error Messages — 7 this month / 15 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Micro Focus Cobol Error Messages

- **From:** MFDEVP <member27090@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-10T20:22:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2753633.1050006128@dbforums.com>`

```

I am starting new thread to keep track of all the Micro focus
error message.

Error 114:

I am getting error code - 114 . Attempt to access item beyond bounds
of memory.

When this happens it is not showing in which program this
is occurring.How to resolve this problem .
would anybody please post the solution to this problem.
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus Cobol Error Messages

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-04-10T22:24:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E95EFC3.C74ABD0B@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<2753633.1050006128@dbforums.com>`

```


MFDEVP wrote:

> I am starting new thread to keep track of all the Micro focus
> error message.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You haven't given us any code to clue in on. Most often it will apply to
mismatching on sending and receiving parameters (via Linkage) whether
CALLING (non OO) or INVOKING (in OO).

Try and follow it through with the Animator putting Breakpoints where
you think the error may be occurring.

If this doesn't help - some more explicit information please.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus Cobol Error Messages

- **From:** MFDEVP <member27090@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-11T14:11:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2756522.1050070270@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<2753633.1050006128@dbforums.com>`

```

Thanks for your reply.This is happening only when we set up the
server(CICS) level of security to "normal".If we change back the
security level to "none" then we are having any problems.However ,
down the line
we need to have the security level to "normal".It's kind of confusing
from where this is ocurring(server level or porgramming level).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus Cobol Error Messages

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-11T16:30:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e96ec43.26790483@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2753633.1050006128@dbforums.com> <2756522.1050070270@dbforums.com>`

```
MFDEVP <member27090@dbforums.com> wrote:

>
>Thanks for your reply.This is happening only when we set up the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>from where this is ocurring(server level or porgramming level).

That narrows it down. Look for pointer-based structures dealing with security.
Are they the right size? If they are allocated too small or referenced too big,
that will cause a memory error. 

There is a remote chance the error is caused by a errant subscript. Try turning
on bounds checking if it's not already on. I doubt this is your problem, but
it's an easy test.
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus Cobol Error Messages

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-11T16:01:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e96e549.25004354@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2753633.1050006128@dbforums.com>`

```
MFDEVP <member27090@dbforums.com> wrote:

>
>I am starting new thread to keep track of all the Micro focus
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>would anybody please post the solution to this problem.

Since I use pointers so much, I occasionally get a 114. It means invalid
pointer. More than 98% of the time, the pointer is null. Look at linkage-section
or local-storage structures. The pointer to one of them is bad. The error could
be as simple as a parameter not being passed. 

My MicroFocus does give the name of the dynamically called program. If you have
a lot of static binding, consider making them dynamic.
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus Cobol Error Messages

- **From:** MFDEVP <member27090@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-11T19:03:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2757937.1050087801@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<2753633.1050006128@dbforums.com>`

```

I don't understand how come the problem is at programming side.This is
happening only when there is a security level change at the server
level.In my earlier E-mail I haven't expalined properly.

If the security level = "Normal" -- We had a problem with Error - 144.

if the security level = "none" -- We had no problem.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus Cobol Error Messages

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-12T09:29:57+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b77c67$211$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<2753633.1050006128@dbforums.com> <2757937.1050087801@dbforums.com>`

```
MFDEVP wrote:

> I don't understand how come the problem is at programming side.

Then, being programmers, we probably can't help you.

> This is
> happening only when there is a security level change at the server
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> if the security level = "none" -- We had no problem.

Are you providing security information to the server ?  Perhaps you should 
be.  If it works when it doesn't check security but fails when it does 
check security then what is it checking ?  Is it something that you are 
passing in a parameter, or should be but aren't ?
```

<a id="t-9fb7fff00f"></a>
## Thread: Capex Module Running — 3 msgs

### Capex Module Running

- **From:** mwhandi@hotmail.com (chiTownVet)
- **Date:** 2003-04-11T06:14:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5cc91e47.0304110514.1ac0a0df@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello.  I have a module that is showing it was compiled with the Capex
Optimizer back in 1988.  This module is running as part of a daily job
and the module that calls it was compiled last in 1997.  What troubles
me is that we no longer have the Capex runtime environment installed
anywhere in our sysplex.  Accordingly, Capex modules should not be
able to run.  Any ideas how this can be happening?  Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Capex Module Running

- **From:** charles.godwin@ca.com (Charles Godwin)
- **Date:** 2003-04-11T10:13:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8035ce7.0304110913.3749bf76@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<5cc91e47.0304110514.1ac0a0df@posting.google.com>`

```
I work for Computer Associates, the company that bought Capex many,
many years ago. I did some research for you. Here's what I learned.

Your program was probably compiled using an old Capex release, one
that was licensed by the customer before CA acquired Capex, back when
there was no run time library requirement.  Just because it was
compiled in 1988 doesn't mean the optimizer release is from 1988.

Charles Godwin
Computer Associates
Development Manager
Advantage CA-Realia II Workbench (COBOL)


mwhandi@hotmail.com (chiTownVet) wrote in message news:<5cc91e47.0304110514.1ac0a0df@posting.google.com>...
> Hello.  I have a module that is showing it was compiled with the Capex
> Optimizer back in 1988.  This module is running as part of a daily job
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> able to run.  Any ideas how this can be happening?  Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Capex Module Running

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-11T18:32:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PSDla.631$ug7.355220@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<5cc91e47.0304110514.1ac0a0df@posting.google.com>`

```
"chiTownVet" <mwhandi@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:5cc91e47.0304110514.1ac0a0df@posting.google.com...
> Hello.  I have a module that is showing it was compiled with the Capex
> Optimizer back in 1988.  This module is running as part of a daily job
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> able to run.  Any ideas how this can be happening?  Thanks.

(from one who had never heard of Capex Optimizer before now)

Perhaps the Capex runtime is used only when a program is optimized; that is,
it is not required to execute a program which has already been
"Capex-optimized."

MCM
```

## Thread: "Value is External" and standards — 3 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### "Value is External" and standards

- **From:** "Richard Maher" <maher_rj@hotspamnotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-13T15:28:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b7bvm4$7oj$1@titan.btinternet.com>`

```
Hi,

Does anyone know if there is anything in the COBOL standards about what
should be permissable as a value for VALUE IS EXTERNAL?

DEC COBOL says any Word or Longword value, but in this age of 64-bits surely
a quadword value should be ok? (If using something like 9(18) comp.
Obviously)

Regards Richard Maher
```

#### ↳ Re: "Value is External" and standards

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-13T12:30:49-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v9j485phpg9e9a@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b7bvm4$7oj$1@titan.btinternet.com>`

```

Richard Maher <maher_rj@hotspamnotmail.com> wrote in message
news:b7bvm4$7oj$1@titan.btinternet.com...
> Hi,
>
> Does anyone know if there is anything in the COBOL standards about what
> should be permissable as a value for VALUE IS EXTERNAL?

VALUE IS EXTERNAL (and VALUE IS REFERENCE) are
not standard COBOL, they, apparently, are DEC COBOL
extensions. Therefore the "COBOL standards" say nothing
about what is permissible with these extensions.

Having said that, I do not know enough about DEC COBOL
to answer the following question regarding 'quadwords'.

> DEC COBOL says any Word or Longword value, but in this age of 64-bits
surely
> a quadword value should be ok? (If using something like 9(18) comp.
> Obviously)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```


### Need a Little help

- **From:** Lee Itson <litson@columbus.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-13T15:10:13-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b7ccmm$cfi$1@news.cis.ohio-state.edu>`

```
Ok guys, I am just starting with COBOL in a class I'm taking and I'm 
sure these questions are SOOOOO elementary that it isnt funny, but i 
wanted to make sure I'm doing it right.

1.  The report I need to make has page headings and column headings.  Do 
I need to defined those in the File Definitions of the Data Division?

2. On the topic of headings, the column headings need to be underlined. 
  I havent been able to pick up the book we are using yet, and out prof 
hasn't spoken about it, so was wondering what the command for underline was.

Thanks in Advance Guys.

Lee Itson
```

#### ↳ Re: Need a Little help

- **From:** "Lorne Sunley" <lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-04-13T19:27:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<b7ccmm$cfi$1@news.cis.ohio-state.edu>`

```
On Sun, 13 Apr 2003 19:10:13 UTC, Lee Itson <litson@columbus.rr.com> 
wrote:

> Ok guys, I am just starting with COBOL in a class I'm taking and I'm 
> sure these questions are SOOOOO elementary that it isnt funny, but i 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

No, those would usually be defined in working storage, where you can 
assign a literal value to the headings.

> 2. On the topic of headings, the column headings need to be underlined. 
>   I havent been able to pick up the book we are using yet, and out prof 
> hasn't spoken about it, so was wondering what the command for underline was.
> 

You can issue printer control commands to select a font with 
"underline" or you can print another line with "-" characters in the 
appropriate spots.

> Thanks in Advance Guys.
> 
> Lee Itson
> 
```

```
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Lorne Sunley

     I know of no reliable way to underline something.  Printing a line
of underlines on top of the first line does not work on laser printers,
and using printer control commands does not normally
work on line printers.
```

#### ↳ Re: Need a Little help

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJS@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-13T16:41:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E99D982.70009@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<b7ccmm$cfi$1@news.cis.ohio-state.edu>`

```
Lee Itson wrote:
> 2. On the topic of headings, the column headings need to be underlined. 
>  I havent been able to pick up the book we are using yet, and out prof 
> hasn't spoken about it, so was wondering what the command for underline 
> was.

You might want to investigate the WRITE ... BEFORE ADVANCING syntax. 
Using it, you can define two records in working-storage - one with text, 
and one with underscores.  Then, you can "overprint" the underscores on 
the text.  It's tricky, but your textbook or COBOL Language Reference 
Manual will detail it for you.

~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```

```
and columns on a print page.
```

#### ↳ Re: Need a Little help

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-17T07:00:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dsmdneRum8rvCgOjXTWJiw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<b7ccmm$cfi$1@news.cis.ohio-state.edu>`

```

"Lee Itson" <litson@columbus.rr.com> wrote in message
news:b7ccmm$cfi$1@news.cis.ohio-state.edu...
> Ok guys, I am just starting with COBOL in a class I'm taking and I'm
> sure these questions are SOOOOO elementary that it isnt funny, but i
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> hasn't spoken about it, so was wondering what the command for underline
was.

Look at the purpose, not the symptom.

Underlining is an artifact left from the days of the typewriter to emphasise
a word or phrase. By so doing, the typist was trying to emulate the printed
world with the tools she had available.

You can now use the same visual tools as the typographer - bold, italic,
even a different font. Go back to the concept of printing and use bold.
Don't try to emulate a typewriter that's compensating as it trys to emulate
a printing press.
```

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJS@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-17T20:41:46-05:00

### Converting an acucobol database

- **From:** "Lorenzo Lutti" <lorenzo.lutti@DOHtiscalinet.it>
- **Date:** 2003-04-13T20:47:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d1kma.34781$iy5.1025177@twister2.libero.it>`

```
I have to extract the data contained in an acucobol database (Siber Data
Viewer reports it as "ACU indexed format"). I don't know anything about
cobol, but since I know the record structure I tried to guess the format
and write a little parsing program; without success.

Is there a free export tool for this type of format? I don't look for
nothing sophysticated, a plain text output would be enough.

Alternatively, is there some tecnhical documentation on this format?
I've found something for the RM-COBOL, but apparently it's different.

Thank you.
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting an acucobol database

- **From:** ray@rays-web.com (Ray Smith)
- **Date:** 2003-04-13T21:46:11-07:00

- **References:** `<d1kma.34781$iy5.1025177@twister2.libero.it>`

```
Hi,

AcuCOBOL comes with a utility called "vutil" (vutil32.exe on Windows).
Use this utility to dump the data into text files using 

vutil -extract {filename} > {textfilename}

If you don't have a copy of vutil you should be able to run it on your clients 
computer who should have it.

Regards,

Ray Smith
```

- **Message-ID:** `<3E9AF2C1.AFBA2C57@ieee.org>`
- **References:** `<a07fdaf75d40819dcfb045002b337d2d@news.teranews.com> <3E88CC0F.9EF1D6BB@ieee.org> <b7dfen$3qd$1@loghost1.eu.boschrexroth.com>`

```
Hans Dampf wrote:
> 
> what ist the "hidden window trick"?
> 
A hidden window is a pop-up (You must have seen those!) whose display
coordinates put it off screen (or sometimes make it one pixel by one
pixel) and which runs a java script that can do pretty much anything. If
you see unexplained internet traffic to or from your machine after
visiting a website, either check (if you have the software) or close all
the visible the browser windows. If the traffic continues, the browser
is still running and there's a hidden window at work. Confirm with
(<ctrl-alt-del>, use "the close program" button, then start over.

Jerry
```

## Thread: Advise on program structure needed — 28 this month / 32 total

### Advise on program structure needed

- **From:** aliensite@excite.com (aliensite)
- **Date:** 2003-04-13T23:01:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ca0d9b0f.0304132201.76fc0f6e@posting.google.com>`

```
I am learning COBOL and the programs structure in my text don't always
seem logical. Luckly my professor is a COBOL programmer who gives us
real world tips. He advises to use a period after each statement, but
I find one period after a paragraph easier to read. I don't like big
words since I am not a good spell checker, and I try to write code
that is modular, easy to read and modify. My final project produces a
report, I listed the paragraphs below. Is this structure ok? Thanks.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.                                              
    PERFORM 1000-INIT                                            
    PERFORM 2000-READ                                            
    PERFORM 3000-MAIN UNTIL EOF-SW = "Y"                         
    PERFORM 4000-TERM                                            
    GOBACK.                                                      

1000-INIT.

2000-READ.

3000-MAIN.
3100-PAGE-HEADINGS.
3200-DETAIL-LINE.
3210-VALIDATE.
3300-ADD-TOTALS.

4000-TERM.
4100-TOTAL-LINE.
4200-CLOSE-FILES.
```

#### ↳ Re: Advise on program structure needed

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-14T07:24:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e9a5dd7.39995547@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<ca0d9b0f.0304132201.76fc0f6e@posting.google.com>`

```
aliensite@excite.com (aliensite) wrote:

>I am learning COBOL and the programs structure in my text don't always
>seem logical. Luckly my professor is a COBOL programmer who gives us
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>4200-CLOSE-FILES.


Your structure is ok -- it has a beginning, middle and end. But your
nomenclature sucks. Get rid of those four digit numbers. They were invented for
working on hard-copy program listings. Now that we use text editors, they just
get in the way. And stop writing in upper case. 

I would change 3000-MAIN to 'one-record' or 'one-detail-record'. I would put
TOTAL-LINE on par with DETAIL-LINE.
```

- **References:** `<ca0d9b0f.0304132201.76fc0f6e@posting.google.com> <3e9a5dd7.39995547@news.optonline.net>`

```

> Get rid of those four digit numbers. They were invented for

>get in the way. 

In addition to the other comments on this it may be that he has to turn in a

makes the screen messy).  Also can be useful with the cross reference that
alphabetises so they will list in order as coded.  Useful at the beginning -
kinda like training wheels.

>And stop writing in upper case. 

Not only does WYLBUR require upper case - so does the mainframe, at least all
the ones I've worked on so far .  While I may type in lower case the editor
upper cases it automatically and if I were to cut/paste the code here it would
be in upper case.  Please get the flea out of your undergarment on this one.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Advise on program structure needed

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-04-16T05:44:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<16e2f010.0304160444.e54a291@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3e9a5dd7.39995547@news.optonline.net> <20030415225150.18724.00000658@mb-fn.aol.com> <b7ip34$peh$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<b7ip34$peh$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>...
> "YukonMama" <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote in message
> news:20030415225150.18724.00000658@mb-fn.aol.com...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> What tools are you using that require upper-case source code?

ISPF tends to default to "CAPS ON", which will uppercase each line as
it is entered.

This default can be changed, but only if you know about it.  It is
additionally complicated by any site installation options and user
modes that can alter the behavior (like CAPS(ON) on a custom edit
panel).

This can be turned off in ones profile, but ISPF will also flip it
back on if it ever notices you editing an all uppercase document in
"CAPS OFF" mode.  It is just trying to be helpful.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Advise on program structure needed

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-04-16T05:44:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<16e2f010.0304160444.3d73c222@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3e9a5dd7.39995547@news.optonline.net> <20030415225150.18724.00000658@mb-fn.aol.com> <b7ip34$peh$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<b7ip34$peh$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>...
> "YukonMama" <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote in message
> news:20030415225150.18724.00000658@mb-fn.aol.com...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> What tools are you using that require upper-case source code?

ISPF tends to default to "CAPS ON", which will uppercase each line as
it is entered.

This default can be changed, but only if you know about it.  It is
additionally complicated by any site installation options and user
modes that can alter the behavior (like CAPS(ON) on a custom edit
panel).

This can be turned off in ones profile, but ISPF will also flip it
back on if it ever notices you editing an all uppercase document in
"CAPS OFF" mode.  It is just trying to be helpful.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Advise on program structure needed

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2003-04-18T02:32:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030417223243.18914.00000095@mb-m18.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<b7ip34$peh$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
>From: "William M. Klein" wmklein@ix.netcom.com 
>Date: 4/16/03 1:18 AM Eastern Daylight Time

>This surprises me.  Even in the late '80s when VS COBOL II first started
>accepting mixed case COBOL, I used ISPF settings for COBOL source code (with
>the IBM environment) that fully supported mixed case.
>
>What tools are you using that require upper-case source code?


Perhaps 'require' was a poor choice of word.   I'm a predominately Roscoe user
and the default is upper case.  One can change the default to mixed case but in
all shops that I've been in that use it have it with the default.

I'm not sure what the default is on my ISPF as I don't use it for source code
editing and when file editing it just does what it wants which is usually what
I don't :).  However those in my shop that do use Elipse I believe also end up
with upper case as the default.

There isn't any source code in the shop that isn't in upper case.  Any lower
case is in literals.
```

```
Hi A,

Well done, but the real challenge comes with the addition of control
breaks. Fer instance, how do you code your report pgm when the
xactions contain order info for products invoiced on differing dates
for various accounts managed by each of the sales districts in the
organization you work for?

BTW, FWIW, numbered pgraphs always gave me a "feel" for where I was in
the code when viewing it on paper or in an editor. Sometimes it's
easier to page up or down than to issue a "find".

I find that it's "safer" to copy code from one place to another in a
pgm when there's no chance for a period to prematurely terminate an
"IF" stmt.

Just can't get used to upper/lower case code. One question: Are
My-Fld, MY-FLD, my-fld, equivilant? Or will referencing one by another
cause a compiler error?

Regards, Jack.
```

```
"Jack Sleight" <jacksleight@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:8a2d426e.0304141300.78cf76d9@posting.google.com...
> Hi A,
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

    Classroom programs are unlikely to be big enough
to make numbered paragraphs useful.  As with so many
other options, it all depends on what you get used to.

    I used to be happy with a 25x80 screen.  They now make
me feel like I am peering thru a keyhole.

    The jury is still out on periods.  The "stick a period everywhere
they will fit" school causes extra work when gathering several
lines into a single if, and as you noted, can terminate an if
prematurely.

    On the other hand, the "one and only one period" school
can have trouble with If's that extend farther than you would like.

RE  (View in courier)

IF A = B
        DO THIS
ELSE
        IF A = C
              DO THAT
END-IF                        *> If you put a period here,
                                   *> the error goes away
MORE CODE
END OF PARAGRAPH.


MORE-CODE will be part of the if, but clearly (if the indents survive),
should not.  But it will (should?) compile cleanly.


    I personally think that the "few periods" style will win, now
that we have END-IF.  I can't tell you how many times END-IF
has saved me a bug by forceing a compile error, ie

IF A = B
             DO THIS
             DO THIS ALSO.            *> NOTE PERIOD
             MORE STUFF
END-IF.

        This will fail to compile.






> Just can't get used to upper/lower case code. One question: Are
> My-Fld, MY-FLD, my-fld, equivilant? Or will referencing one by another
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
Hi Russell,

I would have coded it:


IF A = B
   DO THIS
ELSE
   IF A = C
      DO THAT
   END-IF      *> extra end-if
END-IF                        *> If you put a period here,
                                   *> the error goes away
MORE CODE
END OF PARAGRAPH
.              *> separate line in the event I need to copy "end of pgraph" 

On the other hand I'm not a purist. If the end-ifs got out of hand I'd use a period.

Regards, Jack.
```

#### ↳ Re: Advise on program structure needed

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-14T09:58:01-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-525FED.09580014042003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ca0d9b0f.0304132201.76fc0f6e@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <ca0d9b0f.0304132201.76fc0f6e@posting.google.com>,
 aliensite@excite.com (aliensite) wrote:

> I am learning COBOL and the programs structure in my text don't always
> seem logical. Luckly my professor is a COBOL programmer who gives us
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> 4200-CLOSE-FILES.

Structure looks good, but a few comments:

Your EOF-SW = "Y" would be quite nice as an 88-level conditional.  
"PERFORM 3000-MAIN UNTIL END-OF-FILE" reads clearer and ensures you 
never accidently move "X" to EOF-SW or somesuch.

Does your prof require the 4-digit prefixed paragraphs?  They used to be 
useful in finding your way around your listing, but these days they just 
screw up your source control and/or misrepresent the program.
```

#### ↳ Re: Advise on program structure needed

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2003-04-14T18:44:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0304141612.46245c04@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ca0d9b0f.0304132201.76fc0f6e@posting.google.com>`

```
aliensite@excite.com (aliensite) wrote in message news:<ca0d9b0f.0304132201.76fc0f6e@posting.google.com>...
> I am learning COBOL and the programs structure in my text don't always
> seem logical. Luckly my professor is a COBOL programmer who gives us
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> 4200-CLOSE-FILES.

I generally agree with the comments made by other people, though I see
no harm in using prefixes the way that you have.

However, I would not have 2000-READ where it is, but subordinate to
3000-MAIN, where it would be the first procedure executed.

You could consider a 0000-CONTROL procedure name at the very start of
the program, good for anarchists!.

You could consider a 9xxx- series for error handling procedures,
alternatively you might prefer to use declaratives, especially if you
have a 2002 standard compiler.

For larger programs, you could consider an 8xxx- series for your I-O
procedures, especially for those that can be performed from a variety
of places.  Similarly a 7xxx- series for commonly invoked non-I-O
procedures.

I generally prefer alphanumeric prefixes such as in A-CONTROL,
B-INITIALISE, C-PROCESS, D-END-OF-JOB, with subordinate procedures to
these prefixed with additional letters such as BA-SET-VALUES,
BA-OPEN-FILES, etc.  But this is a matter of style.  On EBCDIC systems
the numbers follow the letters, but in any case there are 26 letters
as opposed to 10 digits, so more complex programs with a flattish
profile are more easily accommodated.  Use of letters (or digits) as
prefixes in this manner is arguably more flexible, as more complex
programs can use any number of letters in a prefix.

I agree that such prefixes, either alphanumeric or numeric can make
shuffling the procedures around during program development and
subsequent maintenance/development more difficult, though when used
well, they make program organisation easier to comprehend to other
programmers and even to the author several months later.  I would like
to see program editors with an outlining facility that would handle
this semi-automatically, but appreciate that I couldn't write one
myself, well I don't think I could.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Advise on program structure needed

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-04-15T03:58:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E9B848C.D4C5DF34@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ca0d9b0f.0304132201.76fc0f6e@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0304141612.46245c04@posting.google.com>`

```


Robert Jones wrote:

> aliensite@excite.com (aliensite) wrote in message news:<ca0d9b0f.0304132201.76fc0f6e@posting.google.com>...
> > I am learning COBOL and the programs structure in my text don't always
…[63 more quoted lines elided]…
> myself, well I don't think I could.

Robert,

Actually I bet you could write a non-OO template <G>. So happens in the case of Net Express that M/F does
provide some OO templates - but the very basics. Certainly a good starting point for beginners in OO. (I don't
use them preferring my own customized format where I use the word 'FACTORY' - doesn't appear in the M/F
template because they firmed up on them quite some years ago).

By no means 'smooth' but triggered by something Richard wrote a short while back, I have backtracked in OO -
where I have a dialog I've now written program templates that create (1) the 'guts' of the Business Class, and
(2) a Parameter Class which passes properties to a generic Dialog Class. Based on the controls in the Dialog
the methods for the returned events are written into (1) the Business Class above. Get the thing running really
smoothly and I will have saved myself about 80% of the repetitive coding. Even copy file entries can be
renamed.

How you would do it non-OO depends. Is it an 'all embracing program' or is it split out into a Three Tier
System - (1) Business Logic (Public Domain - oooh  I hate that fancy academic name !), (2) UI (Screen
Section/green screen/GUIs ?) and (3) DBI
(Database Interface - COBOL files or DB tables).

Consider the template just for COBOL File handling - come up with a PERFORM PARAGRAPH for each file verb -
allow for copy/replace on filename and keys etc.
Couple these paragraphs with checking file status (my preference) or offer as an alternative DECLARATIVES. The
Beginner has a template of all file verbs and just 'chops out' the paragraphs (s)he doesn't need.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Advise on program structure needed

- **From:** poopiehead <member15404@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-18T17:25:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2782630.1050686758@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<ca0d9b0f.0304132201.76fc0f6e@posting.google.com>`

```

YOUR UPPER CASE STYLE IS THE WAY TO GO.  ITS CRAZY TO CHANGE YEARS OF
PRESIDENCE BECAUSE A COMPILER ACCEPTS A NEW STYLE.  THERE IS NO
PERFORMANCE INCREASE WITH LOWER CASE AND MOST COBOL PROGRAMMERS THAT
AREN'T MENTALLY HANDICAPPED DON'T THINK YOUR SCREAMING IN YOUR CODE.
CONTINUE TO PROGRAM IN UPPERCASE BECAUSE THAT WAS AND IS THE STANDARD.

PS:  NO MY KEYBOARD IS NOT BROKE AND I'M NOT MAD.  IDIOTS JUST WANTED TO
     EXPRESS EMOTIONS ON THE WEB AND CERTAIN MORONS FOLLOWED THE IDIOTS
     OF THE CLIFFS.  DON'T FOLLOW!!!!!  COBOL IS PROGRAMMED IN
     UPPERCASE!!!  LEAVE CASES TO JAVA PROGRAMMERS BECAUSE ITS THEIR
     KIND THAT CHANGED THE RULES.
```

#### ↳ Re: Advise on program structure needed

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-21T15:18:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8124d$19r$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<ca0d9b0f.0304132201.76fc0f6e@posting.google.com>`

```

On 14-Apr-2003, aliensite@excite.com (aliensite) wrote:

> I am learning COBOL and the programs structure in my text don't always
> seem logical. Luckly my professor is a COBOL programmer who gives us
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> report, I listed the paragraphs below. Is this structure ok? Thanks.

When you get out of his class, get rid of the periods that he asks for, if you
like (they are all style if you use terminators).   While you're in his class
pretend he's an employer with strict standards.   Real life IS like that.

Your structure needs a bit more fleshing out for me to know whether it would be
acceptable at my shop.

This is style, not substance - but typically if I am in a shop that names its
paragraphs like that, I would number them like this:

0000-MAIN
0100-INIT.
1000-MAIN.

5000-CLEAN-UP.

8000-READ.
9000-WRITE.
9100-PAGE-HEADINGS.

We put the I/O paragraphs at the end, and leave lots of room for sub-paragraphs
functionally after 1000-MAIN.
```

<a id="t-5d94edaf01"></a>
## Thread: Calling COBOL DLL from JAVA — 3 msgs

### Calling COBOL DLL from JAVA

- **From:** An.Stark@web.de (Noses)
- **Date:** 2003-04-14T04:36:11-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb8e7f43.0304140336.3f168d1f@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello together,
a short description of my problem is that I have generated a dll
including some COBOL programs ported from host to pc. The dll is
created with Fujitsu NetCOBOL V7. Calling programs contained in this
dll from another COBOL executable works fine (easy job). What I have
to do now ist to write JAVA code for a frontend which should call one
of the programs contained in the dll. Only thing I found in Fujitsu's
documentation is that COBOL can be called the simplest way from JAVA
by using something called "J Business Kit". Searching the web for this
I found nothing about that kit apart from some japanese coded sites
:-(
Can anyone help me solving this job? How du I have to form the
handling of data between JAVA and COBOL? Perhaps a documentation of
this "J Business Kit" in any other language than Japanese (I prefer
english or german) could help me.
Thanks for every tip

  Andre Stark

Corrections of my english are also welcome, it is always nice to have
a correct syntax :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling COBOL DLL from JAVA

- **From:** "Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-14T12:52:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4ayma.35277$Vzu.18712@news02.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`
- **References:** `<bb8e7f43.0304140336.3f168d1f@posting.google.com>`

```
DLL is a standard format in Windows platform and it is indenpendent of
COBOL.

Once you got the DLL, you can use JNI (Java native interface) to call DLL.
But JNI is actually is an interface between Java and C/C++. Therefore you
have to find your function prototype in term of C/C++.

Find the type converson and make up a prototype file for your dll
or it is already generated by your compiler. look for something like *.h.
Make sure your DLL can be called by C/C++.

Suggestion: test a simple one and then do your real work.


Thomas Li
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Calling COBOL DLL from JAVA

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-04-14T18:41:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0304141551.3532c94c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bb8e7f43.0304140336.3f168d1f@posting.google.com> <4ayma.35277$Vzu.18712@news02.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
Given Thomas' excellent advice, there is a great chapter in the
Fujitsu windows users guide that details how to call a Fujitsu DLL
from C.  There is a necessary Runtime load call, and runtime unload,
along with the requirement to construct your procedure division "WITH
STDCALL LINKAGE".

"Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com> wrote in message news:<4ayma.35277$Vzu.18712@news02.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>...
> DLL is a standard format in Windows platform and it is indenpendent of
> COBOL.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thomas Li
```

```
Could you help me get hold of an utility which will convert Mark IV
code to Cobol? its ok if it doesnt convert 100% of the code, but shud
be good enough to take care of 60-70% of the manual work. I looked
around using google/et all , but without success.

Thnks in advance,
l_0_l.
```

#### ↳ Re: MARK IV to Cobol Conversion Utility

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-21T16:36:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b816m3$3f4$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<c59c88e9.0304150414.2e550621@posting.google.com>`

```

On 15-Apr-2003, l_0_l@fsmail.net (KiK) wrote:

> Could you help me get hold of an utility which will convert Mark IV
> code to Cobol? its ok if it doesn't convert 100% of the code, but shud
> be good enough to take care of 60-70% of the manual work. I looked
> around using google/et all , but without success.

I've done such conversion - what it really was was figure out what the Mark IV
does, and write a CoBOL program to duplicate it.   Mark IV is like RPG 2 in that
it just doesn't fit into conversion to CoBOL, and there are a lot fewer Mark IV
programs out there.
```

```
Not really - learning how programs work can help in your understanding 
of systems in general.  For example, I would LOVE our network folks here 
to understand the importance of a -persistent connection- to the Telnet 
protocol.

You'll find that most folks here aren't interested in -doing- homework. 
  You will, however, find that some people here (including myself) are 
more than happy to give pointers, tips, and other help to folks that we 
feel are making an effort themselves.  If it's a basic COBOL class, 
chances are these projects aren't too bad...
```

```
Every place I've worked allowed Telnet to stay up indefinately. Tell them to

What they don't get is why FTP shouldn't be timed out. I have to log on 10-20

PITA.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some simple COBOL code - WILL PAY WELL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJS@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-17T20:35:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E9F5668.8050603@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<fb16150.0304150944.5160675f@posting.google.com> <b7hinn$59p$1@panix1.panix.com> <fb16150.0304151729.2a4c7d19@posting.google.com> <3E9CC61C.8010900@Knology.net> <3e9e08d0.280409131@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> LX-i <DanielJS@Knology.net> wrote:
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> check with their peers at other shops. 

It's not that they time it out...  They just don't understand that 
unplugging one cable and plugging it into another jack in the same 
router could have negative effects on people.  "Aw, they'll never notice 
this..."  Then, the mainframe gets confused because it thinks we're 
still on, and we have to go and kill everyone's runs so they can get 
back in.  grrr....

And then we see the slides, showing 99.8% uptime...  It's enough to make 
your brain explode sometimes.  Then, we've got firewalls on our 
firewalls (and somehow are still constantly loading patches on our own 
machines), and when one of these firewalls decides that it's going to 
route stuff through the other one for a while, that messes us up even more.

We're secure, if nothing else...  Might not be getting much work done 
sometimes, but we're secure...
```

```
parents) not only tuition but also the money you could have been making. Four

League, the toll would be hundreds of thousands.

educate yourself. This high-school dropout did it.

real thing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some simple COBOL code - WILL PAY WELL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-17T17:36:59-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E9F1E7B.60901@newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<fb16150.0304150944.5160675f@posting.google.com> <b7hinn$59p$1@panix1.panix.com> <fb16150.0304151729.2a4c7d19@posting.google.com> <3e9ccdbf.199740430@news.optonline.net> <6lcr9v8epmcebiebgsl8hc33vgv3utl9d3@4ax.com>`

```
Bob Wolfe wrote:

 > For some occupations, I agree that self-taught is as good (and often a
 > better education).

I doubt this can be generalized simply to "occupation".  I'll agree that
in some occupations, some people can do just as well without formal
training.  I can't think of an occupation where this necessarily holds
true for all practitioners.

As a programmer, I'm constantly finding bugs in other people's code
which I recognize, and wouldn't make myself, thanks to things I learned
in school.  Obviously it's possible to pick all the same knowledge up in
the field; but a *good* CS education should provide significantly more
of it in the same amount of time.  That's what it's optimized for, after
all.  Of course practical experience is still also critically important.
No CS curriculum could cover every real-world ramification of the
concepts it teaches, and I'm not aware of any that aspire to.

What field of endeavor isn't best served by a variety of practitioners?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some simple COBOL code - WILL PAY WELL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-16T17:55:33-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E9DD155.4010009@newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<fb16150.0304150944.5160675f@posting.google.com> <b7hinn$59p$1@panix1.panix.com> <fb16150.0304151729.2a4c7d19@posting.google.com> <3e9ccdbf.199740430@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
 > I think going to college four years is stupid. It costs you (actually
 >  your parents) not only tuition but also the money you could have
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
 > hundreds of thousands.

I spent five years getting my baccalaureate degrees at a private
university (in a five-year program), paid for it myself, and made quite
a lot of money at the same time, working for IBM.  Then after a year of
working full time I spent another five years at a relatively expensive
state university doing the resident portion of my PhD work, paid for it
myself, and made quite a lot of money at the same time, working for
Creative Systems Interface and later Micro Focus.

Maybe if you had gone to college you'd know a bit more about how it
works - or how it can work, if you do it correctly.

Or perhaps it's just a problem if you only go for four years, and your
statement is correct.  Seems unlikely, though.
```

```
Doctor Dwarf:

shouldn't have to concern themselves with that silly thermodynamics

PHM in the making!

hmmmmmm...."pseudo"  false  "grapher"  writer

.....false writer  makes sense.




Bob Wolfe
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some simple COBOL code - WILL PAY WELL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-04-16T22:50:23-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b7l4pf$qqn$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<fb16150.0304150944.5160675f@posting.google.com> <3e9cb139.192437228@news.optonline.net> <b7jk9f$cju$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e9e0b32.281019440@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <3e9e0b32.281019440@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>No, but hired guns are whores.

Mr Wagner, here's two simple, direct cites for you from a single, online 
dictionary at http://www.m-w.com :

hired gun: an expert hired to do a specific job

whore: 1 : a woman who engages in sexual acts for money: PROSTITUTE;
       also : a promiscuous or immoral woman
       2 : a male who engages in sexual acts for money
       3 : a venal or unscrupulous person

... so, at least according to one commonly accepted source of definition 
you are wrong.

>Offer me $60/hr and I'll do ANYthing.

You are you, not all 'hired guns' without qualification; what you will or 
will not do reflects more on yourself than anyone else.

>I'll do
>production support and write C programs. I might even serve as 'methodoligy
>facilitator' if I'm hungry enough. 

My, my... all that only for the offering?  What you might do if someone 
actually thought you were worth the paying of such a sum is... best left 
for another place and time, I am sure.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need some simple COBOL code - WILL PAY WELL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-04-17T08:14:25-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<16e2f010.0304170714.6eee9c6c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<fb16150.0304150944.5160675f@posting.google.com> <3e9cb139.192437228@news.optonline.net> <b7jk9f$cju$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e9e0b32.281019440@news.optonline.net> <b7l4pf$qqn$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote in message news:<b7l4pf$qqn$1@panix1.panix.com>...
>
> Mr Wagner, here's two simple, direct cites for you from a single, online 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>        3 : a venal or unscrupulous person
 
 
The Jargon Dictionary offers:
 
hired gun     n.     A contract programmer, as opposed to a full-time
staff member. All the connotations of this term suggested by
innumerable spaghetti Westerns are intentional.
 
 
And Zitzelbergers addendum:
 
Contract Programmer   n.   A hired gun that is expert at the specific
job of  making subtle changes to software designs to maximize billable
hours and increase potential for future callbacks for work on a
software system.
 
 
Personal experience with hired guns from the US tend to fit the later
description rather well.  While none of those that I've encountered
from India fit the following.  A totally opinion based, nonscientific,
observation of a possible cultural difference.

In light of the politically correct times, should we perhaps consider
changing the usage of "hired guns" to something a bit more sensitive
and appropriate, perhaps "Creators of Mass Instructions"?
```

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-04-18T09:24:12-04:00

- **Message-ID:** `<b7ou9s$ldh$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>search on his name. 

Mr Wagner, you have given the name of the composer, your listening habits 
and ranking and a suggestion as to where recordings might be found.  None 
of these appears to answer the question of 'whom do I contact to insure 
that my entrance is accompanied by eerie ocarina music?'.

The workings of memory and association can be curious things... but this
causes me to imagine a situation where a programmer submits a sample
report to a user.

'Where do I find the breakdowns by region that I asked for in the email?'

'The breakdowns by region are called 'region totals'.  Some managers want 
to see them once a week.  They're some of the favorite totals.  Perhaps 
you can go to the totals-by-branch page and group those by region.'

DD
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
"Don Leahy" <xdb2imsN@nospam.whatever.net> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>in an audit program to help an embezzler cover his tracks?

No, I wouldn't. 

I once worked for a major credit card company, on the verge of takeover, where
management insisted I write off all credit balances, taking them as Other
Income. It was fundamentally Wrong. I considered quitting but ended up doing it.
What would you have done?

After the company was acquired, I restored credit balances to cardholders. 

>Do hired guns have a code of ethics?  Other than "don't get caught".  :-)

The Prime rule is 'don't get fired'. Contractors are almost never fired for
technical reasons; they are fired because they alienate in some way a perm. The
rule is be non-committal, never talk about sex, politics or religion (emotional
hot buttons for some). Just crank code and avoid social interaction. When
unavoidable, talk about the weather.
```

```
email addresses.
```

<a id="t-4e06fab59f"></a>
## Thread: Functions - arguement input and "type" of returned value — 1 msgs

### Functions - arguement input and "type" of returned value

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-15T14:01:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b7hkup$rsh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Given recent "discussions" on "integer" and similar things, I thought that I
would point out the fact that the '89 ANSI Intrinsic Function Amendment
provided a table showing what type of arguments (input) each function would
accept and what the TYPE of the returned value is.  For an online version of
a SIMILAR (not identical - as it includes many IBM extensions - functions
and returned types) see,

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGY3LR10/7.1.2

However, from the ISO 2002 Standard, the following information describing
the types of returned values MIGHT help others to understand how/when COBOL
(or at least Standard COBOL) uses terms such as "integer"

NOTE WELL:
   This list includes some types of functions that do *NOT* exist in the '89
Amendment - but were introduced in the 2002 Standard.  For a full view of
all the 2002 Standard functions, see page 566 and following

"15.1 Types of functions

Types of intrinsic functions are:

1) Alphanumeric functions. These are of the class and category alphanumeric.
The number of character positions
in this data item is specified in the function definition. Alphanumeric
functions have an implicit usage display.
Unless stated otherwise in the definition of a function, the data item is
represented in the alphanumeric coded
character set in effect when the function is referenced at runtime.

2) Boolean functions. These are of the class and category boolean. The
number of boolean positions in this data
item is specified in the function definition. Boolean functions have an
implicit usage bit.

3) National functions. These are of the class and category national. The
number of character positions in this data
item is specified in the function definition. National functions have an
implicit usage national. Unless stated
otherwise in the definition of a function, the data item is represented in
the national coded character set in
effect when the function is referenced at runtime.

4) Numeric functions. These are of the class and category numeric. A numeric
function has an operational sign.

5) Integer functions. These are of the class and category numeric. An
integer function has an operational sign
and no digits to the right of the decimal point.

6) Index functions. These are of the class and category index."
```

---

<a id="t-00ba9f27a1"></a>
## Thread: What is an integer - in COBOL? — 5 msgs

### What is an integer - in COBOL?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-15T18:14:44-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b7i3pb$47i$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
It may be of interest to this group that J4 and WG4 actually spent a
SIGNIFICANT amount of time and resources on updating the definition of
"integer" in the 2002 COBOL Standard.  The following is the specification
from the 2002 Standard and should answer the question of what an "integer"
is ** in COBOL**- which may or may not have any relevance to how the term is
used in other disciplines.

"5.4 Integer operands

1) When the term 'integer-n' (n = 1, 2, ...) is used in a general format and
associated rules, it refers to a fixed-point integer literal that shall be
unsigned and non-zero unless otherwise specified in the associated rules.

2) When the term 'integer' is used as a constraint for an operand in a
syntax rule, then

    a) if that operand is a literal, it shall be an integer literal, as
defined in 8.3.1.2.2.1, Fixed-point numeric literals;
    b) if that operand is a data-name or an identifier, it shall reference
one of the following:
        1. an integer intrinsic function,
        2. a fixed-point numeric data item, other than an intrinsic
function, whose description does not include any digit positions to the
right of the radix point.

3) When the term 'integer' is used as a constraint for an operand in a
general rule, that operand shall evaluate at runtime as follows:

    a) If native arithmetic is in effect, the implementor shall define when
the operand is an integer.
    b) If standard arithmetic is in effect, the operand shall be equal to a
standard intermediate data item that has the unique value zero or whose
decimal fixed-point representation contains only zeros to the right of the
decimal point.

        NOTES
            1 If the value of the exponent is greater than 31, the value of
the standard intermediate data item is an integer.
            2 If the value of the exponent is less than 1 then the value of
the standard intermediate data item is not an integer.

    c) If native arithmetic is in effect, the implementor shall define when
an arithmetic expression is an integer."

(for context, see page 20 of the 2002 Standard)
```

#### ↳ Re: What is an integer - in COBOL?

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-15T18:59:19-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3pydnbCu955LAQGjXTWcow@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<b7i3pb$47i$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b7i3pb$47i$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net...
> It may be of interest to this group that J4 and WG4 actually spent a
> SIGNIFICANT amount of time and resources on updating the definition of
> "integer" in the 2002 COBOL Standard....

The Supreme Court tried with equal dedication to define 'obscenity.' With
about as much success.

I'm reminded of a student at U of Michigan who was ticketed for having a
bumper-sticker that read:

"FUCK OHIO STATE"

on his car violating a city ordinance prohibiting lewd, indecent, or obscene
signs. The magistrate heard the evidence, then announced:

"I have applied the recent Supreme Court tests to the issue at hand, as
follows:

1. "Appeals to prurient interests" - I find the bumper sticker in question
does not inasmuch as it is clearly impossible to have sexual relations of
any kind with the entire corpus of Ohio State University.

2. "The work, taken as a whole, is utterly without redeeming social
value." - I hold that there is redeeming social value to the sentiment
expressed.

3. "Offends contemporary community standards." - I hold the bumper sticker
accurately reflects contemporary community standards.

For these reasons, case dismissed.
```

#### ↳ Re: What is an integer - in COBOL?

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-19T02:36:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea0b543.15867911@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b7i3pb$47i$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I have no quarrel with any of that. But it does not define what an integer is,
only where integers must be used. It tacitly assumes the reader knows the
definition of integer FROM ANOTHER DISCIPLINE, to wit math. Therefore, the
mathematical definition has been implicitly adopted into the COBOL standard. 

I say it does not define because 5.4.2.b.2 defines integer as a fixed-point
numeric data item. Fixed-point data items are undefined. 

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>It may be of interest to this group that J4 and WG4 actually spent a
>SIGNIFICANT amount of time and resources on updating the definition of
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What is an integer - in COBOL?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-19T09:55:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b7ro1e$agd$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b7i3pb$47i$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea0b543.15867911@news.optonline.net>`

```
RW is semi-correct (but reaching the wrong conclusion).   It is true that
the '85 Standard (and possibly even the 2002 Standard, I am not positive of
this) does *not* define the term "fixed-point data item".  Therefore, it is
true that it "assumes" the normal mathematical meaning of "fixed-point" (as
opposed to "floating-point" much less imaginary numbers).  HOWEVER, it does
define what an integer is (in the text referred to) as a subset of that
(well understood term) "fixed-point" items.

It should also be noted that the '85 (and 2002) Standards do include
definitions of two separate items:
  - integer literal
 - integer data item

That is because these two items are used separately in the Standard (i.e.
there are places one may use an integer literal, but not an integer data
item).  Integer functions are a subset of integer data items.  They may be
used in a subset of places that an integer data item may be used (i.e. as
sending items, but not as receiving items).  Their is no ambiguity in the
2002 (or really '89) Standards about what a VALID integer data item is, for
example
  - a numeric data item defined with *no* positions right of the (assumed)
decimal point
  - an integer function

Finally, it should be noted that the '85 Standard really never needs to
"specify" fixed-point (either literals or data items) because there is NO
inclusion of floating-point items.  The 2002 Standard, on the other hand,
*does* provide support for both fixed-point and floating-point literals and
data-items.  Therefore, the distinction needs to be clearer.  So far, I
haven't found a "good" specification of the difference in the 2002 Standard
and I have asked J4 and WG4 whether I missed this - or if this is (in deed)
a specification that should be improved in the future - even though these
terms *are* used in COBOL in their "normal" mathematical senses.
```

```
I searched the 2002 standard without finding a definition. There is a good
definition of fixed-point literal, but not fixed-point data item.
```

<a id="t-268bf69b32"></a>
## Thread: Modules using large amounts of memory — 4 msgs

### Modules using large amounts of memory

- **From:** matthew.handi@thehartford.com (Anonymous)
- **Date:** 2003-04-17T06:37:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<24f86bec.0304170537.1f017078@posting.google.com>`

```
Is anybody aware of a tool that can analyze a library or a job and
scan for which modules within that library or job are using the most
memory? We have a private storage area located below the line that
holds 10000 K. Currently, some of our jobs are defined with a region
of 9500 K and will sometimes run above that, which will inevitably
push against and sometimes past the line, which of course causes the
system to freeze. I'd like to look at how a job is running and which
modules are using the most amount of memory or have a large Working
Storage section.
```

#### ↳ Re: Modules using large amounts of memory

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-04-17T14:49:00-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E9EE90C.70372D9B@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<24f86bec.0304170537.1f017078@posting.google.com>`

```
Note this response assumes an IBM mainframe with either an OS390 or z/OS
operating system.

I would be very surprised if there is such a tool.  Various IDR listing
runs will tell you which modules are linkedited to run below the line
(RMODE 24) and there are programs on the CBT tape that would allow you
to find COBOL modules compiled with the DATA(24) option.  Neither will
tell you how much storage is obtained by the module.  Since storage
obtained by the module is normally far greater than the storage
represented by the load module size itself this may be of limited value.

The SMF 30 step termination records (sub-type 4 I think) will contain
information about memory usage.  These probably are being produced every
time a job is run.  See your technical staff (systems programming,
systems support, etc.) for details.     

Anonymous wrote:
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Modules using large amounts of memory

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-17T16:47:21-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E9F3D09.DCAA70E4@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<24f86bec.0304170537.1f017078@posting.google.com> <3E9EE90C.70372D9B@istar.ca>`

```
Let me add to Clark's assumption, and assume you are running with Language
Environment as your run time library.  If so, turn RPTOPTS and RPTSTG.
RPTSTG is really the one that could tell you the most.  You may see large
amounts of Stack or Heap storage being used.

If this doesn't tell you all you need to know, there are lots more ways to
figure out what's going on, so get back to us.

Also, why even try to run large apps "below the line"?  The operating system
and COBOL have supported 31-bit addressing and Residence Mode ANY for
decades already.  It's time to take advantage!
=====
"Clark F. Morris, Jr." wrote:

> Note this response assumes an IBM mainframe with either an OS390 or z/OS
> operating system.
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Storage section.
```

#### ↳ Re: Modules using large amounts of memory

- **From:** Steve Thompson <n_o_spam.steve_t@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-20T01:14:15-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EA22CA7.8030104@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<24f86bec.0304170537.1f017078@posting.google.com>`

```
Anonymous wrote:
> Is anybody aware of a tool that can analyze a library or a job and
> scan for which modules within that library or job are using the most
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Storage section.

You are probably wanting something called an AMBLIST. You will 
need to ask your systems people about it.

I am assuming that your modules are STATICALLY linked, and that 
you do not care if a GETMAIN is being done to obtain storage. If 
you are obtaining modules DYNAMICALLY and doing GETMAIN(s), there 
is no way to obtain the information you want without using an 
execution tool such as STROBE.
```

```
"Though even a small child will understand what we mean by natural numbers,
their definition has not been easy. The Peano postulates essentially uniquely
describe the set of natural numbers, which is denoted by N or [big N]

Every natural number a has a successor, denoted by a + 1.  "

"Z = {... -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, ... } = integers"

If the set jumped from 20030331 to 20030401, it would not be an integer set, it
would be called something else like ... I don't know, how about Gregorian
Objects.

>... which disagrees with the assertion that 'integer' specifies a 'set 
>with no holes in it'.

Same as above, plus this:

"The elements of Z can be paired off one-to-one with the elements of N, the set
of natural numbers, with no elements being left out of either set. 

In infinite sets, the existence of a one-to-one correspondence is the litmus
test for determining cardinality, or size. The set of natural numbers and the
set of rational numbers> have the same cardinality as Z. However, the sets of
real numbers, imaginary numbers, and complex numbers have cardinality larger
than that of Z."

What part of "no elements being left out" do you find unclear?

>
>... which disagrees with the assertion that 'integer' specifies a 'set 
>with no holes in it' 

Same as above:

. . }, negative whole numbers {-1, -2, -3, . . . }, and zero {0}. 2. A complete
unit or entity.  "

> and is a citing from the AHD. This specifically

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>--end quoted text

definition appears in a 'common usage' dictionary. I didn't want someone
claiming that the math definition is esoteric, technical or somehow different
from common usage. 

>Mr Wagner, to say that something 'specifies a set' is an assertion which

>member of the group of posters to newsgroup comp.lang.cobol'.

The set "group of posters" is defined by individual posters. The putative
sentence makes sense when  "is posters" is changed to "is a poster".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is an integer?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>

```
RW - please note that in your quotes (all that I have read so far) it talks
about "the set of ALL integers" or even "integers" - At least what you
present below *NEVER* claims that a subset of ALL the integers, is not also
a set of integers.  And at least so far, you haven't given any quotation
talking about what a SINGLE integer is defined as.

I don't think that anyone disagrees with you that the set of all integers
includes no "gaps".  But (again) any subset of those same items does not
change any INDIVIDUAL item from being an INTEGER.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is an integer?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-20T00:44:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea1ceba.87933012@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ea05612.431282100@news.optonline.net> <b7rmlf$3f0$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ea17b9e.66653889@news.optonline.net> <b7sdkm$23a$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>RW - please note that in your quotes (all that I have read so far) it talks
>about "the set of ALL integers" or even "integers" - At least what you
>present below *NEVER* claims that a subset of ALL the integers, is not also
>a set of integers.  And at least so far, you haven't given any quotation
>talking about what a SINGLE integer is defined as.

The first reference _defines_ a single integer as having this relationship to
its neighbor:

       a(n) = a(n-1) + 1

>I don't think that anyone disagrees with you that the set of all integers
>includes no "gaps".  But (again) any subset of those same items does not
>change any INDIVIDUAL item from being an INTEGER.

The mathematical definition of integer was formed in a field named Set Theory,
which is generally credited to Georg Cantor, who invented it in 1874. 

In set theory, a 'thing' cannot be defined in isolation, only as a member of a
set. A number in isolation is simply a whole number. In order to be an integer
(singular), it must be in the set of 'all integers'.

The COBOL standard follows that definition. It calls numbers "numeric data
items" rather than integers. The word integer is used for operands and function
classes, which in all cases but two _does_ mean all numbers without gaps. The
exceptions are ACCEPT .. FROM DATE and FUNCTION DATE-OF-INTEGER. Since there is
no definition of 'numeric operand', those two should have been alphanumeric.
They _can_ be written as such, thanks to weak type checking.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is an integer?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-21T08:47:25-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EA3E85D.4010208@newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<3ea05612.431282100@news.optonline.net> <b7rmlf$3f0$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ea17b9e.66653889@news.optonline.net> <b7sdkm$23a$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea1ceba.87933012@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

 > The mathematical definition of integer was formed in a field named
 > Set Theory, which is generally credited to Georg Cantor, who invented
 > it in 1874.

Set theory is *a* foundation for mathematics, including the theory of
integers, and it appears to be the most popular choice of foundation
among those who care, but it is not the only foundation.  (And glossing
"foundation for the theory of integers" as "mathematical definition of
integer" is dubious in any case.  Note also that here "foundation"
simply means "axiomatic basis sufficient for deriving the remainder of
mathematics, with the usual Godellian completeness/consistency caveat".)

Peano arithmetic is both a foundation for mathematics and arguably
closer to being a "definition of integer", since it's first and foremost
a theory of integers.  Frege's theory of integers is apparently
essentially equivalent to Peano arithmetic, but I'm not familiar with it
myself.

Apparently there's some opinion that category theory is also a
foundation, and not just (just!) a superstructure built on set theory.

Some folks seem to feel you have to start with first-order logic as your
foundation, and everything else is built on that.

(It might also be worth noting that there are now multiple competing set
theories, of essentially equivalent degree of logical consistency, and
hence suitability for defining integers.)

In short, I don't think the appeal to Cantor is going to help support
your argument.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is an integer?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-22T16:48:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea56f6e.325717319@news.optonline.net>`

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is an integer?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-23T09:37:52-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EA69730.5050503@newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<3ea05612.431282100@news.optonline.net> <b7rmlf$3f0$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ea17b9e.66653889@news.optonline.net> <b7sdkm$23a$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea1ceba.87933012@news.optonline.net> <3EA3E85D.4010208@newsguy.com> <3ea56f6e.325717319@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
 > Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:
 >
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
 > definition of integer.

I don't think I claimed otherwise; I just said the appeal to Cantor
didn't appear to support your argument.  Set theory is not the only
foundation for mathematics; thus it does not provide "the" (one, only)
definition of an integer; thus appealing to the authority of its
originator is of limited rhetorical effectiveness.  But that last is
really an aside - my main point was that set theory isn't the sole
foundation available for mathematics.

 > Why don't you explain integer here? Since you're a mathematician AND
 > work for our favorite <g> compiler company, your words will be more
 > credible than mine.

I'm hardly a mathematician.  My degrees are in CS and English.  I get by
with typical non-specialist math that might come up in my work (enough
calculus to integrate polynomials, take partial derivatives, and so
forth; discrete math through basic group theory and a little category
theory; basic linear algebra; basic probability and statistics), and I
occasionally poke into number theory or game theory or what have you for
amusement, but I know some mathematicians, and I'm not one.

I don't think introducing more definitions of integer into the
discussion is useful at this point - and indeed it may never have been.
  Whether you use a cardinal definition like Frege's or the typical
set-theoric approach (the natural numbers are the cardinality
equivalence classes of sets or other collections - "3" is the class of
all collections with three things in them and so forth), or an ordinal
definition as in number theory (where each natural number is the result
of the successor function applied to the previous number), or some other
kind of definition entirely, you're still sidestepping the original
question, which is whether a member of a set with "holes in it" could be
an integer.  Obviously it can.  Other posters have pointed out that
membership in arbitrary finite sets (eg {1, 2, 4}), with or without
"holes", does not preclude being an integer.  We might also refer to
infinite cardinality and the equivalence of aleph-null infinite sets to
note that, say, the set of all even numbers *is equivalent to* the set
of all integers, even though clearly the set of all even numbers has
"holes" when compared to the integers (ie if E is the set of even
numbers, and Z is the integers, then Z - E is not empty).

But even this distracts from the actual issue, which is whether
something like "20030430" (I've forgotten the exact example that was
originally posed) is an integer, possibly with the qualification "when
treated as a date".  Of course it is, and it is not, depending on what
assumptions you which to make.  It is more precise to say something like
"under the system commonly used for representing numbers in writing
using the numeral characters found in the Latin-1 alphabet, the series
of characters '20030430' outside any special context represents an
integer".  Obviously "20030430" is not *in itself* an integer, any more
than any string of numerals is.

The question then becomes "when 20030430 is interpreted as a date, do we
have grounds for stating that it still represents an integer?".  No
amount of mathematics solves this problem (except for a most basic
understanding that an integer is a whole number or negation thereof).
Certainly we have *some* grounds.  "20030430" is identical to the string
used to represent a certain integer (20030430) under the
widely-recognized representation system described above; thus we have
syntactic grounds.  It is reasonable to argue that these are not
sufficient in themselves.  Consisder then semantic or functional ones.
The "add one" argument, which I believe you (Robert) introduced, goes
like this: integers have a function for adding one, which is a bijection
Z->Z; that is, add 1 to an integer, and you get another integer.  Adding
1 to the integer 20030430 produces another integer, 20030431, but
*considered as a date* 20030431 is invalid.  Thus the members of the set
"integers considered as dates" do not have the "add one" bijection, and
so are not integers.

However, as has been noted, being a member of "integers considered as
dates" does not preclude being a member of "integers".  "20030430" is a
"integer considered as date" but also an "integer".

The distinction you're trying to draw would be better established in
category theory, I think, where collections are associated with
functors.  There you could say that the category D, consisting of the
set "integers considered as dates" and the bijections upon them, is
distinct from the category I, consisting of the set of integers and
bijections upon them.  And then you could get to whatever your point
about integers-considered-as-dates was going to be.  Um, maybe that "add
  one" wasn't a bijection on them?  (Of course, D does include a
bijective successor function, and that function can be defined in terms
of the bijections defined for I.  It's a funny old world.)

But IANAM.

 > Argumentum ad verecumdiam.

Indeed.  (I am, on the other hand, something of a rhetorician.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is an integer?

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-04-19T17:48:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0304191648.e73d1cb@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ea05612.431282100@news.optonline.net> <b7rmlf$3f0$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ea17b9e.66653889@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> You overlooked the definition in the center of the page, which reads:
> 
>           a(n) = a(n-1) + 1
> 
> You also overlooked the big graph showing "no holes".

You failed to read it correctly:

"""The positive integers are the numbers 1, 2, 3, ... (Sloane's
A000027),
                       ^ plural
 sometimes called the counting numbers or natural numbers. 
                                     ^ plural           ^ plural 
 They are the solution to the simple linear recurrence equation ..."""
 ^^^^ plural

If I wanted _all_ positive integers I would use this.

If I want _one_ (an) integer then it only need be _one_ of that set.

As 20030415 is a member of the set of all integer (as defined above),
and as being a member of that set is enough to define it (singular) as
being an integer then 20030415 _is_ an integer.

How hard is that ?

Of course 20032765 is also a member of the set of all integers and
isn't part of the set of integers that can represent dates, but that
doesn't stop either of them being integers.
```
