###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is an integer?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-20T03:12:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea1f9de.98978853@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ea05612.431282100@news.optonline.net> <b7rmlf$3f0$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ea17b9e.66653889@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304191648.e73d1cb@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>How hard is that ?

You're describing numbers on the Intersection (denoted by upsidedown big U) of
two sets. In this case Dates and Integers. Yes, 20030415  is a member of both
sets. But when it is used to represent a date, it is not an integer because all
integers are not dates. It is a member of the set Date. 

>Of course 20032765 is also a member of the set of all integers and
>isn't part of the set of integers that can represent dates, but that
>doesn't stop either of them being integers.

That depends on what 20032765 represents. If it is supposed to be a date then it
is an Invalid Date. If it is a counter, it is an integer.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is an integer?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-19T22:56:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b7t5p0$bms$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3ea05612.431282100@news.optonline.net> <b7rmlf$3f0$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ea17b9e.66653889@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304191648.e73d1cb@posting.google.com> <3ea1f9de.98978853@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert,
  Why don't you give up on this one?  You will notice that *no one* supports
your view (of what THEORETICALLY the mathematical or non-COBOL meaning is of
the term "integer" - not "integers".)

It really is simply that 20030323 is an integer, no matter HOW it is used.
Similarly, if you have 123-45-6789 stored as a social security number, then
it is NOT an integer.  OTOH 123456789 *is* ALWAYS an integer, whether its
use in a COBOL program is as a counter, a SSN, a phone number, a
postal-code, or an internal-use-only field.

COBOL is clear, (again) that *in COBOL* ANY data item described with a
picture clause

   PIC S9999PP

is an integer data item.  Every value that such a field can compatibly hold
is an integer, but the set of all numeric values that can be held in such a
field do NOT form a sequence of whole numbers without any gap.

  ***

You may not want to use integer fields to hold all data that can be
expressed in a set of gap-free "whole numbers" - but that doesn't stop them
from being integers, and a subset of the set of ALL integers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is an integer?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-04-20T07:59:58-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b7u23u$e6e$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3ea05612.431282100@news.optonline.net> <3ea17b9e.66653889@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304191648.e73d1cb@posting.google.com> <3ea1f9de.98978853@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <3ea1f9de.98978853@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>>You failed to read it correctly:

[snip]

>>As 20030415 is a member of the set of all integer (as defined above),
>>and as being a member of that set is enough to define it (singular) as
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>integers are not dates. It is a member of the set Date. 

Mr Wagner, if there are two intersecting sets and a item is a member of 
both sets then... the item is a member of both sets, it does not stop 
being a member of one set or the other.  That is a definition of an 
'intersection set'.

Consider the logic, please:

All A are B.

Not all B are A.

All valid dates expressed in the format YYYYMMDD are integers.

Not all integers are valid dates expressed in the format YYYYMMDD.

>
>>Of course 20032765 is also a member of the set of all integers and
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>is an Invalid Date.

Exactly.  It is an integer but it is not a valid date, since not all 
integers are valid dates expressed in the format YYYYMMDD.

DD
```

> U) of

> both sets. 

So, you _do_ understand.  Good.

No, it is still an integer.  It doesn't _stop_ being an integer because we

In article <3ea17b9e.66653889@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…

Mr Wagner, the variable n is a single number, not a 'set' of any kind.  

>
>You also overlooked the big graph showing "no holes".

Mr Wagner, the graph is one of 'integers', not 'integer'.

> 
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Every natural number a has a successor, denoted by a + 1.  "

Mr Wagner, it defines integers (plural) as such, not 'integer', singular; 
your assertion, as noted above, is in the singular and not in the plural.
>
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Objects.

It talks about sets, Mr Wagner, but it does not state that 'integer 
specifies a set'.  It seems there is showing a pattern in which you 
orignally asserted something about a singular and are attempting to slide 
into a plural.

>
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Same as above.

Quite rightly, as noted... it disagrees with your assertion about 
'integer'.

>
>>>http://searchvb.techtarget.com/gDefinition/0,294236,sid8_gci212358,00.html
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>What part of "no elements being left out" do you find unclear?

The part where 'An integer (pronounced IN-tuh-jer) is a whole number (not
a fractional number) that can be positive, negative, or zero' (quoted from
said page) can be equated to saying that 'integer' specifies a 'set with
no holes in it'.

The two statements are nowhere near identical, Mr Wagner.  What part of a 
simple comparison is beyond your capabilities?

>
>>>http://www.bartleby.com/61/58/I0175800.html
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>unit or entity.  "

'integer' specifies this set.  Is this difference apparent to you yet?

>
>> and is a citing from the AHD. This specifically 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>from common usage. 

There's always a Very Good Reason, I am sure.

>
>>Mr Wagner, to say that something 'specifies a set' is an assertion which 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>sentence makes sense when  "is posters" is changed to "is a poster".

*Exactly*, Mr Wagner... but to say 'a person is posters' IS NOT THE SAME 
THING (emphasis original) as saying 'a person is a poster'.

You stated, originally, that integer specifies a set.  All supporting 
links you have given show that intefer is a member of a set.  To be a set 
and to be a member of a set are not the same thing.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: What is an integer?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-19T09:45:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b7rnel$9gv$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3ea05612.431282100@news.optonline.net>`

```
DD has gone thru these in detail, but the bottom-line (which I think *is*
consistent with the COBOL usage) is that

A) it is true that the *SET* of *ALL* integers (in normal mathematical
terminology) is a set with "no holes in it"

B) Any specific member of the set (above) *IS* an integer - regardless of
what specific sub-set you are talking about, e.g. a set comprising only a
single integer,  a set of the "even number" integers, or the set of integers
that represent valid dates in YYYYMMDD format, or the set of non-negative
integers "evenly divisible by 100" and which are less than 100,000 which
would be held in an integer data item in COBOL defined by
   Pic  999PP

        ***

Mr. Wagner seems unable (even when reading his own references) to accept
that he is error about what *an* "integer" means in either COBOL or
non-COBOL contexts.  He seems to think that any subset of integers, is by
<his erroneous> "definition" made up of something OTHER than integers, just
because the specified set does not include ALL integers.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What is an integer?

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-19T17:43:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea18640.69376711@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ea05612.431282100@news.optonline.net> <b7rnel$9gv$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>DD has gone thru these in detail, but the bottom-line (which I think *is*
>consistent with the COBOL usage) is that
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>   Pic  999PP

The point I was trying to make got lost in the shouting. It was: integers should
not be used to store all things numeric. For instance, they should not be used
for part numbers, telephone numbers, social security numbers and _dates_. Those
things should be stored in containers with pic x. 

The litmus test is whether adding 1 will produce a valid result. If you cannot
do arithmetic on the data, what's the reason for using pic 9? Some said saving
space. If space is really a concern, there are compression algorithms much more
efficient than packed or binary. In fact, packed or binary may actually hamper
them. 

A practical reason to avoid pic 9 is mainframes' predilection to terminate jobs
when numeric fields contain non-numeric data.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is an integer?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-19T16:11:06-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b7se39$466$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3ea05612.431282100@news.optonline.net> <b7rnel$9gv$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea18640.69376711@news.optonline.net>`

```
Under the theory that you list below, it would be impossible to include ANY
item in an "integer" data item, because *ALL* COBOL numeric data items have
an upper-limit (a number where if one "ADDs 1", one gets an error).  Such
errors may be "truncation", "on size error" or even run-time terminations.

Nevertheless, are you not simply going back to your original argument that
ALTHOUGH PERFECTLY LEGAL COBOL *with* PERFECTLY PREDICTABLE RESULTS, that
you PERSONALLY don't "like" using integer data items for storing data for
which adding +1 (even if that is never done in the program) does not yield a
"meaningful" result?

That is certainly a "reasonable" personal opinion - and if that is all you
have been TRYING to say, I don't think anyone will argue that YOU shouldn't
have that opinion.  Others may well provide what THEIR personal opinions are
for data to be stored in integer data items (such as ANY item that might
ever be used for reference modification or subscripting - or any other
personal opinion).

The problem arises when you say that the COBOL (well defined) specification
of what a *single* INTEGER is or what an "integer data item"  is in error -
or even conflicts with a "common" definition outside of COBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is an integer?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-20T00:44:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea1da37.90874758@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ea05612.431282100@news.optonline.net> <b7rnel$9gv$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea18640.69376711@news.optonline.net> <b7se39$466$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>Under the theory that you list below, it would be impossible to include ANY
>item in an "integer" data item, because *ALL* COBOL numeric data items have
>an upper-limit (a number where if one "ADDs 1", one gets an error).  Such
>errors may be "truncation", "on size error" or even run-time terminations.

For that very reason, I qualified the definition in one of my first posts.
Richard accused me of changing the subject. I said:

"In math, per below references, the range of an integer is negative
infinity to positive infinity. We're not doing math, we're doing
computer programming. There is no such thing as infinity in a
computer. The range of an computer integer is usually determined by
machine word size and/or compiler type size (e.g. COBOL's 18 digits).
One could write a library or OO class which does arithmetic on
million-digit integers, but there must be a limit somewhere (due to
finite disk space)."

>Nevertheless, are you now simply going back to your original argument that
>ALTHOUGH PERFECTLY LEGAL COBOL *with* PERFECTLY PREDICTABLE RESULTS, that
>you PERSONALLY don't "like" using integer data items for storing data for
>which adding +1 (even if that is never done in the program) does not yield a
>"meaningful" result?

>That is certainly a "reasonable" personal opinion - and if that is all you
>have been TRYING to say, I don't think anyone will argue that YOU shouldn't
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>personal opinion).

The integer thread was an explanation of _why_ I don't like to store
non-integers in numeric fields. The reason is more than capricious style, it's
based on a rigorous math definition. 

>The problem arises when you say that the COBOL (well defined) specification
>of what a *single* INTEGER is or what an "integer data item"  is in error -
>or even conflicts with a "common" definition outside of COBOL.

It seems to me the committee would have been smart to run the definition by a
math professor acting as 'facts checker'. This is routine procedure at
magazines, journals, movies, etc.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is an integer?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-04-21T14:45:16-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0304211345.780243bd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ea05612.431282100@news.optonline.net> <b7rnel$9gv$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea18640.69376711@news.optonline.net> <b7se39$466$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea1da37.90874758@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

>  I said:
> 
> "... We're not doing math, we're doing computer programming. .."

> It seems to me the committee would have been smart to run the definition by a
> math professor acting as 'facts checker'. 

Which is it ?  We are not doing maths, or what ?


> The integer thread was an explanation of _why_ I don't like to store
> non-integers in numeric fields.

The problem with it was that your argument was completely flawed, both
in maths definitions and in Cobol.

For example your argument would have it that if we wanted to store
even numbers only we should put them in strings because there are
"holes in the range".

In this country we discarded 1 and 2 cent coins (and replaced our 1
and 2 dollar notes by coins).  This means that many organisations set
their retail prices at 5 cent intervals.  Obviously, you would then
store these as strings, because (according to you):

        the range has holes in it
        adding 1 gives an unusable number
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is an integer?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-23T00:01:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea5bebc.7310508@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ea05612.431282100@news.optonline.net> <b7rnel$9gv$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea18640.69376711@news.optonline.net> <b7se39$466$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea1da37.90874758@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304211345.780243bd@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Which is it ?  We are not doing maths, or what ?

The first sentence introduced a _qualification_ to the math definition by
eliminating infinity. You quoted it out of context in an attempt to have me say
the opposite of what I actually said. 

Were you once a lawyer?


>In this country we discarded 1 and 2 cent coins (and replaced our 1
>and 2 dollar notes by coins).  This means that many organisations set
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>        adding 1 gives an unusable number

I'll wager some stores price things 'three for a dollar'. In that case, the unit
price is .34 and the volume discount (on the third item) is .02.

Retail point-of-sale systems are not developed in-house; they are written by
third parties, who attempt to make them as general as possible. So long as one
retailer anywhere in the world uses penny pricing, prices cannot be scaled to
.05.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Loonies and toonies was Re: What is an integer?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-04-23T11:52:45-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b86csd$pel$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3ea5bebc.7310508@news.optonline.net> <3EA5F6DD.DC7BFA78@shaw.ca> <b3638c46.0304230327.5748976b@posting.google.com> <3EA689E4.BB0E05FB@istar.ca>`

```
In article <3EA689E4.BB0E05FB@istar.ca>,
Clark F. Morris, Jr. <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:

[snip]

>I don't know what a loon (note the correct spelling) looks like but it
>is native to Canada and there is at least one Loon Lake.  I believe that
>it is a water bird.

Google can be your friend.  A search for 'loon' (no quotes) using the 
Images option yielded, among other things, 

http://aviary.owls.com/loon/loon.html

... *with* a recording of the call.

>I haven't seen the spelling twonie for the two
>dollar coin and think that the general spelling is toony or toonie as in
>Loony Toons.  Also the silver coloured metal in the toony is not silver
>but some zinc or aluminum.

I recall reading somewhere that the term 'toony' is a triple pun:

1) The association of 'loony, toony' with the series of 'Loony Tunes' 
cartoons.

2) A pun on the denominational value of 'two loons'.

3) A pun on the fact that the coin is bimetallic.

DD
```

"Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote in
>
> I don't know what a loon (note the correct spelling) looks like but it
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

The loon is native to the lake country of northern north America.  Lots of
them in upper New York and Michegan too.  They are indeed a water bird, and
have one of the most eerie and captivating calls of any bird. When you hear
a loon, you know you are in forests and lakes.

Donald
```

news:3EA716C1.E2D46E7@shaw.ca...
>
> Seems daft - you and I post to CLC and you would think it would be as
simple as routing
> from that source each time a contributor enquires; well, apparently not
so.
> I know you are probably restricted to choice depending upon the size of
your township. Are
> you and Richard both in N. Island - could you switch to the same ISP as
him ?
>
> I'll write to Shaw.
>

Yes, it IS odd... I saw this message without any problem...

I access Usenet direct via the Internet, rather than using a News server
provided by my ISP, which may or may not have the Newgroups on it that I
want to see. I tried using the local ISP here to access CLC some years ago
and it was so pathetic that I went direct to Usenet in disgust. Maybe it was
just a temporary problem.

I'll contact Richard's ISP as another option.

Cheers,

Pete.




----== Posted via Newsfeed.Com - Unlimited-Uncensored-Secure Usenet News==----
http://www.newsfeed.com The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World! >100,000 Newsgroups
---= 19 East/West-Coast Specialized Servers - Total Privacy via Encryption =---
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is an integer?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-23T14:20:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b867g7$g1k$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3ea05612.431282100@news.optonline.net> <b7rnel$9gv$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea18640.69376711@news.optonline.net> <b7se39$466$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea1da37.90874758@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304211345.780243bd@posting.google.com> <3ea5bebc.7310508@news.optonline.net>`

```

On 22-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> Retail point-of-sale systems are not developed in-house; they are written by
> third parties, who attempt to make them as general as possible. So long as one
> retailer anywhere in the world uses penny pricing, prices cannot be scaled to
> 05.

The US Constitution mentions mills as legal tender.  But since they aren't
available, checks made out with mills are rejected (at least the one I tried
was).    Stores would like to eliminate pennies, and have penny bowls to save
time.   A few tried it, but the state wouldn't allow them to round taxes.   I
have a few Mexican centavos from when I lived there in the Kennedy
administration.   The metal content is worth far more than the face value.

But sure prices can be scaled.  They have been scaled that way in many
currencies in the world.  All it takes is a law to allow or mandate it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Currency and remediation (was: Re: What is an integer?)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-23T13:46:26-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vadkef2i45r688@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3ea05612.431282100@news.optonline.net> <b7rnel$9gv$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea18640.69376711@news.optonline.net> <b7se39$466$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea1da37.90874758@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304211345.780243bd@posting.google.com> <3ea5bebc.7310508@news.optonline.net> <b867g7$g1k$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:b867g7$g1k$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
[snip]
> The US Constitution mentions mills as legal tender.  But since they aren't
> available, checks made out with mills are rejected (at least the one I
tried
> was).

I can find no mention of 'mills' or 'mill' in the Constitution.

What the Constitution says is:
"Article I. ...Section. 8. The Congress shall have Power ... To
coin Money, regulate the Value thereof, and of foreign Coin,
and fix the Standard of Weights and Measures; ..."

This suggests, that which is "legal tender" in the United States
is determined solely by the acts of Congress.

I could care less about this were it not for the question: Should
the US Congress decide to drop the penny as legal tender,
would the remediation effort to comply with that change be
greater than the effort for Y2K?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is an integer?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-20T20:35:53+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b7sc35$g1o$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ea05612.431282100@news.optonline.net> <b7rnel$9gv$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea18640.69376711@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> The point I was trying to make got lost in the shouting. 

No. It got lost in all the mistakes that you were making up to try to 
support a lost cause.

> It was: integers should not be used to store all things numeric. 

What exactly are you using as 'integer' now ? You still fail to distinguish 
between the container and the number.

Are you saying "'PIC S9(9) COMP-5' should not be used to store ... " ? and 
using 'integer' because you think a 32bit computer word should be called an 
'integer' ?  When in fact it is an _interger_field_ (ie a container).

A PIC 9(8) DISPLAY field can store an integer number.

> For instance, they should
> not be used for part numbers, telephone numbers, social security numbers
> and _dates_. Those things should be stored in containers with pic x.

Rant on.
 
> The litmus test is whether adding 1 will produce a valid result. 

That may be you _opinion_, but it is just an _opinion_.  It is just 
something that you have made up.

> In fact, packed or binary may actually hamper them.

Have you any evidence to support this factoid ?  Which particular 
compression algorithms would be hampered ?  Ceratinly not any that are RLL.

> A practical reason to avoid pic 9 is mainframes' predilection to terminate
> jobs when numeric fields contain non-numeric data.

Which particular dates of the form YYYYMMDD will have non-numeric 
characters in them ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is an integer?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-20T00:44:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea1de99.91996595@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ea05612.431282100@news.optonline.net> <b7rnel$9gv$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea18640.69376711@news.optonline.net> <b7sc35$g1o$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>> It was: integers should not be used to store all things numeric. 
>
>What exactly are you using as 'integer' now ? You still fail to distinguish 
>between the container and the number.

I misspoke. I meant to say 'numeric data items should not be used to store
non-integers'.

>> The litmus test is whether adding 1 will produce a valid result. 
>
>That may be you _opinion_, but it is just an _opinion_.  It is just 
>something that you have made up.

It's the well defined test for an integer, supported by references. As to
whether non-integers should be stored in numeric fields, I have no objection if
they are used in computations. For example a table of primes. If you're never
going to do arithmetic on them nor use them as subscripts, what's he point? 

>> In fact, packed or binary may actually hamper them.
>
>Have you any evidence to support this factoid ?  Which particular 
>compression algorithms would be hampered ?  Ceratinly not any that are RLL.

A dictionary-based algorithm would be more likely to add strings of displayable
characters to its dictionary.

>> A practical reason to avoid pic 9 is mainframes' predilection to terminate
>> jobs when numeric fields contain non-numeric data.
>
>Which particular dates of the form YYYYMMDD will have non-numeric 
>characters in them ?

It's obvious you haven't done any mainframe production support. This 'feature'
causes a significant percentage of failures. We edit data entered by keyboard,
but often do not edit computer files imported from other systems. They are
assumed to be clean. They usually are, except the other system may have been
designed by people who understand math, and thus don't regard dates and part
numbers as numeric. The fields were pic x or group items on their machine, so a
date might contain spaces.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is an integer?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-20T16:57:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea2b649.147220919@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ea05612.431282100@news.optonline.net> <3ea18640.69376711@news.optonline.net> <b7sc35$g1o$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ea1de99.91996595@news.optonline.net> <b7t0kt$r34$1@panix1.panix.com> <b7t1hs$i82$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b7t0kt$r34$1@panix1.panix.com...
>> In article <3ea1de99.91996595@news.optonline.net>,
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>be stored internally (or used in arithmetic).

Internally, I often store US _pennies_ amounts as integers. It seems equivocal
to say you're counting dollars when, in fact, you're counting pennies. 

Most of the data I work with is in thousands of dollars. But the data will
occasionally read '1.05', meaning $1,050. The simplest solution is integer
accumulators containing dollars. 

Not only is it linguistically and conceptually clearer, it is also faster.
Compilers sometimes generate additional instructions to handle scaling, even
when all numbers are scaled the same.
```

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-04-21T14:26:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0304211326.132270f3@posting.google.com>`

robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> Internally, I often store US _pennies_ amounts as integers. It seems equivocal
> to say you're counting dollars when, in fact, you're counting pennies.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is an integer?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-21T06:01:40+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b7td84$tra$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ea05612.431282100@news.optonline.net> <3ea18640.69376711@news.optonline.net> <b7sc35$g1o$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ea1de99.91996595@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> I misspoke. I meant to say 'numeric data items should not be used to store
> non-integers'.

Thank you.
 
> It's the well defined test for an integer, supported by references. 

No. It is the test for a 'set of all integers', but is _not_ a test for 'an 
integer'.

For example I can add 1 to 4.7635 and gat a valid number, that doesn't make 
4.7635 'an integer'.

> As to
> whether non-integers should be stored in numeric fields, I have no
> objection if they are used in computations. 

Why would you have any objection at all to what _I_ do ?  It is not like 
anyone is trying to force you to do it.  Whereas, ...

> A dictionary-based algorithm would be more likely to add strings of
> displayable characters to its dictionary.

A dictionary based algoritm can just as easily put 200030415 into its 
dictionary whether it is display, packed or binary. Every occurance can use 
the same dictionary entry.  It may decide that it can usefully break it 
down by bytes if it is in any format.  

>>Which particular dates of the form YYYYMMDD will have non-numeric
>>characters in them ?
> 
> It's obvious you haven't done any mainframe production support. 

Not for 25 years or more, no.  Fortunately.

> This
> 'feature' causes a significant percentage of failures. We edit data
> entered by keyboard, but often do not edit computer files imported from
> other systems. They are assumed to be clean. 

Why would _anyone_ assume that any imported data was clean ?
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-20T16:57:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>> 'feature' causes a significant percentage of failures. We edit data
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Why would _anyone_ assume that any imported data was clean ?

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b7usie$iih$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ea05612.431282100@news.optonline.net> <b7sc35$g1o$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ea1de99.91996595@news.optonline.net> <b7td84$tra$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ea2cab7.152451690@news.optonline.net>`

>>Why would _anyone_ assume that any imported data was clean ?
>  
> Because planners and management types think developing a filter would be a
> waste of time and money. As a result, errors become production support

<a id="t-d1821f0b3b"></a>
## Thread: Help — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help

- **From:** "Ken" <kenlynn@knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-19T08:27:08-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<va2jim5rke4ld1@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I have been given the assignment of interviewing someone in the field I'm
studying. I don't know any programmers. PLEASE will someone answer the
questions and repost, or email me?
I would greatly appreciate it. I'm almost done with my associates degree,
and my Technical Report Writing instructor likes us to do interviews. Kinda
sucks when you don't know anyone.
Anyway, Thanks very much, and the questions are below.


How long have you been working in the Programming field?




How hard was it to get your first job programming?



Is there much job security in programming?



What made you decide to go into programming in the first place?




Do you enjoy your work?



What languages do you recommend I learn, and which are most widely used?




How much of your work is done by group collaboration and how much is a solo
effort?




Which do you prefer, group or solo projects?




Why?






Thank you very much.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-04-19T10:56:36-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b7ro34$ba3$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<va2jim5rke4ld1@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <va2jim5rke4ld1@corp.supernews.com>,
Ken <kenlynn@knology.net> wrote:
>I have been given the assignment of interviewing someone in the field I'm
>studying. I don't know any programmers. PLEASE will someone answer the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>How long have you been working in the Programming field?

A goodly spell.

>How hard was it to get your first job programming?

Not particularly.

>Is there much job security in programming?

As much as anywhere else.


>What made you decide to go into programming in the first place?

It seemed like a good idea at the time.


>Do you enjoy your work?

Sometimes.

>What languages do you recommend I learn, and which are most widely used?

That's two questions.  Learn the one that does what you want it to do and 
use varies by region.


>How much of your work is done by group collaboration and how much is a solo
>effort?

Enough so that the job gets done.

>Which do you prefer, group or solo projects?

Whatever is available so that I can pay the bills.


>Why?

So that I can pay the bills.


>Thank you very much.

Anytime... I am sure it is worth double what I have asked you to pay for 
it.

DD
```

```
In article <va2jim5rke4ld1@corp.supernews.com>,
 "Ken" <kenlynn@knology.net> wrote:

> I have been given the assignment of interviewing someone in the field I'm
> studying. I don't know any programmers. PLEASE will someone answer the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> How long have you been working in the Programming field?

It depends on the meaning of the word "work".  I've been paid by people 
to indulge my hobby for about 20 years.  With some gaps for other gigs.

> How hard was it to get your first job programming?

Not hard at all.  I went to high school with the son of a small 
businessperson.  She paid some contractors to develop a system for her, 
and they operated it for the first cycle as proof of success.  When the 
second cycle came around the instructions they left her didn't work, 
neither did the software.  Her son got me involved and we fixed it up in 
about two weeks.

> Is there much job security in programming?

That depends on if you are a good programmer or not.  There is a 
tendancy to slow, or halt, new development when the economy is in the 
toilet.  But there are always places looking for skillful programmers.

> What made you decide to go into programming in the first place?

I actually did not want to go into programming.  I feared that it would 
destroy my enjoyment of my hobby.  I spent time pursuing other careers 
like driving trucks, bartending, and a stint in the infantry.  People 
always seemed to be coming to me for help with their computers (unbreak 
the dispatch system, teach the old man how to use his new laptop, rework 
the inventory system, etc).

I decided not to re-up after [coke-addled, draft-dodging, sleeze] Bill 
Clinton made a mess of Mogadishu.  I took a gig with a bank and have 
been programming full time since.

> Do you enjoy your work?

People actually pay me to play with computers -- how cool is that?  Who 
wouldn't enjoy it.

> What languages do you recommend I learn, and which are most widely used?

I won't touch 'most widely used'.

But I recommend you learn at least one assembler, even if you never use 
it.  Knowing how the machine works will make all of your code better.

If all you have is a hammer, the whole world is your nail.  I also 
recommend you learn at least two high-level languages very well so that 
you can always view problems through their intersection.  Knowing two 
languages might not be a great cure-all for the nail problem, but it 
does help you remember that there are more tools in the box.  Very often 
people will get into the habit of thinking that they are "Cobol 
Programmers" or "Java Programmers" and they forget that knowing the 
language does not mean you know how to program any more than owning a 
hammer makes you a master carpenter.

If I had to make a suggestion -- avoid the "visual" things.  You should 
be capible with nothing more than a text-editor and a command line 
compiler before you start drawing windows and dragging buttons around. 

Java, C and Cobol are all widely used in production environments and you 
should get a good exposure to each (Avoid Fortran and Basic, they 
destroy brain cells ;-).

Perl, Ruby, Python, Javascript and VBscript are quite popular for 
scripting and see some production use.  Expose yourself to a bit of each 
and pick one you are comfortable with and learn it will.  There are many 
needs for scripting in the course of development and it will serve you 
well throughout your career.

At last, very incomplete, count, I found a web site that cataloged over 
400 seperate languages -- not seperate implementations, but separate 
languages.  There are a lot to pick from.  Some interesting ones that 
you will likely never see production code written in, yet still have 
something to offer.  Lisp and other functional languages provide an 
interesting view on how algorithims can be constructed.  Smalltalk 
provides a purists view of object oriented programming.  Pascal does the 
same for structured procedural programming.

Don't let the number scare you.  After you have learned one language 
well it should take you about a week with a reference manual and a 
compiler to feel comfortable in another language.  Most of all, learn 
algorithms in a language independant fashion.

> How much of your work is done by group collaboration and how much is a solo
> effort?

Design is usually done by team with a whiteboard, much caffine and much 
discussion.  Implementation of subsystems within that design are done as 
solo efforts with team reviews.

> Which do you prefer, group or solo projects?

Depends on the group.

> Why?

There is nothing nicer than building a software product with a group of 
capible, competent professionals that know what they are doing.  But 
there is nothing more frustrating than babysitting an incompetent 
programmer.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJS@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-19T16:42:19-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EA1C2BB.4080706@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<va2jim5rke4ld1@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Ken wrote:
> How long have you been working in the Programming field?

Professionally, almost 6 years.  Amateur, 14 years.

> How hard was it to get your first job programming?

Really easy.  (I'm in the military, so when they say "program", I do...)

> Is there much job security in programming?

Again, my professional career has been in the military up to this point, 
but I've had some side jobs, and I'd have to say that the security in 
"programming" is directly related to the skill and common sense of the 
management of the project.

> What made you decide to go into programming in the first place?

I loved messing with computers.  I've loved them since my first 
Commodore 64 that I got in 1984.  Over time, I've written BASIC for the 
64, learned applications from DOS, WP 3.1, NT 3.51, 4, W2K, and XP Pro. 
  At one time I had a two-node BBS set up, which was (for a couple of 
months, anyway) the most popular in Chattanooga, TN.  I've been writing 
HTML since 1994...

I was looking into the Air Force for different reasons, but programming 
was a lot better than the other jobs they were offering me.  I 
qualified, joined, was trained, and arrived in Montgomery, where I've 
been ever since.  I've learned COBOL, DML (Unisys 2200), JavaScript, 
Visual Basic, VBScript (client and server side), and SQL in my time here.

> Do you enjoy your work?

Absolutely.  :)  In fact, my co-workers think I'm nuts to read this 
newsgroup on my own time.  The way I look at it, I can stay in my little 
world for 9 years, and only know that little corner, or I can keep up 
with what's current, and be a lot more marketable when the time comes to 
rejoin the private sector.

> What languages do you recommend I learn, and which are most widely used?

Well, since you posted this to a COBOL newsgroup...  :)  COBOL has a lot 
of benefits.  With a strong basis in COBOL, I've had a lot easier of a 
time learning some of the other languages I've learned.  Plus, it's been 
demonstrated that people who learn to write clean COBOL code write a lot 
cleaner and more maintainable code in other languages, where formatting 
is not as strictly enforced as COBOL.

> How much of your work is done by group collaboration and how much is a solo
> effort?

A lot of our current work effort is discussed in a group, then one 
person is selected as the point person on that given task.

> Which do you prefer, group or solo projects?  Why?

Group, by far.  It's a great help to have another person or two to be 
able to function as a sounding board for designs and coding issues.

> Thank you very much.

You're welcome.  :)  You're smart, posting this on a weekend...

>From: "Ken" kenlynn@knology.net

A quarter century.

>

Back when I started a lot of programmers came up through the operations ranks

break into the ranks but my Cobol instructor in college gave my name to the
manager of the IT department of a Fortune 100 company here and told him he
should hire me - and he did.

>

In programming yes.  In a specific language maybe, but the more you know the
more you're marketable.

>
>What made you decide to go into programming in the first place?

It was my kid brother's idea.  I couldn't find a good genetics program in the

## Thread: What's Missing in a COBOL Style Guide? — 19 this month / 258 total

_Thread started [2003-03](2003-03.md#t-df0bc5c7d1)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is supported (was: What's Missing in a COBOL Style Guide?

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-19T16:07:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea16766.61476936@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3E6FC7EC.8EF66036@attglobal.net> <3e768cca.43241859@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303180131.1ccbd4df@posting.google.com> <3e775283.93866179@news.optonline.net> <b58496$ttb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b586kl$2h1$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3e77b35b.7601275@news.optonline.net> <v7fkcvfdqatlf5@corp.supernews.com> <3e78f7f6.28054222@news.optonline.net> <v7insvfn4je22e@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3e78f7f6.28054222@news.optonline.net...
…[50 more quoted lines elided]…
>eventhough the document is complex and difficult to follow.

The only difference between the two forms of ADDRESS OF would be a statement
where the pointer was a receiving operand -- something like MOVE ADDRESS OF
record-1 TO ADDRESS OF record-2. But that statement is invalid in the standard.
Also invalid is:

 CALL 'a-program' USING ADDRESS OF record-2, record-1

One must say BY CONTENT ADDRESS OF record-2, which is the same as USING
record-2. The reason for the prohibition of passing pointers BY REFERENCE is not
syntactic simplicity, it is to protect them from modification outside the SET
verb.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is supported (was: What's Missing in a COBOL Style Guide?

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-19T14:21:05-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<va35257evgc5e0@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3E6FC7EC.8EF66036@attglobal.net> <3e768cca.43241859@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303180131.1ccbd4df@posting.google.com> <3e775283.93866179@news.optonline.net> <b58496$ttb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b586kl$2h1$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3e77b35b.7601275@news.optonline.net> <v7fkcvfdqatlf5@corp.supernews.com> <3e78f7f6.28054222@news.optonline.net> <v7insvfn4je22e@corp.supernews.com> <3ea16766.61476936@news.optonline.net>`

```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ea16766.61476936@news.optonline.net...
[snip]
> Also invalid is:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> record-2.

Assuming that record-2 describes a group item defined in
the file, working-storage, local-storage, linkage, or
communication section of a program, the following applies.

The CALL statement, Format 2, specifies that operands in the
USING phase, whether BY REFERENCE or BY CONTENT,
are described as identifier-2. [FDIS page 420]

The CALL statement, Syntax rules, futher state that identifier-2
"shall reference an address-identifier or a data item defined ...".
[FDIS page 421]

Identifiers, Format 9, describes an address-identifier as
data-address-identifier-1 or program-address identifier-1.
[FDIS page 88]

Data-address-identifier is described as ADDRESS OF identifier-1
and "shall reference a data item defined ..." and "creates a unique
data item of class pointer and category data-pointer that contains
the address of ...". [FDIS page 99]

Also, some restrictions apply in some cases; but not this general
case.

The conformance rules for parameters states "If either the
argument or the formal parameter is of class pointer, the
corresponding formal parameter or argument shall be of class
pointer and the corresponding items shall be of the same
category." [FDIS page 405]

Thus, whether passed by reference or content, ADDRESS OF
record-2 will cause the argument to be passed as a data-pointer
containing the address of record-2, and the corresponding
parameter in the called program must be described as USAGE
POINTER.

This is as I understand it.

> The reason for the prohibition of passing pointers BY REFERENCE is not
> syntactic simplicity, it is to protect them from modification outside the
SET
> verb.

Since the prohibition does not exist, your speculation is
not applicable.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is supported (was: What's Missing in a COBOL Style Guide?

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-19T16:11:27-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<va3bujgtgr7f0f@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3E6FC7EC.8EF66036@attglobal.net> <3e768cca.43241859@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303180131.1ccbd4df@posting.google.com> <3e775283.93866179@news.optonline.net> <b58496$ttb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b586kl$2h1$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3e77b35b.7601275@news.optonline.net> <v7fkcvfdqatlf5@corp.supernews.com> <3e78f7f6.28054222@news.optonline.net> <v7insvfn4je22e@corp.supernews.com> <3ea16766.61476936@news.optonline.net> <va35257evgc5e0@corp.supernews.com>`

```

Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:va35257evgc5e0@corp.supernews.com...
>
> Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > record-2.

[snipped long description]

Or, I could have shown that one need not say BY CONTENT
by supplying the example from Annex E.9.1, Data-addresses
and data-pointers. [FDIS pages 745-746]

--- example
Consider a second example based on the fact that many Application
Program Interfaces (APIs) require a pointer as a parameter. The data
division contains the following declarations:

  01 p2 usage pointer.
  01 data-record. >* the full record layout is described
    02 ...

If you want to pass a pointer to the program process-record, you could
code:

  Set p2 to address of data-record
  Call "process-record" using p2

Or, you could pass the address of data-record with the following single
statement:

  Call "process-record" using address of data-record
--- end example
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is supported (was: What's Missing in a COBOL Style Guide?

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-19T22:36:56-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<va4238qa5miqd6@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3E6FC7EC.8EF66036@attglobal.net> <3e768cca.43241859@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303180131.1ccbd4df@posting.google.com> <3e775283.93866179@news.optonline.net> <b58496$ttb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b586kl$2h1$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3e77b35b.7601275@news.optonline.net> <v7fkcvfdqatlf5@corp.supernews.com> <3e78f7f6.28054222@news.optonline.net> <v7insvfn4je22e@corp.supernews.com> <3ea16766.61476936@news.optonline.net> <va35257evgc5e0@corp.supernews.com> <va3bujgtgr7f0f@corp.supernews.com> <3ea1e804.94408627@news.optonline.net>`

```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ea1e804.94408627@news.optonline.net...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> > One must say BY CONTENT ADDRESS OF record-2, which is the same as
USING
> >> > record-2.
> >
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> They should have said Call "process-record" using data-record.

There is a 'strangeness', as I recall, from an old discussion,
that, in standard COBOL, items passed BY REFERENCE
are not passed as addresses or pointers. Keep in mind that
this is in standard COBOL; not necessarily its implementation.

If I recalled correctly,
    call "process-record" using data-record
means that the standard only requires that a reference to
data-record be passed; not its address. For many(?)
implementations, there is no difference. However, the COBOL
standard needs to make a distinction for the purpose of
specifiying the language.

Thus, where you see "a pointer to a pointer", the standard
calls it a reference to a pointer and all the rules rely on what
the standard calls it! <g>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is supported (was: What's Missing in a COBOL Style Guide?

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-20T16:57:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea2bada.148390123@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3E6FC7EC.8EF66036@attglobal.net> <3e768cca.43241859@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303180131.1ccbd4df@posting.google.com> <3e775283.93866179@news.optonline.net> <b58496$ttb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b586kl$2h1$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3e77b35b.7601275@news.optonline.net> <v7fkcvfdqatlf5@corp.supernews.com> <3e78f7f6.28054222@news.optonline.net> <v7insvfn4je22e@corp.supernews.com> <3ea16766.61476936@news.optonline.net> <va35257evgc5e0@corp.supernews.com> <va3bujgtgr7f0f@corp.supernews.com> <3ea1e804.94408627@news.optonline.net> <va4238qa5miqd6@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

>
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
>this is in standard COBOL; not necessarily its implementation.

In that case, programs written in other languages would not work. In the course
of writing assembly language on a variety of platforms, I've never seen COBOL
pass anything other than a pointer. 

>If I recalled correctly,
>    call "process-record" using data-record
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>the standard calls it! <g>

The standard says in Appendix C:

"A run unit may also contain runtime modules and data storage areas derived from
the compilation of compilation
units written in languages other than COBOL; in this case the requirements for
the relationship between the COBOL
and the non-COBOL modules are defined by the implementors."

I cannot imagine an implementation which did not support communication with
other languages, therefore parameter passing is, as a practical matter, always
defined by the implementor.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is supported (was: What's Missing in a COBOL Style Guide?

_(thread depth: 33)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-20T15:19:32-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<va5snv2th69i70@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3E6FC7EC.8EF66036@attglobal.net> <3e768cca.43241859@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303180131.1ccbd4df@posting.google.com> <3e775283.93866179@news.optonline.net> <b58496$ttb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b586kl$2h1$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3e77b35b.7601275@news.optonline.net> <v7fkcvfdqatlf5@corp.supernews.com> <3e78f7f6.28054222@news.optonline.net> <v7insvfn4je22e@corp.supernews.com> <3ea16766.61476936@news.optonline.net> <va35257evgc5e0@corp.supernews.com> <va3bujgtgr7f0f@corp.supernews.com> <3ea1e804.94408627@news.optonline.net> <va4238qa5miqd6@corp.supernews.com> <3ea2bada.148390123@news.optonline.net>`

```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ea2bada.148390123@news.optonline.net...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
[snip]
> >There is a 'strangeness', as I recall, from an old discussion,
> >that, in standard COBOL, items passed BY REFERENCE
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> In that case, programs written in other languages would not work. In the
course
> of writing assembly language on a variety of platforms, I've never seen
COBOL
> pass anything other than a pointer.

In many of those cases, I have no doubt that extensions were
used to facilitate calling programs in those other languages.
The 2002 COBOL standard is the first to provide non-COBOL
language support.

> >If I recalled correctly,
> >    call "process-record" using data-record
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> "A run unit may also contain runtime modules and data storage areas
derived from
> the compilation of compilation
> units written in languages other than COBOL; in this case the requirements
for
> the relationship between the COBOL
> and the non-COBOL modules are defined by the implementors."
>
> I cannot imagine an implementation which did not support communication
with
> other languages, therefore parameter passing is, as a practical matter,
always
> defined by the implementor.

As a practical matter, the COBOL standard is incomplete.
It has a requirement for implementors to 'fill-in' certain items
that are "implementor defined." What the new standard does
accomplish is to make it easier for implementors to do such
things as support non-COBOL modules in a 'standard' way;
that is, one which requires fewer source code changes between
implementations.

One of these is the availability of ADDRESS OF, which, strictly
speaking, is not required for a single implementor, COBOL-only
solution.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Syntax error in standard? (was: Re: What is supported (was: What's Missing in a COBOL Style Guide?)

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-19T21:53:53-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<va3vl890ob5c7c@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3E6FC7EC.8EF66036@attglobal.net> <3e768cca.43241859@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303180131.1ccbd4df@posting.google.com> <3e775283.93866179@news.optonline.net> <b58496$ttb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b586kl$2h1$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3e77b35b.7601275@news.optonline.net> <v7fkcvfdqatlf5@corp.supernews.com> <3e78f7f6.28054222@news.optonline.net> <v7insvfn4je22e@corp.supernews.com> <3ea16766.61476936@news.optonline.net> <va35257evgc5e0@corp.supernews.com> <va3bujgtgr7f0f@corp.supernews.com>`

```

Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:va3bujgtgr7f0f@corp.supernews.com...
[snip]
> Or, I could have shown that one need not say BY CONTENT
> by supplying the example from Annex E.9.1, Data-addresses
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>   Call "process-record" using p2

Bill, to add more confusion, see page 422, CALL, SR(11),
Formats 1 and 2.

"11) If the BY REFERENCE phrase is specified or implied
for an identifier-2, that identifier shall be neither a strongly-typed
group item nor a data item of class object or pointer."

The CALL statement appears to be passing a data item of
class pointer by reference. What am I missing?

>
> Or, you could pass the address of data-record with the following single
> statement:
>
>   Call "process-record" using address of data-record

Also ADDRESS OF data-record is an unique data item of
class pointer, again, passed by reference.

> --- end example
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Syntax error in standard? (was: Re: What is supported (was: What's Missing in a COBOL Style Guide?)

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-19T21:25:12-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b7t0ea$m0u$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3E6FC7EC.8EF66036@attglobal.net> <3e768cca.43241859@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303180131.1ccbd4df@posting.google.com> <3e775283.93866179@news.optonline.net> <b58496$ttb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b586kl$2h1$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3e77b35b.7601275@news.optonline.net> <v7fkcvfdqatlf5@corp.supernews.com> <3e78f7f6.28054222@news.optonline.net> <v7insvfn4je22e@corp.supernews.com> <3ea16766.61476936@news.optonline.net> <va35257evgc5e0@corp.supernews.com> <va3bujgtgr7f0f@corp.supernews.com> <va3vl890ob5c7c@corp.supernews.com>`

```
SR(11) applies to Format 1 and 2 - not to Format 3 (which must have a
program-prototype). When there *is* a program-prototype, then there is no
restriction on indenter-2 being an address-identifier (I think).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is supported (was: What's Missing in a COBOL Style Guide?

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-19T16:23:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b7seot$ten$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3E6FC7EC.8EF66036@attglobal.net> <3e768cca.43241859@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303180131.1ccbd4df@posting.google.com> <3e775283.93866179@news.optonline.net> <b58496$ttb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b586kl$2h1$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3e77b35b.7601275@news.optonline.net> <v7fkcvfdqatlf5@corp.supernews.com> <3e78f7f6.28054222@news.optonline.net> <v7insvfn4je22e@corp.supernews.com> <3ea16766.61476936@news.optonline.net> <va35257evgc5e0@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick,
   I think that I made the same mistake when I first started looking at
this.

GR(4) on page 421 states,

"4) If the BY REFERENCE phrase is not specified or implied for an
identifier-2 or if identifier-2 is an addressidentifier, identifier-2 is a
sending operand."

However, 14.5.7 Sending and receiving operands on page 389 says,

"An operand is a sending operand if its contents prior to the execution of a
statement may be used by the execution of the statement. An operand is a
receiving operand if its contents may be changed by the execution of the
statement."

I read this to mean that an "ADDRESS OF" data-pointer may be used in a CALL
BY REFERENCE (identifier-2) - but *only* if that data-item is *not* changed
by any action of the CALLed subprogram.

This seems a little "misleading" to me, but does (as far as I read it)
reflect the 2002 Standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is supported (was: What's Missing in a COBOL Style Guide?

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-20T14:33:50-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<va5q62ai9pqrf8@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3E6FC7EC.8EF66036@attglobal.net> <3e768cca.43241859@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303180131.1ccbd4df@posting.google.com> <3e775283.93866179@news.optonline.net> <b58496$ttb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b586kl$2h1$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3e77b35b.7601275@news.optonline.net> <v7fkcvfdqatlf5@corp.supernews.com> <3e78f7f6.28054222@news.optonline.net> <v7insvfn4je22e@corp.supernews.com> <3ea16766.61476936@news.optonline.net> <va35257evgc5e0@corp.supernews.com> <b7seot$ten$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill,
I reviewed the FDIS and came to the conclusion that my
understanding is correct; that is, when an address-identifier
is identifier-2, it meets the requirements for all formats by way
of SR(4). When indentifer-2 is a pointer data item (not an
address-identifier), it is not allowed by SR(11) for formats 1 and 2
and only for BY REFERENCE.

I then wrote a set of programs to test the concept using the older
MF COBOL/2 3.2. My thinking is that it should have the same
behavior as the standard, even though the syntax is slightly
different. MF uses ENTRY for ADDRESS OF PROGRAM,
PROCEDURE-POINTER for PROGRAM-POINTER, and uses
the linkage section rather than BASED items.

The programs pass only the addresses of pointers on calls,
including both program (procedure) pointers and data pointers.

Note that where a prefix is 'adr-of-' the content is the address
of a pointer and must be understood as holding a pointer to
a pointer of either program or data of some type.

----- The test program
       program-id. main.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       1 next-program procedure-pointer.
       1 next-data-ptr pointer.
       procedure division.
           set next-program to null
           set next-data-ptr to null
           perform until exit
               call "get-prog"
                   using address of next-program
                         address of next-data-ptr
               if next-program = null
                   exit perform
               end-if
               call next-program
                 using address of next-data-ptr
           end-perform
           stop run.
       end program main.

       program-id. get-prog.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       1 current-program-number comp pic 9(4).
       66 random-value renames current-program-number.
       1 next-data.
        2 text-msg pic x(30).
       linkage section.
       1 adr-of-next-program pointer.
       1 adr-of-next-data-ptr pointer.
       1 next-program procedure-pointer.
       1 next-data-ptr pointer.
       procedure division
         using adr-of-next-program adr-of-next-data-ptr.
           set address of next-program to adr-of-next-program
           if next-program = null
               compute random-value =
                 function integer (
                   function random (
                     function numval (
                       function current-date (9:8))) * 6 ) + 1
               set address of next-data-ptr to adr-of-next-data-ptr
               set next-data-ptr to address of next-data
           else
               subtract 1 from current-program-number
           end-if
           evaluate current-program-number
             when 6
               set next-program to entry "prog-6"
               move "In program 6" to text-msg
             when 5
               set next-program to entry "prog-5"
               move "In program 5" to text-msg
             when 4
               set next-program to entry "prog-4"
               move "In program 4" to text-msg
             when 3
               set next-program to entry "prog-3"
               move "In program 3" to text-msg
             when 2
               set next-program to entry "prog-2"
               move "In program 2" to text-msg
             when 1
               set next-program to entry "prog-1"
               move "In program 1" to text-msg
             when other
               set next-program to null
               move "Done" to text-msg
           end-evaluate
           exit program.
       end program get-prog.

       program-id. prog-1.
       data division.
       linkage section.
       1 adr-of-next-data-ptr pointer.
       1 next-data-ptr pointer.
       1 next-data.
        2 text-msg pic x(30).
       procedure division using adr-of-next-data-ptr.
           set address of next-data-ptr to adr-of-next-data-ptr
           set address of next-data to next-data-ptr
           display text-msg
           exit program.
       end program prog-1.

       program-id. prog-2.
       data division.
       linkage section.
       1 adr-of-next-data-ptr pointer.
       1 next-data-ptr pointer.
       1 next-data.
        2 text-msg pic x(30).
       procedure division using adr-of-next-data-ptr.
           set address of next-data-ptr to adr-of-next-data-ptr
           set address of next-data to next-data-ptr
           display text-msg
           exit program.
       end program prog-2.

       program-id. prog-3.
       data division.
       linkage section.
       1 adr-of-next-data-ptr pointer.
       1 next-data-ptr pointer.
       1 next-data.
        2 text-msg pic x(30).
       procedure division using adr-of-next-data-ptr.
           set address of next-data-ptr to adr-of-next-data-ptr
           set address of next-data to next-data-ptr
           display text-msg
           exit program.
       end program prog-3.

       program-id. prog-4.
       data division.
       linkage section.
       1 adr-of-next-data-ptr pointer.
       1 next-data-ptr pointer.
       1 next-data.
        2 text-msg pic x(30).
       procedure division using adr-of-next-data-ptr.
           set address of next-data-ptr to adr-of-next-data-ptr
           set address of next-data to next-data-ptr
           display text-msg
           exit program.
       end program prog-4.

       program-id. prog-5.
       data division.
       linkage section.
       1 adr-of-next-data-ptr pointer.
       1 next-data-ptr pointer.
       1 next-data.
        2 text-msg pic x(30).
       procedure division using adr-of-next-data-ptr.
           set address of next-data-ptr to adr-of-next-data-ptr
           set address of next-data to next-data-ptr
           display text-msg
           exit program.
       end program prog-5.

       program-id. prog-6.
       data division.
       linkage section.
       1 adr-of-next-data-ptr pointer.
       1 next-data-ptr pointer.
       1 next-data.
        2 text-msg pic x(30).
       procedure division using adr-of-next-data-ptr.
           set address of next-data-ptr to adr-of-next-data-ptr
           set address of next-data to next-data-ptr
           display text-msg
           exit program.
       end program prog-6.
-----

William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b7seot$ten$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net...
> Rick,
>    I think that I made the same mistake when I first started looking at
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> "An operand is a sending operand if its contents prior to the execution of
a
> statement may be used by the execution of the statement. An operand is a
> receiving operand if its contents may be changed by the execution of the
> statement."
>
> I read this to mean that an "ADDRESS OF" data-pointer may be used in a
CALL
> BY REFERENCE (identifier-2) - but *only* if that data-item is *not*
changed
> by any action of the CALLed subprogram.
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > One must say BY CONTENT ADDRESS OF record-2, which is the same as
USING
> > > record-2.
> >
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
> > not applicable.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is supported (was: What's Missing in a COBOL Style Guide?

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-20T22:55:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea2fcc7.165270154@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3E6FC7EC.8EF66036@attglobal.net> <3e768cca.43241859@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303180131.1ccbd4df@posting.google.com> <3e775283.93866179@news.optonline.net> <b58496$ttb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b586kl$2h1$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3e77b35b.7601275@news.optonline.net> <v7fkcvfdqatlf5@corp.supernews.com> <3e78f7f6.28054222@news.optonline.net> <v7insvfn4je22e@corp.supernews.com> <3ea16766.61476936@news.optonline.net> <va35257evgc5e0@corp.supernews.com> <b7seot$ten$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <va5q62ai9pqrf8@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

>Bill,
>I reviewed the FDIS and came to the conclusion that my
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>and only for BY REFERENCE.

When the referenced data item is in local-storage, its address-identifier will
be the contents of the data-pointer on which it is BASED. They are one and the
same. I tried to show that in the examples. 

As a practical matter, it will work the same in the other sections. The only
difference being the pointers are not visible; they don't have a name. 

>I then wrote a set of programs to test the concept using the older
>MF COBOL/2 3.2. My thinking is that it should have the same
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>the linkage section rather than BASED items.

Micro Focus does not have the restriction introduced by SR(11). If you had a
conforming 2002 compiler, the CALL in your first program would not have
compiled.

What's objectionable about SR(11) is the unstated implication that structures
containing pointers cannot be passed BY REFERENCE. Using my Best Practices Demo
Program as an example, it contains structures like this:

01  line-entry.
     05  line-length		comp-5 pic s9(04).
     05  fixed-portion.
         10  previous-line		pointer.
         10  next-line		pointer.
         10  line-number		comp-5 pic s9(09).
         10  word-count		comp-5 pic s9(04).
         10  first-word		pointer.
         10  last-word		pointer.
     05  line-text.
         10  line-byte occurs 1 to 256 depending on line-length pic x.

     call 'mfree' using line-entry

The intention of SR(11) is to protect pointers from being modified. Because
line-entry contains pointers, SR(11) implies it cannot be passed in this way ..
because mfree could modify those pointers. This is a SERIOUS structural problem.

--- history follows --

>The programs pass only the addresses of pointers on calls,
>including both program (procedure) pointers and data pointers.
…[259 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is supported (was: What's Missing in a COBOL Style Guide?

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-20T23:57:47-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<va6r4ni3b5u5f2@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3E6FC7EC.8EF66036@attglobal.net> <3e768cca.43241859@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303180131.1ccbd4df@posting.google.com> <3e775283.93866179@news.optonline.net> <b58496$ttb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b586kl$2h1$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3e77b35b.7601275@news.optonline.net> <v7fkcvfdqatlf5@corp.supernews.com> <3e78f7f6.28054222@news.optonline.net> <v7insvfn4je22e@corp.supernews.com> <3ea16766.61476936@news.optonline.net> <va35257evgc5e0@corp.supernews.com> <b7seot$ten$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <va5q62ai9pqrf8@corp.supernews.com> <3ea2fcc7.165270154@news.optonline.net>`

```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ea2fcc7.165270154@news.optonline.net...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> When the referenced data item is in local-storage, its address-identifier
will
> be the contents of the data-pointer on which it is BASED. They are one and
the
> same. I tried to show that in the examples.

Your example was, in part:

 working-storage section.
 01  a-pointer  data-pointer.
 01  record-1  pic x(80).
 local-storage section.
 01  record-2  pic x(80) BASED a-pointer.

The standard, as approved, to the best of my knowledge,
and according to the FDIS, does not use BASED in the
way you used it here. PL/I uses it that way and an older
committee draft may have had such syntax. The correct
data description entry would be:

 01  record-2  pic x(80) BASED.

that is, record-2 would have its own implicit pointer which
is modified by the SET statement, such as,

  set address of record-2 to a-pointer.

What this means is than when passing the address of a
based item, there is no way that a called program can change
the location of a based item in the calling program, for example.

> As a practical matter, it will work the same in the other sections. The
only
> difference being the pointers are not visible; they don't have a name.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Micro Focus does not have the restriction introduced by SR(11). If you had
a
> conforming 2002 compiler, the CALL in your first program would not have
> compiled.

As I tried to say, I believe the restriction in SR(11) does not
apply. This is a result of the use of 'address-identifier' in SR(3)
and SR(4).

> What's objectionable about SR(11) is the unstated implication that
structures
> containing pointers cannot be passed BY REFERENCE.

SR(11) does not apply when calls are made BY REFERENCE
with the conformance checking of a program prototype and the
restriction on structures is only for strongly-typed group items.

USAGE SR(13), FDIS page 362,
"13) A USAGE clause with the OBJECT REFERENCE,
POINTER, or PROGRAM-POINTER phrase may be specified
only for an elementary data item with level 1 or subordinate to
a type declaration that includes the STRONG phrase."

Use a program prototype and the problem goes away.

> Using my Best Practices Demo
> Program as an example, it contains structures like this:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> The intention of SR(11) is to protect pointers from being modified.
Because
> line-entry contains pointers, SR(11) implies it cannot be passed in this
way ..
> because mfree could modify those pointers. This is a SERIOUS structural
problem.

See above.

> --- history follows --
>
…[175 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> identifier-2 or if identifier-2 is an addressidentifier, identifier-2
is a
> >> sending operand."
> >>
> >> However, 14.5.7 Sending and receiving operands on page 389 says,
> >>
> >> "An operand is a sending operand if its contents prior to the execution
of
> >a
> >> statement may be used by the execution of the statement. An operand is
a
> >> receiving operand if its contents may be changed by the execution of
the
> >> statement."
> >>
…[65 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> > > The reason for the prohibition of passing pointers BY REFERENCE is
not
> >> > > syntactic simplicity, it is to protect them from modification
outside
> >> the
> >> > SET
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is supported (was: What's Missing in a COBOL Style Guide?

_(thread depth: 33)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-21T16:06:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea4113b.236052308@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3E6FC7EC.8EF66036@attglobal.net> <3e768cca.43241859@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303180131.1ccbd4df@posting.google.com> <3e775283.93866179@news.optonline.net> <b58496$ttb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b586kl$2h1$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3e77b35b.7601275@news.optonline.net> <v7fkcvfdqatlf5@corp.supernews.com> <3e78f7f6.28054222@news.optonline.net> <v7insvfn4je22e@corp.supernews.com> <3ea16766.61476936@news.optonline.net> <va35257evgc5e0@corp.supernews.com> <b7seot$ten$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <va5q62ai9pqrf8@corp.supernews.com> <3ea2fcc7.165270154@news.optonline.net> <va6r4ni3b5u5f2@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

>
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>committee draft may have had such syntax.

My draft --1.8 -- says the pointer is optional.

> 01  record-2  pic x(80) BASED.
>
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…

Are we talking about the same passage? Mine reads:

"If the BY REFERENCE phrase is specified or implied for an identifier-2, that
identifier shall not be a strongly typed group item, object reference, or
pointer."

>USAGE SR(13), FDIS page 362,
>"13) A USAGE clause with the OBJECT REFERENCE,
>POINTER, or PROGRAM-POINTER phrase may be specified
>only for an elementary data item with level 1 or subordinate to
>a type declaration that includes the STRONG phrase."

I missed that passage. I think tt answers my question -- structures containing
pointers may not be passed BY REFERENCE.

The FDIS (Final Draft International Standard) does not

had

…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Are we talking about the same passage?

I'll rephrase. When a program prototype is used, Format 3 applies.
In Format 3, identifier-2 items may only be passed BY REFERENCE;
and because Format 3 is used SR(11) does not apply. [Strongly-
typed group items are reference in USAGE SR(13) mentioned, below.]

> Mine reads:
>
> "If the BY REFERENCE phrase is specified or implied for an identifier-2,
that
> identifier shall not be a strongly typed group item, object reference, or
> pointer."

FYI, the FDIS reads:
"FORMATS 1 AND 2
11) If the BY REFERENCE phrase is specified or implied for
an identifier-2, that identifier shall be neither a strongly-typed
group item nor a data item of class object or pointer."

> >USAGE SR(13), FDIS page 362,
> >"13) A USAGE clause with the OBJECT REFERENCE,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> I missed that passage. I think tt answers my question -- structures
containing
> pointers may not be passed BY REFERENCE.

In Format 3 (program-prototype), structures containing pointers
may be passed BY REFERENCE.

> >Use a program prototype and the problem goes away.

As I said, "Use a program prototype and the problem goes away."
And, keep in mind, that we are discussing the writing of programs
that conform to the standard.
------------
Rick
