###### ↳ ↳ ↳ BASED Clause (was: What is supported (was: What's Missing in a COBOL Style Guide?

_(thread depth: 34)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-21T14:41:15-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b81hgv$lg$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3E6FC7EC.8EF66036@attglobal.net> <3e768cca.43241859@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303180131.1ccbd4df@posting.google.com> <3e775283.93866179@news.optonline.net> <b58496$ttb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b586kl$2h1$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3e77b35b.7601275@news.optonline.net> <v7fkcvfdqatlf5@corp.supernews.com> <3e78f7f6.28054222@news.optonline.net> <v7insvfn4je22e@corp.supernews.com> <3ea16766.61476936@news.optonline.net> <va35257evgc5e0@corp.supernews.com> <b7seot$ten$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <va5q62ai9pqrf8@corp.supernews.com> <3ea2fcc7.165270154@news.optonline.net> <va6r4ni3b5u5f2@corp.supernews.com> <3ea4113b.236052308@news.optonline.net>`

```
Just so there is a "clear" thread on this (although Rick has already
provided the correct answer).

The final 2002 Standard does *NOT* include any optional "pointer" data item
in the BASED Clause.  See Page 263 of the FDIS.

RW - if you are going to comment on the 2002 Standard, I really suggest that
you get a copy of this document. It is still available for free from:

 http://www.incits.org/tc_home/j4.htm
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is supported (was: What's Missing in a COBOL Style Guide?

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-19T21:27:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea1b9fa.82620167@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3E6FC7EC.8EF66036@attglobal.net> <3e768cca.43241859@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303180131.1ccbd4df@posting.google.com> <3e775283.93866179@news.optonline.net> <b58496$ttb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b586kl$2h1$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3e77b35b.7601275@news.optonline.net> <v7fkcvfdqatlf5@corp.supernews.com> <3e78f7f6.28054222@news.optonline.net> <v7insvfn4je22e@corp.supernews.com> <3ea16766.61476936@news.optonline.net> <va35257evgc5e0@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

>
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
>not applicable.

Page 421 says:

10) If the BY REFERENCE phrase is specified or implied for an identifier-2, that
identifier shall not be a strongly typed group item, object reference, or
pointer.

Here is an example:

 working-storage section.
 01  a-pointer  data-pointer.
 01  record-1  pic x(80).
 local-storage section.
 01  record-2  pic x(80) BASED a-pointer.

The following two statements do the same thing:
 SET ADDRESS OF record-2 TO ADDRESS OF record-1
 SET a-pointer TO ADDRESS OF record-1

Likewise, the following are equivalent. 
 CALL 'a-program' USING record-2
 CALL 'a-program' USING BY CONTENT ADDRESS OF record-2
 CALL 'a-program' USING BY CONTENT a-pointer

But the following would not pass a pointer to record-2, as you said, they would
pass a pointer to a-pointer:
 CALL 'a-program' USING ADDRESS OF record-2
 CALL 'a-program' USING a-pointer

This would enable the called program to modify the pointer. The committee was
afraid this would happen:

a-program
 linkage section.
 01  a-record   pic x(80).
 
 MOVE SPACES TO a-record

..which would put spaces into a-pointer. The correct syntax should be:

a-program
 local-storage section.
 01  a-record   pic x(80) BASED a-pointer.
 linkage section.
 01  a-pointer   data-pointer. 

 MOVE SPACES to a-record

The committee is trying to prevent us from shooting ourselves in the foot. Other
languages go in the other direction; they make the syntax easy to use but hard
(for some) to understand. For instance, in C it would be **a_record.

I find this restriction a SERIOUS flaw because it implies, without explicitly
saying, that not only is pointer prohibited in the USING but also a group item
containing pointers cannot be passed BY REFERENCE.  I routinely work with
structures which contain pointers to other structures and I routinely pass their
address as a parameter. This limitation, not found in implimentor extensions to
COBOL 85, would invalidate every one of my programs. 

Tell me if I'm mistaken about group items. I can't find where the standard says
one way or the other.
```

```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ea1b9fa.82620167@news.optonline.net...
[snip]
> Page 421 says:
>
> 10) If the BY REFERENCE phrase is specified or implied for an
identifier-2, that
> identifier shall not be a strongly typed group item, object reference, or
> pointer.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> But the following would not pass a pointer to record-2, as you said, they
would
> pass a pointer to a-pointer:
>  CALL 'a-program' USING ADDRESS OF record-2
>  CALL 'a-program' USING a-pointer
>
> This would enable the called program to modify the pointer. The committee
was
> afraid this would happen:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> The committee is trying to prevent us from shooting ourselves in the foot.
Other
> languages go in the other direction; they make the syntax easy to use but
hard
> (for some) to understand. For instance, in C it would be **a_record.
>
> I find this restriction a SERIOUS flaw because it implies, without
explicitly
> saying, that not only is pointer prohibited in the USING but also a group
item
> containing pointers cannot be passed BY REFERENCE.  I routinely work with
> structures which contain pointers to other structures and I routinely pass
their
> address as a parameter. This limitation, not found in implimentor
extensions to
> COBOL 85, would invalidate every one of my programs.
>
> Tell me if I'm mistaken about group items. I can't find where the standard
says
> one way or the other.

I just spent several hours looking through the FDIS and
even wrote a test program that should have the same
behavior as the standard. I can find no restriction on using
pointers that would prevent using pointers in COBOL in a
manner similar to how they may be used in C, which, as we
*all* know, is the benchmark for "do whatever you want."

The one restriction that COBOL places on the use of
pointers is, what I might call, 'single-level access'; that is
one must set a data item to the address contained in the
pointer before accessing the data 'pointed to'. C permits,
what I might call, 'multi-level access' with a single expression.

I think "the committee," as you call them, gave us an automatic
weapon with a hair-trigger. One might shoot one's self in the
foot or destroy it entirely depending upon how one uses
pointers. It is just what I wanted! <g>
```

## Thread: Sets and subsets — 345 this month / 439 total

### Sets and subsets

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-20T01:30:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
As a follow on to RW's incorrect view that an integer that *also* belongs to
the set of whole numbers (or integers) that represent dates in the form
YYYYMMDD "loses" its identity as an integer,  let's see if I have this
correct.

The set of all human beings includes both mammals who (when mature and
healthy) produce sperm or ova.

Robert Wagner belongs to the set of all human beings (I assume).

Robert Wagner belongs to the SUB-set of human beings that are male human
beings, i.e. the subset that (when mature and healthy) produce sperm

Therefore, as Robert Wagner cannot produce BOTH sperm and ova, he/it ceases
to be considered a human being.

   ***

Do I have that logic "correct" (i.e. following the same set of logic as RW
applies to "an integer" versus "the set of all integers")?
```

#### ↳ Re: Sets and subsets

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-20T16:57:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea2c17e.150090828@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>As a follow on to RW's incorrect view that an integer that *also* belongs to
>the set of whole numbers (or integers) that represent dates in the form
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>applies to "an integer" versus "the set of all integers")?

Suppose I brought you a horse and a cow. 

WMK: I see you brought me two animals. 
RW:   Yes, both are ungulates too. One is a horse and the other, a cow.
WMK: I prefer to call them animals; a matter of style.
--later--
WMK: Please milk the first animal.
RW:   Err, which one? The mare and cow both have teats.
DD:    RW has no common sense. It's obvious which one.
RP:    You just made that up. Can you _prove_ the mare has teats? 
          I thought not. This demonstrates that everything you say is wrong.
WMK: The Good Book says both are animals. [Recites entire passage]
-- end of parable --

We create taxonomies to improve communication. Those who refuse to use them
diminish it. 

This integer debate has become repetitious and turned into an ignoratio elenchi.
I'll not respond to any more posts about integers. 

Instead, I'll have a picnic lunch with my lady. On the menu: a berry/lemon cloud
galette and French picnic tart.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT PETA and the Law (Was Sets and subsets)

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-20T22:55:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea3053f.167438700@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea2c17e.150090828@news.optonline.net> <0rKcnXkFKPSMeD-jXTWJjg@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:

>Meat is murder,
>Chicken is manslaughter,
>Eggs are kidnapping,
>Milk is sexual harrassment.

You omitted my favorite: honey is slave labor. 

I'm not an animal rights advocate. I've been vegetarian 30 years for CULINARY
reasons. Meat is boring. People who eat meat every day have no imagination. If
you take away their meat, they'd make mac & cheese the first day, pizza the
second, stir-fry the third, and be out of ideas on the fourth day.

A typical restaurant menu looks more like a butcher shop list than an offering
of food dishes. 'Would you rather have the steak or the chicken?' What culinary
bankruptcy.

Having worked in the supermarket industry, I know the distribution of sales by
department. It is 50% grocery and frozen, 25% meat, 20% non-food and
bakery/deli, 5% produce. Think about that .. 5% real food and 95% mass produced.
The distribution in my cart is 75% produce and 25% other, mostly grains. It's
actually not a single cart; the produce comes from a high-quality produce
market. I make almost everything from scratch. 

When I cook for guests, they enter with a look of doom, depart asking for
recipes. My cuisine is eclectic, with a leaning toward Mediterranean,
Middle-Eastern and French.  I don't use tofu nor shop in health-food stores.

Does that answer your taunt? Now, go have another double-cheese Big Mac.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT PETA and the Law (Was Sets and subsets)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-20T19:52:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cfycnVAg3pFq3T6jXTWJjg@giganews.com>`

20 years ago I looked, felt, and acted 20 years younger than my current age.

Since my heart attack I do diet, but notice that the results from blood test are
much more closely related to my exercise and stress than they do to diet and
medicine.   (I haven't exercised much lately when my wife got laid off, my
grandson was born, and my daughter-in-law had her gall bladder removed).   My
physician wants me to double a medicine I am taking but doubts it will be
enough.   I bet if I work out more, it will be more than enough.

We're all dying, some faster than others, and that's really sad. My mother, age
82, cannot look up words in the dictionary because she can't remember the order
of letters in the alphabet. She has something like Parkinson's. My sense of
balance (disequilibrium) is all screwed up. Who knows why? It's in the otolith
organs of the inner ear, about the size of a pea and containing six organs --
semicircular canals (3), otolith (2) and hearing (1). 

As a vegetarian who walks to work and home for lunch every day (total two
miles), I get plenty of diet and exercise. Yet I'm still degenerating. 

There have been many studies where the experimental group did everything 'right'
-- no smoking, no drinking, diet, exercise, etc. -- and the control group were
couch potatoes. Assessement of their health after a few years found it to be ...
exactly the same. You don't read about these studies in the popular press
because they are politically incorrect.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT PETA and the Law (Was Sets and subsets)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-24T11:21:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea7b7f2.7311191@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea2c17e.150090828@news.optonline.net> <0rKcnXkFKPSMeD-jXTWJjg@giganews.com> <3ea3053f.167438700@news.optonline.net> <cfycnVAg3pFq3T6jXTWJjg@giganews.com> <3ea35e30.190210459@news.optonline.net> <3ea3ea25_2@127.0.0.1> <3ea4ab46.275494005@news.optonline.net> <b83e9h$680$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea60de7.27579751@news.optonline.net> <b866nj$fjr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>My younger brother has small cell lung cancer.  He has never smoked, but his
>first wife was a heavy smoker.   Hardly anybody gets this without being a
>smoker.  He does everything for his health including lots of running.

As former keeper of the FAQ and one of the principal writers in alt.smokers (ca.
1990-94), I've spent hundreds of hours studying the link between second hand
smoke and lung cancer. 

Bottom line: not one valid scientific study has found a causal relationship.
More than a thousand have tried. A few claimed 'success' (thanks to funding
bias), but were later found to be flawed or (gasp) deliberately manipulated. At
least three of those 'disproofs' were authored by me, through the use of better
statistical analysis. 

The most common flaw in these studies is failing to discount the effect of
socioeconomic status. Poor people get sick more often than rich people. If one
looked for a link between watching professional wrestling and getting lung
cancer, (s)he would find a corelation. Tellingly, many studies have found a
negative dose-response relationship -- spouses of one pack a day smokers get
lung cancer more often than spouses of three pack a day smokers. In fact, three
pack a day are back to the baseline of no exposure.  You won't read that in Time
magazine; you must go to original sources. The explanation is simple: poor
people cannot afford to smoke three packs a day. Designers of fraudulent studies
use this knowledge to introduce bias by manipulating demographics. It is
blatantly obvious in the Fontham study, for instance. 

There are 140,000 new cases of lung cancer in the US per year, broken down as:
20K never smokers, 120K ever smokers. Your brother is one of the 20K. 

Why do poor people get sick so much? It's not lack of medical care nor any other
single reason. If it were, epidemiologists would have found it decades ago. It's
a combination of environmental factors aka lifestyle choices aka bad habits. I
believe, with admittedly weak substantiation, the main culprit is diet. Poor
people eat junk food, fried food, hydrogenated fat (margarine), etc. etc. 

Robert
```

On 21-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> >You have
> >no way of knowing my religious orientation (if any).

> >emphasise you had no way of knowing before your pejoritive, I belong to a

Most religious people cite surveys that say that the majority of Americans are
religious.   But I know of lots of people who are willing to go along and say
they believe in the religion of their families.   "Belief" is so subjective
anyway, not to mention the pressure to go along, that I doubt if anybody has a
halfway decent idea on what the majority believes.

But you seem pretty sure.   Maybe your analysis is better than the stats I have
read about.

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ea2c17e.150090828@news.optonline.net...
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>
> >As a follow on to RW's incorrect view that an integer that *also* belongs
to
> >the set of whole numbers (or integers) that represent dates in the form
> >YYYYMMDD "loses" its identity as an integer,  let's see if I have this
> >correct.
> >

How could such a number NOT be an integer?

It has no decimal point and the collection of digits of which it is composed
(irrespective of what these digits REPRESENT to US) lies on the Real number
line. We do not normally use imaginary numbers to represent dates (although
Stephen Hawking is happy to do so when representing the Time component of
SpaceTime, for convenience, when describing the boundless but finite nature
of the Universe...<G>).

If the above is a fair statement of your position, Robert, I would strongly
advise you to abandon it now...<G>


> >The set of all human beings includes both mammals who (when mature and
> >healthy) produce sperm or ova.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Therefore, as Robert Wagner cannot produce BOTH sperm and ova, he/it
ceases
> >to be considered a human being.
> >
> >   ***
> >

The above is covered in a separate post...


> >Do I have that logic "correct" (i.e. following the same set of logic as
RW
> >applies to "an integer" versus "the set of all integers")?
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>           I thought not. This demonstrates that everything you say is
wrong.
> WMK: The Good Book says both are animals. [Recites entire passage]
> -- end of parable --
>

LOL! A clever exchange, but it makes your position no more correct...<G>


> We create taxonomies to improve communication. Those who refuse to use
them
> diminish it.
>

I contend that there are few here who are qualified to comment on the nature
of communication.

What I see in these threads is way short of effective interaction and
exchange of particles...

Your comment on taxonomy is just one symptom of the lack of duplication that
occurs here.

A: "Send reinforcements, we are going to advance.."
B: ("duplicates" message received from A as..)"Send three and fourpence, we
are going to a dance..."

However it IS entertaining (sometimes...when it isn't pedantic and tedious)

> This integer debate has become repetitious and turned into an ignoratio
elenchi.
> I'll not respond to any more posts about integers.
>
Ah, so I see you are taking my advice and "bugging out" <G>

While this is not the ideal mechanism for leaving an untenable position when
under fire (The leopard walk or the monkey crawl are the favoured miltary
options <G>) it is certainly effective. Don't be under the illusion that it
leaves your dignity intact, though...

> Instead, I'll have a picnic lunch with my lady. On the menu: a berry/lemon
cloud
> galette and French picnic tart.

You have very quickly learned about the nature of CLC. A "deflection germ"
such as this can be almost guaranteed to generate a whole heap of posts
about French tarts and the nature and value of various foods, under which
smokescreen an effective withdrawal from the integer position can be
achieved <G>.

While this may qualify as "logical germ warfare" it is better than the
nuclear alternative which would be to simply leave and never return...

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sets and subsets

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-20T22:55:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea30ee4.169907599@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea2c17e.150090828@news.optonline.net> <3ea2f308_2@127.0.0.1>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3ea2c17e.150090828@news.optonline.net...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>How could such a number NOT be an integer?

You arrived too late. The party's over. :)

>What I see in these threads is way short of effective interaction and
>exchange of particles...

Particle exchange belongs in the physics newsgroup, although it might be
appropriate here under the rubric 'communication'. How does an electron know the
nucleus is there to be circled around? The answer's not gravity. They're
constantly exchanging quarks so they know where the other is. You say 'that's
fascinating, I wonder what the communication protocol is.' Then jack-booted QM
guys squash the thread by telling us every revolution spawns a new universe. 

>> This integer debate has become repetitious and turned into an ignoratio
>elenchi.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>leaves your dignity intact, though...

One would think Set Theory would be a natural for programmers. It looks like
logic because it uses intersections and unions. The evidence shows I was
mistaken. Programmers think in terms of bits and bytes and simplistic
definitions, not abstractions. 

I had a similar experience on the opposite side of the spectrum when I hired a
recent college grad with a degree in the mathematical specialty called Logic.
Naively, I thought this is a natural fit. Logic is the bread and butter of
programming, right? Wrong. He was extremely intelligent (PhD at 22) but had
difficulty grasping basic programming concepts. To gain a better insight into
his thinking, I read his thesis. It had a lot of set theory plus some
specialized algebra more advanced than sets. Its structure and flow wasn't very
'logical'. 

A year later I was about to fire him. He saved me the trouble by finding a
teaching position at a well-known university. 

The smartest programmer I ever knew was a shadowy figure who went by an obvious
pseudonym: Mason Dixon. He got a full scholarship at MIT's School of Education.
I didn't know MIT had one, neither did he. He hitchhiked from Fort Worth to
Cambridge. Three months later he was back, saying "I didn't know anything about
teaching. I thought they were supposed to be the experts. It turned out they
didn't know anything either." That guy was really bright and an outstanding
programmer. He quit the day after I did.

>> Instead, I'll have a picnic lunch with my lady. On the menu: a berry/lemon
>>cloud galette and French picnic tart.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>achieved <G>.

Would posting the recipe help? I'm quite proud of it. I started with Mollie
Katzan's and improved it over half a dozen iterations. Kinda like posting source
code, eh? <G>

How about Best Practices Cooking offering modernization tips such as  'A dash of
bitters improves any pilaf or risotto, especially when made from barley.' and
'You MUST use a ceramic knife. High-carbon steel is Old School and obsolete. As
for stainless steel, don't get me started.' The latter should _really_ start a
flame war. <G>
```

>
> Particle exchange belongs in the physics newsgroup, although it might be
> appropriate here under the rubric 'communication'. How does an electron
know the
> nucleus is there to be circled around? The answer's not gravity. They're

No they're not. You are confusing quarks with the (yet to be confirmed)
graviton. Besides, it has nothing to do with gravity or quarks, it has to do
with the weak nuclear force and the Yang Mills field.

>You say 'that's
> fascinating, I wonder what the communication protocol is.' Then
jack-booted QM
> guys squash the thread by telling us every revolution spawns a new
universe.

According to Richard Feynman, they might be right. Personally, I doubt it,
but only because I don't WANT to believe it...<G> That particular model
(involving sum over histories) has not been proven wrong (so far...)

>
> >> This integer debate has become repetitious and turned into an ignoratio
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >While this is not the ideal mechanism for leaving an untenable position
when

it

> One would think Set Theory would be a natural for programmers. It looks
like
> logic because it uses intersections and unions. The evidence shows I was
> mistaken. Programmers think in terms of bits and bytes and simplistic
> definitions, not abstractions.

I'm a programmer. I understand set theory. And I have imagination. Also, I
can cope with abstractions... and, I know what an integer is <G>.

(I refute your allegations with the above. It's a technique I picked up from
Dr. Johnson... When it was suggested to him  that everything is an illusion,
he slammed his foot into a stone and said "I refute it, thus..." He couldn't
walk for a week but felt it was worth it to win the argument... My approach
is less painful (for me, at least <G>))


<snipped deflection germ>

Pete.

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3ea30ee4.169907599@news.optonline.net...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>with the weak nuclear force and the Yang Mills field.

Very good! But the weak force is mediated by the exchange of intermediate vector
bosons W and Z. W changes the flavor of quarks, which is why I simplified it to
"exchange of quarks".  An explanation is here:

http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/forces/funfor.html

>>You say 'that's
>> fascinating, I wonder what the communication protocol is.' Then
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>(involving sum over histories) has not been proven wrong (so far...)

You're right. And it has stood up to criticism better than the alternatives. 

>> >> This integer debate has become repetitious and turned into an ignoratio
>> >elenchi.
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>is less painful (for me, at least <G>))

You probably have a digital camera. Could you post a picture? <G>

But you still don't know what an integer is.

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> RP:    You just made that up. Can you _prove_ the mare has teats?

Horses are mammals, Mares have teats and can be milked:

"""Mare's milk is a good substitute for individuals with cow's milk allergy.
The aim of this study was to investigate, in vitro and in vivo, the 
allergenicity of mare's milk in a population of selected children with 
severe IgE-mediated cow's milk allergy. In 25 Cow?s milk allergic children, 
96% were able to tolerate mare?s milk. This suggests that mare's milk can 
be regarded as a good substitute of cow's milk in most children with severe 
IgE-mediated cow's milk allergy."""

"""Airag: Fermented mare's milk is one of the classic Mongolian drinks. The 
milk put into a container, made of cow skin and made sour. It is pounded 
vigorously with stick for an hour to help fermentation. The milk is then 
allowed to ferment for 3-4 hours."""

>           I thought not. This demonstrates that everything you say is
>           wrong.

You tend to 'make up' ideas without reference to the outside world and then 
you create arguments around that idea designed to defend it rather than to 
reflect reality.

I pointed out that you would not understand why people told you to 'go 
away' rathger forcefully as you related had happened on several occasions.

It is because you had learnt over decades that if you keep 'making it up' 
as you go along you will eventually 'win' the argument by making everyone 
else give up or tell you to piss off.

But it wasn't winning at all.
```

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>IgE-mediated cow's milk allergy."""

I'm pleased to see you quoting (apparently) Primary Sources. 


>>           I thought not. This demonstrates that everything you say is
>>           wrong.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>reflect reality.

Don't all humans do that? I prefer to think my ideas are creative. Do you have
someing against creativity?

>It is because you had learnt over decades that if you keep 'making it up' 
>as you go along you will eventually 'win' the argument by making everyone 
>else give up or tell you to piss off.
>
>But it wasn't winning at all.

That's not my technique. I present corrigible evidence or theory. If they can't
rebut it, they lose.That's what happened here in the 'integer' thread. All
rebuttal was from Secondary Sources such as programming language standards and
'home-grown' logic. Nobody groked the ideas of set theory. 

I didn't make up set theory. It's documented galore in the literature of math.
Programmers simply didn't 'get it'.
```

riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

How do we determine truth? We test it against reality. Reality is the one thing
we all have in common, that we all agree on (except crazy people). In the case
of abstractions like integer, the only reality is rigorous proof (I hate that
word) by many mathematicians. I provided pointers to that body of work and they
were brushed aside.

>> >as you go along you will eventually 'win' the argument by making everyone

can't
>> rebut it, they lose.

>understood ordinals, but never quite moved past that.

I'm still confused by ordinals. If the set of irrational numbers is larger than
the set of ordinals, how do we assign them an ordinal? We'll run out of numbers,
even though our ordinals are infinite.  

>> That's what happened here in the 'integer' thread. All
>> rebuttal was from Secondary Sources such as programming language standards
and
>> 'home-grown' logic. Nobody groked the ideas of set theory.

>deny.  In your own mind you can never be wrong, your ego can never
>accept failure, but failure is what you really are.

>> I didn't make up set theory. It's documented galore in the literature of
math.
>> Programmers simply didn't 'get it'.
>
>You didn't 'make up' set theory, no.  You did, however, claim what you
>made up _was_ set theory and it wasn't.

You didn't 'get it' so you rant on.

I cannot speak for Mr Plinston but I recall being taught something along
the lines of 'Since ratio exists as a matter of degree there can be no
ratio between existence and nonexistence; 'not' is a matter of condition
instead of degree.  Since human beings are by nature finite then any
attempts to encompass the infinite will be, by this definition, irrational
(without ratio).'

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - InifinityRe: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-04-22T12:12:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0304221112.78f62350@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea4782a.262407453@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304212127.59859a3a@posting.google.com> <b82nt7$69d$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b82r79$eeb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b83esd$6fo$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote

> > No one understand infinity, that is impossible.
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> How do you determine what is possible and what is impossible to understand?

Well, you said it in 'flavour'.  It is entirely possible to understand
an abstraction of infinity, or a subset or flavour of infinity.

But, just as you cannot know what happens in a black hole (because
there is no information flow), you cannot understand the whole of
infinity.  If you understand as much as the number of brain cells you
have will hold, there will always be one more factor left out.  That
is why it is called infinity.

(At least that is my understanding of how infinity is represented in
the abstraction that I do understand).
```

 > robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
 >> I'm still confused by ordinals. If the set of irrational numbers is
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
 >  infinite.

Wasn't it RW who brought up Cantor in the first place?  Does the term

I think when a participant in a debate about mathematics doesn't appear
to understand the cardinality of infinite sets and countability, we can
justify a degree of suspicion about his other claims.  That's a matter
of basic discrete mathematics.  (C'mon.  Diagonalization.  The
continuum.  *Basic* stuff.  If you don't know this much, don't wave your
hand at set theory.)

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-23T09:58:28-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EA69C04.7090208@newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea2c17e.150090828@news.optonline.net> <b7utlb$j1k$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ea33042.178450965@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304202059.377ac2c3@posting.google.com> <3ea4782a.262407453@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304212127.59859a3a@posting.google.com> <3EA55E79.6090702@newsguy.com> <3ea5ed65.19257366@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
 > Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:
 >
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
 > I understand that alpeh-one is greater than aleph-null.

Well, that was hardly clear from what you wrote.  "We'll run out of
numbers" is on the right track, of course, but you appeared to be saying
you didn't understand infinite cardinality.  For someone who started a
debate about set theory, that looked bad.

My comment was also directed in part at Richard, though I didn't phrase
it well.  His "cannot 'run out' of an infitite set" is even worse, since
it implies that there's only one infinite cardinality.

 > I was looking for a supporter rather than an antagonist.

Coming after a protracted battle over set theory, that was an
antagonizing statement.  If I engaged in some drawn-out fight here over
some fine point of COBOL syntax, and then admitted that I didn't really
know how, say, EVALUATE worked, don't you think folks would have some
reason to question my authority?  Yes, that can easily wander into
various sorts of fallacy (particularly ad hominem), but demonstrating a
basic failure of knowledge in a field is usually symptomatic of a more
pervasive lack of understanding about that field.

 > "justify a degree of suspicion about his other claims" .. talk about
 > hand waving.

Fair enough - though I meant (and should have specified) only claims
regarding set theory.

Robert, I've lurked here long enough to understand the dynamic between
you and several of the other regulars here.  I don't think you need
another antagonist, and it's not a role I relish in any case.  I'm
always inclined to try to judge each post - from whatever source - on
its own merits and the *factual* content of any responses it generates.
I've been reading Usenet since 1991, and while battle of the
net.personalities is occasionally an amusing sport to watch, I haven't
the time to play it myself.
```

```
Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:

>Coming after a protracted battle over set theory, that was an
>antagonizing statement.  If I engaged in some drawn-out fight here over
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>pervasive lack of understanding about that field.

That's the fallacy of ad hominem.  If the debater doesn't present himself as an
authority then his personal failings (if any) are IRRELEVANT. It's not a
question of manners; it's a question of relevancy. 

I didn't claim to be an expert on mathematics. Those who wave their hands and
say I 'don't know what I'm talking about' completely miss that point. Their
arguments are based on emotion rather than reason. 
>
> > "justify a degree of suspicion about his other claims" .. talk about
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>regarding set theory.

ultracrepidarian: One who tries to work outside his field of knowledge,
    where he has no business being.
autodidact: A self-taught individual.
aristarch: A severe but fair critic.
lexiphane: Person with an inflated vocabulary.

<g>

>Robert, I've lurked here long enough to understand the dynamic between
>you and several of the other regulars here.  I don't think you need
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>the time to play it myself.

We all have the same time: 24 hours in a day. How we choose to spend it is not
about availability, it's about priorities.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-25T15:47:18+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8ab71$1dv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3EA55E79.6090702@newsguy.com> <3ea5ed65.19257366@news.optonline.net> <3EA69C04.7090208@newsguy.com> <3ea88130.21326254@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> That's the fallacy of ad hominem.  If the debater doesn't present himself
> as an authority then his personal failings (if any) are IRRELEVANT. It's
> not a question of manners; it's a question of relevancy.

It is true that you did not make an explicit claim to being an authority on 
maths - though you have on other matters which you also failed to display 
such skills. However, when you make statements of an authoritive nature in 
a form that you expect to be accepted then this is an implicit claim.

> I didn't claim to be an expert on mathematics. Those who wave their hands
> and say I 'don't know what I'm talking about' completely miss that point.
> Their arguments are based on emotion rather than reason.

No.  The argument is based on factual evidence.  You make a statement.  If 
it has no basis in fact then "You don't know what you are talking about" is 
not ad hominem, is not 'emotional', it is fair comment.  It is attempting 
to get you to notice that you are wrong and should learn more before making 
further statements.

Of course what you are actually getting at is that you finally realised 
that your claims about mathematics, and especially set theory, were 
incorrect, wrong, and based on your complete misunderstanding of what the 
texts said.

You now want, once again, to dismiss and deflect all responsibility and 
protect your ego by claiming that this is IRRELEVANT.  You weren't really 
wrong because you had your fingers crossed or something, oh that was it: 
you never explicitly claimed you were an authority on that actual subject.  
Once again Robert Wagner is never wrong, those bits don't count.

So what are you an expert in ?  Something they have a lot of in Texas for 
sure.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-26T18:42:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eaaa1e8.160795747@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3EA55E79.6090702@newsguy.com> <3ea5ed65.19257366@news.optonline.net> <3EA69C04.7090208@newsguy.com> <3ea88130.21326254@news.optonline.net> <b8ab71$1dv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>a form that you expect to be accepted then this is an implicit claim.

The issue is rhetorical style rather than claiming to be an authority.  When
people are criticized here for bad programming practice, their defense is 'a
matter of style'. Most posters seem to regard that as valid rebuttal. If it's
valid for programming, it should be valid for English as well. 

Now that we have the Web, reliance on expert knowledge of facts (but not
judgment) has almost become obsolete. We can look it up ourselves rather than
relying on someone's word that it's true. (Excepting the COBOL standard, which
is priced out of reach to the general public.)

>> I didn't claim to be an expert on mathematics. Those who wave their hands
>> and say I 'don't know what I'm talking about' completely miss that point.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>further statements.

Unless I claimed to be an authority AND asked readers to take my word for
something, remarks about my lack of knowledge are _not_ fair comment. You should
present a counter to my argument, not to my persona. If your goal is getting me
to admit error, rebuttal based on fact or superior logic is much more likely to
be successful ... as a practical matter. How often do people say, 'Gee, he's
right, I am a jerk?' It seldom happens. 

If your technique is browbeating me into acquiescence ... professional
brainwashers have failed, several times. The defense is actually very simple --
don't let them get to your emotions. Pretend you're Mr. Spock. It's easier to do
here than when falling from a second floor window. :)
 
>Of course what you are actually getting at is that you finally realised 
>that your claims about mathematics, and especially set theory, were 
>incorrect, wrong, and based on your complete misunderstanding of what the 
>texts said.

I didn't realize I was wrong; I realized we at a deadlock. 

>You now want, once again, to dismiss and deflect all responsibility and 
>protect your ego by claiming that this is IRRELEVANT.  You weren't really 
>wrong because you had your fingers crossed or something, oh that was it: 
>you never explicitly claimed you were an authority on that actual subject.  
>Once again Robert Wagner is never wrong, those bits don't count.

People don't read CLC to learn about Robert Wagner; they read it to learn about
COBOL. That's another reason your arguments are irrelevant. 

>So what are you an expert in ?  Something they have a lot of in Texas for 
>sure.

What's plentiful in _West_ Texas is courtesy and respect. I'll withhold comment
on East Texas (and Arkansas, Eastern Oklahoma and especially Louisiana). :)

My expertise in other topics is irrelevant here. The subject here is COBOL.
```

>Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>valid for programming, it should be valid for English as well. 

This might be true, Mr Wagner, were the purposes of programming and 
English the same.  To the best of my ability to view such they are not; 
the purpose of programming is to give a series of instructions to a 
machine while the purpose of English is the same as that of any other 
language used by human beings between each other.

DD

I said I wasn't going to get involved in this, but I just have to.  The 
purpose of source code is to communicate with maintenance programmers 
and others that might have to support your object code.

If all you were concerned with was communicating with the machine you 
could toss the source out once you had object.

I suggest that the purpose of programming is to communicate with the

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-27T14:28:23+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eab4057_2@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea88130.21326254@news.optonline.net> <b8ab71$1dv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eaaa1e8.160795747@news.optonline.net> <b8eseu$2fv$1@panix1.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-B235AC.19492726042003@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message
news:joe_zitzelberger-B235AC.19492726042003@corp.supernews.com...
> I said I wasn't going to get involved in this, but I just have to.  The
> purpose of source code is to communicate with maintenance programmers
> and others that might have to support your object code.
>

Only for as long as you still develop computer systems that NEED to have
source code maintained. The modern trend is away from maintaining source
code towards the use of re-usable components, procedures, and packages that
manipulate objects. Rather than change source code you simply invoke a
method of the object. This is a very hard concept for most COBOL people to
get their heads round, especially as there is strong resistance to OOP in
COBOL shops. I believe this is the major reason why COBOL is being sidelined
and the major factor contributing towards COBOL disuse. (I agree it is
arguable...<G>)

> If all you were concerned with was communicating with the machine you
> could toss the source out once you had object.
>

That is exactly what we do when we buy third party components. We never HAVE
access to the source code. Instead we are focussed on the Business
functionality and how well a given object supports it. If it meets the
business requirement we "plug it in". The concept of modifying it is not up
for discussion. The best analogy I can think of is if you were building an
engine for your car. You would buy the parts, crankshaft, bearings, etc. and
then assemble them. You would NOT decide to re-mill the crankshaft or plane
the head yourself. If you wanted these performance enhancing features you
would buy a "sport pack" which already incorporated them and had been found
to work in thousands of engines just like yours. This is one way to minimise
the risk of an engine rebuild.... Modern software engineering is very close
to this.

> I suggest that the purpose of programming is to communicate with the
> machine and other humans equally.

I disagree. Programming is to communicate with the machine. Written and
spoken language is to communicate with other Human Beings.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-28T09:05:51-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-D1AADE.09055128042003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eaaa1e8.160795747@news.optonline.net> <b8eseu$2fv$1@panix1.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-B235AC.19492726042003@corp.supernews.com> <b8fps7$doe$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

> Joe Zitzelberger  <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >machine and other humans equally.

In article <b8fps7$doe$1@panix1.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
> That conclusion seems to require that source code be the equivalent of 
> programming... those who would write in machine language or hard-wire the 
> patch-boards might disagree with that.

My primary language is OS/390 assembler, and I have occasionally coded 
in pure machine language style (e.g. sans assembler), and I said that.  
Source code is not programming, but it is the language that both  
programmers and machines must both speak if communication is to occur.

It would be completely valid Cobol from me to allocate some storage, 
fill it with some opcodes, create a procedure pointer to it and start 
executing.  The machine would not give it an electrons thought -- any 
programmer, including myself a few days later, might wonder what the 
hell was going on.

Clairity of source is important first for the originating programmer as 
they are writting; second for the emergency middle-of-the-night fix that 
"will never be necessary"; and thirdly if the program ever needs to be 
enhanced.

If machine communication is the ONLY concern -- then why bother with ANY 
high level language?  The machine prefers the lower level ones and 
performs better when presented with hand-coded assembler.  High level 
languages are a luxury for the human programmer end of the communication.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-04-28T09:57:53-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8jc11$r3$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <joe_zitzelberger-B235AC.19492726042003@corp.supernews.com> <b8fps7$doe$1@panix1.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-D1AADE.09055128042003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <joe_zitzelberger-D1AADE.09055128042003@corp.supernews.com>,
Joe Zitzelberger  <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
>
>> Joe Zitzelberger  <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>programmers and machines must both speak if communication is to occur.

You have written in machine language and you say that 'Source code... is 
the language that both programmers and machines must both speak'; this 
does not appear to contradict your assertion that 'Source code is not 
programming' which supports the conjecture I provided.

[snip]

>If machine communication is the ONLY concern -- then why bother with ANY 
>high level language?

Because human beings are involved in communicating with each other as to 
what the programming is to make the machine do.  Consider:

COMPUTE A = (B * C).

COMPUTE GS = (HR * HW).

COMPUTE GROSS-SALARY = (HOURLY-RATE * HOURS-WORKED).

Programmatically these are identical and (given the same data-types and
values in the variables ) they will compile to the same ASM statements; 
the machine will react identically to all of them.

Given a 3:am S0C7 and a two-year programmer the programmer's reaction 
might not be as sanguine, granted.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-28T17:33:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ead60cd.340763290@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eaaa1e8.160795747@news.optonline.net> <b8eseu$2fv$1@panix1.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-B235AC.19492726042003@corp.supernews.com> <b8fps7$doe$1@panix1.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-D1AADE.09055128042003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:


>If machine communication is the ONLY concern -- then why bother with ANY 
>high level language?  The machine prefers the lower level ones and 
>performs better when presented with hand-coded assembler.  High level 
>languages are a luxury for the human programmer end of the communication.

Because it's faster to program in an HLL. I tested this twice at times when I
very fluent in assembly language -- once IBM mainframe and once Intel. I 'raced
myself' to write the same function in assembly and COBOL. I thought times would
be about the same. COBOL was three times faster in both tests.
```

docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

it's
>>>>valid for programming, it should be valid for English as well. 
>>>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>comparative... you'll only wind up disappointed.'

I was advised to make it clearer that I was writing personal opinion, as opposed

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-04-27T12:17:47-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8gvrb$sn3$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eab4d82.204732010@news.optonline.net> <b8fq3d$eo4$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eabf0d6.246550635@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <3eabf0d6.246550635@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This is a variant, Mr Wagner, of something I call a 'Brooklyn Bridge 
defense', in memory of my Sainted Paternal Grandmother's - may she sleep 
with the angels! - habit of saying 'Everyone is doing (x)?  Fine, everyone 
is jumping off the Brooklyn Bridge... you'll jump, too?'

Likewise, Mr Wagner, if you were advised to, say, empty your bowels 
into your hat and then pull it over your ears...

But such matters aside, then... are you changing the basis of your 
statement, Mr Wagner, from what 'programming' is to what 'I, Mr Wagner, 
attempt to do when I write source code'?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-28T01:26:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eac2f14.262486545@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eab4d82.204732010@news.optonline.net> <b8fq3d$eo4$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eabf0d6.246550635@news.optonline.net> <b8gvrb$sn3$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3eabf0d6.246550635@news.optonline.net>,
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>>>>> That's the fallacy of ad hominem.  If the debater doesn't present
himself
>>>>>>>> as an authority then his personal failings (if any) are IRRELEVANT.
It's
>>>>>>>> not a question of manners; it's a question of relevancy.
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>It is true that you did not make an explicit claim to being an authority
on
>>
>>>>>>>maths - though you have on other matters which you also failed to display

>>>>>>>such skills. However, when you make statements of an authoritive nature
in 
>>>>>>>a form that you expect to be accepted then this is an implicit claim.
>>>>>>
>>>>>>The issue is rhetorical style rather than claiming to be an authority.
When
>>>>>>people are criticized here for bad programming practice, their defense is
'a
>>>>>>matter of style'. Most posters seem to regard that as valid rebuttal. If
>>it's
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>>I was advised to make it clearer that I was writing personal opinion, as
opposed
>>to dictum. 
>>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>attempt to do when I write source code'?

I'm not following your argument. I did not say everyone was doing something; I
changed my style. Whereas in the past I might have said 'all programs should be
written for human readability', making it sound like a commandment, now I phrase
it "When I program ...", thereby limiting the scope to one person. 

Three posters whom I respect independently gave the same advice. As we used to
say in the Corps, "If it happens once, it's an incident. If it happens twice,
it's a coincidence. If it happens three times, it's a pattern." Of course, if
we'd said that here, we'd have been flamed for failing to understand statistical
sampling theory. <g>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-28T20:19:09+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eace40d_2@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eab4d82.204732010@news.optonline.net> <b8fq3d$eo4$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eabf0d6.246550635@news.optonline.net> <b8gvrb$sn3$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eac2f14.262486545@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3eac2f14.262486545@news.optonline.net...
> docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
> Three posters whom I respect independently gave the same advice. As we
used to
> say in the Corps, "If it happens once, it's an incident. If it happens
twice,
> it's a coincidence. If it happens three times, it's a pattern." Of course,
if
> we'd said that here, we'd have been flamed for failing to understand
statistical
> sampling theory. <g>
>

I heard the same idea expressed as:

"Once is happenstance,
 Twice is coincidence,
 Three times is enemy action..."

But my favourite logician expresses it best of all in his epic poem, "The
hunting of the Snark":

 "Just the place for a Snark!" the Bellman cried,
		As he landed his crew with care;
	Supporting each man on the top of the tide
		By a finger entwined in his hair.

	"Just the place for a Snark! I have said it twice:
		That alone should encourage the crew.
	Just the place for a Snark! I have said it thrice:
		What I tell you three times is true."

"What I tell you THREE times, is TRUE..."

It always amused me that three identical computers are used on Boeing 747s.
One is flyng the plane, one is watching the one that's flying the plane, and
the third is a backup in case either of the other two fail  <G> (I've been
to the cockpit in flight and confirmed this for myself (back in the days
when they would LET you go to the cockpit... I wouldn't recommend even
ASKING to do so nowadays...<G>)).

Pete.

third is tiebreaker. Number four is doing routine administrative tasks. The
fifth is backup.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-04-29T10:06:17-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8m0sp$iol$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <b8jhig$do3$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b8ji7t$a6u$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eadaa65_2@127.0.0.1>`

```
In article <3eadaa65_2@127.0.0.1>,
Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b8ji7t$a6u$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>could do this one step at a time?

In this case they appear to be identical, Mr Dashwood... consider a case 
like:

A: (assertion)

B: (disproof of assertion)

A: 'I should have said (second assertion).'

Now I might agree with you, Mr Dashwood, that A's second assertion 
'represents(s) some progress' if it includes an explicit acknowledgement 
of the disproof of A's first statement, such as:

A: 'I should have said (second assertion); (assertion) is wrong.'

>
>"Blessed are the Merciful, for they shall obtain Mercy..."

Ahhhhhhh... in this context I am of very mixed feelings about such things, 
Mr Dashwood.  Consider the contradiction inherent between the two 
following statements:

'When offering criticisms or corrections you should always leave the 
object of them A Way Out; people tend to fight harder and less rationally 
when they feel 'cornered'.'

... and ...

'To permit Sloppy Work is to encourage Sloppy Work.'

I have never felt that I have been able to establish a Proper Balance 
between these two... good thing I've avoided Positions of Management, eh?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b8napr$31v$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <3eaf2140_2@127.0.0.1>,

> >> In article <3eadaa65_2@127.0.0.1>,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I agree, having seen this on several occasions. I have seen managers I liked
and respected, displaced by more politically astute colleagues who couldn't
manage their way out of a wet paper bag.

But it cuts both ways...I have also seen bad managers moved sideways or
replaced when staff turnover and production in their managed areas became
unacceptable... It would be nice if the problems could be recognised BEFORE
they become this serious, but senior management simply don't have the time
to be monitoring the personal performance of every middle manager.

Personally, I remain optimistic. But that's just me...<G>

One of the nice things about being a contractor is that you are not there to
make a career with the company and so are not threatening any of the
incumbents (although it can be a problem (as I have found to my cost) if you
show them up, even unintentionally...<G>).

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-04-30T10:24:35-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8omb3$9bb$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eaf95fa_1@127.0.0.1> <b8oh7h$85v$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eafcc3c_2@127.0.0.1>`

```
In article <3eafcc3c_2@127.0.0.1>,
Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b8oh7h$85v$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>it and I'm sure you don't either...<G>

One of my refrains, Mr Dashwood, is 'Hey... if it were easy then 
*everyone* would be doing it.'

>
>The good bit is when their initial hostility gets turned around as they
>realise you might actually be able to add value to the place...or even
>improve their lot...

I have seen that happen, true, and it has been a Pleasant Thing... but 
also I have seen things devolve into 'Hey, give it to the contractor, he's 
shown that he can grow roses no matter *what* kind of fertiliser you dump 
in his lap!'

>
>When I left the last place they had a party for me (not usual for
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>them today.

This is happening to me, now, and things are getting a bit... delicate 
with it.  I made it clear that I'd be available to deal with loose ends 
and unexpected minor messes... but I was not to be used as a free resource 
or a reference-book.  So far, so good.

But first... a bit of history.  I started at this client's site back in 
November, 2000, in a different department for a 'six-to-nine-month 
contract'.  In mid-July of '02 I was given three weeks' notice... not a 
bad run, I got a party at the end (oh boy, greasy pizza!) and I kept in 
touch.  August usually isn't a good month for finding work - too many 
folks on vacation - so I coasted, took a few courses (I'm now one exam 
away from being a certified Oracle DBA) and otherwise enjoyed time on the 
beach.

Second week in September came and I started sending off papers... and I 
get a call from a different manager at the old client, am I available?  
Yeah, sure, no problem, I come back in early October.

(My agency screwed me out of a referral-fee for referring myself to 
them... but that's another story.)

Anyhow, before end-of-project, before my stuff was even moved into Prod, I 
was given three-and-a-half-days' notice.  Not the most... pleasant of 
situations but hey, I'm what they call A Professional, I can deal with it 
and I do so.

That was about three weeks back, the beginning of April... last Thursday I 
got a call from the pimp, am I still available to go back to that client 
for a month's work?  I said this would not be a problem... but my rate 
would be US$n/hr.

The pimp squawked, of course... the *last* thing a pimp wants to do is to 
go to the client and say 'This will cost more money'.  She asked me why 
and I said I was disturbed by the precedents of 'bring me on, fire me, 
bring me back on again, fire me' and that these circumstances effectively 
decreased my billing rate (when annualised).

The pimp said 'this sounded reasonable'... and I've heard nothing back 
yet.

>
>My point is that it isn't always hostile and it certainly doesn't HAVE to
>be.

Of course, Mr Dashwood... nothing is always anything, including this 
statement!

DD
```

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:

…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It is far more serious than 'senior management not having the time'. Reflect on your
own experiences. This is not solely related to EDP. All too often senior management,
(even at director level), choose an unsuitable candidate. I am aware of one instance
where outside consultants recommended against one candidate but the director still
chose him. Here's the tricky part - it is never the man that chose the 'unworthy' who
eventually fires him !

So knowing of that reality - I'm not optimistic like you are. Human nature being what

Jimmy

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-27T21:07:21+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eab9ddb_1@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea88130.21326254@news.optonline.net> <b8ab71$1dv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eaaa1e8.160795747@news.optonline.net> <b8eseu$2fv$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eab4d82.204732010@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3eab4d82.204732010@news.optonline.net...
> docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> No no no. When I program, I'm not talking to the machine, I'm talking to
the
> next programmer who must understand my code.

That has certainly been the role of COBOL over the years. It's main strength
has been it's "English like" nature which meant it could communicate to the
next programmer (or bean counter) as well as to the machine.

However, if you think about this a bit it fails on several counts...

1. ANY language (spoken or programming) communicates to people who know that
language.
2. It is no longer desirable (or, in some cases, even possible...) to
maintain the corporate Network with a single language. Certainly it is
possible to obtain multi-lingual (in the computer sense) programmers, but
they cost more, and more and more companies are simply cutting their losses
and outsourcing their IT.
3. There is only a requirement for "the next programmer to understand my
code" if that code is likely to need maintenance. The use of components and
re-usable objects (often purchased from third parties) renders this
requirement obsolete for all but the last bastions of Fortress COBOL. The
world is changing. COBOL sites are not... Logical conclusion; COBOL will
die.

(Actually, I'm becoming hopeful that it will survive for things like Batch
processing which we are still going to need for some time yet, and I see
building business components in COBOL as a very viable use for the Language,
but the traditional use of COBOL as the "one language wonder" has a very
limited life span. I wrote a few years ago that I expect to see it gone by
2015. I still hold that view.)

Pete.

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>next programmer (or bean counter) as well as to the machine.

COBOL's English-like grammar is a convenience to the programmer, but it is truly
a programming language. A non-programmer cannot really understand it; he can
only read at a superficial level, recognizing words here and there. Have you
ever watched a movie in a foreign language you understand slightly but aren't
fluent in? That's what it's like for an accountant trying to read COBOL. 

>However, if you think about this a bit it fails on several counts...
>
>1. ANY language (spoken or programming) communicates to people who know that
>language.

I just agreed. 

>2. It is no longer desirable (or, in some cases, even possible...) to
>maintain the corporate Network with a single language. Certainly it is
>possible to obtain multi-lingual (in the computer sense) programmers, but
>they cost more, and more and more companies are simply cutting their losses
>and outsourcing their IT.

Outsourcing doesn't remove people from the loop. They outsource to the companies
I work for, ultimately to me. 

>3. There is only a requirement for "the next programmer to understand my
>code" if that code is likely to need maintenance. The use of components and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>die.

Do you realize what you're saying in this COBOL forum? You have more guts than
me. <g>

What language will these reusable objects be written in? How about COBOL? It
seems to me that OO is just adding one level of isolation to the architecture. I
would liken it to going from assembly language to HLL. 

I'm not criticizing nor minimalizing OO. I love OO, although for different
reasons. If nothing else, it portends to raise the quality of code by taking it
out of the hands of corporate IT departments, which have a poor track record
over the last 40 years. 

>(Actually, I'm becoming hopeful that it will survive for things like Batch
>processing which we are still going to need for some time yet, and I see
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>2015. I still hold that view.)

We've been reading similar predictions for more than 20 years. I recall hearing
visionary Ed Yourdon predict 1990 in the late '70s. The deadline always seems to
be 10-15 years out. Yourdon thought C and Unix would replace COBOL and
mainframes. Well, there was _some_ validity in his prediction.
```

can

I don't think so. However, I would agree that it depends on the COBOL.


> >However, if you think about this a bit it fails on several counts...
> >
> >1. ANY language (spoken or programming) communicates to people who know
that
> >language.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >they cost more, and more and more companies are simply cutting their
losses
> >and outsourcing their IT.
>
> Outsourcing doesn't remove people from the loop. They outsource to the
companies
> I work for, ultimately to me.
>

And these companies are becoming fewer and fewer as the major players take
over the market. No wonder there is less and less scope for COBOL
programmers within industry.

> >3. There is only a requirement for "the next programmer to understand my
> >code" if that code is likely to need maintenance. The use of components
and
> >re-usable objects (often purchased from third parties) renders this
> >requirement obsolete for all but the last bastions of Fortress COBOL. The
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Do you realize what you're saying in this COBOL forum? You have more guts
than
> me. <g>
>

It requires no particular courage to express an unpopular opinion, provided
it is backed up with the evidence as to how you reached it. Also, regulars
here know that I am committed to the COBOL language and it is very dear to
my heart.

However, I would be stupid if I let my love for the language blind me to
what is happening in the market place. I have genuine concerns about the
careers of youngsters trying to get into commercial computer programming and
maybe thinking that COBOL will do the trick. It did for us, but that was
thirty years ago... There IS still a place for COBOL but it is not
altogether clear what that place is at the moment. It is almost certain that
it will NOT be the place it has traditionally held.

Whether I am courageous or not is debatable; whether I am stupid or not, is
not...<G>


> What language will these reusable objects be written in? How about COBOL?
It
> seems to me that OO is just adding one level of isolation to the
architecture. I
> would liken it to going from assembly language to HLL.
>

My opinion about using the best tools for the job (including COBOL) is a
matter of record.  The objects will be (and are being) written in the
language that best suits the purpose for which they are intended. At the
moment I am the only person I know who regularly writes components in COBOL
(if anyone reading this is doing this too, please drop me a note; it gets
lonely out here on the frontier...<G>). I am doing everything I can to
persuade others to do the same. Some of the regular posters here are using
OO COBOL and doing a good job of it. I see components as being "the next
step" on from this.

> I'm not criticizing nor minimalizing OO. I love OO, although for different
> reasons. If nothing else, it portends to raise the quality of code by
taking it
> out of the hands of corporate IT departments, which have a poor track
record
> over the last 40 years.
>
> >(Actually, I'm becoming hopeful that it will survive for things like
Batch
> >processing which we are still going to need for some time yet, and I see
> >building business components in COBOL as a very viable use for the
Language,
> >but the traditional use of COBOL as the "one language wonder" has a very
> >limited life span. I wrote a few years ago that I expect to see it gone
by
> >2015. I still hold that view.)
>
> We've been reading similar predictions for more than 20 years.

Well that dismisses mine then...<G>

The "we've heard it all before" reaction is fairly typical and what I have
come to expect. It is exactly this attitude that is causing a false
complacency amongst some COBOL people. And yet we see more and more COBOL
jobs disappearing and people being scrapped. I warned of this 7 years ago
and suggested what could be done about it.  I have consistently during that
time advocated the expansion of skill sets in just about everything I have
written. Some people took that advice and are working today.

I really don't care whether you agree with my position or not. (at the end
of the day, it is not me who suffers...) I simply call them like I see them.

Pete.

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message

>> Outsourcing doesn't remove people from the loop. They outsource to the
>companies
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>over the market. 

I've worked for the major players -- EDS, Accenture, Keane -- as well as minor
players. The difference is majors roll in their own management team and
methodology whereas small players are just body shops. The number of jobs
doesn't change, just the methodology.

Company management thinks they're saving money by going with small players.
Wrong. Bill rate is about the same -- $100-150/hour either way.

>No wonder there is less and less scope for COBOL
>programmers within industry.

While that might be true, I don't think it should be attributed to sourcing.
It's primarily a function of CS-grads' language preference. They've been taught
to hate COBOL --  too verbose, dinosaur language, etc. 

I worked for a very bright and ambitious manager, three years out of a
prestigious college, who didn't even know COBOL was a language. I said something
like 'it's written in COBOL' to which he responded, "No it's not. It's written
in Batch." He thought COBOL was a scripting language, and he was directly
supervising COBOL programmers,

All new development in that shop was C++. COBOL was maintenance only. 

At another shop, the manager said, "If we have to make extensive changes, we'll
just rewrite the whole program in C. It's easier."

>The objects will be (and are being) written in the
>language that best suits the purpose for which they are intended. 

They'll be written in the language de jour, currently C#, Java and maybe Python.
Next year, something else. Selection isn't based on suitability for the job,
it's based on fashion. 

>At the
>moment I am the only person I know who regularly writes components in COBOL
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>step" on from this.

I'm tempted to join you.
```
