###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu and ISO 2002 (was: special names

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-26T11:23:39+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea9d1a3_2@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<b8anq9$eos$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be> <b8aq0u$vpa$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b8at79$gi1$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be> <3EA973FA.D29D690B@shaw.ca> <b8c0cp$vt$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b8c0cp$vt$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net...
>
> For example,

Well, Gee, Bill, let's have a look at how deprived us Fujitsu users
are...<G>

(The following are the views of ONE user...me.)

>
>  - Local-Storage and Recursion (for non-OO programs)

Wouldn't use recursion outside OO.

>  - Table sort

Never a problem. Do it with a perform using bubble or tournament. If it's a
large table, I load the entries in sorted order with binary chop against
initial high values. I can't actually remember the last time I sorted an
internal table... I think it was on the mainframe so it must have been at
least 20 years ago...

>  - Unicode / ISO 10646 national language support

Callable routines provide for this on the extremely rare occasions when it
is needed (usually in interface to Java)

>  - Locale support

Are we talking clocks and currency here? Works OK on my system. For example,
I just reset my system to NZ from UK. Now all the COBOL modules produce $
instead of � signs... (I note in passing that ASP has just been enhanced to
provide locale support by the LCID property of the Response Object. The
Session Object always supported it but the new facility extends it. If I
needed to do this from COBOL I'd use the API, but it is far more likely I'd
simply do it from ASP...)

>  - Standard arithmetic

Have no idea what you're talking about. Fujitsu RTS performs arithmetic (and
conversion) flawlessly.

>  - Common Exception handling (outside OO)

Fair enough. If I wanted this I'd use Declaratives. Most of what I'm doing
is OO anyway so, no problem...

>  - Validate statement

Never wanted/needed it and consider it is a waste of time even including it
in the standard. It does nothing I can't/don't do already. Nowadays, I use
components to validate input anyway, so, at least as far as I'm concerned,
it has been overtaken by events before it was ever implemented.

>  - user-defined functions

Agree this could be useful, but the same functionality is obtainable by
CALL, PERFORM, nested programs, or components. Certainly not a show-stopper
for me.

>  - SUPPORT for GoBack statement

No idea what you are talking about. Fujitsu supports GOBACK exactly as EXIT
PROGRAM. (Well, it does in MY code <G>. Maybe you are talking about how the
standard says it SHOULD work. I am quite happy with the way it DOES work.)

>  - Most of the new 2002 intrinsic functions

Fujitsu supports intrinsic functions and I'm sure they'll implement the new
ones just as quickly as anybody else. Meantime I have lost zero sleep over
it...

>  - 2002 Standard "split-key" syntax

Fujitsu supports split keys and always has done. It may not accord with the
new STANDARD but it does everything I want.

I can't remember the last time I used ISAM files for development, never mind
split key ISAM... It's like living in a time warp when you talk standards...

>  - Stop Run / Exit Program WITH STATUS

RETURNING does it for me... It also sets the special register whose name
escapes me at the moment but if I ever wanted to use it I could look it up
<G>.

>
> etc, Etc, ETC ...
>

This is a rhetorical device that simply means: "I can't think of anything
else to say but there has to be more...(I think)"

> If you look at their .NET product, it doesn't even include full support
for
> ANSI '85 syntax (with such things as file SORT's and printer output)
>

Won't comment on .NET offering as I have never used it and can't afford it.
I agree that ANSI 85 standard has proven itself to be useful.

> I am *not* saying whether Fujitsu's current products do or do not met
THEIR
> customer needs

As a long time customer I can say they serve MY needs very well. That's why
I am a long term customer. That's why I no longer use Micro Focus products
(they did NOT serve my needs very well...To be fair, MF today is not the
company it was at the time of VISOC and I'm sure the current offerings are
very good; but for me, the damage was done...).


> - I simply find it VERY disturbing when people think that
> their current products are particularly "close" to the 2002 Standard
> *required* features.
>

Well that's fair comment, Bill.

It does presuppose that one cares... (I don't...).

It comes down to the fundamental argument of whether a vendor should support
the Standards Committee or the perceived needs of the Market Place.

Ideally, we would have a responsive Standards Authority who could actually
produce something within a reasonable time frame, and would take into
account the requirements of the Market Place by liaising with vendors and
soliciting feedback from users in a simple accessible way (maybe via the
Internet). That didn't happen (despite protestations that it did) so vendors
HAD to meet the market need.

Do you think I'm going to beat up Fujitsu for providing me with OO, three
years before the Standards people got their act together?  (It STILL isn't
resolved...). No way. On the contrary, if they haven't conformed totally to
the proposed standard I'll cut them some slack and say: "Who needs it?". I
can use what they've provided and I couldn't care less whether it conforms
to some "Standard" that has no credibility, is still being debated, and is
largely irrelevant.

There might have been a case 20 years ago when source code was king and we
couldn't transport anything to another platform unless it conformed. That is
no longer true (at least for me it isn't...I write componentized systems and
the components can run anywhere and be written in whatever language is
appropriate for the function they are required to perform...). The COBOL
standard has demonstrated that it is pointless in today's world by us
waitiing 17 years for it and then finding it doesn't give us anything we
really need. The last effective standard was 85. If the vendors had done
this, we wouldn't have a COBOL language today.

I won't be discarding Fujitsu (standard or non-standard) any time soon.

Pete.
```

```
contains the header plus some "shop standard" configuration section
entries.)

MCM
```

```
Hello,

I'm a student and I'm having trouble compling this code. Can somebody help
me please! When I try to compile I get this error message:

Micro Focus NetExpress V3
Version 3.0.14 Copyright (C) 1984-1999 Micro Focus Ltd.
URN AXCGG/AA0/00000
* Micro Focus NetExpress UE          V3.0 revision 255 Compiler
* Copyright (C) 1984-1999 Micro Focus Ltd.     URN AXCGG/AA0/00000
   151         COMPUTE BUS-MJR = TOTAL-SCL * 100 /SCL-CODE

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. A-7-DJ.

      ***************************************************

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.

           SELECT STUDENT-FILE  ASSIGN TO 'C0805.DAT'

               ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

           05  STU-NAME                        PIC X(21).

               88 FRESHMAN                     VALUE '1'.
               88 SOPHOMORE                    VALUE '2'.
               88 JUNIOR                       VALUE '3'.
               88 SENIOR                       VALUE '4'.
           05  SCL-CODE                        PIC X.
               88 BUSINESS                     VALUE '1'.
               88 LIBERALARTS                  VALUE '2'.
               88 ENGINEERING                  VALUE '3'.
           05  GPA                             PIC 9(3)V99.
           05  CRD-EARNED                      PIC 9(3).

       FD  OUTPUT-FILE
           RECORD CONTAINS 80 CHARACTERS.
       01    OUT-SALARY-REC                    PIC X(80).
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  LINE-CT                             PIC 99     VALUE 0.
       01  ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS              PIC XXX    VALUE 'YES'.

       01  WS-DATE.
           05 WS-YEAR                          PIC 9999.
           05 WS-MONTH                         PIC 99.
           05 WS-DAY                           PIC 99.
       01  DETAIL-LINE.
           05                                  PIC X(4)   VALUE SPACES.
           05                                  PIC X(5)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  LOWR-THAN-TWO                   PIC 9(3)V99.
           05                                  PIC X(2)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  BTN-TWO-AND-THREE               PIC 9(3)V99.
           05                                  PIC X(2)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  GRT-THEN-THREE                  PIC 9(3)V99.
           05                                  PIC X(2)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  BUS-MJR                         PIC ZZZV99.
           05                                  PIC X(2)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  LIB-ARTS-MJR                    PIC ZZZV99.
           05                                  PIC X(2)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  ENG-MJR                         PIC ZZZV99.
           05                                  PIC X(2)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  MORE-THAN-HUND                  PIC 9(3)V99.
           05                                  PIC X(2)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  FRHMEN                          PIC 9(3)V99.
           05                                  PIC X(2)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  SPHMORS                         PIC 9(3)V99.
           05                                  PIC X(2)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  JUNRS                           PIC 9(3)V99.
           05                                  PIC X(2)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  SENRS                           PIC 9(2)V99.
           05                                  PIC X(2)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  GPA-SUB-TOTAL                   PIC 9(3)V99.
           05                                  PIC X(2)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  TOTAL-SCL                       PIC 9(3)V99.

       01  HDR-1.
           05                                  PIC X(15)  VALUE SPACES.
           05                                  PIC X(26)
                 VALUE 'PRINT SUMMARY OF DATA %'.
           05                                  PIC X(22)  VALUE SPACES.
           05  DATE-OUT.
               10 MONTH-OUT                    PIC XX.
               10                              PIC X      VALUE '/'.
               10 DAY-OUT                      PIC XX.
               10                              PIC X      VALUE '/'.
               10 YEAR-OUT                     PIC XXXX.
           05                                  PIC X(11).
           05                                  PIC X(5)   VALUE 'PAGE'.
           05  PAGE-NO                         PIC 99     VALUE ZERO.
       01  HDR-2.
           05                                  PIC X(2)   VALUE SPACES.
           05                                  PIC X(25)
                VALUE 'GPA<2%  GPA>2%&<3  GPA>3%'.
           05                                  PIC X(46)
                VALUE 'GPA>3% FOR BUSSINES  LIBERAL ARTS  ENGINEERING'.
       01  HDR-3.
           05                                  PIC X(2)   VALUE SPACES.
           05                                  PIC X(25).
           05                                  PIC X(23)
                VALUE '>100 CREDITS AND GPS<2%'.
           05                                  PIC X(46)
                VALUE 'GPA>3%  FRESHMAN  SOPHOMORES  JUNIORS  SENIORS'.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
      ****************************************************
      *   Main module controls overall program logic     *
      ****************************************************
       100-MAIN-MODULE.
           OPEN INPUT  STUDENT-FILE
                OUTPUT OUTPUT-FILE

           PERFORM UNTIL ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS = 'NO '
               READ STUDENT-FILE
                   AT END
                      MOVE 'NO ' TO ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS
                   NOT AT END
                      PERFORM 200-GPA-RTN
               END-READ
           END-PERFORM
           CLOSE STUDENT-FILE
                 OUTPUT-FILE
           STOP RUN.
      ****************************************************
      *    Performed from 100-MAIN-MODULE, this routine  *
      ****************************************************

       200-GPA-RTN.
      ****************************************************
      * GPA'S % OF <2, >2 AND <3 AND >3 DECISION         *
      ****************************************************
           IF  GPA < 2
           THEN
               COMPUTE LOWR-THAN-TWO = GPA-SUB-TOTAL * 100 / GPA
           END-IF
           IF  GPA > 2  AND < 3
           THEN
               COMPUTE BTN-TWO-AND-THREE = GPA-SUB-TOTAL * 100 / GPA
           END-IF
           IF  GPA > 3
           THEN
               COMPUTE GRT-THEN-THREE= GPA-SUB-TOTAL * 100 / GPA
           END-IF
      ******************************************************
      *  % OF STUDENTS WITH GPA'S GREATER THAN 3.0 IN      *
      *  BUS, LIB ARTS, ENG.                               *
      ******************************************************
           MOVE GRT-THEN-THREE TO TOTAL-SCL.

           IF SCL-CODE = '1'
           THEN
               COMPUTE BUS-MJR = TOTAL-SCL * 100 /SCL-CODE
           END-IF
           IF SCL-CODE = '2'
           THEN
               COMPUTE LIB-ARTS-MJR = TOTAL-SCL * 100 /SCL-CODE
           END-IF
           IF SCL-CODE = '3'
           THEN
                COMPUTE ENG-MJR = TOTAL-SCL * 100 /SCL-CODE
           END-IF

      ********************************************************
      *   STUDENTS WITH MORE THAN 100 CREDITS AND HAVE A GPA *
      *   OF LESS THAN 2.0%                                  *
      ********************************************************
           IF GPA > 2 AND CRD-EARNED < 100
           THEN
               COMPUTE MORE-THAN-HUND = GPA-SUB-TOTAL * 100 / GPA
           END-IF
      ********************************************************
      *    % OF STUDENTS WITH GPA GREATER THAN 3.0 WHO ARE   *
      *    FRESHMAN, SOPHOMORES, JUNNIORS, AND SENIORS       *
      ********************************************************
           IF  GPA < 3 AND CLS-CODE= '1'
           THEN
               COMPUTE FRHMEN = GPA-SUB-TOTAL * 100 / GPA
           END-IF

           IF  GPA < 3 AND CLS-CODE = '2'
           THEN
               COMPUTE SPHMORS = GPA-SUB-TOTAL * 100 / GPA
           END-IF

           IF  GPA < 3 AND CLS-CODE= '3'
           THEN
               COMPUTE JUNRS = GPA-SUB-TOTAL * 100 / GPA
           END-IF

           IF  GPA < 3 AND CLS-CODE = '4'
           THEN
               COMPUTE SENRS = GPA-SUB-TOTAL * 100 / GPA
           END-IF
           WRITE OUT-SALARY-REC.


Here is the data file( 'C0805.DAT') :
```

#### ↳ Re: Help!

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-04-25T18:41:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EA97D8B.BE15DB19@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<mEeqa.9128$h%2.967139@read1.cgocable.net>`

```


Dan J wrote:

> Hello,
>
> I'm a student and I'm having trouble compling this code. Can somebody help
> me please! When I try to compile I get this error message:
>

You are on the right track but you can't COMPUTE with Pic x. Revamp to read as
follows :-

           05  CLS-CODE                        pic 9. *****.
               88 FRESHMAN                     VALUE '1'.
               88 SOPHOMORE                    VALUE '2'.
               88 JUNIOR                       VALUE '3'.
               88 SENIOR                       VALUE '4'.
           05  SCL-CODE                        pic 9. *****.
               88 BUSINESS                     VALUE '1'.
               88 LIBERALARTS                  VALUE '2'.
               88 ENGINEERING                  VALUE '3'.

Now remove the quotes from around the above 88's, e.g.

88 Business         Value 1.

Try using an EVALUATE to replace following - where you can use the Condition
names :-
      ******************************************************
      *  % OF STUDENTS WITH GPA'S GREATER THAN 3.0 IN      *
      *  BUS, LIB ARTS, ENG.                               *
      ******************************************************
           MOVE GRT-THEN-THREE TO TOTAL-SCL.

           IF SCL-CODE = '1'
           THEN
               COMPUTE BUS-MJR = TOTAL-SCL * 100 /SCL-CODE
           END-IF
           IF SCL-CODE = '2'
           THEN
               COMPUTE LIB-ARTS-MJR = TOTAL-SCL * 100 /SCL-CODE
           END-IF
           IF SCL-CODE = '3'
           THEN
                COMPUTE ENG-MJR = TOTAL-SCL * 100 /SCL-CODE
           END-IF

      ********************************************************

Evaluate true
    when BUSINESS
               COMPUTE BUS-MJR = TOTAL-SCL * 100 /SCL-CODE
    when LIBERALARTS
               COMPUTE LIB-ARTS-MJR = TOTAL-SCL * 100 /SCL-CODE
    when ENGINEERING
                COMPUTE ENG-MJR = TOTAL-SCL * 100 /SCL-CODE
    when other +++ I've got an error !!! Called XP(eXtreme Programming) - you
                don't need this - but just think about it for the future.
End-evaluate

Really personal preference, but this is an alternative to what you have :-

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  LINE-CT                             PIC 99     VALUE 0.
       01  ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS              PIC XXX    VALUE 'YES'.
           PERFORM UNTIL ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS = 'NO '
               READ STUDENT-FILE
                   AT END
                      MOVE 'NO ' TO ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS
                   NOT AT END
                      PERFORM 200-GPA-RTN
               END-READ
           END-PERFORM

*******
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  LINE-CT                             PIC 99     VALUE 0.
        01 FileFlag                                pic 99.
            88 MoreRecords                   value 0.
            88 NoMoreRecords               value 1.

            set MoreRecords to true
           PERFORM UNTIL NoMoreRecords
               READ STUDENT-FILE
                   AT END
                      set NoMoreRecords to true
                  NOT AT END
                      PERFORM 200-GPA-RTN
               END-READ
           END-PERFORM

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Help!

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-25T13:44:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8bvn5$ess$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<mEeqa.9128$h%2.967139@read1.cgocable.net>`

```
Compute requires numeric sending items

Data items that are defined as numeric require numeric literals for VALUE
clauses - or value clauses of associated condition-names.

Believe your compiler messages - and when all error messages are fixed -
then your program should compile cleanly.
```

```
They're always written in upper case. They always have a type mismatch between
```

#### ↳ Re: Help!

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-04-26T12:45:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0304261145.5e7ecda4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<mEeqa.9128$h%2.967139@read1.cgocable.net>`

```
"Dan J" <dali@cogeco.ca> wrote 

> Other than that will this work properly?

No. Not at all.
 
>            05  CLS-CODE                        PIC X.
>                88 FRESHMAN                     VALUE '1'.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>                88 ENGINEERING                  VALUE '3'.

>       ******************************************************
>       *  % OF STUDENTS WITH GPA'S GREATER THAN 3.0 IN      *
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>                COMPUTE BUS-MJR = TOTAL-SCL * 100 /SCL-CODE

Why is 'SCL-CODE' part of this calculation ?  Why are you dividing by
'BUSINESS'?

If you want the % of students then this is upside down anyway.  What
you may want to do is:

         Make Scl-Code numeric (PIC 9) and the 88 VALUES numeric.

         Create a table Student-Count.

         For each student, when it is read:
           IF GPA > 3
              ADD 1    TO Student-Count(Scl-Code)
              ADD 1    TO Total-Scl

         Then _after_ all reads, after the PERFORM that does the READ,
         for each Scl-Code (PERFORM):
  
         COMPUTE Bus-Mjr = Student-count(SCL-Code) * 100 / Total-Scl

         will give you the % of students in each 'Scl-Code'.
                 

What I don't know is whether the percentage is to be of the total
number of students (as here) or of the students in each group.
```

---

```
In article <20030425141115.02826.00000104@mb-m13.aol.com>,
GeorgeL704 <georgel704@aol.com> wrote:
>I have been out of work for 17 months. No one seems to need Cobol/CICS/OS 390
>mainframe programmers anymore. I have 20 years experience. 
>Respond If you have any leads. Thank you

As my Sainted Paternal Grandfather - may he sleep with the angels! - used 
to say, before he died... come to think of it he didn't say much that I 
heard afterwards... 'If they ain't strikin' at what you're tossin' then it 
might be time to change your bait.'

17 months is long enough to be unemployed for five months and still have a 
year to, say, get certified as an Oracle SQL developer (or 'coder').

Granted that a newbie Oracle developer doesn't make as much as a 20-year
COBOL jockey might... but an employed Oracle developer usually makes more
than an unemployed COBOL jockey does.

Don't want to develop Oracle?  Fine... it is a wonderful world out there 
with new things to learn in it.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: I am an unemployed Cobol pgmr.

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-26T03:31:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea9d18f.107449787@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<20030425141115.02826.00000104@mb-m13.aol.com>`

```
georgel704@aol.com (GeorgeL704) wrote:

>I have been out of work for 17 months. No one seems to need Cobol/CICS/OS 390
>mainframe programmers anymore. I have 20 years experience. 
>Respond If you have any leads. Thank you

You should be looking at dice.com every day, and half a dozen others at least
once a week. Jobs for mainframe Cobol/CICS/DB2 programmers appear every day on
Web sites. 

You should be available to work anywhere in the US. If you're rooted to one city
because of home/family/inertia, seek perm rather than contract. In that case you
need to 'network' with decision makers. Join a social club so you can rub elbows
with them. 

Sending resumes via mail is the worst possible approach. You become one of
thousands.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: I am an unemployed Cobol pgmr.

```
If it`s anything like in the UK then those jobs probably don`t exist. Here
the IT industry is in a right state but some IT job websites are claiming
recently to be getting a record number of vacancy advertisements but if you
speak to those in the know, it simply isn`t true, there are few jobs around
and many people looking for work. The websites aren`t to blame, it`s the
agencies who advertise on them. 2+ years of unemployment even for the most
highly trained in various languages is becoming common place.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I am an unemployed Cobol pgmr.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-27T11:50:23+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eab1b4f_2@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<20030425141115.02826.00000104@mb-m13.aol.com> <3ea9d18f.107449787@news.optonline.net> <b8dd1e$48n$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <3eaad61e.174163599@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3eaad61e.174163599@news.optonline.net...
> "GeeGee" <gege@NOSPAM.com> wrote:
>
>
> Realize what that does to our average salary. Suppose you work for a year
and
> make $80K, then spend two years seeking the next adventure. Your average
income
> is only $27K per year. You could earn twice that in nearly any other
occupation.
>
For the past 27 years I have worked as a Freelance in Europe. (UK, Spain,
and Germany).

I worked when I wanted and stopped when I didn't.

This gave me a brilliant lifestyle and I have no regrets whatsoever about
it.

In all that time I was never unemployed for more than 10 days when I didn't
want to be.

Until last year.

The last contract took 3 months to find (to be fair, I turned down several
possibilities because they didn't appeal) but, nevertheless, I was starting
to get decidedly anxious by the time the "right" job came along.

I am convinced that the "boom" is over (well...Duhhhh <G>) and we will never
make the money we made in the 80s again.

However, there are many ways to skin a cat and writing COBOL is not the only
satisfying way to make a living.

Certainly the skills and business knowledge acquired from being a long term
COBOL programmer can be leveraged into other areas. These days I find there
is a lot more money in management than there is in programming and, as a
result, I haven't made a living from COBOL for close on 15 years now.

Others who are younger may find it advantageous to acquire more technical
skills. It is like ANY business; find out what is needed and wanted, then
provide it.

(Now where did I leave my macrame...<G>?)

Pete.


> >"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message

Cobol/CICS/OS
> >390
> >> >mainframe programmers anymore. I have 20 years experience.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> need to 'network' with decision makers. Join a social club so you can
rub
> >elbows
> >> with them.
> >>
> >> Sending resumes via mail is the worst possible approach. You become one
of
> >> thousands.
> >>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>to get decidedly anxious by the time the "right" job came along.

Same here. Never more than a month until the last time, which took 5.5 months. I
was anxious because I was almost out of money. 

I used to say "My security is in the vibrancy of the marketplace rather than the
goodwill of one employer." Well, the market has stopped vibrating. 

>I am convinced that the "boom" is over (well...Duhhhh <G>) and we will never
>make the money we made in the 80s again.

Bill rates are as high as ever -- $100-150/hr. The problem is our cut keeps
going down while the contracting company's cut goes up. I know for a fact I'm
currently making less than 50% of my bill rate. 

My contracting company sent an email with the names of their contractors listed
in CC. Nine out of ten were Indian names. I'm guessing they work for 1/3 the
bill rate. 

>However, there are many ways to skin a cat and writing COBOL is not the only
>satisfying way to make a living.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>result, I haven't made a living from COBOL for close on 15 years now.

I went the opposite way. I started in management and moved to programming.
There's too much politics in management, and I'm not very good at it.
```

```
LARGE companies have 6 digits. I worked on the Sears US payroll system, which
paid 400,000 active employees. Wal-Mart has well over 1,000,000 in the US.

A supermarket company where I worked had 20,000 employees, annual sales $2B. We
did all computer work with an IBM 4361 (very small mainframe), five programmers
and me. Is that large?  I don't think so.

One of our competitors, with similar employee count and sales, had several big
mainframes, 80 programmers and analysts. They thought they were a large company.
```

```
campuses
> and is a subset of The State of Colorado.

This same contract clause has been tested in court in the UK and found to be
a "restrictive practice". (You cannot stop someone from making a living.)
Nevertheless, agencies still include it in their standard contracts and
threaten all kinds of Armageddon if the contractor violates it.

Pete.
```

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-30T12:12:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<p4Pra.5751$%_3.3926704@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<20030425141115.02826.00000104@mb-m13.aol.com> <3ea9d18f.107449787@news.optonline.net> <b8dd1e$48n$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <3eaad61e.174163599@news.optonline.net> <3eab1b4f_2@127.0.0.1> <3eabc2f5.234803425@news.optonline.net> <b8jgu6$dg6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eaea6ad.424199912@news.optonline.net> <b8mkg9$82p$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eaf1f7a$1_2@127.0.0.1>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3eaf1f7a$1_2@127.0.0.1...
>
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> This same contract clause has been tested in court in the UK and found to
be
> a "restrictive practice". (You cannot stop someone from making a living.)
> Nevertheless, agencies still include it in their standard contracts...

I really do not mind those clauses as long as it's "this *particular*
customer" and the time restriction is reasonable. It seems fair that a
'contracting service company'  not be unwillingly made into a "recruiting
company offering contract-to-hire" personnel (Contact-to-hire is very
popular here). Costs too much to locate people and be responsible for their
work.

Most of the firms in this part of the US use one year as a bolierplate
restricted period where you may not be employed or directly engaged by the
customer . That's not really fair for someone like me, who can be in and out
in two weeks. What is fair, I think, is "length of contract, minimum 90
days, maximum one year."  YMMV.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I am an unemployed Cobol pgmr.

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-30T17:20:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eafe001.504423793@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<20030425141115.02826.00000104@mb-m13.aol.com> <3ea9d18f.107449787@news.optonline.net> <b8dd1e$48n$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <3eaad61e.174163599@news.optonline.net> <3eab1b4f_2@127.0.0.1> <3eabc2f5.234803425@news.optonline.net> <b8jgu6$dg6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eaea6ad.424199912@news.optonline.net> <b8mkg9$82p$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eaf1f7a$1_2@127.0.0.1>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
>news:b8mkg9$82p$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>threaten all kinds of Armageddon if the contractor violates it.

In the US, non-compete agreements have been invalidated on similar grounds.

Agencies get around the 'indentured servitude' situation discussed above by
attaching a financial penalty rather than saying the contractor cannot work. The
penalty is the amount he agency would have made if the contractor had stayed on
board. Courts think this is ok. The issue is damages rather than restriction. 

All Big Five companies do something similer with training. They bring workers
from other countries, often India, put them through a training program said to
be worth about $8K, and make them sign a promissory note for that amount. If
they stay on board two years, the 'loan' is forgiven. During those two years,
their salary is half the going rate for independents. The workers really believe
it's impossible to quit. They can't comprehend that they could make $8K more in
the first MONTH of being independent, even when I do the math on paper.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I am an unemployed Cobol pgmr.

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-30T12:54:42+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eaf1eda_2@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<20030425141115.02826.00000104@mb-m13.aol.com> <3ea9d18f.107449787@news.optonline.net> <b8dd1e$48n$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <3eaad61e.174163599@news.optonline.net> <3eab1b4f_2@127.0.0.1> <3eabc2f5.234803425@news.optonline.net> <b8jgu6$dg6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eaea6ad.424199912@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3eaea6ad.424199912@news.optonline.net...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
>
> It is impossible for an independent to work for LARGE organizations. They
all
> have Approved Vendor Lists. It is possible to work for medium- and
small-sized
> companies.
>
Be careful about use of "impossible" Robert. I have just completed a direct
contract with a major corporation. (Turnover 2 billion POUNDs a year...).
Such contracts are possible if you have an established track record and can
approach managers directly.

> I thought the normal split was worker 70%, contracting company 30%.
>

That is usual in the UK, but in the current market, it is also negotiable
(most things are, and that is why negotiation skills are worth
acquiring...). I had a number of contractors on a team of over 20 and none
of them were on more than 15% agency markup. I negotiated this with the
agencies concerned on the basis that, "if they wanted to place people..."
(Most did...) The quality of people was outstanding and these are some of
the brightest and most capable people I have ever worked with. I actually
learned a lot from managing them.

 This same corporation is currently rationalising all their contract
requirements (they have around 400 IT contractors) to be hadled through one
"interface" department, with the hope that economies of scale and squeezing
agency margins will result in a better deal for them and the contractors.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I am an unemployed Cobol pgmr.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-30T14:09:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8olfi$hjr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<20030425141115.02826.00000104@mb-m13.aol.com> <3ea9d18f.107449787@news.optonline.net> <b8dd1e$48n$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <3eaad61e.174163599@news.optonline.net> <3eab1b4f_2@127.0.0.1> <3eabc2f5.234803425@news.optonline.net> <b8jgu6$dg6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eaea6ad.424199912@news.optonline.net> <3eaf1eda_2@127.0.0.1>`

```

On 29-Apr-2003, "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> Be careful about use of "impossible" Robert. I have just completed a direct
> contract with a major corporation. (Turnover 2 billion POUNDs a year...).
> Such contracts are possible if you have an established track record and can
> approach managers directly.

The important part is that established track record.

An employer wanted to hire me (I was contracting with them at the time).   As
per rules, it posted the job opening - but in a place that was cheap and
unlikely to be seen.   I checked with them to find out when I needed to wear my
suit, and was told that I already had my job interview.

Good managers know how to use the system, and they know how to get what they
want despite the system.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I am an unemployed Cobol pgmr.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

```
>

…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>approach managers directly.

The smallest company on the Fortune _500_ has sales of more than $3B. When I
said LARGE, I was thinking of Fortune 100, where the cutoff is around $30-40B. 

> This same corporation is currently rationalising all their contract
>requirements (they have around 400 IT contractors) to be hadled through one
>"interface" department, with the hope that economies of scale and squeezing
>agency margins will result in a better deal for them and the contractors.

Many US companies are doing the same. They keep deleting agencies from their
Approved Vendor List. Problem is, the ones being deleted are the small,
low-margin operators; the ones retained are Big Five and middle tier. I don't
see how that reduces cost.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I am an unemployed Cobol pgmr.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2003-04-29T02:17:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030428221723.18135.00000275@mb-m29.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3eabc2f5.234803425@news.optonline.net>`

```
>From: robert@wagner.net  (Robert Wagner)
>Date: 4/27/03 10:56 AM Eastern Daylight Time

>Bill rates are as high as ever -- $100-150/hr. The problem is our cut keeps
>going down while the contracting company's cut goes up. I know for a fact I'm
>currently making less than 50% of my bill rate. 
>

Interesting.  I work as a consultant (they don't like us calling ourselves
contractors ::shrug::) for a large international consulting firm.  As well as
my duties at the client site I'm also what they call a staff manager.  This
entails such mundane duties as interfacing with the office on such issues as
handicap parking places, last minute client training requests, administrating
performance appraisals yada yada.  In this capacity I have 7 people under me. 
I know their bill rates and what they make. I know the percentages of bill rate
of the company's overhead, taxes and profit margin.  NONE of the people in my
group have a bill rate of $150/hr let alone $100/hr and ALL are making a very
good living wage for this area of the country. 

If you are working as a W2 employee you may look at your bill rate and see that
it is say $60/hr and you are grossing $25 or $27/hr.  Hey you say - that's less
than 50%.  Of course you don't take into account that the company you are
working for can't bill for your vacation time yet you are paid, they can't bill
for holidays yet you are paid, and they are paying for part of your benefits. 
Put this all together and you are getting more than 50% of your bill rate.
(Figures used for ease of math).

If on the other hand you have a different arraingement then you did some lousy
negotiating.

BTW - I chose to be a W2 with this firm because I prefer someone else doing the
hassle of paying ss and medicare and the cost of insurance, paid educational
benefits and so on.  Your mileage may vary.
```

```
Actually Peter, at least in the U.S., I am convinced we are the very
beginning of another high upswing. It will be different this time, with
traditional companies looking for ways to make money off cutting cost and
improving efficiency in their IT, DP, and MIS departments.

Innovation is probably going to play a really central role, but not in the
same way it did in the dot.com world. This time, management will have more
control, as well as be more tightly controlled. At least for a while.

One thing that COBOL and IBM SYSPROGs have got to realize though, they can't
come off with the typical arrogance that we displayed in the 1980s and
1990s. They really have to become team players and carry out management
policy without being obnoxious.

You would not believe the sour taste in the mouth of so many top level
management players over here. They have been burned and burned and burned
again by software people who think they know how to run a business better
than the management types who built the business in the first place.

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I am an unemployed Cobol pgmr.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-28T11:24:12+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eac66ad_2@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<20030425141115.02826.00000104@mb-m13.aol.com> <3ea9d18f.107449787@news.optonline.net> <b8dd1e$48n$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <3eaad61e.174163599@news.optonline.net> <3eab1b4f_2@127.0.0.1> <m4Uqa.28919$DX.1413227@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```

"TurkeyTrot" <turkeytrot@lycos.com> wrote in message
news:m4Uqa.28919$DX.1413227@twister.austin.rr.com...
> Actually Peter, at least in the U.S., I am convinced we are the very
> beginning of another high upswing. It will be different this time, with
> traditional companies looking for ways to make money off cutting cost and
> improving efficiency in their IT, DP, and MIS departments.
>

Man, I really hope you're right...

It grieves me to see so many good people out of work. For myself, my career
is almost over and I'm quite happy to take it easy for a while in a place
where the cost of living is about one third that of Europe and it is
certainly no hardship to be here.

> Innovation is probably going to play a really central role, but not in the
> same way it did in the dot.com world. This time, management will have more
> control, as well as be more tightly controlled. At least for a while.
>
> One thing that COBOL and IBM SYSPROGs have got to realize though, they
can't
> come off with the typical arrogance that we displayed in the 1980s and
> 1990s. They really have to become team players and carry out management
> policy without being obnoxious.
>

This has ALWAYS been a problem, particularly on Fortress COBOL sites. Over
many years both users and management were treated with a disdain that
bordered on contempt by the "technocrats". I have seen it time and again and
have had to spend more time repairing working relationships than actually
developing systems. I covered it in:
http://66.152.52.10/archives/v4/v40503.asp

(It must have struck a chord with a number of people; over 25,000 people
read this article within 4 weeks of it being published...)

Fortunately, the programmers of today are much more geared to working as a
team, as you mentioned.


> You would not believe the sour taste in the mouth of so many top level
> management players over here. They have been burned and burned and burned
> again by software people who think they know how to run a business better
> than the management types who built the business in the first place.
>

I certainly would believe it. It is not just endemic to the USA and you are
right that there is still smouldering resentment on the part of some
managers that dates back to the 70s. In those days these same people (who
are now "Captains of Industry") were department heads who had to try and get
IT (EDP , as it was then...) support from the Computer department. The way
they were treated has left scars that are deep to this day. Many of them are
THRILLED that the advent of Client/Server computing and the proliferation of
computers and IT skills has breached the walls of Fortress COBOL and they
have no sympathy whatsoever for the COBOL people. (In fact, even when there
are problems with their new Client/Server networks, they are much more
forgiving and understanding than they would be with their mainframe COBOL
people... It isn't fair, but it is the legacy of the 70s.

This problem will gradually resolve itself as the mainframe becomes more
integrated with the network, and support teams work more closely together.
Also many of these managers will retire in the next few years and the
upcoming breed of manager has no particular axe to grind, just sees the
mainframe as another (big) server.

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: I am an unemployed Cobol pgmr.

- **From:** richardson@eclecticsoftwaresolutions.com (Chris Richardson)
- **Date:** 2003-04-26T14:32:09-07:00

- **Message-ID:** `<b11feff.0304261332.5d8a663b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<20030425141115.02826.00000104@mb-m13.aol.com>`

```
I agree will all of the suggestions given so far. So, this is just one
more bit of supportive information...

(While this may seem like a self-serving, self-promoting post - it
actually is not...)

I wrote a book (recently published) exactly for those in your
situation (or potentially about to be in that situation). The title of
the book is "COBOL and Visual Basic on .NET: A Guide for the Reformed
Mainframe Programmer". I truely believe that this book (assuming that
you read it, really read it, practice the code samples, etc.) will
increase your chances of getting your next job assignment.

Becoming a mainframe programmer that understands both sides of the
fence - meaning on the mainframe and off of the mainframe - offers
great advantages to the typical employer. This has opened many doors
for me (it may work for you as well). The last few mainframe jobs that
I had were not pure mainframe jobs but rather jobs that dealt with
host integration (every thing from writing cobol batch pgms that
extracted and downloaded data to pump into SQL server to writing
Visual Basic Web programs that screen scraped mainframe CICS screens).

I beieve your COBOL/CICS skills are valuable. You need to continue
believing that as well....just repackage and enhance your offering.
Sale and promote yourself as an "opportunity" for corporations to
further leverage their existing investments, etc. Mainframe
programming is alive and well. It just seems that the mainframe is not
the "island" that it once was.

To find out more please visit either of the following 3 links:

http://www.amazon.com/exec/obidos/ASIN/1590590481/eclecticsoftw-20

http://www.eclecticsoftwaresolutions.com

http://apress.com/book/bookDisplay.html?bID=112

Good luck on your job search!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I am an unemployed Cobol pgmr.

- **From:** richardson@eclecticsoftwaresolutions.com (Chris Richardson)
- **Date:** 2003-04-27T02:25:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b11feff.0304270125.28130cdb@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<20030425141115.02826.00000104@mb-m13.aol.com> <b11feff.0304261332.5d8a663b@posting.google.com> <3eab1cac_2@127.0.0.1>`

```
I have followed several posts on this google thread and respect it as
one of the few serious places to hear voices to which I have long
wanted to speak to.

I realize that I'm a new poster to this thread. A new voice, I do
respect the opinions of others. As you know, I have a few opinions of
my own.

additional response below: <cr>

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3eab1cac_2@127.0.0.1>...
> "Chris Richardson" <richardson@eclecticsoftwaresolutions.com> wrote in
> message news:b11feff.0304261332.5d8a663b@posting.google.com...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Well every post you made so far has been... glad you are giving it up..<G>

So, Promote, Promote, Promote...but why?

Believe it or not, I promote my book...not for capital gains, but for
the same reasons that I wrote my book in the first place...to teach,
to give back to the mainframe community, the community that has served
me well through these years (I am serious).

G I realize that some very good products today are not on the market
any longer simply because of poor marketing. Yes, the one with the
vested interest lost out...but also, those that stood to reap the
benefits offered by the product lost out as well. If no one knows that
this book exists, what good is it? I'm not comfortable with just
saying "Oh, well, too bad for those that lost out."

Think about it...If I was writing/publishing a book for the Windows
and Web community, this would be an entirely different story. I could
simply advertise the product via an MSDN channel (i.e. PDC 2003,
Microsoft's Dev Days, TechNET), Fawcette's family of Magazines, PC
Magazine, on and on and on. But for the mainframe community, I have
this google group thread and a few website reviews. That's it.
Honorable, yes. But, certainly at a disadvantage...not just to the one
with the vested interest (me and my publisher)...but more importantly
for those that "may" stand to benefit from using the product (the
mainframe community that may want to learn about .NET).

On a slightly different note...

Yes, to promote a book (any book, certainly one that I wrote) may seem
at best self serving. While to WRITE a book, to spend a year or two
pouring out every ounce of thought, wisdom, and knowledge - and
opinion, on a subject that only few (it seems at times) believe in, is
hardly self serving at all (IMHO). If I was interested in capital
gains alone, I could have done better (monetarily speaking for the
number of hours spent) working minimum wage some where...or better
yet...perhaps contracting some where (average wages) cranking out
lines of code. <cr>

> 
> > I wrote a book (recently published) exactly for those in your
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> that." Anything else is flimflam.

I did not promise based on READING a book, I said that he/she would
have to "read it, really read it, practice the code samples". While
that is not a promise...I do not think it is flimflam. Well, I am
real. And Yes, I do believe that a person could increase their chances
of getting their next job assignment by reading my book - that is, if
the person was looking for a .NET, Windows and Web development type of
job. (All right, perhaps I should have qualified my earlier statements
as such).

Outrageous to make such of a claim? Not hardly (IMHO). I think that if
I had anything less to say while expecting someone to put their hard
earned dollars down to buy a book (not cheap) ...well (IMHO), now that
would be outrageous. OK, I guess, I'm taking a stand. Maybe it comes
down to how much you believe in what you are suggesting. Maybe this
sounds like too much of a stretch...perhaps that IS the
difference....REALLY believing in what you are saying....and really
practicing what you preach, etc.

Hey....what about the earlier post that suggested that learning Oracle
would be a good idea....Who's to say that that was really a good idea
or not? Did that post include stats on why Oracle or any other
relational database product would be time well spent? Why is that
opinion not Outrageous  or flimflam? If that poster had had a good
source to recommend to the job seeker to expedite their Oracle
training, would it had been ok to suggest it (EVEN if the poster
happened to have been the author of the source)?

The real point (still) is that to increase your chances of finding a
job (in today's environment, versus the Y2K days) you do need to "keep
your saw sharpened". I think we all agree on this point.

My post is suggesting expanding your skillset with .NET (and then
some). The only real difference, is that I happen to be the author of
a book that speaks to this training direction. At the same time, I
know that there is not another book that presents the .NET topic to
the COBOL/CICS community the way that I do. Ok, this sounds like an
opinion...but until someone proves otherwise, I'm feeling that it is
nearing "fact" status. Show me any other book that speaks to the
mainframe community, from the perspective of the mainframe progammer
that completely addresses the Windows/Web platform. I stand open for
correction...I have not found any BOOK that does that...that's the
ONLY reason that I wrote the book - to share, to transfer knowledge.

If there were tons of books (or even one or two for that matter)
serving the purpose, filling the nitch, the void, that I'm trying to
fill, I would have never gone through the trouble (I guess, my
publisher would not have either)....I challenage you to tell me of
just ONE other book that does this as I have done (presenting the .NET
Windows and Web technologies to the mainframe community from the
perspective of a mainframe programmer providing COBOl.NET and Visual
Basic.NET code examples).

(partially restating) People should write books to share information
that they believe in....and maybe/hopefully...share information that
is not easily obtainable elsewhere. To do otherwise...now that is
flimflam. To expect people to spend their hard earned dollars on
something that you don't believe in, that you are unwilling to take a
stand on...again..THAT is flimflam (IMHO).

By the way, thanks for taking the time to read through these
responses. <cr>

> 
> > Becoming a mainframe programmer that understands both sides of the
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…
> ---= 19 East/West-Coast Specialized Servers - Total Privacy via Encryption =---
```

### OT: Microsoft Worm

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-04-25T19:47:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EA98CE6.32E2687A@shaw.ca>`

```
I see the SOBs are still targeting us claiming it is an MS Security
Fixpack. I say 'us' because I saw some recognizable names without going
through the whole list - Foodman (still that might improve his coding
style <G>), Arnold, moi of course and RW.

Immediate delete of course !

I had one innocent looking worm some time back - didn't appear to be
doing any harm. Then when I looked closely it was generating just
garbage - from memory a file such as, say, excomm.exe would give me an
extra excomm.exe.vbs, or some such. What triggered it when I used
Explorer was I found whole lots of junk ABCD.vbs, EGH.vbs etc....

The irritating thing is you can't do a damn thing to eliminate them in
Windows, (or not in anything I've found). Fortunately I was aware of the
ATTRIBUTE feature in DOS. I also have two old PC Magazine routines "DR"
and "CO" which are text versions of Explorer. Laboriously using DR and
COM to list directories then for the most part use ATTRIB to change the
properties and then for the majority do a wildcard delete. Worked - but
a pain in the ass. Initially the worm was recreating the spurious files
after I had deleted them - that meant locating and eliminating their
'worm' files first, which were doing the generating.

The troubling thing is - If MS totally delete any reference to the old
DOS routines, (I'm currently using Win 98), - then where the hell are we
!

Jimmy
```

```
Right click, pick properties
Check (or uncheck) Set Attribute/Read Only/Hidden
Select OK

> DOS routines, (I'm currently using Win 98), - then where the hell are we
```

```
I have received this virus at least 5 or 6 times.  My ISP deletes
them, but I have been careful to use their web interface to preview
all email, since they offer an optional spam filter.  The web
interface also allows me to check my home email from work.  I get
about 80 emails a day, and virtually all of them are spam.  I don't
need to tell you what kind of crap I receive, including spam in
Chinese!

> 
> The interesting thing is that I went out to a couple of anti-virus
> sites, and I found no reference to this e-mail.  That's odd (unless the

The recent ones have been discussed on Steve Gibson's security
newsgroup, and in other fora.
http://grc.com/default.htm


> 
> (I'm -this close- to figuring out how to set an auto-delete rule in
> Netscape...  I know how to do it in Outlook, but I switched to NS a
> while back because I didn't like going to a separate app for NGs...)

About a year or more ago, I uninstalled Windows Scripting Host,
specifically to prevent programs executing from email attachments.  My
feeling is that it's *my* computer, and it should be my choice as to
which programs run on my computer.

But it's getting much harder to control what actually runs on our
computers.
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

>Fixpack. I say 'us' because I saw some recognizable names without going

I currently have 19 .exe files in my email \download directory, accumulated over
a month. I just scanned them with the latest McAfree, which found no viruses in
any of them. They all have the same size -- 155,648. I'm guessing their purpose
is not destruction but rather getting you to spend money at their sponsor's Web
site.

Almost all email worms target MS Outlook because it's so widely used. For this
reason, I don't use Outlook, instead I use Eudora. Kim Kommando, talk radio
computer guru, said she uses Eurora for the same reason.
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ea9de1d.110664366@news.optonline.net...
> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>

this
> reason, I don't use Outlook, instead I use Eudora. Kim Kommando, talk
radio
> computer guru, said she uses Eurora for the same reason.

I use Outlook but I keep it updated (MS provide about 3 small security plugs
a month that are quick to install.)

I also have, and sometimes use, Eudora. It is a very good package, but I'm
not persuaded that it is any more virus proof than anything else. My local
ISP was very smug for some time about his Unix system not being subject to
attack and even advised the user base to drop MicroSoft and use other tools.
Until he was hit...

The trouble is that it's easy to become overconfident because you are using
a non-MS product...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Microsoft Worm

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-04-26T13:08:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0304261208.138d3fe0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3EA98CE6.32E2687A@shaw.ca> <3ea9de1d.110664366@news.optonline.net> <3eaa1c24_1@127.0.0.1>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote 

> I use Outlook but I keep it updated (MS provide about 3 small security plugs
> a month that are quick to install.)

Doesn't it occur as to _why_ it needs 3 plugs a month ?

There is a fundemental flaw in Outlook in that it tries to be
'helpful' and execute or display whatever EMail comes in.  When
associated with a flawed 'file type' system that relies on parts of
the name, and almost zero security, then it is very easy to get
Outlook to run malicious code without the user being involved.

Of course it may be that you believe that MS is EMailing you security
updates.  These are _not_ from MS.  Just because they claim to be MS
security updates does not mean they are not viruses.
 
> I also have, and sometimes use, Eudora. It is a very good package, but I'm
> not persuaded that it is any more virus proof than anything else. 

Ask yourself: does it execute attachments automatically and silently
when an EMail is opened ?  No.

> My local
> ISP was very smug for some time about his Unix system not being subject to
> attack and even advised the user base to drop MicroSoft and use other tools.
> Until he was hit...

What by ?  There are 'hits' that are completely system independent,
such as denial of service attacks.  That's not a virus.
 
> The trouble is that it's easy to become overconfident because you are using
> a non-MS product...

Certainly the major problem is that MS is almost a monoculture.  This
makes it an attractive target (ie little wastage, very high hit
count). Unix/Linux systems are extremely diverse.  Even if two
machines have the same distribution and version they may have
different compiles, and thus such things as buffer overruns simply
don't work.

But Unix/Linux just does not have the fundemental lack of security
built in from the very fabric of Windows.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Microsoft Worm

```
patches over and above SP1. If you apply them all you are pretty secure. I
do, and I don't worry about it. I also run Panda antivirus which creamed the
top 6 packages in benchmarks 18 months ago. It was the ONLY one that
detected every virus, trojan, and worm, and gave NO false positives. These
stupid posts which prompted this thread were intercepted and destroyed long
before my ISP got on the case.

> Of course it may be that you believe that MS is EMailing you security
> updates.  These are _not_ from MS.  Just because they claim to be MS

The updates are not mailed to me. They are downloaded from a secure website
with my full knowledge and approval (or not) and it is up to me whether I
accept them, and, if I do, whether and when and under what circumstances I
apply them. The auto update in XP is a very useful feature and it seems to
work very effectively. I have had  a couple of small annoying bugs in both
Excel and PowerPoint fixed by applying these updates. It is a free service
that is worth more than that <G>.

>

I don't know (or care <G>). I don't want to descend into a debate over the
relative merits of Unix or MS (I'm not qualified to do so, for one thing). I
use what works for me. As some of the software I develop has to be certified
on an intel chip using MS OSes, I run a P4 under XP pro. It is not because I
am a MicroSoft fanatic...

it because it has been patched not to. One could argue that it should never
have happened in the first place, but I have yet to see computer software in
ANY environment that was perfect (including, sadly, my own...<G>). There are
no perfect programs; there are programs that haven't crashed
YET...(fortunately, I have written a few of these and one of them is still
running after 31 years...<G>). That is just as true for non-MS environments
as it is for MS.


> But Unix/Linux just does not have the fundemental lack of security
> built in from the very fabric of Windows.

I don't know, but I'll take your word for it. (I'd be areful about judging
software against "fundamentals" that were true 10 years ago... a lot of
water has gone under the bridge since then. There is no comparison between
Windows XP and Win 3.1, irrespective of whether or not they are
"fundamentally" the same...). As long as you remember that the world of MS
software changes rapidly and the resources of one of the World's largest
corporations are applied to constantly improving it. I have to say that XP
is the first MS OS I have been completely happy with. It has never crashed
or frozen or lost data and it handles application abends in an exemplary
fashion. MS obviously spent a huge amount of time and money to improve the
quality. It shows.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Microsoft Worm

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-27T18:57:43+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8fv4f$ije$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3EA98CE6.32E2687A@shaw.ca> <3ea9de1d.110664366@news.optonline.net> <3eaa1c24_1@127.0.0.1> <217e491a.0304261208.138d3fe0@posting.google.com> <b8fq3b01snp@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```
Grinder wrote:

>  The "buffer overflow problem" is not unique to
> windows, however.

Certainly not.  But to get a useful result from a buffer overrun it must 
overrun into a specific place and set the bytes to exactly the right value.

If all copies of, say IIS version x, are identiacal then what works on one 
copy will work on the next.  Because Apache will be recompiled for each 
distro and may use different librariy versions, different compiler options, 
even different target CPUs (386 to 686) then the bytes needed to cause a 
useful overrun will be different for every copy, or at least will be wrong 
for the majority of them.

This means that, while a buffer overrun may corrupt, say, Apache, and may 
cause it to fail, it is unlikely to cause it to execute specific malicious 
code.

Having Apache fall over is a much lesser problem than what happens with IIS 
where malicious code executes on a buffer overrun and this turns IIS into a 
weapon to attack other sites.
```

## Thread: MicroFocus Cobol Sequential Files — 13 this month / 14 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### MicroFocus Cobol Sequential Files

- **From:** "John Simpson" <jasimp@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-25T20:32:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YXgqa.1933$XE.48494@news1.east.cox.net>`

```
Hi All,

Forgive me if this topic has been covered, but I'm new to the group and a
search on the subject has not been fruitful.

I have an application where I must create sequential files with variable
length records. This is easy, but the problem is that the end user (HIPAA)
cannot tolerate the CRLF record terminator at the end of each record.

Do any of you Guru's have a solution to write these records without the
CRLF?

Thanks in advance.

John
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus Cobol Sequential Files

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-26T09:19:09+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8c8r8$rqq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<YXgqa.1933$XE.48494@news1.east.cox.net>`

```
John Simpson wrote:

> I have an application where I must create sequential files with variable
> length records. This is easy, but the problem is that the end user (HIPAA)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> CRLF?

Variable length records need to have a terminator of some sort to indicate 
the end of the record.  Either you are using another character to indicate 
end or are putting size counts at the start.

For example EDIFACT uses apostrophe as a segment terminator.

One way to do it is to use a single byte sized sequential file and write 
each byte.  This can be done in some low level routine that is passed each 
complete record.  It can be slow but is standard.

Some vendors offer CALLable routines to do this.  see your vendors user 
manual.
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus Cobol Sequential Files

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-25T22:02:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Nfiqa.3518$%_3.2808196@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<YXgqa.1933$XE.48494@news1.east.cox.net>`

```
"John Simpson" <jasimp@earthlink.net> wrote in message
news:YXgqa.1933$XE.48494@news1.east.cox.net...
> Hi All,
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> CRLF?

A. HIPAA is not a user
B. HIPAA specs say nothing about CRLF termination of anything.
C. Records in ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL files only end with CRLF or LF if
you put them there. (But records in ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL files
will on PC (CRLF) or 'Nix (LF)).
D. Look up RECORD IS VARYING for the FD

If your user is on PC, there is no such thing as 'variable length records'
which are not terminated (delimited). I suspect what you are trying to do is
create ANSI ASC EDI and are trying to end each record with the segment
terminator character rather than CRLF.

Depending on what O/S you are on, what compiler you use, and maybe even what
software your user is using, this can be a hell of a trick using COBOL. One
way which works more or less 'universally'  is to create the file as
ORGANIZATION SEQUENTIAL with a fixed record size, then count the characters
in each record yourself, wrapping when necessary. When the file is saved to
disk, it will look like a 'continuous stream wrapped'  EDI file. I did this
for AllState Insurance about eight years ago.. real time creation of an ANSI
interchange in response to a CICS transaction. It took me more than an
afternoon. (A lot more!).

Expert EDI progamming assistance available at contact info below.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus Cobol Sequential Files

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-25T20:53:19-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WdSdndjvV9ATezSjXTWJhQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<YXgqa.1933$XE.48494@news1.east.cox.net> <Nfiqa.3518$%_3.2808196@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```

"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

> > Hi All,
> >
> > Forgive me if this topic has been covered, but I'm new to the group and
a
> > search on the subject has not been fruitful.
> >
> > I have an application where I must create sequential files with variable
> > length records. This is easy, but the problem is that the end user
(HIPAA)
> > cannot tolerate the CRLF record terminator at the end of each record.
> >
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> which are not terminated (delimited). I suspect what you are trying to do
is
> create ANSI ASC EDI and are trying to end each record with the segment
> terminator character rather than CRLF.

Not so. In some incarnations of PC COBOL, variable length records do not
have terminators, CRLF or otherwise. These records do, however, have a
record predecessor - that is, some amount of bytes on the front of each
record containing the length of the following record.

LINE SEQUENTIAL records do have a CRLF terminator and often these records
are trailing-blank truncated. While these records are of variable lengths, I
don't think you should call them variable length records (even though they
are).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus Cobol Sequential Files

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-26T14:42:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1Vwqa.3702$%_3.2978000@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<YXgqa.1933$XE.48494@news1.east.cox.net> <Nfiqa.3518$%_3.2808196@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <WdSdndjvV9ATezSjXTWJhQ@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:WdSdndjvV9ATezSjXTWJhQ@giganews.com...
>
> > If your user is on PC, there is no such thing as 'variable length
records'
> > which are not terminated (delimited). I suspect what you are trying to
do
> is
> > create ANSI ASC EDI and are trying to end each record with the segment
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> record containing the length of the following record.

That's why I said to some extent solving this challenge may depend on the
user's software: to interpret data arranged as suggested...

<length-word><data-of-length-length-word><length-word><data-of-length-length
-word><length-word><data-of-length-length-word>.....

.. the user's software must be ready to handle it. For example, Notepad will
not interpret the above correctly.

It would be best if OP  described exactly the format of the output data, and
on what O/S-compiler this output is to be created..then we could point in
the right direction. (I have thirty cents says what he wants is a stream of
data representing an ANSI ASC X12 interchange).

MCM
```

```
Granted, the ANS X12 (EDI) standard is generalized. In practice, aren't messages
usually comma delimited ASCII terminated by cr-lf or nl? The ones I've seen have
been.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus Cobol Sequential Files

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-27T17:16:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cgUqa.4025$%_3.3229368@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<YXgqa.1933$XE.48494@news1.east.cox.net> <Nfiqa.3518$%_3.2808196@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <3eaae64d.178306662@news.optonline.net> <xMFqa.3854$%_3.3083513@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <3eabd449.239240589@news.optonline.net>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3eabd449.239240589@news.optonline.net...
>
> Granted, the ANS X12 (EDI) standard is generalized. In practice, aren't
messages
> usually comma delimited ASCII terminated by cr-lf or nl? The ones I've
seen have
> been.

Usually? No. Matter of fact I've never seen comma used as a control
character in ANSI ASC X12 and I've been working with the format for twenty
years (back to when WalMart used a proprietary 'flat' file rather than ANSI
ASC X12). The most common element separator is the asterisk. (No empirical
data, that's just gut feel).

'n/l'  is only a common "printed symbol for the segment terminator" and may
in fact represent different characters. The most common segment terminator
characters I've encountered are "@" , "~" and x'0A' (line feed).

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus Cobol Sequential Files

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-28T01:26:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eac2d2f.262001840@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<YXgqa.1933$XE.48494@news1.east.cox.net> <Nfiqa.3518$%_3.2808196@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <3eaae64d.178306662@news.optonline.net> <xMFqa.3854$%_3.3083513@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <3eabd449.239240589@news.optonline.net> <cgUqa.4025$%_3.3229368@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3eabd449.239240589@news.optonline.net...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>characters I've encountered are "@" , "~" and x'0A' (line feed).

In Unix terminology, x'0A' is called New Line (nl). If Mr. Simpson is going to
send the file via FTP, the solution may be simple. FTP will convert the PC
record terminator 0D0A to the Unix terminator 0A.
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus Cobol Sequential Files

- **From:** "Paul Hoberg" <nospamphoberg@att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-26T19:08:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uOAqa.632544$L1.180396@sccrnsc02>`
- **References:** `<YXgqa.1933$XE.48494@news1.east.cox.net>`

```
Hey, John,
I have used Byte-stream files for years.  You can do anything you want - add
whatever number of bytes to the end of the file, read or rewrite anywhere in
the file, etc.  So build your own records, comprised of anything you need,
and use byte-stream to write them.  You just need to keep track of how long
the file is, so you can continue to append each record with your specified
length, then add that length to the current file size, to be ready for the
next record.  And, IT IS THE FASTEST FILE ACCESS I HAVE EVER USED IN
MICROFOCUS COBOL!  Maybe by an order of magnitude!
Good luck,
Paul

"John Simpson" <jasimp@earthlink.net> wrote in message
news:YXgqa.1933$XE.48494@news1.east.cox.net...

Hi All,

Forgive me if this topic has been covered, but I'm new to the group and a
search on the subject has not been fruitful.

I have an application where I must create sequential files with variable
length records. This is easy, but the problem is that the end user (HIPAA)
cannot tolerate the CRLF record terminator at the end of each record.

Do any of you Guru's have a solution to write these records without the
CRLF?

Thanks in advance.

John
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus Cobol Sequential Files

- **From:** "Paul Hoberg" <nospamphoberg@att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-28T23:12:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nzira.677929$S_4.726673@rwcrnsc53>`
- **References:** `<YXgqa.1933$XE.48494@news1.east.cox.net> <uOAqa.632544$L1.180396@sccrnsc02>`

```
I didn't mention that NetExpress' help tells how to deal with byte-stream
files.  Here's what it says about byte-stream files, followed by a
description of one of the routines - how to read from the file:
------------------------------------------------------------------------
The byte-stream file routines enable you to read and write data files
without the need to adhere to COBOL record definitions.

CBL_CLOSE_FILE Close byte-stream file
CBL_CREATE_FILE Create byte-stream file
CBL_FLUSH_FILE Flush byte-stream file buffers to disk
CBL_FREE_RECORD_LOCK Release a record lock on a file
CBL_GET_RECORD_LOCK Release a record lock on a file
CBL_OPEN_FILE Open byte-stream file
CBL_READ_FILE Read byte-stream file
CBL_TEST_RECORD_LOCK Obtain a record lock on a file
CBL_WRITE_FILE Write byte-stream file

For all these routines, if the routine is successful the RETURN-CODE
register is set to zero. If the routine fails, the RETURN-CODE register
contains a file status value indicating the failure. This file status is
always the standard ANSI'74 file status value. If no ANSI'74 file status is
defined for the error, an extended file status is returned (9/
nnn where nnn is the run-time system error number).

For this to work you should use RETURN-CODE and not have a RETURNING clause.
If RETURN-CODE is nonzero after calling a byte-stream routine, move it to a
PIC XX COMP-X data item to process it as a file status. For example:

 01 file-stat      pic xx comp-x.

 01    redefines file-stat.
     03 fs-byte-1  pic x.
     03 fs-byte-2  pic x comp-x.
   . . .
     call "CBL_xxx_FILE" using <parameters>
     if return-code not = 0
        move return-code to file-stat
           . . .

At this point fs-byte-1 contains "9" and fs-byte-2 contains the run-time
system error number.

Copyright (C) 2000 MERANT International Ltd. All rights reserved.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Read bytes from a file.

call "CBL_READ_FILE" using file-handle file-offset byte-count flags buffer

Parameters:

file-handle             pic x(4).

file-offset             pic x(8) comp-x.
byte-count           pic x(4) comp-x.
flags                    pic x comp-x.
buffer                  pic x(n).

On Entry:

file-handle The file handle returned when the file was opened.
file-offset The offset in the file at which to read. This field is currently
limited to a maximum value of x"00FFFFFFFF".
byte-count The number of bytes to read. This field is currently limited to a
maximum value of x"00FFFF".
flags This parameter can take the following values:
 0 Standard read
 128 Return the current file size in file-offset

On Exit:

file-offset Contains the current file size on return if flags is set to 128
on entry.

buffer The buffer into which the bytes are read. It is your responsibility
to ensure that the buffer is large enough to hold the number of bytes to be
read.

Comments:

The success of the call can be checked by examining RETURN-CODE.

When using this routine to read a file that is contained in a .lbr file, the
end-of-file status is not returned. To ensure you only read the file you
want, obtain the size of the file first (set flags to 128), and only read up
to that size.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

"Paul Hoberg" <nospamphoberg@att.net> wrote in message
news:uOAqa.632544$L1.180396@sccrnsc02...

Hey, John,
I have used Byte-stream files for years.  You can do anything you want - add
whatever number of bytes to the end of the file, read or rewrite anywhere in
the file, etc.  So build your own records, comprised of anything you need,
and use byte-stream to write them.  You just need to keep track of how long
the file is, so you can continue to append each record with your specified
length, then add that length to the current file size, to be ready for the
next record.  And, IT IS THE FASTEST FILE ACCESS I HAVE EVER USED IN
MICROFOCUS COBOL!  Maybe by an order of magnitude!
Good luck,
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus Cobol Sequential Files

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-04-27T23:54:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0304272254.729f743c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<YXgqa.1933$XE.48494@news1.east.cox.net>`

```
"John Simpson" <jasimp@earthlink.net> wrote

> Do any of you Guru's have a solution to write these records without the
> CRLF?

I don't think that you have been asked whether you mean 'without cr
and lf' or 'without cr or lf'.  Do you want it without any control
characters at all because the printable segment terminator character
is sufficient.

I have found that with EDIFACT messages some user want CRLF, some LF
only, some neither, just the segments.

Reading these EDIFACT messages I process the segments and ignore any
control characters.  For output I have a configuration item, per
destination user, for any control character requirements.
```

```
LX-i <DanielJS@Knology.net> wrote 

> Has anyone here ever used COBOLScript?

Yes. I bought a copy for Linux and use it to prototype SQL stuff.  It
is not  Cobol but near enough to be useful.  Unfortunately it can't do
CALL/CANCEL of another CobolScript program so COPY is the only way to
build programs from standard parts.

But for simple scripting that looks like Cobol its OK.  Get the
professional for SQL, its cheap.

Oh yeah, it _requires_ full stops on every simple statement.
```

```
Well, with the other web scripting languages I've used, we pretty much

sounds like it would do pretty much the same thing.

Someone suggested it as a way for us to utilize the COBOL coders' 
knowledge base, while writing web-based applications.  I looked into it, 
and as someone who already knows VBScript (ASP) and PHP, it looked like 
a lot of extra typing.  (Of course, comparatively, COBOL generally -is- 
a lot more typing that those other languages...)

Does it support SQL through ODBC, or are there native libraries for 
different databases?

> Oh yeah, it _requires_ full stops on every simple statement.

Did you notice how well it performed?  Did the site you built get 
stressed that much?  I'm curious as to its performance as compared with 
other languages.
```
