## Thread: Portability of 100 percent display was Re: Style (was: Sets and subsets — 3 this month / 5 total

### Portability of 100 percent display was Re: Style (was: Sets and subsets

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-04-29T11:15:18-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EAE88F6.8687CB15@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3EA55E79.6090702@newsguy.com> <3ea5ed65.19257366@news.optonline.net> <3EA69C04.7090208@newsguy.com> <3ea88130.21326254@news.optonline.net> <b8ab71$1dv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eaaa1e8.160795747@news.optonline.net> <b8h8s8$c6r$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eac5f18.274780521@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304272216.24c66f64@posting.google.com> <3ead12ba.320773761@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> 
> riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> I meant exportable as flat files, after using the file system's copy tool.

100 percent display is not necessarily easily portable between systems. 
Which code page of EBCDIC or ISO 646 are you writing?  It could even be
ISO 10646 or Unicode which are variable multibyte or double byte code
sets.  Is the file itself delimited by size or by delimiters and if so
which delimiters?  XML also presumes a code set so this is a particular
encoding and depending on the platform might have to be declared in the
program.  EBCDIC won't work too well with XML.
 
> 
> >But even with 100% DISPLAY and ignoring the minor issues that C-ISAM
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> internal use.
```

<a id="t-5c2ac2261d"></a>
## Thread: Signed data in seq. file not being converted properly — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Signed data in seq. file not being converted properly

- **From:** jsteele@garvin-allen.com (Jackie Steele)
- **Date:** 2003-04-29T07:45:35-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e331c06f.0304290645.6eecc394@posting.google.com>`

```
I am just trying out the Fujitsu 3.0 version of cobol.  I have
compiled a program which reads sequential records from a file and
performs calculations on the data.  The program originated from an IBM
AS400.

The data has signed values in it i.e. 0000270{ which when read and
displayed should be 00002700 but it is giving me 0000270; so it fails
the NUMERIC test.

Here is another example:

0000743} should be 7430- but I gte 743=

Now if the signed value is A-I it will convert it properly.

0000579b should be 5792 and it is.

Any ideas on my problem.  This have never been an issue on mini/main
frame systems.
```

#### ↳ Re: Signed data in seq. file not being converted properly

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-04-29T14:32:16-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EAEB720.5F869AA9@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<e331c06f.0304290645.6eecc394@posting.google.com>`

```
Jackie Steele wrote:
> 
> I am just trying out the Fujitsu 3.0 version of cobol.  I have
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> frame systems.

It reads like you have just gotten into the EBCDIC ASCII (ISO)
conversion problem.  This is because the code for +0 or -0 on the
mainframe using your default codepage doesn't translate to +0 or -0 in
codepage on the target system.  Using SIGN IS SEPARATE on your data and
doing the appropriate conversion should work.
```

<a id="t-424a21fcdf"></a>
## Thread: does methode (paragraphe) in cobol has input arguments and output arguments — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### does methode (paragraphe) in cobol has input arguments and output arguments

- **From:** "Reda" <Bendraou_Reda@yahoo.fr>
- **Date:** 2003-04-29T18:03:08+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8m7ii$js1$1@vishnu.jussieu.fr>`

```
hi, Im  trying to map cobol concepts to Java, and im wondering if pargraphes
in cobol have arguments in/output and we spicifie that, It would be nice, if
i could have an example.

Thank you for all whou can give me a helping hand.
```

#### ↳ Re: does methode (paragraphe) in cobol has input arguments and output arguments

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-29T12:19:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8mc72$hfs$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b8m7ii$js1$1@vishnu.jussieu.fr>`

```
No,  COBOL paragraphs do not take "arguments".  However, nested programs do.
Also, COBOL supports OO Objects and methods - which obviously DO also take
arguments.  (This was an extension to the '85 Standard but is included in
the 2002 ISO COBOL Standard).
```

---

<a id="t-cd2606160a"></a>
## Thread: Has anyone used the new Debug Tool w/Assembler? — 3 this month / 4 total

### Has anyone used the new Debug Tool w/Assembler?

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attgloabl.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-29T13:34:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EAEE1C5.B97F27EE@attgloabl.net>`

```
We have received the new V3R1 of IBM Debug Tool, which will offer a kind
of low level Assembler Language debugging support (no symbolics, but
otherwise, normal facilities, I think).  It should be installed in a few
days (the Sys Prog told me he had to order and install a prerequiisite
first).

I was wondering whether anyone has used this version to debug Assembler
code?  If so, what do you think of it?  I've used the Assembler Debugger
that comes with the HLASM Toolkit, and I don't much like it - maybe it
is just "too VM/CMS" to suit me.

I'm working on a COBOL replacement of a 35 year old Assembler program
that does manufacturing assembly item "explosions".  I plan to try to
put the Assembler program and the COBOL program both under Debug Tool,
so that I can test them side by side.  If the plan doesn't work out, I
can always use Debug Tool for the COBOL and the HLASM Toolkit's Debugger
for the Assembler program.
```

- **Date:** 2003-04-30T00:00:59+02:00

- **Message-ID:** `<b8msf3$27h$1@innferno.news.tiscali.de>`
- **References:** `<3EAEE1C5.B97F27EE@attgloabl.net>`

```
Colin,

we just installed this version but I only run some tests using Cobol and
C/C++.
Both works fin if you also apply the LE maintenance.

I might be able to run a test using assembler. However I feel this should be
discussed
in a HLASM newsgroup

Roland

"Colin Campbell" <cmcampb@attgloabl.net> schrieb im Newsbeitrag
news:3EAEE1C5.B97F27EE@attgloabl.net...

> of low level Assembler Language debugging support (no symbolics, but
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Has anyone used the new Debug Tool w/Assembler?

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attgloabl.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-29T16:28:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.asm370,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EAF0AAA.5339DD26@attgloabl.net>`
- **References:** `<3EAEE1C5.B97F27EE@attgloabl.net> <b8msf3$27h$1@innferno.news.tiscali.de>`

```
Roland,
Thank you for the response.  One reason that I posted to the COBOL group as well
as the ASM370 group is that Debug Tool can be used on COBOL programs that were
not compiled with the TEST option, through use of what is called the
"disassembly view".  In other words, I think the same feature that lets you
debug true Assembly Language code is what allows you to debug COBOL compiled
without TEST.

Did you get the Advanced Functions optional part of the product?  Some of our
programmers are fans of Xpediter, because it will show the variables used in
each statement as it is executed.  Advanced Functions gives Debug Tool that
capability.
Colin

Roland Schiradin wrote:

> Colin,
>
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…

## Thread: PLUG: txt2pdf 6.5 — 2 this month / 3 total

### PLUG: txt2pdf 6.5

- **From:** "Sanface Software" <sanface@sanface.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-30T09:39:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f2a191bd471c95dc3308e9a61a7f9441.93245@mygate.mailgate.org>`

```
We would like to announce txt2pdf 6.5 version. 
http://www.sanface.com/txt2pdf.html
txt2pdf is shareware; it is a very flexible and powerful Perl5 script
that converts text files to PDF format files, so you can use it in every
operating systems supported by Perl5, including MPE/iX, OpenVMS and
EPOC.
It's simple to design background like invoices, orders etc.
Here nice examples made using txt2pdf PRO
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/Purchase_Order.pdf
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/invoice.pdf
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/hfmus.pdf
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/heraldbill.pdf
If you prefer we also distribute executables for Windows, Linux,
Solaris, AIX, HP-UX, and Mac OS X. Inside the Windows version is Visual
txt2pdf, a VB GUI.
Remember to read the Hachette Filipacchi Media U.S. project at
http://www.aboutlegacycoding.com/?AURL=%2FArticles%2FV%2FV40603%2Easp

What's new in this version

Simplified Chinese font support

Test txt2pdf 6.5!
You can find it at http://www.sanface.com/txt2pdf.html
```

<a id="t-1de8143414"></a>
## Thread: SMF Processing via COBOL PGMS — 1 msgs

### SMF Processing via COBOL PGMS

- **From:** Steve Thompson <n_o_spam.steve_t@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-30T23:53:53-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EB09A51.5020703@ix.netcom.com>`

```
Anyone know of COBOL based programs for processing the SMF data 
on a mainframe? I've started writing such, and processing the 110 
record is not going to be much fun. The type 30 is painful enough.

For those of you who know what SMF records look like, yes, I can 
write all the routines in ALC. However, some of the records can 
be handled within a COBOL program, and once done, the person to 
maintain this will have an easier time of things (than I am in 
doing the original development).
```
